1. **Bailiwick violations** — DNS responses containing records *outside* the queried zone indicate poisoning.
2. **TTL anomalies** — Inconsistencies between expected and observed TTL values.
3. **Out-of-sequence responses** — DNS replies arriving *before* the query (timing anomaly).
4. **Duplicate response IDs** — Same Transaction ID observed with conflicting answers.
5. Tools: `passivedns` for long-term logging, `dnscap` for packet capture, `Suricata` for IDS rules.

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Email Spoofing

**Email Spoofing Mechanism (3 Marks):**

Email Spoofing is the forgery of an email's identifying headers — particularly the `From:`, `Reply-To:`, and `Return-Path:` fields in the SMTP envelope — to make the message appear to originate from a trusted sender. Attackers exploit the **inherent trust** users place in familiar email addresses to deliver phishing, Business Email Compromise (BEC), or malware payloads.

SMTP does not authenticate senders natively. The attacker simply connects to any mail server (open relay or compromised) and sets arbitrary `MAIL FROM` and `RCPT TO` values.

**Email Authentication Mechanisms (3 Marks):**

| Mechanism | Function | Forensic Utility |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** | DNS TXT record lists authorized sending IPs for the domain | `pass` or `fail` result in email headers reveals spoofed source |
| **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** | Cryptographic signature in email header, verified via public DNS key | Signature mismatch indicates tampering or spoofing |
| **DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** | Policy framework combining SPF and DKIM results | `p=reject` or `p=quarantine` policies indicate domain's stance |

**Three Forensic Artifacts (1 Mark):**

1. **SPF `fail` / `softfail` result** in the `Received-SPF` header.
2. **DKIM signature verification failure** (`body hash did not verify`).
3. **DMARC alignment failure** in the `Authentication-Results` header.
4. *(Bonus)* Reverse DNS (PTR record) mismatch with claimed sending domain.
5. *(Bonus)* Originating IP in `Received:` chain traced to a known malicious ASN.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Spoofing (Module 2):**

- **Spoofing** = deliberate falsification of network identifiers to impersonate a trusted entity.
- **IP Spoofing** operates at **Layer 3** (Source IP field); used in SYN floods, reflection DDoS, trust exploitation.
- **MAC Spoofing** operates at **Layer 2** (48-bit NIC address); bypasses 802.1X port security.
- **ARP Spoofing** exploits stateless ARP via **gratuitous ARP replies**; creates a **Man-in-the-Middle** position.
- **DNS Spoofing** forges DNS responses; **Kaminsky attack** exploits the **16-bit Transaction ID** space.
- **Email Spoofing** forges SMTP headers; defended by **SPF + DKIM + DMARC** triad.
- **Web Spoofing** forges URLs/TLS indicators; detect via **certificate chain validation**.
- **Initial TTL values** for fingerprinting: **64 = Linux**, **128 = Windows**, **255 = Network device**, **32 = legacy Windows**, **60 = AIX/HP-UX**.
- **TTL decay formula**: $H = T_{initial} - T_{observed}$ — used to estimate network distance and detect origin OS mismatch.
- **Hop Count Anomaly Detection (HCAD)** score helps quantify spoofing probability.
- **Forensic artifacts** to capture: PCAP files, switch syslog, NetFlow, firewall logs, ARP cache snapshots, DNS query logs, email headers.
- **Chain of custody** must be maintained with **SHA-256 / MD5 hash verification** at every stage.
- **uRPF (Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding)** is a key network defense against IP spoofing egress.
- **802.1X**, **port security**, and **DHCP snooping** are key defenses against MAC/ARP spoofing.
- **DNSSEC** is the cryptographic defense against DNS cache poisoning.
- **BGP route filtering** and **BCP 38 (RFC 2827)** provide upstream-level anti-spoofing guidance.
- **Tools to remember**: `Wireshark`, `tcpdump`, `Scapy`, `ettercap`, `arpspoof`, `bettercap`, `arpwatch`, `passivedns`, `pypolicyd-spf`, `opendmarc`.
- **Always correlate** Layer 2, 3, 4, and 7 artifacts for robust forensic conclusions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Network Forensics**, **Modification** is formally defined as a class of integrity-violating security incident in which an unauthorized entity — internal or external — intentionally or unintentionally alters, tampers, or manipulates data, network packets, system files, log entries, or digital evidence while it is in transit across a network, at rest in storage, or during the investigative process. It is one of the four classical pillars of the **STRIDE** threat model (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege), where Modification directly corresponds to the **Tampering (T)** category.

From a forensic standpoint, Modification is a multi-phase investigative discipline involving:
1. **Pre-incident baseline establishment** (capturing known-good states via cryptographic hashing).
2. **Anomaly detection** (identifying delta states between baseline and current).
3. **Differential forensic analysis** (pinpointing exact bytes, packets, or records altered).
4. **Chain-of-custody preservation** (ensuring investigative actions do not themselves constitute modification of evidence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT864 Module 2):** Modification is examined under *Content Integrity Verification*, *Hash-based Forensics*, and *Anti-Forensic Countermeasure Detection*. Students are expected to know MD5/SHA-family integrity checks, HMAC verification, MAC timestamp comparison, and tamper-evident log analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wax-Sealed Letter"

Imagine you are a 19th-century magistrate receiving a diplomatic pouch from a foreign embassy. The pouch is sealed with a unique **wax imprint** bearing the ambassador's ring. If the wax is intact, unbroken, and bears the exact imprint you have on file, you are assured the contents are **authentic and unaltered**. If the wax is cracked, replaced, or shows a different ring, you know **modification has occurred** — and the question immediately becomes: *by whom, when, and to what extent?*

In modern network forensics:
- The **wax seal** = a **cryptographic hash digest** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256).
- The **letter inside** = the **payload data** (file, packet, log record).
- The **magistrate's ledger of known seals** = the **baseline integrity database**.
- The **forensic examiner** = the analyst running **differential comparison** between baseline and current state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle (C.I.A. Triad):** Modification attacks are *integrity* violations. Integrity is one of the three pillars of information security (along with Confidentiality and Availability). A modification attack does not necessarily steal data — it *corrupts* trust in data.

## 1.3 Classification of Modification Attacks

Modification attacks in network forensics are taxonomized along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Category A | Category B | Category C |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Vector** | Active MITM (on-path) | Insider privilege abuse | Malware/rootkit |
| **Persistence** | Transient (in-flight packet) | Persistent (on-disk file) | Latent (log poisoning) |
| **Detectability** | Signature-based | Anomaly-based | Hash-based (cryptographic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Standard Reference:** Cryptographic hash standards are governed by **NIST FIPS 180-4** (Secure Hash Standard) and **NIST FIPS 198-1** (Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code). HMAC-SHA256 output length is **256 bits (64 hexadecimal characters)** with a fixed block size of **512 bits** and a recommended key length of **≥ 256 bits**.

## 1.4 Why Modification Matters in Forensics

Modification is uniquely dangerous because it can target **three separate layers of reality**:

1. **The Data Layer** — The actual payload (database records, configuration files, executable binaries).
2. **The Metadata Layer** — File timestamps (`$STANDARD_INFORMATION` in NTFS), MAC times, log timestamps, packet sequence numbers.
3. **The Evidence Layer** — The forensic investigation artifacts themselves (disk images, memory dumps, log files used as proof).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Self-Modification):** A forensic investigator who mounts a writeable copy of evidence and runs operating-system-level operations on it has, technically, *modified the evidence*. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the concept of **bit-stream imaging** versus **logical imaging** to prevent self-induced modification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of Modification Forensics

A rigorous modification analysis follows a strict three-phase methodology, mirroring the **Locard Exchange Principle** in physical forensics ("every contact leaves a trace"):

### Phase I — Baseline Establishment (Pre-Incident)
- Compute cryptographic digests of all critical files at a known-clean state.
- Store digests in a **tamper-evident database** (e.g., signed manifest, write-once media).
- Standard tools: `md5sum`, `sha256sum`, `Tripwire`, `AIDE`, `OSSEC`.

### Phase II — Delta Detection (Incident Response)
- Re-compute digests on the suspect system.
- Perform **set-difference** between baseline hashes and current hashes.
- Any mismatch indicates modification. The specific hash function (MD5 vs SHA-256) determines collision-resistance guarantees.

### Phase III — Scope Determination
- For each mismatched file, examine:
  - **Content delta** (byte-level diff using `xdelta`, `rsync --checksum`).
  - **Metadata delta** (MAC times, ownership, ACLs).
  - **Volatile state delta** (process list, network sockets, kernel modules).

## 2.2 Cryptographic Hash Functions — The Mathematical Foundation

A cryptographic hash function $H$ maps an input message $M$ of arbitrary length to a fixed-length digest $d$ of length $n$ bits:

$$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$

For forensic integrity, $H$ must satisfy three properties:

1. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $d$, finding any $M$ such that $H(M) = d$ is computationally infeasible.
2. **Second pre-image resistance:** Given $M_1$, finding $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is computationally infeasible.
3. **Collision resistance:** Finding any pair $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is computationally infeasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MD5 is broken for collision resistance** (Wang et al., 2004 — collisions generated in ~1 hour on an IBM p690 cluster). For forensic use, KTU examiners expect **SHA-256 or stronger** for any new investigation.

## 2.3 The Merkle-Damgård Construction (SHA Family)

The SHA-1 and SHA-2 family uses the **Merkle-Damgård iterative construction**:

$$H_0 = IV \quad \text{(Initial Value, fixed constant)}$$
$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, t$$
$$H(M) = H_t$$

where $f$ is a compression function, $M_i$ are $n$-bit message blocks, and $IV$ is algorithm-specific (e.g., `0x6A09E667` for SHA-256).

## 2.4 HMAC — Keyed-Hash Message Authentication

When a shared secret $K$ is involved, modification detection uses **HMAC**:

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left( (K \oplus opad) \parallel H\left( (K \oplus ipad) \parallel M \right) \right)$$

where:
- $opad = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times (outer pad)
- $ipad = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times (inner pad)
- $B$ = block size of underlying hash (64 bytes for SHA-256)
- $\parallel$ = concatenation
- $\oplus$ = XOR

> [!NOTE]
> HMAC provides both **integrity** and **authenticity** — it detects modification AND proves the message came from a holder of $K$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H(M)$ | Cryptographic hash of message $M$ | $\{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | $n$ = digest size in bits |
| $n_{\text{MD5}}$ | MD5 digest length | 128 | bits |
| $n_{\text{SHA-1}}$ | SHA-1 digest length | 160 | bits |
| $n_{\text{SHA-256}}$ | SHA-256 digest length | 256 | bits |
| $n_{\text{SHA-512}}$ | SHA-512 digest length | 512 | bits |
| $B$ | SHA-256 block size | 512 | bits |
| $K_{\text{min}}$ | Minimum recommended HMAC key | $\geq 256$ | bits |
| $P_{\text{collision}}$ | Birthday-bound collision probability | $\approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}}$ | dimensionless |
| $k$ | Number of hashes drawn | variable | dimensionless |
| $N_{\text{samples}}$ | Forensic samples for 99% confidence | $\approx 4.6 \cdot 10^7 / n^2$ (50% bound) | dimensionless |
| $\text{Avalanche}(H)$ | Bit-flip sensitivity | $\approx 50\%$ | percentage |
| $\Delta_{\text{MAC}}$ | Time delta between Modified-Accessed-Created | $T_{\text{mod}} - T_{\text{acc}}$ | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Birthday Bound:** To find a collision in an $n$-bit hash with $\geq 50\%$ probability, an attacker needs $\approx 2^{n/2}$ evaluations. For SHA-256, this is $\approx 2^{128}$ — computationally infeasible with current technology.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Modification forensics underpins:
- **SCADA/ICS security** in power grids (detecting tampered PLC firmware).
- **Financial transaction integrity** (blockchain Merkle trees).
- **Software supply-chain security** (e.g., Sigstore, The Update Framework).
- **Legal admissibility** under the **Daubert Standard** (US) and **Section 65B Indian Evidence Act** (for KTU-affiliated students in India) — the demonstrable chain of integrity is a prerequisite for digital evidence admissibility.
- **Anti-forensics detection** — modification is the most common anti-forensic technique (log wiping, timestamp stomping, timestomping tools like `timestomp.exe`).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Birthday Bound for Collision Probability

**Goal:** Prove that the probability of finding *any* collision among $k$ uniformly random $n$-bit digests is approximately $1 - e^{-k^2/2^{n+1}}$.

**Step 1 — Setup the Probability Space.**
Let the $n$-bit hash produce $N = 2^n$ possible output values. We draw $k$ samples uniformly at random with replacement.

**Step 2 — Compute Complement Probability.**
The probability that **no** collision occurs is:

$$P_{\text{no collision}} = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Inequality.**
Using the well-known bound $1 - x \leq e^{-x}$ for $x \in [0,1]$:

$$P_{\text{no collision}} = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right) \leq \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} e^{-i/N} = e^{-\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i / N}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Sum.**
The sum $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i = \frac{k(k-1)}{2} \approx \frac{k^2}{2}$ for large $k$.

$$P_{\text{no collision}} \leq e^{-k^2 / (2N)} = e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Collision Probability.**

$$P_{\text{collision}} \geq 1 - e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $k$ at 50% probability.**

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}} \Rightarrow e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5 \Rightarrow k^2 = 2^{n+1} \ln 2 \Rightarrow k \approx 2^{n/2} \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln 2} \approx 1.1774 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$

**Engineering Implication:** For SHA-256, $k \approx 1.1774 \cdot 2^{128} \approx 4.07 \times 10^{38}$ samples are needed for a 50% collision probability — a number exceeding the number of atoms in the observable universe ($\approx 10^{80}$ atoms is many orders of magnitude larger, but the hash evaluation cost dominates).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — HMAC Security Reduction

**Goal:** Show that a forgery against HMAC implies a forgery against the underlying hash's compression function.

**Statement (Bellare, Canetti, Krawczyk, 1996):** If $H$ is a pseudo-random function (PRF), then HMAC is also a PRF. The security bound is:

$$\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{PRF}}(t, q, \ell) \leq \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{PRF}}(t', q, \ell) + \frac{q^2 \ell^2}{2^{n+1}} + \frac{4 q^2 \ell}{2^n}$$

where $t$ = adversary time, $q$ = number of queries, $\ell$ = maximum query length (in blocks), $n$ = digest size, and $t' \approx t$ within a small constant factor.

**Interpretation:**
- The first term is the advantage against the underlying PRF.
- The second term is the **birthday-bound collision term** — even if $H$ is weak against collisions, HMAC inherits $\leq 2^{n/2}$ security.
- The third term accounts for **inner-key collisions** and is dominated by the second for typical parameters.

**Forensic Consequence:** An attacker who can forge an HMAC-SHA-256 tag in fewer than $2^{128}$ queries has, in effect, broken SHA-256 itself — a feat currently considered impossible. This is why **HMAC-SHA-256 is the gold standard** for network packet integrity verification (e.g., IPsec AH, TLS 1.3 record MAC).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Avalanche Effect Quantification

**Goal:** Quantify what fraction of output bits change when a single input bit is flipped.

**Definition:**

$$\text{Avalanche}(H) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}\left[ H(M)_j \oplus H(M \oplus e_i)_j \right]$$

where $e_i$ is the unit vector with a 1 in the $i$-th bit position, and the expectation is taken over uniform $M$ and uniform $i$.

**Ideal Avalanche:** $\text{Avalanche}(H) = 0.5$ (50% of output bits change per input bit flip).

**Step-by-Step Test (Strict Avalanche Criterion — SAC):**
1. Choose random $M$ and random $i$.
2. Compute $d_1 = H(M)$ and $d_2 = H(M \oplus e_i)$.
3. Compute Hamming distance $w = \text{HammingWeight}(d_1 \oplus d_2)$.
4. Repeat for $\geq 10{,}000$ trials.
5. Compute sample mean $\bar{w}/n$.

**Forensic Significance:** A weak avalanche effect (e.g., $\bar{w}/n < 0.4$) means **localized input changes produce localized output changes**, allowing an attacker to craft *targeted* modifications that preserve a large portion of the digest — enabling **partial collision exploitation**. SHA-256 and SHA-3 (Keccak) achieve avalanche ratios within $0.4999 \pm 0.0001$ empirically.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hash Function Selection for Forensics

| Property | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 | SHA-3-256 | BLAKE2b-256 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Digest size (bits) | 128 | 160 | 256 | 256 | 256 |
| Block size (bits) | 512 | 512 | 512 | 1088 (rate) | 512 |
| Collision resistance (effective bits) | $\leq 20$ | $\leq 60$ | 128 | 128 | 128 |
| Speed (MB/s, x86-64) | ~700 | ~400 | ~250 | ~200 | ~700 |
| Status (2026) | **Broken** | **Deprecated** | Secure | Secure | Secure |
| Recommended for new forensic cases? | No (legacy only) | No (transition only) | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Used in KTU 2024 syllabus? | Yes (conceptual) | Yes (conceptual) | **Yes (primary)** | Yes (advanced) | Optional |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Detecting a Single-Byte Modification

**Scenario:** A 4 GB NTFS disk image is captured. The original hash (baseline) is:

$$\text{SHA-256}_{\text{baseline}} = \texttt{9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08}$$

(This is the well-known SHA-256 of the ASCII string "test".)

**Step 1 — Re-hash the suspect image:**

$$\text{SHA-256}_{\text{suspect}} = \texttt{9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a09}$$

(Note the last hex digit changed from `8` to `9` — a 1-bit flip in the output.)

**Step 2 — Compare digests:**
$9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08 \neq 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a09$

→ **Modification detected.**

**Step 3 — Compute Hamming distance of digests:**

$$d_{\text{baseline}} \oplus d_{\text{suspect}} = 0 \cdots 0 \vert 1$$

A 1-bit difference in the 256-bit digest space.

**Step 4 — Probabilistic inference:**
Probability of a random input producing a digest that differs in *exactly* 1 bit is:

$$P_{\text{exactly 1 bit}} = \binom{256}{1} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{256} = \frac{256}{2^{256}} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-74}$$

**Conclusion:** The probability that a random, unmodified file coincidentally produces a 1-bit digest difference is astronomically low. Therefore, the modification is **deemed forensically real** (subject to investigator's chain-of-custody verification).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modification Forensic Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Establishment of Baseline State] --> B[Capture Known-Good Hash Digests]
    B --> C[Store Digests in Tamper-Evident Vault]
    C --> D[Incident Detection Trigger]
    D --> E[Acquire Suspect Artifact Bit-by-Bit]
    E --> F[Recompute Cryptographic Hashes]
    F --> G{Digest Match?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Conclude: No Modification]
    G -- No --> I[Invoke Differential Analysis Engine]
    I --> J[Compute Byte-Level Diff]
    J --> K[Inspect MAC Time Anomalies]
    K --> L[Check Anti-Forensic Tool Signatures]
    L --> M[Generate Modification Report]
    M --> N[Update Chain of Custody]
    N --> O[Present Evidence to Court]
```

**Figure 4.1 — Block Diagram:** The forensic investigator moves through eight distinct stages, beginning with baseline establishment and culminating in court-admissible evidence. Each arrow represents a verifiable state transition, and each block has a corresponding audit log entry.

## 4.2 Hash-Based Modification Detection Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[Input Capture]
        FileIn[Suspect File F]
        BaselineIn[Baseline Digest DB]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingStage[Hash Engine]
        SHA256Unit[SHA-256 Engine]
        HMACUnit[HMAC-SHA256 Engine]
        Comparator[Byte-Level Comparator]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage[Verdict]
        MatchOut[Verdict: MATCH]
        MismatchOut[Verdict: MODIFICATION DETECTED]
        ReportOut[Forensic Report Generator]
    end

    FileIn --> SHA256Unit
    BaselineIn --> SHA256Unit
    SHA256Unit --> Comparator
    FileIn --> HMACUnit
    HMACUnit --> Comparator
    Comparator --> MatchOut
    Comparator --> MismatchOut
    MismatchOut --> ReportOut
```

**Figure 4.2 — Block Diagram:** The detection subsystem ingests the suspect file and the baseline database in parallel, then routes them through the SHA-256 engine (for one-way integrity) or the HMAC-SHA256 engine (for authenticated integrity). The comparator produces a binary verdict that drives the report generator.

## 4.3 Modification Attack Surface Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RootMod[Modification Attack Surface]

    RootMod --> DataLayer[Data Layer Modification]
    RootMod --> MetadataLayer[Metadata Layer Modification]
    RootMod --> EvidenceLayer[Evidence Layer Modification]

    DataLayer --> DB1[Database Record Tampering]
    DataLayer --> File1[File Content Alteration]
    DataLayer --> Pkt1[In-Flight Packet Manipulation]

    MetadataLayer --> TS1[MAC Time Tampering]
    MetadataLayer --> Own1[Ownership ACL Modification]
    MetadataLayer --> Log1[Log Entry Injection]

    EvidenceLayer --> Image1[Bit-Stream Image Alteration]
    EvidenceLayer --> Mem1[Memory Dump Corruption]
    EvidenceLayer --> Cust1[Chain of Custody Break]
```

**Figure 4.3 — Block Diagram:** The Modification attack surface decomposes into three primary layers, each containing three specific sub-categories. This taxonomy is exam-essential for KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions.

## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Mitigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Layer 7: Application-Level Signatures]
    L6[Layer 6: TLS 1.3 Record MAC]
    L5[Layer 5: IPsec ESP Authentication Header]
    L4[Layer 4: SSH HMAC Channel]
    L3[Layer 3: Network IDS with Integrity Rules]
    L2[Layer 2: File Integrity Monitor FIM]
    L1[Layer 1: Hardware TPM Measured Boot]

    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

**Figure 4.4 — Block Diagram:** A defense-in-depth posture stacks integrity mechanisms at each network layer. A single breach at one layer is contained by the next. This is the architectural model endorsed by the **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** control family **SI-7 (Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Modification in the context of network forensics. List any three modification attack vectors.**

**Model Answer:**
Modification is a class of integrity violation in which an unauthorized party alters, manipulates, or tampers with data, packets, or evidence during transit, storage, or investigation. It is one of the four pillars of the **STRIDE** threat model under the **T** (Tampering) category.

**Three modification attack vectors:**
1. **In-flight packet manipulation** (e.g., BGP route injection, DNS cache poisoning with modified A-records).
2. **On-disk file tampering** (e.g., `/etc/passwd` alteration, binary backdooring of `ls` or `ps`).
3. **Log entry injection** (e.g., inserting forged entries into `/var/log/auth.log` to hide privilege escalation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Definition = 1 mark, Three vectors = 1.5 marks (0.5 each), Example = 0.5 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 with respect to their suitability for forensic modification detection.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Digest size | 128 bits | 160 bits | 256 bits |
| Collision resistance | Broken (Wang, 2004) | Deprecated (Shattered, 2017) | Secure |
| Speed | Fastest | Fast | Moderate |
| Forensic suitability | **Not recommended** (legacy compatibility only) | **Not recommended** (NIST deprecation since 2011) | **Recommended** for all new investigations |

MD5 and SHA-1 are **inadmissible as sole integrity proofs** in modern forensic reports under the Daubert Standard. SHA-256 (or stronger) is the KTU 2024 prescribed baseline.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 2):** Each question has an internal choice. Both Question A and Question B carry 7+7 = 14 marks. Full mark answers require diagrams, derivations, and real-world examples.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the cryptographic foundations of HMAC. Derive the HMAC construction and discuss its security properties relevant to modification detection in network forensics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A forensic investigator captures two disk images. The SHA-256 digest of the first image is `e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855` (empty file). The second image's digest is `2c26b46b68ffc68ff99b453c1d30413413422d706483bfa0f98a5e886266e7ae`. Determine whether modification has occurred and, if so, justify your reasoning cryptographically. Also discuss the practical forensic steps that should follow such detection.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Define HMAC.**
HMAC (Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code) is a specific construction for calculating a Message Authentication Code (MAC) involving a cryptographic hash function in combination with a secret key. It is standardized in **NIST FIPS 198-1** and **RFC 2104**.

**Step 2 — Derive the HMAC Construction.**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left( (K \oplus opad) \parallel H\left( (K \oplus ipad) \parallel M \right) \right)$$

The derivation proceeds as follows:
1. If the key $K$ is longer than the block size $B$, replace it with its hash: $K \leftarrow H(K)$.
2. If $K$ is shorter than $B$, pad with zeros to length $B$.
3. Compute $K_{\text{inner}} = K \oplus ipad$ and $K_{\text{outer}} = K \oplus opad$.
4. Compute the inner hash: $H_{\text{in}} = H(K_{\text{inner}} \parallel M)$.
5. Compute the outer hash: $\text{HMAC} = H(K_{\text{outer}} \parallel H_{\text{in}})$.

**Step 3 — Security Properties.**

| Property | Justification |
|---|---|
| **Pre-image resistance** | Inherited from underlying $H$ |
| **Pseudo-random function (PRF)** | BCK 1996 security reduction |
| **Length extension resistance** | Outer hash wrapper prevents classic length-extension attacks on SHA-2 |
| **Key recovery infeasibility** | Attacker needs $2^n$ work to recover $K$ from HMAC tags |

**Step 4 — Relevance to Network Forensics.**
HMAC is the basis of:
- **IPsec ESP** and **AH** (RFC 4303, 4302) — packet integrity.
- **TLS 1.2/1.3 record layer** — handshake MAC (now HKDF-based).
- **DNSSEC** chain-of-trust signing.
- **Kerberos** ticket integrity.

A forensic investigator can verify that a captured packet was **not modified in transit** by recomputing HMAC over the packet payload using the session key extracted from the TLS handshake or IPsec SA.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Recognize the Digests.**

$$\text{SHA-256}(\text{empty}) = \texttt{e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855}$$

$$\text{SHA-256}(\text{"foo"}) = \texttt{2c26b46b68ffc68ff99b453c1d30413413422d706483bfa0f98a5e886266e7ae}$$

**Step 2 — Compare Digests.**
The two digests are completely different. Therefore, the file contents are different. **Modification has occurred** (assuming the investigator had reason to believe the second image should have been empty, e.g., from a baseline manifest).

**Step 3 — Cryptographic Justification.**
The SHA-256 algorithm exhibits a near-ideal avalanche effect ($\approx 49.999\%$ average bit-flip per input bit change). The probability of two *semantically different* inputs producing the *same* SHA-256 digest (a collision) is $\approx 2^{-128}$. Therefore, with overwhelming cryptographic certainty, the digests differing implies the inputs differ.

**Step 4 — Practical Forensic Steps.**
1. **Preserve the original image** in write-once media; mount a working copy read-only.
2. **Compute the SHA-256 of the working copy** independently to confirm the suspect digest.
3. **Run differential analysis** (`xdelta3 -e`, `rsync --checksum`) between the original (empty) baseline and the suspect image.
4. **Inspect file system metadata** — creation time, modification time, owner UID/GID, MAC times.
5. **Check the bash history**, `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`, and process accounting logs (`/var/log/account/pacct`).
6. **Look for timestomping tool artifacts** (e.g., `timestomp.exe`, `libfaketime`).
7. **Generate a forensic report** with the chain-of-custody record and the digest comparison table.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mistake):** Students frequently confuse "different digests" with "definitely modified." The correct forensic phrasing is: "The digests differ, *which is cryptographically consistent with* the inputs differing; combined with contextual evidence (logs, timestamps, user testimony), modification is established *beyond reasonable doubt*." This distinction is worth 1 mark in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the Merkle-Damgård construction. Explain why SHA-256 inherits length-extension vulnerability, and how HMAC overcomes this limitation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a forensic investigation, a 1 GB disk image is acquired. The investigator computes SHA-256 of the image at $T_0$. Subsequently, the investigator runs `strings` and `exiftool` on the image. Should the investigator re-compute the SHA-256 at $T_1$? Justify with reference to read-only mounting, bit-stream imaging, and the principle of evidence integrity.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Merkle-Damgård Construction.**

The Merkle-Damgård construction is an iterative method for building collision-resistant hash functions from collision-resistant compression functions. Given a compression function $f: \{0,1\}^{n+b} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ (where $b$ is the block size) and a message $M$ padded to length divisible by $b$:

$$H_0 = IV$$
$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, t$$
$$H(M) = H_t$$

**Step 2 — Length-Extension Vulnerability.**

Given $H(M)$ and $\text{len}(M)$, an attacker can compute $H(M \parallel \text{pad} \parallel X)$ for *any* $X$ **without knowing $M$**. This is because:

$$H(M \parallel \text{pad} \parallel X) = f(H(M), X \parallel \text{pad}')$$

The attacker simply resumes the iteration from $H(M)$ with the appended block.

**Step 3 — Impact.**
If an API uses $H(\text{secret} \parallel M)$ as an authentication tag (a "naive MAC"), the attacker can forge tags for $\text{secret} \parallel M \parallel X$ without knowing the secret.

**Step 4 — How HMAC Overcomes It.**

HMAC uses a *nested* hashing structure: $H(K \oplus opad \parallel H(K \oplus ipad \parallel M))$.

The outer hash begins with a secret-derived state $K \oplus opad$, so the attacker cannot "extend" because they do not know $K \oplus opad$. The outer hash's pre-image is unknown, defeating the length-extension attack.

**Step 5 — Engineering Consequence.**
This is why:
- **MAC-then-encrypt** in older protocols was replaced by **encrypt-then-MAC** (IPsec, modern TLS).
- **Naive $H(\text{secret} \parallel M)$ schemes** (e.g., Flickr API signing) are deprecated.
- **HMAC-SHA-256** is the recommended KTU-prescribed primitive for forensic message authentication.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — The Forensic Rule of Evidence Integrity.**

The cardinal rule: **the evidence image must remain identical to the original at all times, including bit-by-bit.** Any deviation constitutes spoliation of evidence.

**Step 2 — Was the Image Modified by the Investigator?**

Tools like `strings` and `exiftool` are **read-only** with respect to the underlying file content. They parse the file but do not write back to the source image. However:

- If the image was mounted **writable** (`mount -o rw`), the kernel may update atime (access time) on the underlying filesystem — a *self-induced modification*.
- If the image was processed by a tool that creates **temporary files in the same directory**, those temps may overlap with image blocks on certain filesystems.

**Step 3 — Best Practice Workflow.**

1. **Acquire the image using a hardware write-blocker** or software equivalent (`dcfldd if=/dev/sdb of=image.dd hash=sha256`).
2. **Mount the image read-only**: `mount -o ro,loop,noatime,nodiratime image.dd /mnt/evidence`.
3. The `noatime` flag prevents access-time updates on the mounted image.
4. Run forensic tools (`strings`, `exiftool`, `sleuthkit`'s `fls`, `mmls`) on the read-only mount.
5. **Re-compute SHA-256 after all processing** as a final integrity check. If the re-computed digest matches the original $T_0$ digest, the investigator has provably not modified the evidence.

**Step 4 — Justification Conclusion.**

Yes, the investigator **should** re-compute SHA-256 at $T_1$ as a procedural best practice, even when read-only mounting is used. The re-computation serves as a **cryptographic chain-of-custody witness**. The expected result is *no change*; any change would immediately alert the investigator to a tooling anomaly (e.g., a `tmpfs` spilling onto the image, a kernel bug, or a malicious tool).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mistake):** Many students answer "No, the image was not modified" without specifying the read-only mount flags. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for omitting the `ro,loop,noatime` mount options. The full correct answer must mention **all three** flags: `ro` (read-only), `loop` (loopback device), and `noatime` (suppress access time updates).

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Modification Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating MD5 with SHA-256** in digests (e.g., writing a 32-hex MD5 digest when the question specifies SHA-256, which is 64-hex). -1 mark.
> 2. **Omitting the avalanche effect** when justifying why a 1-bit change invalidates a hash. -1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing integrity with confidentiality** — students write about "encryption" when the question is about "integrity." -1 mark.
> 4. **Failing to distinguish HMAC from plain hash** in authentication contexts. -1.5 marks.
> 5. **Not specifying NIST FIPS / RFC numbers** for cryptographic primitives when the KTU rubric explicitly asks for standards. -0.5 mark per omission.
> 6. **Hand-waving "investigate the logs"** without specifying which logs (`/var/log/auth.log`, `syslog`, `journalctl`, `evt` for Windows). -1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modification (Module 2, PECCT864):**

- ☐ **Modification = Integrity violation = STRIDE "T" (Tampering)**.
- ☐ **CIA Triad position:** Modification attacks the **Integrity** vertex, not Confidentiality or Availability.
- ☐ **Hash digest sizes:** MD5 = 128 bits, SHA-1 = 160 bits, SHA-256 = 256 bits, SHA-512 = 512 bits.
- ☐ **MD5 is broken for collision resistance** (Wang, 2004). SHA-1 is deprecated (Shattered attack, 2017). SHA-256 is the KTU 2024 baseline.
- ☐ **HMAC formula:** $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$.
- ☐ **Birthday bound:** Collision probability $\approx 1 - e^{-k^2/2^{n+1}}$; 50%-bound at $k \approx 2^{n/2}$.
- ☐ **Merkle-Damgård** construction makes SHA-2 vulnerable to **length-extension**; HMAC prevents this.
- ☐ **Three modification attack layers:** Data Layer, Metadata Layer, Evidence Layer.
- ☐ **Avalanche effect** ideal value = **50%** per input bit flip.
- ☐ **NIST standards to remember:** FIPS 180-4 (SHA), FIPS 198-1 (HMAC), SP 800-53 SI-7 (Integrity control family).
- ☐ **Forensic rule:** Always re-hash evidence after every operation; mount images with `ro,loop,noatime`.
- ☐ **Chain of custody** = unbroken sequence of custody records; SHA-256 re-computation is the cryptographic witness.
- ☐ **Tools to know:** `sha256sum`, `md5sum`, `dcfldd`, `ewfexport`, `xdelta3`, `Autopsy`, `The Sleuth Kit`, `Volatility`, `Tripwire`, `AIDE`.
- ☐ **Real-world deployment:** TLS 1.3 uses HMAC-SHA-256 (via HKDF) for record integrity; IPsec ESP uses HMAC-SHA-256-128 (truncated to 128 bits).
- ☐ **Anti-forensics counter:** `timestomp.exe`, `libfaketime`, log wipers (`wipe`, `srm`), and steganographic hiding — all are detectable via differential hashing.
- ☐ **Section 65B (Indian Evidence Act)** + **Daubert Standard** — chain of integrity is the legal admissibility prerequisite.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross-Site Scripting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cross-Site Scripting # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECCT864 – Module 2)** > **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** is a client-side code injection attack in which an adversary injects malicious scripts (typically **JavaScript**, but also **HTML**, **VBScript**, or **ActiveX**) into the content of a trusted, otherwise legitimate web application. The victim's browser executes the injected script within the **same-origin context** of the vulnerable site, thereby bypassing the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** and the browser's trust model. Because the malicious payload runs under the trusted site's origin, it inherits full access to the victim's session cookies, **HTTP authentication tokens**, DOM objects, and any sensitive data presented in the current page. The **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP)** consistently ranks XSS in the **Top 10 Web Application Security Risks**, classifying it under the broader category of **Injection** (CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation). > [!NOTE] **Core Trust Violation** > XSS fundamentally violates the browser's **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** assumption: the browser believes that any script coming from `https://bank.com` was authored by `bank.com`. XSS is the technical mechanism by which this assumption is broken, allowing an attacker to "borrow" the bank's identity to attack its own customers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Trojan Letter Imagine a **secure corporate mailbox** at your office. The mailroom trusts every envelope that comes through because each one bears the company's official letterhead. An attacker does not need to break into the building; instead, they slip a **fake letter with the company's letterhead** into the outgoing mail pile. A manager, trusting the letterhead, opens it, follows the instructions inside (e.g., "wire money to account X"), and never realizes the instructions came from a criminal. In this analogy: - **The company letterhead** = the trusted origin (`bank.com`) - **The mailroom** = the browser's Same-Origin Policy enforcement - **The forged letter** = the injected JavaScript payload - **The trusting manager** = the victim's browser executing the script - **The malicious instructions** = session theft, credential harvesting, defacement The attacker exploits a **missing security check** — the company did not verify the *contents* of the envelope, only its *cover*. ## 1.3 The Three Primary XSS Variants | Variant | Persistence | Server Involvement | Primary Vector | |---|---|---|---| | **Stored (Persistent) XSS** | Permanent in the database | Server stores the payload and serves it to all visitors | Comment forms, profile fields, forum posts | | **Reflected (Non-Persistent) XSS** | One-shot, in a single HTTP response | Server reflects the payload back from the request (URL, search box) | Phishing emails, malicious links | | **DOM-Based XSS** | Client-side only | Server never sees the payload; JS reads from untrusted DOM source | `document.location.hash`, `window.name` | > [!NOTE] **Module 2 Context — Network Forensics** > In Network Forensics, XSS is studied not only as a vulnerability but as a **traceable network artifact**. The forensic investigator must reconstruct: (a) the **injection point** in the HTTP request, (b) the **propagation path** through the server, and (c) the **execution vector** in the victim's browser. Each layer leaves a distinct forensic fingerprint in PCAP captures, proxy logs, and server access logs. ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Frequently Tested) - **Payload** — The actual malicious code snippet executed on the victim's browser. - **Sink** — A dangerous JavaScript function/attribute where untrusted data is executed (e.g., `innerHTML`, `eval()`, `document.write()`). - **Source** — A JavaScript property that reads attacker-controllable data (e.g., `location.search`, `document.referrer`). - **Sink-Source Taint Flow** — The path tainted data takes from a Source to a Sink. - **Session Hijacking** — Theft of the victim's session cookie to impersonate them. - **CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)** — Often combined with XSS; XSS bypasses CSRF tokens since the script runs in the trusted origin. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix ## 2.1 Attack Operational Phases The XSS attack lifecycle can be decomposed into **five discrete phases** for forensic and analytical clarity: 1. **Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)** - Attacker enumerates every input field, URL parameter, HTTP header, and JavaScript endpoint of the target application. - Tools: **Burp Suite Spider**, **OWASP ZAP**, manual parameter fuzzing. 2. **Injection Point Identification** - The attacker locates the precise *sink* where user input is rendered without proper sanitization. Examples: - `
Welcome, $USERNAME$
` rendered in HTML body. - `` embedded in inline JS. - `Click here` inside an attribute context. 3. **Payload Crafting** - The attacker writes a **context-aware payload** that escapes the surrounding HTML/JS/URL grammar. - **Context taxonomy:** - **HTML context** → use `` | `">` | `` in hash | > [!WARNING] **Encoding Pitfall** > **HTML encoding** (`<`) does NOT protect a **JavaScript context** sink. You must use **JavaScript string encoding** (e.g., `\x3Cscript\x3E` or backslash-escaping) inside script blocks. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 2.4 Context-Specific Encoding Requirements | Output Context | Required Encoding | Example Safe Function | |---|---|---| | HTML Body | HTML Entity Encoding | `htmlspecialchars()` in PHP, OWASP Java Encoder | | HTML Attribute | HTML Attribute Encoding | Same as above, plus quote-escaping | | JavaScript Block | JavaScript Encoding | `encodeForJavaScript()` | | URL Parameter | URL/Percent Encoding | `encodeURIComponent()` | | CSS | CSS Escaping | `\65` for letter 'e' | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility XSS understanding is foundational for: - **Web Application Firewalls (WAFs)** — signature design (e.g., ModSecurity OWASP CRS). - **SIEM rule engineering** — detecting `} $$ **Step 4:** Browser parsing interpretation. - The browser receives the HTTP response body containing the string `` as part of legitimate HTML. - The HTML parser builds the DOM and **executes** the script, displaying an alert dialog. **Step 5:** Replace the proof-of-concept with an exfiltration payload. $$ \text{Final URL: } \texttt{https://target.com/vulnerable.php?q=} $$ - When the victim clicks this link, their browser sends a request to `evil.com` with the session cookie appended as a query parameter. The attacker now possesses the session token. ### 3.1.3 Forensic Reconstruction from PCAP A network forensic analyst can reconstruct this attack by: **Step A:** Extract the HTTP GET request from the PCAP — the URL contains the inline `} $$ **Step 3:** Server-side code (e.g., `INSERT INTO comments (body) VALUES ('$input')`) stores the raw string into the database without sanitization. **Step 4:** Every subsequent visitor to the blog post receives an HTML response containing the comment. Their browser executes the script, sending a silent request to `evil.com` with their location and cookies. **Step 5:** The attacker now collects a log of every victim's session cookie and origin URL — enabling mass session hijacking. ### 3.2.3 Forensic Indicators of Stored XSS | Layer | Indicator | |---|---| | **Database** | Row containing `` | | Attribute | `" onmouseover="alert(1)` | | JavaScript String | `'-alert(1)-'` | | URL (href) | `javascript:alert(1)` | | SVG (File Upload) | `` **Step 3: Comparative Analysis (3 Marks)** | Dimension | Reflected XSS | DOM-Based XSS | |---|---|---| | Server log of payload | Yes (in URL) | No (in fragment) | | Primary detection layer | WAF, server-side input validation | Client-side static analysis, browser CSP | | Attack surface | Server-rendered pages | JS-driven SPA frameworks (React, Vue, Angular) | | Network artifact in PCAP | Full payload visible in HTTP request | Only page request visible; payload is fragment-only | | Forensic difficulty | Moderate | High (server-side blind spot) | **Forensic Implication:** Reflected XSS leaves **rich server-side artifacts** in access logs, while DOM-Based XSS requires **endpoint forensics** and **client-side code review** for detection. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Defense-in-Depth Strategy** **Layer 1: Input Validation (1 Mark)** Apply **whitelist-based input validation** at the server. Reject any input that does not match an expected pattern (e.g., email regex for email fields). While insufficient alone, it eliminates the most obvious attack vectors. **Layer 2: Context-Aware Output Encoding (2 Marks)** Encode all user-controllable data **at the point of output** based on the rendering context: - HTML body → HTML entity encoding - Attribute → attribute encoding - JavaScript → JavaScript string escaping - URL → percent-encoding Tools: **OWASP Java Encoder**, **Microsoft AntiXSS**, **ESAPI**. **Layer 3: Content Security Policy (1.5 Marks)** Deploy a strict CSP header: $$\texttt{Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://trusted.cdn.com; object-src 'none'}$$ This prevents inline script execution and restricts script sources, neutralizing most XSS payloads even when input validation fails. **Layer 4: HTTP Cookie Hardening (1 Mark)** Set the `HttpOnly` flag on session cookies to prevent JavaScript access, and the `Secure` flag to enforce HTTPS-only transmission: $$\texttt{Set-Cookie: session=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict}$$ **Layer 5: HTTP Response Headers (1 Mark)** - `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` — prevents MIME-sniffing attacks. - `X-Frame-Options: DENY` — prevents clickjacking that may chain with XSS. - `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` — enforces HTTPS. **Layer 6: Modern Browser Features (0.5 Marks)** Adopt **Trusted Types API** in JavaScript to enforce that only pre-approved sanitization functions can write to dangerous sinks like `innerHTML`. **Conclusion:** No single layer is sufficient. Defense-in-depth ensures that the failure of one control (e.g., missed input validation) is caught by another (e.g., CSP blocking inline script). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] **Common Mark Deduction Traps (Module 2 — XSS)** > 1. **Conflating XSS with CSRF (2-3 mark loss):** XSS executes arbitrary code in the victim's browser; CSRF forges authenticated requests. They are related but distinct — do not interchange them. > 2. **Ignoring the role of the server in DOM-Based XSS (1-2 mark loss):** Many students incorrectly state that DOM-Based XSS "doesn't involve the browser." The browser is the *primary* execution environment. > 3. **Failing to mention output encoding context (2-3 mark loss):** Generic answers like "use input validation" score poorly. KTU examiners expect **context-specific** encoding mention (HTML vs JS vs URL). > 4. **Skipping forensic artifact analysis (1-2 mark loss):** Module 2 of Network Forensics requires explicit discussion of **PCAP, server logs, and database evidence** — not just the attack mechanism. > 5. **No mention of CSP or Trusted Types (1 mark loss in modern questions):** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes current best practices. Failing to mention CSP is considered outdated. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist** - **XSS = Client-side code injection**; runs in the **victim's browser** under the **trusted site's origin**. - **Three types:** **Stored** (persistent, server-stored), **Reflected** (request-reflected), **DOM-Based** (client-side only). - **SOP bypass mechanism:** Injected script is "born" as the trusted origin, escaping cross-origin restrictions. - **Common sinks:** `innerHTML`, `document.write()`, `eval()`, `setTimeout(string)`, `location.href`. - **Common sources:** `location.search`, `location.hash`, `document.referrer`, `window.name`, `postMessage`. - **Primary defenses (Layered):** Input validation → Context-aware output encoding → CSP → `HttpOnly` cookies → WAF. - **Forensic artifacts (PCAP):** POST with payload, GET reflection, outbound exfiltration to attacker domain. - **Forensic artifacts (Server):** Database rows with `

topics.title: DNS Spoofing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** DNS Spoofing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **DNS Spoofing** (also known as **DNS Cache Poisoning** or **DNS Redirection**) is a malicious cyberattack in which an attacker injects falsified Domain Name System (DNS) records into the DNS resolver's cache, causing the resolver to return an incorrect IP address for a legitimate domain name. This directs the victim's traffic to an attacker-controlled server without the user's knowledge or consent. In the context of **Network Forensics**, DNS Spoofing refers to the investigative analysis of DNS traffic, resolver caches, log files, and packet captures (PCAPs) to detect, reconstruct, and attribute fraudulent DNS responses that have been used to facilitate phishing, malware delivery, man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks, or data exfiltration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *DNS Spoofing is the unauthorized modification or fabrication of DNS response packets transmitted to a victim DNS resolver, with the intent of mapping a legitimate domain name to a malicious IP address, thereby hijacking the victim's intended network destination.* ### Synonymous Terminology (Board-Important) | Term | Context | |---|---| | **DNS Cache Poisoning** | Corruption of the recursive resolver's cache | | **DNS Redirection** | General term for rerouting DNS queries | | **Pharming** | End-user-facing outcome of DNS spoofing | | **DNS Hijacking** | Modifying DNS settings on a client or router | | **Kaminsky Attack** | A specific, sophisticated variant (2008) | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the internet's **Domain Name System (DNS)** as a massive, public **phone directory**. When you want to call your bank, you don't memorize its phone number; you simply look up "MyBank.com" in the directory and dial the number you are given. Now, imagine a **forger** sneaks into the printing press and changes the listing for "MyBank.com" so that the number printed next to it is the forger's own personal phone number. When you call "MyBank," you are unknowingly connected to the forger, who impersonates the bank and tricks you into revealing your credentials. **DNS Spoofing is exactly this forgery, but at the speed of the internet.** The "directory" is the DNS resolver cache, and the "forged number" is a malicious IP address. Forensically, investigators must identify *when* the directory was tampered with, *how* the forged entry was inserted, and *which* victims were redirected to the rogue destination. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** DNS is essentially a **trust-based, unauthenticated** protocol (in its original design). This inherent trust is the root cause that makes DNS spoofing one of the most critical threats investigated in network forensics. --- ## 1.3 Core Architectural Components Before diving into the attack, students must understand the four hierarchical DNS server tiers involved in every name resolution: 1. **Root DNS Server (.)** — The top of the DNS hierarchy. **13 logical root server clusters** exist globally (A through M). 2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD) Server** — Manages `.com`, `.org`, `.in`, `.edu` etc. 3. **Authoritative DNS Server** — Holds the *actual* A, AAAA, MX, TXT records for a specific domain. 4. **Recursive Resolver (Local DNS)** — The ISP-provided or organization-internal server that performs lookups on behalf of end clients. This is the **primary target of DNS spoofing**. $$ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Recursive Resolver} \rightarrow \text{Root} \rightarrow \text{TLD} \rightarrow \text{Authoritative} $$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Mechanics of DNS Resolution To master DNS Spoofing, a forensic investigator must first internalize the **legitimate DNS resolution flow**. Two query modes exist: ### A. Recursive Query The client asks the resolver, *"Find the IP for example.com and give me the final answer."* The resolver takes full responsibility for the lookup chain. ### B. Iterative Query Between the resolver and the root/TLD/authoritative servers, queries are iterative. The server responds with the *best referral it has*, not the final answer. ### The Legitimate Resolution Path (8 Steps) 1. **Client** sends a UDP/53 DNS query for `www.example.com` (Transaction ID: `0xA3F1`). 2. **Recursive Resolver** checks its local cache. **Cache MISS** → proceeds. 3. Resolver queries **Root Server** (`.`). 4. Root responds with a referral to the **`.com` TLD server**. 5. Resolver queries the **TLD Server**. 6. TLD responds with a referral to `example.com`'s **Authoritative Server** (`ns1.iana-servers.net`). 7. Resolver queries the **Authoritative Server**. 8. Authoritative Server responds with the **A record** (e.g., `93.184.216.34`). Resolver caches this and forwards to the client. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Forensic Note:** The **Transaction ID (TXID)** is a **16-bit field** (range `0x0000` to `0xFFFF`, i.e., **65,536 possible values**). The security of pre-DNSSEC DNS relies on the difficulty of guessing this ID. Early spoofing attacks attempted brute-force ID guessing. --- ## 2.2 The DNS Spoofing Attack Lifecycle A DNS spoofing attack generally progresses through the following structured phases: ### Phase 1: Reconnaissance - Attacker maps the target's DNS infrastructure (using tools like `dig`, `nslookup`, `dnsenum`). - Identifies whether the target resolver is recursive and whether its cache can be reached. ### Phase 2: Transaction ID Prediction / Race Condition The attacker pre-calculates or predicts the TXID and source port of the pending legitimate query and **races** to inject a forged DNS reply before the legitimate authoritative server responds. ### Phase 3: Forged Response Injection The attacker transmits a **UDP/53** packet to the resolver containing: - The correct **Transaction ID**. - A matching **Question Section** (same query). - A malicious **Answer Section** (e.g., `www.example.com A 198.51.100.66`). ### Phase 4: Cache Persistence (The "Poisoning" Effect) The recursive resolver stores the malicious record for the duration of its **TTL (Time-To-Live)** value. Every subsequent client query for that domain is now redirected — this is the **cache poisoning** effect that distinguishes DNS spoofing from a one-time MitM. ### Phase 5: Victim Redirection End users typing `www.mybank.com` are silently sent to a phishing clone hosted at the attacker's IP. --- ## 2.3 Variants of DNS Spoofing (KTU High-Yield Distinction) | Variant | Mechanism | Forensic Signature | |---|---|---| | **Local Cache Poisoning** | Direct injection into resolver cache | Sudden unsolicited DNS response packets | | **Kaminsky Attack (2008)** | Exploits *additional* record forging to bypass TXID entropy | High-volume UDP/53 traffic to resolver, no cache entry for non-existent subdomains | | **DNS ID Spoofing** | Brute-force prediction of 16-bit TXID | Thousands of rapid responses with varied TXIDs | | **Birthday Attack on DNS** | Uses birthday paradox probability | Statistical spike in identical TXIDs | | **Router DNS Hijack** | Modifies firmware/settings on home router | DNS server IP changed in router config | | **Rogue DHCP + DNS** | DHCP server assigns malicious DNS server | DHCP logs show rogue DNS IP option (Option 6) | | **DNS Rebinding Attack** | Combats SOP by fast TTL expiry | Extremely low TTL values in DNS records | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and analytical metric examiners expect a Network Forensics student to know for DNS Spoofing-related problems. | Parameter | Formula / Constant | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **DNS Transaction ID Space** | $2^{16}$ | Total possible TXIDs | IDs | | **DNS Port (Default)** | **53 (UDP/TCP)** | IANA registered port for DNS | Port | | **DNS Label Length Max** | $\vert \text{Label} \vert \leq 63$ | Max characters per label | Characters | | **DNS Name Length Max** | $\vert \text{FQDN} \vert \leq 253$ | Max full domain name length | Characters | | **Birthday Attack Probability** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{16})}$ | Probability of TXID collision after $n$ attempts | Probability | | **Required Forgeries (50%)** | $n \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{16}} \approx 300$ | Spoofs needed for 50% birthday success | Packets | | **Cache TTL Range** | $0 \leq TTL \leq 2^{31} - 1$ | RFC 1035 TTL bounds | Seconds | | **DNSSEC Signature Hash** | SHA-256 (modern) | Cryptographic record authentication | Bits | | **Forensic Hash Standard** | SHA-256 | Evidence integrity hashing (per IT Act 2000/2008) | Bits | | **DNS over HTTPS (DoH)** | Port **443** | Encrypted DNS transport | Port | | **DNS over TLS (DoT)** | Port **853** | Encrypted DNS transport | Port | --- ## 2.5 The Birthday Paradox in DNS Forensics The **Birthday Attack** dramatically reduces the entropy required for TXID collision. The probability that two random 16-bit TXIDs collide among $n$ forged responses is: $$ P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2 \cdot 2^{16}}} $$ To achieve a **50% collision probability**: $$ n \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{16}} \approx 1.177 \cdot 256 \approx 300 \text{ packets} $$ This is why even modest attackers can succeed — they do not need to guess *one specific* TXID; they only need *any collision* with the legitimate query stream. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Forensic Utility DNS Spoofing analysis is central to: - **Incident Response**: Identifying the breach window by correlating resolver logs with phishing reports. - **Phishing Investigation**: Mapping attacker infrastructure from rogue DNS A records. - **Cyber Law**: Producing court-admissible evidence (RFC 3227 guidelines + SHA-256 hash chains). - **Threat Intelligence**: Extracting Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) — malicious domains and IPs. - **Compliance Auditing**: Verifying DNSSEC deployment across the organization. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Reconstruction of a Kaminsky-Style DNS Cache Poisoning Attack Below is the **complete forensic reconstruction** of a typical DNS spoofing incident. This walk-through is what KTU examiners expect when a question asks you to *"explain the DNS spoofing attack with a suitable diagram."* ### **Step 1: Initial Setup — The Victim Resolver** The victim organization uses a recursive DNS resolver at `10.0.0.53`. A legitimate user attempts to visit `www.mybank.com`. ``` Client IP : 10.0.0.100 Resolver IP : 10.0.0.53 Target Domain : www.mybank.com Authoritative : ns1.mybank.com (203.0.113.10) ``` ### **Step 2: Legitimate Query Issued** The client sends a recursive DNS query. The resolver checks its cache. Suppose `mybank.com` is *not* in cache (or has expired). It forwards an iterative query to the root server. $$ \text{Query Packet: SRC=10.0.0.53, DST=198.41.0.4, TXID=0x4B21, Q=mybank.com} $$ ### **Step 3: Attacker's Reconnaissance** The attacker, operating from `198.51.100.77`, has already mapped the resolver. They know: - The resolver accepts recursive queries. - The resolver uses UDP port 53 with predictable source port allocation. - The authoritative server for `mybank.com` is `203.0.113.10`. ### **Step 4: Kaminsky-Style Request for a Non-Existent Subdomain** The attacker queries the resolver for a *random non-existent subdomain* of `mybank.com`, e.g., `xj39d.mybank.com`. This subdomain is not in cache, so the resolver will definitely forward the query outward — creating a guaranteed race window. $$ \text{Attacker Query: SRC=198.51.100.77, DST=10.0.0.53, Q=xj39d.mybank.com} $$ ### **Step 5: The Race Begins** The resolver sends the query upstream to the authoritative server: $$ \text{Upstream Query: SRC=10.0.0.53:Port_{src}, DST=203.0.113.10, TXID=0x7C09} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Kaminsky innovation: by choosing a *unique, never-cached* subdomain, the attacker **prevents the legitimate response from overwriting the poisoned cache entry** later. Previous attacks failed because once a domain was cached, additional spoofed responses were ignored. ### **Step 6: Forged Response Generation** The attacker floods the resolver with **hundreds of forged responses**, each varying the TXID and source port. One example forgery: $$ \text{Forged Packet: SRC=203.0.113.10, DST=10.0.0.53:Port_{src}, TXID=0x7C09} $$ The forged **Answer Section** contains a malicious **glue record**: $$ \text{Answer: xj39d.mybank.com A 198.51.100.99} $$ $$ \text{Authority: mybank.com NS ns.attacker.com} $$ $$ \text{Additional: ns.attacker.com A 198.51.100.99} $$ ### **Step 7: Cache Insertion** When the forged response matches the TXID (Birthday Paradox success), the resolver accepts it and stores: | Field | Legitimate Value | Poisoned Value | |---|---|---| | `xj39d.mybank.com` | NXDOMAIN | A 198.51.100.99 | | `mybank.com NS` | ns1.mybank.com | **ns.attacker.com** | | `ns.attacker.com A` | (none) | 198.51.100.99 | ### **Step 8: Persistent Redirection** The **NS record poisoning** is the crown jewel — it hijacks the entire `mybank.com` zone. Every future query for *any* subdomain of `mybank.com` is now answered by `ns.attacker.com`, which can return arbitrary malicious A records. $$ \forall \, s \in \{a,b,c,\dots\} : s.\text{mybank.com} \rightarrow \text{Attacker IP} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Attack Vector vs. Forensic Indicator This matrix is the **KTU 2024-style analytical breakdown** examiners reward in 14-mark answers. | Forensic Layer | Evidence Source | What to Look For | Tool / Command | |---|---|---|---| | **Network (PCAP)** | Wire capture on span port | UDP/53 with mismatched SRC IP, duplicate TXIDs, unsolicited answers | Wireshark `dns.flags.response == 1` | | **Resolver Cache** | `ipconfig /displaydns`, `rndc dumpdb` | Records with abnormally low TTL, unfamiliar NS entries | `dig` against resolver | | **System Logs** | `/var/log/daemon.log`, Syslog | Sudden mass DNS queries, BIND warnings | `grep named` | | **DHCP Logs** | Router/DHCP server | Option 6 (DNS Server) changed to unknown IP | `grep "Option 6"` | | **Browser Logs** | Browser history + DNS | Visits to legitimate domain but unfamiliar TLS cert | Browser DevTools | | **EDR / Endpoint** | Host-based agent | DNS resolver process spawned unexpectedly | Sysmon Event ID 22 | | **Firewall Logs** | Perimeter device | Outbound 53 traffic to non-internal DNS servers | SIEM correlation | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Birthday Attack Sample Size This derivation frequently appears in KTU numerical-type questions. Given a 16-bit TXID space, find the number of forged packets required for a **95% collision probability**. We start from the exponential approximation: $$ P \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2 \cdot N}} $$ Where: - $N = 2^{16} = 65536$ (TXID space) - $P = 0.95$ (target probability) **Step 1: Isolate the exponent.** $$ 1 - P = e^{-\frac{n^2}{2N}} $$ $$ 0.05 = e^{-\frac{n^2}{2N}} $$ **Step 2: Apply natural logarithm to both sides.** $$ \ln(0.05) = -\frac{n^2}{2N} $$ **Step 3: Solve for $n^2$.** $$ n^2 = -2N \cdot \ln(0.05) $$ $$ n^2 = -2 \cdot 65536 \cdot (-2.9957) $$ $$ n^2 = 392638.3 $$ **Step 4: Take the square root.** $$ n = \sqrt{392638.3} \approx 626.6 $$ **Step 5: Round up (cannot send fractional packets).** $$ \boxed{n = 627 \text{ forged packets required for 95\% collision success}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** An attacker sending roughly **630 packets** within a typical recursive resolver's race window (often 100–500 ms) has a 95% chance of cache poisoning. This is why even modest attackers with a single broadband line can succeed. --- ## 3.4 Forensic Investigation Methodology (NIST SP 800-86 Aligned) The **forensic process** when investigating a DNS spoofing incident follows four pillars: ### Pillar 1: Identification - Detect anomalous DNS resolutions via SIEM rules. - Alert on mismatched TTL values vs. historical baseline. ### Pillar 2: Preservation - Capture full PCAPs of DNS traffic with hash chain (SHA-256). - Export BIND `named_dump.db` and Windows `DNS Audit logs`. - Document chain of custody per **Section 65B (Indian Evidence Act)**. ### Pillar 3: Analysis - Reconstruct the spoofed response using Wireshark and `tcpdump`. - Reverse-lookup the malicious IP for geo-location and ASN mapping. - Pivot on IoCs across the entire enterprise. ### Pillar 4: Presentation - Generate a forensic report with timeline, screenshots, hashes. - Map attack to MITRE ATT&CK technique **T1071.004 (Application Layer Protocol: DNS)**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Legitimate DNS Resolution Flow (Baseline) ```mermaid flowchart LR client1[Client PC
10.0.0.100] resolver1[Recursive Resolver
10.0.0.53] root1[Root Server
198.41.0.4] tld1[TLD Server COM
192.5.6.30] auth1[Authoritative
ns1.mybank.com
203.0.113.10] client1 -->|1 Query www.mybank.com| resolver1 resolver1 -->|2 Query root| root1 root1 -->|3 Refer to TLD| resolver1 resolver1 -->|4 Query TLD| tld1 tld1 -->|5 Refer to Auth| resolver1 resolver1 -->|6 Query Auth| auth1 auth1 -->|7 A 93.184.216.34| resolver1 resolver1 -->|8 Final Answer| client1 ``` --- ## 4.2 DNS Spoofing Attack Sequence (Kaminsky Variant) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Victim as Victim Client participant Resolver as Recursive Resolver participant Attacker as Attacker participant Auth as Authoritative DNS Victim->>Resolver: 1. Query www.mybank.com Resolver->>Attacker: 2. Forward to upstream (race window opens) Note over Attacker,Resolver: Attacker fires 500+ forged responses
with varied TXIDs and ports Attacker->>Resolver: 3. Forged UDP/53 (matching TXID)
Answer: 198.51.100.99
Authority: NS ns.attacker.com Auth->>Resolver: 4. Legitimate response (LATE, ignored) Note over Resolver: 5. Cache poisoned for TTL duration Victim->>Resolver: 6. Subsequent query www.mybank.com Resolver->>Victim: 7. Returns malicious IP 198.51.100.99 ``` --- ## 4.3 Forensic Investigation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Detection["DETECTION LAYER"] SIEM[SIEM Engine] IDS[IDS Suricata] HONEYPOT[DNS Honeypot] end subgraph Collection["EVIDENCE COLLECTION LAYER"] PCAP[PCAP Tcpdump] SYSLOG[Syslog named] DHCP[DHCP Logs] BRO[Bro Zeek Logs] end subgraph Analysis["FORENSIC ANALYSIS LAYER"] WIRESHARK[Wireshark TSV Export] ANALYZER[Timeline Analyzer] GEOIP[GeoIP and ASN Lookup] THREATINTEL[Threat Intel Pivot] end subgraph Preservation["CHAIN OF CUSTODY"] HASH[SHA 256 Hash] TIMESTAMP[RFC 3161 Timestamp] CUSTODY[Custody Log] end SIEM --> PCAP IDS --> PCAP HONEYPOT --> SYSLOG PCAP --> WIRESHARK SYSLOG --> ANALYZER DHCP --> ANALYZER BRO --> ANALYZER WIRESHARK --> GEOIP ANALYZER --> THREATINTEL PCAP --> HASH SYSLOG --> TIMESTAMP HASH --> CUSTODY TIMESTAMP --> CUSTODY ``` --- ## 4.4 Attack Tree — Hierarchical Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[DNS Spoofing Attack] ROOT --> A[Resolver Cache Poisoning] ROOT --> B[Client Side Hijack] ROOT --> C[Network MitM] A --> A1[Kaminsky Attack] A --> A2[Birthday Attack] A --> A3[NS Record Poisoning] B --> B1[Malware Alters hosts file] B --> B2[Rogue DHCP Option 6] B --> B3[Browser Proxy Settings] C --> C1[ARP Poisoning plus DNS] C --> C2[Evil Twin WiFi] C --> C3[BGP Hijack Upstream] A1 --> OUTCOME[Victim redirected to attacker IP] A2 --> OUTCOME A3 --> OUTCOME B1 --> OUTCOME B2 --> OUTCOME B3 --> OUTCOME C1 --> OUTCOME C2 --> OUTCOME C3 --> OUTCOME ``` --- ## 4.5 Defense-in-Depth Stack ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Layer 1 DNSSEC Validation] L2[Layer 2 DoH or DoT Encryption] L3[Layer 3 Resolver Source Port Randomization] L4[Layer 4 0x20 Encoding Bit Randomization] L5[Layer 5 Network Rate Limiting] L6[Layer 6 Continuous SIEM Monitoring] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define DNS Spoofing. Differentiate it from DNS Cache Poisoning.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **DNS Spoofing** is a network attack in which an attacker forges a DNS response packet and transmits it to a DNS resolver (or end client) before the legitimate authoritative server can reply. The goal is to redirect the victim's traffic to an attacker-controlled IP address. | Aspect | DNS Spoofing | DNS Cache Poisoning | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Broader term — any forged DNS response | A *subset* — specifically targets resolver cache | | **Persistence** | Often transient (one-shot MitM) | Persistent for the entire TTL duration | | **Target** | Resolver or end client | Recursive resolver's cache storage | | **Detection Difficulty** | Moderate | Higher (cache may be inspected later) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid for Exam:** *Spoofing is the act of forgery; poisoning is the lasting effect.* **(3 Marks Breakdown:** 1 — Definition, 1 — Differentiating points, 1 — Real-world example **)** --- ### **Question 2: List any three forensic artifacts that indicate a DNS spoofing attack.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** 1. **Unsolicited UDP/53 Response Packets** — DNS responses arriving without a corresponding query (no matching TXID in client log). 2. **Mismatched Source IP in DNS Response** — The responder IP does not belong to any authoritative server for the queried domain. 3. **Abnormally Low TTL Values** — Poisoned records often use very low TTLs to facilitate rebinding attacks. 4. **Anomalous NS Record Changes** — Sudden appearance of unfamiliar name servers (e.g., `ns.attacker.com`) in the resolver's cache. 5. **Discrepancy between `dig` results from different resolvers** — A sign of cache poisoning limited to one resolver. **(3 Marks Breakdown:** 1 — Each correct artifact with brief justification **)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer ONE full question from the choice provided. Each question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): Full DNS Spoofing Attack Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **(a) Explain the architecture of the Domain Name System and describe the step-by-step process of a Kaminsky-style DNS cache poisoning attack with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — DNS Architecture Explanation:** The DNS is a distributed hierarchical naming system with four tiers: - **Root Servers** (13 logical clusters: A–M) - **TLD Servers** (`.com`, `.org`, country codes) - **Authoritative Servers** (zone owners like `ns1.mybank.com`) - **Recursive Resolvers** (ISP or organizational DNS) The flow uses **recursive queries** between client and resolver, and **iterative queries** between resolver and the hierarchy. **Step 2 — Kaminsky Attack Phases:** 1. Attacker identifies a recursive resolver and queries a *unique, non-existent* subdomain of the target (e.g., `xyz123.victim.com`). This bypasses cache and forces an upstream query. 2. The resolver forwards the query to the authoritative server. 3. Attacker floods the resolver with hundreds of forged UDP/53 responses, varying the 16-bit TXID and source port. 4. The forged packet contains malicious answers plus *authority* records pointing the entire `victim.com` zone to attacker's NS server. 5. If a forged response matches the legitimate TXID (Birthday Paradox success), the resolver caches the poisoned records. 6. All future queries for any subdomain of `victim.com` are answered by the attacker's authoritative server. **Diagram:** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Attacker participant R as Resolver participant Auth as Authoritative A->>R: Query xyz123.victim.com R->>Auth: Forward upstream A->>R: Flood forged responses (NS=ns.evil.com) Auth->>R: Legitimate reply (LATE) Note over R: Cache poisoned R->>A: All future victim.com queries ``` **(7 Marks Breakdown:** 2 — Architecture, 3 — Attack steps, 1 — Diagram, 1 — Persistence explanation **)** --- #### **(b) Discuss the forensic investigation methodology for DNS spoofing incidents. Include at least four forensic artifacts and their evidentiary value. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Phase 1 — Identification:** Use SIEM correlation rules to detect anomalies such as: - DNS responses with source IPs outside the known authoritative set. - Mismatched TXIDs across multiple responses. - Sudden change in NS records. **Phase 2 — Preservation:** - Capture wire-level PCAP using `tcpdump -i eth0 -w dns_evidence.pcap port 53`. - Export BIND cache via `rndc dumpdb -all`. - Generate SHA-256 hashes of all evidence files; maintain chain-of-custody log compliant with **IT Act 2000/2008, Section 65B**. **Phase 3 — Four Forensic Artifacts:** | Artifact | Source | Evidentiary Value | |---|---|---| | **PCAP (UDP/53)** | Span port | Proves attacker sent forged packet, reveals malicious IP | | **BIND `named_dump.db`** | Resolver | Shows poisoned entries with timestamps and TTLs | | **DHCP Logs (Option 6)** | DHCP server | Indicates if DNS settings were hijacked at network level | | **Sysmon Event ID 22** | Endpoint | Records DNS queries per process — links malicious destination to a specific executable | | **Zeek/Bro `dns.log`** | Network sensor | Structured JSON with all DNS transactions for timeline reconstruction | **Phase 4 — Analysis & Reporting:** - Reverse-lookup malicious IPs via VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB. - Pivot on IoCs across the enterprise. - Map the attack to **MITRE ATT&CK T1071.004**. - Produce court-admissible report. **(7 Marks Breakdown:** 2 — Methodology phases, 3 — Artifacts with evidentiary value, 2 — Reporting and MITRE mapping **)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Defense Mechanisms and Mathematical Foundations** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **(a) Explain how DNSSEC mitigates DNS spoofing. Describe its chain-of-trust model and key cryptographic mechanisms. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — DNSSEC Overview:** **DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions)** is defined in **RFC 4033, 4034, 4035**. It adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records, enabling resolvers to verify that responses are authentic and unaltered. **Step 2 — Chain of Trust:** DNSSEC establishes a hierarchy of trust analogous to a TLS certificate chain: - The **Root Zone** is signed with a **Root Key Signing Key (KSK)** — a 2048-bit RSA or ECDSA P-256 key. - Each TLD (`.com`, `.in`) is signed by the root's KSK. - Each domain (`mybank.com`) is signed by its parent TLD using a **Delegation Signer (DS) record**. - Each zone has two key pairs: **KSK (Key Signing Key)** and **ZSK (Zone Signing Key)**. **Step 3 — New DNS Record Types:** | Record | Purpose | |---|---| | **DNSKEY** | Public key of the zone | | **RRSIG** | Cryptographic signature over a record set | | **DS** | Delegation Signer — hash of child zone's KSK, placed in parent zone | | **NSEC / NSEC3** | Authenticated denial of existence (proves a name does not exist) | **Step 4 — Verification Process:** 1. Resolver fetches `mybank.com`'s DNSKEY. 2. Validates the DS record in `.com` zone matches a hash of the DNSKEY. 3. Verifies the `.com` zone is signed by the root (DS in root zone). 4. If all links validate, the A record answer is trusted. **Step 5 — Limitation:** DNSSEC provides **authenticity** and **integrity** but **not confidentiality**. Hence the rise of **DoH (DNS over HTTPS)** and **DoT (DNS over TLS)** to prevent eavesdropping-based spoofing. **(7 Marks Breakdown:** 2 — DNSSEC purpose, 2 — Chain of trust, 2 — Record types, 1 — Limitations **)** --- #### **(b) A 16-bit Transaction ID is used in DNS. Using the Birthday Paradox, calculate the minimum number of forged responses required to achieve a 75% probability of TXID collision.
