(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Transaction ID space $N = 2^{16} = 65536$ - Target probability $P = 0.75$ **Step 1 — Apply the Birthday Attack formula:** $$ P \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2N}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$ 0.75 = 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2 \cdot 65536}} $$ $$ e^{-\frac{n^2}{131072}} = 0.25 $$ **Step 3 — Apply natural logarithm:** $$ -\frac{n^2}{131072} = \ln(0.25) $$ $$ -\frac{n^2}{131072} = -1.3863 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $n^2$:** $$ n^2 = 1.3863 \cdot 131072 $$ $$ n^2 = 181678.2 $$ **Step 5 — Take the square root:** $$ n = \sqrt{181678.2} \approx 426.24 $$ **Step 6 — Round up (packet count must be an integer):** $$ \boxed{n = 427 \text{ forged responses}} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** An attacker needs only **427 forged UDP/53 packets** within the typical race window of a recursive resolver to have a 75% probability of cache poisoning. This illustrates why pre-DNSSEC DNS is fundamentally insecure against moderately resourced attackers. **(7 Marks Breakdown:** 1 — Formula statement, 2 — Substitution and log step, 1 — Algebraic solution, 2 — Final answer with units, 1 — Engineering interpretation **)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in DNS Spoofing Answers:** > > 1. **Confusing DNS Spoofing with Pharming:** Many students use these terms interchangeably. *Spoofing* is the attack mechanism; *Pharming* is the end-user consequence. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this conflation. > > 2. **Skipping the Transaction ID discussion:** A 14-mark answer without explaining the 16-bit TXID and its entropy limitations is considered incomplete. Always mention $2^{16}$ entropy. > > 3. **Forgetting the Forensics Angle:** PECCT864 is a *Network Forensics* course. Students often describe only the attack but ignore **evidence collection, chain of custody, and forensic analysis**. This can cost up to **4 marks**. > > 4. **No diagram in a 7-mark sub-part:** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a *"neat diagram"* for full-marks allocation. A textual-only answer typically scores **5/7 maximum**. > > 5. **Numerical errors in Birthday Paradox derivations:** Students often forget to apply the natural logarithm or compute $\sqrt{}$ incorrectly. Show **every algebraic transition** clearly. > > 6. **Omitting DNSSEC limitations:** DNSSEC does NOT provide confidentiality. Forgetting to mention **DoH / DoT** is a common examiner complaint. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — DNS Spoofing** - ✅ **DNS Spoofing** = injecting forged DNS replies to redirect victims. - ✅ **Cache Poisoning** = the *persistent* result stored in resolver memory. - ✅ **Kaminsky Attack (2008)** = uses unique non-existent subdomains to bypass cache locks. - ✅ **Default DNS Port:** **UDP/53** (TCP/53 for zone transfers and large responses). - ✅ **Transaction ID:** 16-bit, $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ possibilities. - ✅ **Birthday Attack:** ~300 packets for 50%, ~427 packets for 75%, ~627 packets for 95% collision probability. - ✅ **DNS Hierarchy:** Root → TLD → Authoritative → Recursive Resolver. - ✅ **Forensic Artifacts:** PCAP, BIND `named_dump.db`, DHCP Option 6 logs, Sysmon Event 22, Zeek `dns.log`. - ✅ **DNSSEC Records:** DNSKEY, RRSIG, DS, NSEC/NSEC3. - ✅ **Encrypted DNS:** **DoH** (port 443), **DoT** (port 853). - ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK Technique:** **T1071.004** (Application Layer Protocol: DNS). - ✅ **Evidence Standard (India):** IT Act 2000/2008, **Section 65B** — hash chain with SHA-256. - ✅ **Forensic Toolset:** Wireshark, Tcpdump, Splunk, ELK Stack, VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, BIND `rndc`. - ✅ **Difference between Spoofing and Hijacking:** Spoofing = forged response; Hijacking = DNS server settings modified (router/client-level). - ✅ **0x20 Bit Encoding Randomization:** A defense that randomizes case of query letters to add entropy. - ✅ **Source Port Randomization:** Modern BIND and Unbound randomize the resolver's outgoing UDP source port, expanding entropy from $2^{16}$ to $2^{16} \times 2^{16}$. --- *End of Module 2 — DNS Spoofing Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Routing Table Page Poisoning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Routing Table Page Poisoning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Routing Table Poisoning** (also known as **Route Table Manipulation** or **Routing Information Injection**) is a class of network-layer attacks in which an adversary deliberately injects, modifies, or fabricates routing protocol messages to corrupt the **Routing Information Base (RIB)** of one or more routers. Once the routing table is poisoned, legitimate network traffic is misdirected, blackholed, intercepted, or subjected to **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** surveillance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Network Forensics, Module 2), the term is specifically used to denote the **malicious act** of feeding falsified routing updates — and the forensic processes used to detect, investigate, and remediate such tampering.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Distinction**
> Do NOT confuse "Routing Table Poisoning" (an attack) with "Route Poisoning" (a legitimate **distance-vector loop-prevention mechanism** that advertises a route with an **infinity metric = 16** for RIP). The attack exploits or mimics these mechanisms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fake Road Sign" Attack

Imagine a city where GPS navigation systems receive live traffic updates from trusted "road-sign broadcasters."

* **Legitimate case:** A road is closed → broadcaster sends "Route X is blocked" → GPS reroutes all cars safely.
* **Attack case:** A criminal impersonates the broadcaster and broadcasts "Route X is blocked, take the alley behind the warehouse" → every GPS-using car is redirected into a trap.

The GPS navigation map is analogous to a **routing table**, the broadcaster is the **routing protocol**, and the criminal is the **attacker** performing **routing table poisoning**.

## 1.3 Scope and Standard Metrics

Routing table poisoning affects all major dynamic routing protocol families:

| Protocol Family | Standard Port / Protocol | Default Infinity Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **RIP** (v1/v2) | **UDP 520** | **16 hops** |
| **OSPF** | **IP Protocol 89** | Cost (LSInfinity = 0xFFFF, **65535**) |
| **BGP** | **TCP 179** | No fixed metric — governed by **path attributes** |
| **EIGRP** | **IP Protocol 88** | **4294967295** (2^32 − 1) |
| **IS-IS** | **OSI CLNP/CLNP** | **LSP MaxAge = 1200 seconds** |

> [!NOTE] **Key Forensic Insight**
> In KTU board examinations, the most commonly tested protocols are **RIP, OSPF, and BGP**. Always remember their default ports and infinity metrics — they are high-yield marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

A successful routing table poisoning attack follows a structured four-stage lifecycle:

### Stage 1 — Reconnaissance (Why it starts)
* The attacker maps the network using tools such as **Scapy**, **Nmap**, **NetStumbler**, or passive sniffers.
* Identifies the routing protocol in use (via packet capture on **UDP 520**, **TCP 179**, or OSPF Hello packets).
* Identifies the **Autonomous System Number (ASN)** of the victim.

### Stage 2 — Trust Exploitation (How access is gained)
Most routing protocols trust any peer that speaks the protocol correctly. Lack of **authentication** is the root vulnerability:
* **RIP v1** → no authentication at all.
* **RIP v2** → optional plain-text or **MD5** authentication.
* **OSPF** → supports **MD5** and **SHA** authentication, but is often left disabled.
* **BGP** → relies on **TCP MD5 signatures** (RFC 2385) or **TTL security**, frequently misconfigured.

### Stage 3 — Injection (The Poisoning)
The attacker forges routing protocol packets and sends them to the target router:
* **RIP:** Sends a Response packet advertising an attractive route (e.g., **0.0.0.0/0 → 1 hop**).
* **OSPF:** Injects a forged **Link State Advertisement (LSA)** for a non-existent network.
* **BGP:** Announces an **IP prefix** that the attacker does not own (prefix hijacking).
* **ICMP Redirect:** Sends **Type 5** redirect to force a host to use a malicious gateway.

### Stage 4 — Exploitation (The Payload)
The poisoned routing table achieves one or more attack goals:
* **Traffic Blackholing** → packets dropped silently → **Denial of Service**.
* **Traffic Redirection** → traffic through attacker → **Eavesdropping / MITM**.
* **Subnet Hijacking** → attacker owns the IP space → **Reputation theft** (spam, phishing).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU 2024 ESE**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| RIP Infinity Metric | $\text{Metric}_{\infty} = 16$ | **hops** |
| OSPF LSInfinity | $\text{LS}_{\infty} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ | dimensionless cost |
| EIGRP Maximum Metric | $M_{\max} = 2^{32} - 1 = 4294967295$ | dimensionless |
| Administrative Distance (AD) — Static | $\text{AD} = 1$ | router priority |
| Administrative Distance — EIGRP Internal | $\text{AD} = 90$ | router priority |
| Administrative Distance — OSPF | $\text{AD} = 110$ | router priority |
| Administrative Distance — RIP | $\text{AD} = 120$ | router priority |
| Administrative Distance — External BGP | $\text{AD} = 20$ | router priority |
| BGP Path Selection — Highest Weight | $W = 0 \text{ to } 65535$ | Cisco proprietary |
| BGP Path Selection — Shortest AS_PATH | $\text{Length} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{AS}_i$ | hop count in ASNs |
| Hop Count | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{hops}_i$ | integer |
| Composite Cost (OSPF) | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}} = \dfrac{10^{8}}{\text{BW}_{\text{bps}}}$ | dimensionless |
| Poisioned Route Propagation Time (RIP) | $T_p \leq 180\,\text{s}$ | periodic update cycle |
| BGP Hold Timer | $T_h = 180\,\text{s}$ (default) | TCP keepalive |

> [!NOTE] When absolute value bars are needed in formulas (e.g., |x|), always write them as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to prevent breaking markdown tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **ISP Backbone Security:** BGP poisoning has caused global outages (e.g., the **2008 Pakistan Telecom YouTube hijack**).
* **Enterprise Defense:** SOC teams use **RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure)** and **BGP Route Validations** to defend against prefix hijacking.
* **Digital Forensics:** Investigators reconstruct attack timelines by analyzing routing protocol packets captured in **PCAP** files using **Wireshark** or **NetworkMiner**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — RIP Route Poisoning with Metric Manipulation

### Setup (Problem Context)

Consider a router **R1** with the following pre-attack routing table:

| Destination Network | Next Hop | Metric (Hops) | Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.2 | 3 | eth0 |
| 172.16.0.0/12 | 192.168.1.3 | 2 | eth0 |

An attacker host **A** (IP = 192.168.1.99) is on the same Layer-2 broadcast domain as R1's eth0. The attacker sends a forged **RIP v2 Response** packet claiming that **10.0.0.0/8** is reachable via itself with **metric = 1**.

### Step-by-Step Forensics Derivation

**Step 1 — Initial Hop Count Verification (Before Attack)**

The legitimate shortest path to 10.0.0.0/8 has:

$$
H_{\text{initial}} = 3 \text{ hops}
$$

**Step 2 — Attacker Injects Falsified Update**

The attacker crafts a RIP Response packet:

$$
\text{RIP Entry} = \begin{cases} \text{Network} = 10.0.0.0 \\ \text{Mask} = 255.0.0.0 \\ \text{NextHop} = 192.168.1.99 \\ \text{Metric} = 1 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — R1 Receives the Update and Applies Route Selection Logic**

R1 compares the new route against the existing route using the **Administrative Distance and Metric** decision process:

$$
\Delta H = H_{\text{new}} - H_{\text{existing}} = 1 - 3 = -2
$$

Because:

$$
\Delta H < 0 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; H_{\text{new}} \prec H_{\text{existing}}
$$

R1 installs the new route. **The table is now poisoned.**

**Step 4 — Post-Attack Routing Table (Poisoned State)**

| Destination Network | Next Hop | Metric (Hops) | Interface | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10.0.0.0/8 | **192.168.1.99** | **1** | eth0 | **POISONED** |
| 172.16.0.0/12 | 192.168.1.3 | 2 | eth0 | Untouched |

**Step 5 — Forensic Indicator — Anomalous Metric Drop**

The forensic investigator computes the metric deviation:

$$
\sigma_H = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(H_i - \bar{H}\right)^2}
$$

For a stable network, $\sigma_H$ is small. A sudden drop of:

$$
\delta H = H_{\text{post}} - H_{\text{pre}} = 1 - 3 = -2
$$

Flagged as $\vert \delta H \vert \geq 2$, triggers a **routing anomaly alert**.

**Step 6 — Attribution via Packet Header Forensics**

The investigator inspects the captured PCAP file and verifies:

$$
\text{Source IP} = 192.168.1.99, \quad \text{Source Port} = 520
$$

$$
\text{Source MAC} = \text{AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF}
$$

Using **MAC-to-port binding** from the switch CAM table, the attacker's switch port is identified — completing the forensic chain.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Major Protocol Attacks

| Dimension | **RIP Poisoning** | **OSPF LSA Injection** | **BGP Hijacking** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | UDP | IP directly (proto 89) | TCP |
| Default Port | **520** | **OSPF uses IP 89** | **179** |
| Authentication | Plain-text or MD5 (optional) | MD5/SHA (often disabled) | MD5 (often misconfigured) |
| Attack Vector | Fake Response with low metric | Forged LSA Type 1/2/5 | False prefix announcement |
| Blast Radius | Local subnet / adjacent router | OSPF area | **Global Internet** |
| Forensics Tool | Wireshark display filter `udp.port == 520` | Wireshark filter `ospf` | `bgpdump`, `RouteViews` |
| Mitigation | Passive interfaces, MD5 auth | OSPF authentication, stub areas | RPKI, IRR filters, BGPSEC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Attack Flow — Routing Table Poisoning Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker on L2 Segment] --> B[Stage 1 Reconnaissance: Nmap and Scapy Scan]
    B --> C[Identify Protocol and Ports 520 or 179 or OSPF 89]
    C --> D[Stage 2 Craft Falsified Routing Update]
    D --> D1[Falsified RIP Response with Metric 1]
    D --> D2[Forged OSPF LSA Type 5 External]
    D --> D3[BGP Update with Unowned Prefix]
    D1 --> E[Stage 3 Inject Packet into Network]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Target Router Updates RIB]
    F --> G{Routing Table Now Poisoned}
    G -->|Yes| H[Traffic Redirected]
    H --> I[Man in the Middle]
    H --> J[Denial of Service]
    H --> K[Eavesdropping and Sniffing]
    G -->|Detection| L[Forensic Investigator Captures PCAP]
    L --> M[Analyze Anomalous Metrics]
    M --> N[Trace Source MAC and IP]
    N --> O[Attribution and Reporting]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Forensic Detection Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DetectionLayer [Detection Layer]
        D1[IDS and IPS Suricata]
        D2[Routing Anomaly Monitor]
        D3[NetFlow Collector]
    end
    subgraph CaptureLayer [Capture Layer]
        C1[Port Mirror SPAN]
        C2[TAP Device]
        C3[RPKI Validator]
    end
    subgraph AnalysisLayer [Analysis Layer]
        A1[Wireshark]
        A2[NetworkMiner]
        A3[Timesketch]
    end
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    D1 --> A1
    D2 --> A2
    D3 --> A3
    A1 --> E[Evidence and Timeline]
    A2 --> E
    A3 --> E
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> The left-to-right flow shows the logical pipeline of a routing-poisoning forensic investigation: capture live traffic → detect anomalies → analyze artifacts → reconstruct the incident timeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between route poisoning and routing table poisoning.**

**Model Answer:**
* **Route poisoning** is a **legitimate** loop-prevention mechanism used in **distance-vector routing protocols** (RIP, EIGRP) where a route is advertised with an **infinity metric** (e.g., **metric = 16** in RIP) immediately after it goes down, forcing neighbors to drop the route.
* **Routing table poisoning** is a **malicious attack** in which an adversary injects **falsified routing updates** to corrupt the routing table of a target router, redirecting traffic for eavesdropping, MITM, or DoS.
* **Key difference:** Route poisoning is a defensive protocol feature; routing table poisoning is an offensive security exploit.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain ICMP Redirect attack as a form of routing table poisoning.**

**Model Answer:**
* **ICMP Redirect (Type 5)** is normally sent by a router to inform a host of a **better next-hop gateway** for a specific destination.
* An attacker on the same subnet forges an ICMP Redirect packet, claiming *"To reach network X, use gateway Y"* where Y is the attacker's machine.
* The victim's host updates its local routing cache, causing all subsequent traffic to network X to flow through the attacker.
* This is a classic **host-level routing table poisoning** technique enabling **MITM attacks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — **Choice 1**

**(a)** Explain the various types of routing table poisoning attacks with relevant examples for RIP, OSPF, and BGP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **RIP Poisoning** — Attacker sends a forged RIP Response advertising **0.0.0.0/0 (default route)** with **metric = 1**, hijacking all outbound traffic.
2. **OSPF LSA Injection** — Attacker forges an **LSA Type 5 (External)** route, inserting a fictitious external network with **Type 1 metric = 1**, causing all routers in the OSPF domain to redirect traffic.
3. **BGP Hijacking** — Attacker announces an IP prefix they do not own to an upstream provider. Example: In **2008**, Pakistan Telecom accidentally hijacked **YouTube's 208.65.153.0/24** prefix, taking YouTube offline globally.
4. **ICMP Redirect Poisoning** — Discussed in Part A Q2.
5. **Static Route Injection via Compromised Device** — Attacker who has gained privileged access adds malicious static routes with **AD = 1**, overriding dynamic routes.

**(b)** Discuss the forensic investigation methodology for routing table poisoning incidents. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Identification:** Detect anomalies via **Routing Information Base (RIB) comparison** and **NetFlow analysis**.
2. **Preservation:** Capture live traffic via **SPAN ports** or **network TAPs**, save in **PCAP-NG** format with **SHA-256 hashes** for chain-of-custody.
3. **Analysis:** Use **Wireshark** with display filters (`rip`, `bgp`, `ospf`) to extract the forged packet. Verify:
   * Source IP/MAC mismatch with legitimate routing peers.
   * Anomalously low metrics.
4. **Correlation:** Cross-reference with **Syslog**, **AAA logs (RADIUS/TACACS+)**, and **switch CAM tables**.
5. **Attribution:** Map attacker MAC → switch port → physical location.
6. **Reporting:** Document findings using the **NIST SP 800-86** forensic guideline format.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — **Choice 2**

**(a)** Explain BGP prefix hijacking with reference to the **Pakistan Telecom / YouTube incident of 2008**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Background:** YouTube announced its prefix **208.65.153.0/24** to the global BGP table on **24 Feb 2008**.
2. **The Event:** Pakistan Telecom (AS 17557), aiming to block YouTube domestically, announced **208.65.153.0/24** with a more specific **/24** route (and an additional **/24** as a longer prefix).
3. **The Cascade:** Due to **BGP's longest-prefix-match rule**, the more specific Pakistani route was preferred globally by many routers.
4. **The Outage:** YouTube traffic worldwide was redirected to Pakistan Telecom's network and **blackholed**, causing a ~2-hour global outage.
5. **Forensic Lesson:** Lack of **RPKI origin validation** and inadequate **prefix filtering** by upstream providers allowed the hijack to propagate.
6. **Resolution:** YouTube retracted its announcements; Pakistan Telecom's misconfiguration propagated for hours.

**(b)** Describe the mitigation and prevention techniques for routing table poisoning attacks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Protocol-Level Authentication:**
   * Enable **RIP v2 MD5 authentication** (or **RIPng** with IPsec).
   * Enable **OSPF MD5/SHA authentication** on all interfaces.
   * Configure **BGP TCP MD5 signatures** per **RFC 2385** and **TTL Security** (GTSM).
2. **Infrastructure Hardening:**
   * Use **passive interfaces** for routing protocols on user-facing ports.
   * Implement **control-plane policing (CoPP)**.
   * Deploy **infrastructure ACLs (iACLs)** to block routing protocol traffic from unauthorized sources.
3. **Prefix Validation:**
   * Adopt **RPKI** (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) and **ROA** (Route Origin Authorizations).
   * Use **IRR (Internet Routing Registry)** filters and **BGP prefix-list** filtering.
   * Deploy **BGPSEC** (RFC 8205) for path validation.
4. **Monitoring and Detection:**
   * Use **BGP monitoring services**: **RouteViews**, **RIPE RIS**, **BGPmon**.
   * Deploy **SDN-based** detection like **ARTEMIS** for real-time prefix hijack alerts.
5. **Forensic Readiness:**
   * Maintain **router syslog** and **NetFlow** archives for at least **90 days**.
   * Regularly audit **show ip route** snapshots against baseline configurations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark Deductions to Avoid**
> 1. **Confusing Route Poisoning with Routing Table Poisoning** — Examiners specifically deduct marks for failing to differentiate the legitimate vs malicious context. Always clarify which one you are discussing.
> 2. **Forgetting Protocol Numbers and Ports** — Writing "RIP uses port 21" instead of **UDP 520** is an instant 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Skipping the Forensic Chain** — Answers must follow **Identification → Preservation → Analysis → Attribution**; jumping straight to "use Wireshark" loses process marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Authentication Mechanisms** — Never suggest only "use a firewall"; examiners expect protocol-level solutions like **MD5/SHA** and **RPKI**.
> 5. **Misquoting the BGP Hijack Year** — The YouTube incident was **February 24, 2008**. Wrong dates cost marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Routing Table Poisoning** is a malicious attack; **Route Poisoning** is a legitimate loop-prevention mechanism — know the difference.
* RIP default port = **UDP 520**; OSPF = **IP Protocol 89**; BGP = **TCP 179**.
* RIP infinity = **16 hops**; OSPF LSInfinity = **65535**; EIGRP max = **2³² − 1**.
* The three primary attack types are **RIP Response Forgery**, **OSPF LSA Injection**, and **BGP Prefix Hijacking**.
* The **Pakistan Telecom YouTube hijack (Feb 24, 2008)** is the canonical KTU case study — remember the date, ASN (**17557**), and prefix (**208.65.153.0/24**).
* Forensic methodology must follow **NIST SP 800-86**: Identification → Preservation → Analysis → Correlation → Attribution → Reporting.
* **PCAP-NG with SHA-256 hashing** is the gold standard for evidence preservation.
* Mitigation is layered: **Protocol authentication + Prefix filtering + RPKI + Continuous monitoring**.
* **Longest-prefix-match rule** is the fundamental BGP routing principle that enables prefix hijacking.
* Always tie forensic answers to **Wireshark filters** (`rip`, `bgp`, `ospf`) and **NetFlow records** — these keywords score high.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARP Poisoning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ARP Poisoning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**ARP Poisoning** (also called **ARP Spoofing** or **ARP Cache Poisoning**) is a Layer-2 (Data Link Layer) man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack in which an attacker sends forged **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** messages onto a local area network (LAN). The goal is to associate the attacker's **MAC address** with the **IP address** of another host (typically the default gateway or a target victim), thereby redirecting, intercepting, or modifying traffic that was never intended for the attacker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "ARP Poisoning is a network-layer 2 attack technique that exploits the stateless and unauthenticated nature of the Address Resolution Protocol to inject malicious IP-to-MAC address mappings into the ARP cache of legitimate network devices."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Impostor at the Office"

Imagine a **post office** in a corporate building:

- Every employee (host) has a **phone extension** (IP address) and a **personal mailbox number** (MAC address).
- A **directory board** in the lobby lists which mailbox belongs to which person (the **ARP cache**).
- The mail carrier (network switch) trusts anyone who walks up and says: *"Hey, I am John (the CEO). Please update the board so my mail goes to locker #47."*
- An **attacker** walks up and confidently says: *"I am the CEO — please send all my mail to locker #99 (which actually belongs to me)."*
- The directory is updated. From now on, **all of the CEO's mail is silently diverted to the impostor**.

That, in essence, is ARP Poisoning. The protocol **trusts whatever it is told**, with no verification — exactly the vulnerability the attacker exploits.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Network Forensics

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Significance:**
> - ARP Poisoning is one of the **top 5 most common MITM vectors** in enterprise networks.
> - It leaves **distinctive signatures** in switch CAM tables, router logs, and packet captures.
> - The **default TTL** of ARP cache entries on most OSes is typically **\vert 15-45 \vert minutes** (Windows: ~15-45 sec for unused entries, Linux: ~60 sec), which is the investigator's window to capture residual poisoned entries.

## 1.4 Core Technical Entities

| Entity | Definition | Role in Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **ARP Request** | Broadcast packet: *"Who has IP X.X.X.X? Tell me your MAC."* | Legitimate discovery mechanism |
| **ARP Reply** | Unicast packet: *"IP X.X.X.X is at MAC AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF."* | Exploited by attacker (gratuitous ARP) |
| **Gratuitous ARP (GARP)** | Unsolicited ARP reply, broadcast without a request | **Primary weapon of ARP poisoning** |
| **ARP Cache** | Local table mapping IP → MAC | **Target of poisoning** |
| **CAM Table** | Switch's MAC-to-Port mapping | Indirectly affected (MAC flooding variants) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARP Protocol — Operational Primitives

The **Address Resolution Protocol (RFC 826)** operates in **4 canonical steps** when Host A (IP = 192.168.1.10) wants to communicate with Host B (IP = 192.168.1.20):

1. **Lookup Phase:** Host A checks its local ARP cache for 192.168.1.20.
2. **Request Phase (if miss):** Host A broadcasts an *ARP Request* — *"Who has 192.168.1.20? Tell 192.168.1.10."* → Destination MAC: `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.
3. **Reply Phase:** Host B unicasts an *ARP Reply* — *"192.168.1.20 is at MAC_B."*
4. **Update Phase:** Host A stores the mapping in its ARP cache.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Vulnerability:** ARP has **no authentication, no sequence number, and no integrity check**. Any host on the LAN can claim to own any IP at any time.

## 2.2 The ARP Poisoning Attack — Step-by-Step Logic

The attack follows a **6-stage kill chain**:

1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker uses tools like `arping`, `nmap`, or `Wireshark` to identify IP/MAC pairs of victims and the gateway.
2. **Craft Malicious GARP:** Attacker constructs a *gratuitous ARP reply* containing:
   - `Sender IP Address` = **Victim's IP** (e.g., gateway's IP)
   - `Sender MAC Address` = **Attacker's MAC**
3. **Flood the LAN:** The forged GARP is broadcast on the LAN (and can be sent unicast directly to victims to avoid detection).
4. **Cache Overwrite:** Victim's ARP cache is updated — the victim's IP-to-MAC mapping for the gateway is now overwritten with the attacker's MAC.
5. **Traffic Diversion:** All packets the victim sends to the gateway are forwarded by the switch to the attacker's NIC (because the switch consults its CAM table, which is poisoned).
6. **MITM Exploitation:** Attacker can:
   - **Read** the traffic (passive sniffing)
   - **Modify** the traffic (active injection)
   - **Forward** the traffic to the real gateway (transparent MITM)
   - **Drop** the traffic (Denial of Service)

## 2.3 Attack Variants

| Variant | Description | Forensic Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **MITM (Man-in-the-Middle)** | Attacker forwards traffic to legitimate destination after inspection | Duplicate packets, increased latency |
| **DoS (Denial of Service)** | Attacker drops or black-holes traffic | Sudden loss of connectivity |
| **Session Hijacking** | Attacker steals TCP/UDP session cookies | Unauthorized transactions |
| **MAC Flooding** | Saturate CAM table to force hub-like behavior | Switch enters fail-open mode |
| **Gratuitous ARP Storm** | Rapid-fire GARP to keep cache poisoned | Bursts of identical GARP frames |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations & Metrics for KTU Board Exams**

$$
\text{ARP Cache Entry} = \langle \text{IP}_{32\text{-bit}},\ \text{MAC}_{48\text{-bit}},\ \text{Interface},\ \text{TTL} \rangle
$$

$$
\text{Poisoning Rate} = \frac{N_{\text{garp}}}{T_{\text{interval}}} \quad [\text{packets/sec}]
$$

$$
\text{Detection Window} = T_{\text{cache-TTL}} - T_{\text{attack-start}}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput Degradation} = \left(1 - \frac{R_{\text{post}}}{R_{\text{pre}}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{\text{garp}}$ | Number of gratuitous ARPs sent | Attacker-controlled (often 1-5 per second) |
| $T_{\text{interval}}$ | Time interval between GARP bursts | **1-5 seconds** (to outpace legitimate renewals) |
| $T_{\text{cache-TTL}}$ | ARP cache entry lifetime | **15-1200 seconds** (OS-dependent) |
| $R_{\text{pre}}, R_{\text{post}}$ | Network throughput before/after attack | Measured in **Mbps** |
| $L_{\text{arp}}$ | Length of an ARP frame | **28 bytes** (Ethernet) or **42 bytes** (with Ethernet header) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** ARP Poisoning analysis is foundational for **intrusion detection systems (IDS)**, **dynamic ARP inspection (DAI)** in enterprise switches (Cisco, HP), and **forensic timeline reconstruction** of LAN breaches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ARP Cache Update Logic

Let us formally derive how an ARP cache entry is updated upon receiving a packet.

**Step 1 — Cache Lookup:**
When a packet destined for IP $I_d$ is to be sent, the OS queries the ARP cache:

$$
\text{Result} = \begin{cases} \text{Hit} & \text{if } \exists\ \text{entry with IP} = I_d \\ \text{Miss} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — On Miss (ARP Request Generation):**
The OS constructs an ARP Request frame with the following fields:

$$
\text{Frame} = \langle \text{Op} = 1,\ \text{SenderIP} = I_s,\ \text{SenderMAC} = M_s,\ \text{TargetIP} = I_d,\ \text{TargetMAC} = 00\text{:}00\text{:}00\text{:}00\text{:}00\text{:}00 \rangle
$$

This is broadcast to MAC address `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.

**Step 3 — On ARP Reply (Cache Update):**
Upon receiving an ARP Reply (or GARP) with `Op = 2`, the cache is updated **unconditionally**:

$$
\text{Cache}[I_{\text{reported}}] \leftarrow M_{\text{reported}}
$$

There is **no verification** that $M_{\text{reported}}$ actually belongs to $I_{\text{reported}}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Attacker's MAC Resolution

Given a victim $V$ with IP $I_V$ and a gateway $G$ with IP $I_G$, the attacker $A$ (MAC $M_A$) forges the following GARP:

$$
\text{Forged GARP} = \langle \text{Op} = 2,\ \text{SenderIP} = I_G,\ \text{SenderMAC} = M_A,\ \text{TargetIP} = I_V,\ \text{TargetMAC} = M_V \rangle
$$

When $V$ processes this frame, the ARP cache update rule (Step 3 above) yields:

$$
V.\text{Cache}[I_G] \leftarrow M_A
$$

This **mathematically replaces** the legitimate entry $V.\text{Cache}[I_G] = M_G$ with the attacker's MAC. All subsequent frames from $V$ to $G$ are now switched to $A$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU High-Yield Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trick — The "Diffie-Hellman of ARP" Question:**
> KTU frequently tests the difference between **gratuitous ARP** and **ARP reply**. Memorize this matrix.

| Property | Legitimate ARP Reply | Gratuitous ARP (GARP) | Forged GARP (Attack) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triggered by Request? | Yes | No | No |
| Destination MAC | Unicast to requester | Broadcast `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` | Broadcast or unicast |
| Sender IP matches Sender MAC? | **Yes (verified)** | Yes | **No (mismatched)** |
| Cache Effect | Update | Update / Refresh | **Poison** |
| Frequency in healthy network | Low (on demand) | Low (IP change events) | **High (storm)** |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Style — 7 Marks):**
> A network has 3 hosts: **A (IP 10.0.0.1, MAC AA)**, **B (IP 10.0.0.2, MAC BB)**, and **C (IP 10.0.0.3, MAC CC)**. Host C launches an ARP Poisoning attack against A to impersonate B. After the attack, A sends a packet to B's IP. Identify the destination MAC address that A will use, and explain the forensic signature visible in Wireshark.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Before attack, A's ARP cache contains:

$$
A.\text{Cache} = \{ (10.0.0.2 \rightarrow \text{BB}),\ (10.0.0.3 \rightarrow \text{CC}) \}
$$

**Step 2:** Attacker C broadcasts a GARP:

$$
\text{Forged GARP} = \langle \text{SenderIP} = 10.0.0.2,\ \text{SenderMAC} = \text{CC} \rangle
$$

**Step 3:** Applying the cache update rule:

$$
A.\text{Cache}[10.0.0.2] \leftarrow \text{CC}
$$

**Step 4:** When A sends a packet to 10.0.0.2, A consults the cache and uses:

$$
M_{\text{dest}} = \text{CC} \quad (\text{attacker's MAC, not BB})
$$

**Step 5 — Forensic Signature in Wireshark:**

$$
\text{Signature} = \{\text{Multiple GARP frames}\} \cup \{\text{IP-MAC mismatch}\} \cup \{\text{Traffic to legitimate IP but unknown MAC}\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer in one line:** A will use MAC `CC` (attacker's MAC) as the destination. The forensic signature is a sequence of unsolicited ARP replies mapping B's IP (10.0.0.2) to C's MAC (CC).

## 3.5 Derivation of Detection Threshold

A common forensic detection heuristic compares observed GARP frequency against a baseline:

$$
\text{Alert if } f_{\text{garp-observed}} > \mu_{\text{baseline}} + k \cdot \sigma_{\text{baseline}}
$$

where $k$ is typically **2 or 3** (representing **2σ** or **3σ confidence** above the mean). In a healthy network, $\mu_{\text{baseline}}$ is close to **0 GARPs per minute**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Normal ARP Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Host A IP 192.168.1.10 MAC AA] -->|1 ARP Request Broadcast| nodeSwitch[Switch Layer 2 Hub]
    nodeSwitch -->|1 Forward Broadcast| nodeB[Host B IP 192.168.1.20 MAC BB]
    nodeB -->|2 ARP Reply Unicast| nodeSwitch
    nodeSwitch -->|2 Forward to MAC AA| nodeA
    nodeA -->|3 Cache Updated 192.168.1.20 to MAC BB| nodeA
```

## 4.2 ARP Poisoning Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeAttacker[Attacker IP 192.168.1.99 MAC MAL] -->|1 Send Forged GARP SenderIP GatewayIP SenderMAC MAL| nodeVictim[Victim Host IP 192.168.1.10]
    nodeVictim -->|2 ARP Cache Overwritten Gateway IP now mapped to MAL| nodeCache[Poisoned ARP Cache GatewayIP to MAC MAL]
    nodeCache -->|3 Victim sends packet to Gateway wrapped in Ethernet frame dest MAC MAL| nodeAttacker
    nodeAttacker -->|4 Optional Read Modify Forward to Real Gateway| nodeGateway[Real Gateway IP 192.168.1.1 MAC GW]
    nodeAttacker -->|5 Optional Drop causing DoS| nodeDrop[Traffic Blackholed]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Forensic Analysis Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Detection Phase
        step1[Packet Capture Tool Wireshark or tcpdump] --> step2[Filter ARP Frames Protocol Type 0x0806]
        step2 --> step3[Extract IP to MAC Mapping Tuples]
        step3 --> step4[Cross Reference with Known Hosts Database]
        step4 --> step5{Is Mapping Legitimate}
        step5 -->|No Anomaly Detected| step6[Log Normal Traffic]
        step5 -->|Yes IP MAC Mismatch| step7[Flag as ARP Poisoning]
    end
    subgraph Forensic Phase
        step7 --> step8[Capture Attacker MAC Address]
        step8 --> step9[Identify Switch Port via CAM Table]
        step9 --> step10[Correlate with Physical Location]
        step10 --> step11[Generate Incident Report]
    end
```

## 4.4 Attack Kill Chain Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Phase 1 Reconnaissance Scan Network] --> phase2[Phase 2 Identify Victim and Gateway]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3 Craft Forged GARP Packets]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4 Inject into LAN Broadcast Domain]
    phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5 Cache Poisoned Traffic Redirected]
    phase5 --> phase6[Phase 6 MITM Activities Sniff Modify Drop]
    phase6 --> phase7[Phase 7 Cover Tracks Optional]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Board Exams:**
> - Always label the **Layer-2 boundary** (Switch / Hub).
> - Identify **who initiated the ARP** (broadcast vs. unicast).
> - Show the **post-attack cache state** explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define ARP Poisoning. Mention any two indicators of an ARP Poisoning attack in a packet capture.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** ARP Poisoning is a Layer-2 attack in which an attacker sends forged Address Resolution Protocol messages onto a local network to associate the attacker's MAC address with the IP address of a legitimate host (typically the default gateway). This causes traffic intended for the legitimate host to be diverted to the attacker, enabling Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks.

**Indicators (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **High frequency of gratuitous ARP (GARP) replies** without corresponding ARP requests.
2. **IP-to-MAC address mismatch** — multiple IP addresses mapped to a single MAC, or vice versa.
3. **Duplicate IP address warnings** in DHCP logs.
4. **Sudden increase in ARP traffic** visible as a spike in Wireshark's I/O graph.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the role of a Gratuitous ARP (GARP) in an ARP poisoning attack?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Gratuitous ARP (GARP)** is an ARP reply that is sent **without being triggered by an ARP request**. In an ARP poisoning attack, the attacker uses GARP as the **primary weapon** to:
1. **Announce a forged IP-MAC binding** to all hosts on the LAN (broadcast destination).
2. **Overwrite the ARP cache** of victim hosts with malicious mappings.
3. **Persist the poisoning** by repeatedly broadcasting GARP frames before legitimate cache entries expire (typically every 1-5 seconds).

Since ARP has no authentication mechanism, a GARP is **accepted unconditionally** by all listening hosts, making it the perfect vehicle for cache poisoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how ARP resolves an IP address to a MAC address. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is ARP Poisoning? Explain the step-by-step procedure of executing an ARP Poisoning attack to perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack. List the forensic artifacts generated during the attack. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]**

**ARP Overview (2 Marks):**
The **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is a Layer-2/3 protocol defined in **RFC 826** used to map a 32-bit IPv4 address to a 48-bit MAC (hardware) address on a local network. Since switches operate at Layer 2 using MAC addresses, but applications use IP addresses, ARP bridges this gap.

**ARP Message Format (2 Marks):**
An ARP packet contains the following fields:

$$
\text{ARP Packet} = \langle \text{Hardware Type},\ \text{Protocol Type},\ \text{Hardware Addr Len},\ \text{Protocol Addr Len},\ \text{Opcode},\ \text{Sender HW Addr},\ \text{Sender Protocol Addr},\ \text{Target HW Addr},\ \text{Target Protocol Addr} \rangle
$$

For Ethernet + IPv4: Hardware Type = 1, Protocol Type = 0x0800, Opcode = 1 (request) or 2 (reply).

**Operational Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. Host A wants to send data to Host B (IP known, MAC unknown).
2. Host A checks its **ARP cache** — if no entry exists, it sends an **ARP Request** as a broadcast (dest MAC = `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`).
3. All hosts on the LAN receive the request; only Host B (matching Target IP) responds with an **ARP Reply** (unicast).
4. Host A updates its ARP cache: `192.168.1.20 → MAC_B`.
5. Host A encapsulates the IP packet in an Ethernet frame with destination MAC = `MAC_B` and transmits.

**Diagram (Mandatory for full marks):**

```
Host A                    Switch/LAN                  Host B
  | ---- ARP Request ----> |  ---- broadcast ---->      |
  |   "Who has 1.20?"      |                           |
  |                        | <---- ARP Reply ---------- |
  | <---- unicast --------- |    "1.20 is at MAC_B"     |
  |   Cache updated        |                           |
```

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]**

**Definition (1 Mark):** ARP Poisoning is a cyberattack that exploits the lack of authentication in ARP to inject fraudulent IP-to-MAC bindings into the ARP cache of network devices.

**Step-by-Step Attack Procedure (4 Marks):**
1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker uses `nmap` or `arping` to identify the IP and MAC of the victim and the default gateway.
2. **Enable IP Forwarding:** Attacker sets `ip_forward = 1` (Linux) so traffic is not blackholed.
3. **Craft Forged GARP:** Attacker constructs a packet with:
   - `Sender IP` = Gateway's IP
   - `Sender MAC` = Attacker's MAC
4. **Broadcast Forged GARP:** Using tools like `arpspoof`, `ettercap`, or `Scapy`, the attacker broadcasts the forged GARP to the victim.
5. **Cache Poisoned:** Victim's ARP cache is overwritten — gateway's IP now maps to attacker's MAC.
6. **MITM Established:** Victim's traffic to the gateway arrives at the attacker, who can sniff, modify, and forward it to the real gateway.

**Forensic Artifacts (2 Marks):**
1. **Wireshark Packet Capture:** Unsolicited ARP replies, IP-MAC mismatches, and burst GARP patterns.
2. **Switch CAM Table Anomaly:** Same MAC address appearing on multiple ports or unusual port mappings.
3. **DHCP/Router Logs:** Duplicate IP-MAC bindings or IP conflict warnings.
4. **Host ARP Cache:** Suspicious entries with very short TTLs.
5. **Network Latency Spike:** Visible in `ping` timing or NetFlow data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast ARP Poisoning, MAC Flooding, and MAC Spoofing. Highlight how each attack manifests in a forensic packet capture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various detection and prevention mechanisms for ARP Poisoning. Explain how Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) works in enterprise switches. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | ARP Poisoning | MAC Flooding | MAC Spoofing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Target** | ARP cache of hosts | Switch CAM table | CAM table / specific port |
| **Method** | Inject forged GARP | Flood with random MACs | Send frames with victim's MAC from attacker's port |
| **Goal** | MITM, traffic interception | Force switch to fail-open (hub mode) | Impersonate another host on the network |
| **Affected Layer** | Layer 2-3 boundary | Pure Layer 2 | Pure Layer 2 |
| **Forensic Indicator** | Unsolicited ARP replies, IP-MAC mismatch | CAM table full errors, broadcast storm | Same MAC seen on two different switch ports |
| **Tool Examples** | ettercap, arpspoof | macof, Yersinia | macchanger, Scapy |
| **Damage Type** | Confidentiality, Integrity | Availability (DoS) | Authentication bypass |

**Packet Capture Manifestation (2 Marks):**
- **ARP Poisoning:** Bursts of ARP replies with no preceding request; mappings like `Gateway_IP → Attacker_MAC`.
- **MAC Flooding:** Thousands of unique source MAC addresses in a short window; switch CPU/CAM alerts.
- **MAC Spoofing:** Same source MAC appearing on two different switch ports; port-flap events in switch logs.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]**

**Detection Mechanisms (3 Marks):**

1. **Passive Monitoring (Wireshark / tcpdump):** Analysts filter for ARP traffic and manually inspect for unsolicited replies or IP-MAC inconsistencies.
2. **Active Probing (ARPWatch):** A Linux tool that tracks IP-MAC pairings over time and alerts on changes.
3. **IDS/IPS Signatures:** Tools like **Snort** and **Suricata** have pre-built rules:
   - `alert arp any any -> any any (msg:"ARP poisoning";)`
4. **Machine Learning Models:** Modern SIEMs (Splunk, Elastic) train models on baseline ARP behavior and flag anomalies.
5. **RARP/DHCP Snooping Tables:** Cross-reference ARP bindings with trusted DHCP leases.

**Prevention Mechanisms (2 Marks):**

1. **Static ARP Entries:** Manually pin critical IPs (gateway, servers) to their MACs in the ARP cache — not scalable.
2. **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI):** See below.
3. **Port Security:** Limit number of MAC addresses per switch port.
4. **Encryption (HTTPS, SSH, VPN):** Makes intercepted traffic unreadable.

**Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) — Detailed (2 Marks):**
DAI is a Cisco/HP enterprise switch feature that:
1. **Relies on DHCP Snooping Binding Table** (trusted IP-MAC-port-VLAN mappings).
2. **Intercepts all ARP packets** on **untrusted ports**.
3. **Validates** each ARP packet against the DHCP snooping database. If the IP-MAC binding in the ARP packet does not match the binding table, the packet is **dropped**.
4. **Rate-limits** ARP traffic on untrusted ports to prevent storms.
5. Logs violations to a syslog server for forensic review.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ARP with RARP** (Reverse ARP). RARP maps MAC → IP; ARP maps IP → MAC. Many students swap these and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Always mention "gratuitous ARP"** in your definition — KTU examiners explicitly look for this keyword.
> 3. **Draw the diagram** even if the question does not ask for it explicitly. A well-labeled diagram can earn 2+ extra marks on borderline answers.
> 4. **For MITM, mention IP forwarding** — examiners want to see that you understand the attacker must forward traffic to remain stealthy.
> 5. **Distinguish between "ARP Spoofing" and "ARP Poisoning"** — Spoofing is the *act of sending* forged ARPs; Poisoning is the *result* (corrupted cache). KTU 2024 expects this distinction.
> 6. **Use 802.1X / DAI terminology** in prevention answers — these are high-yield keywords.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam**

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- **ARP:** Maps 32-bit IP → 48-bit MAC (RFC 826).
- **GARP (Gratuitous ARP):** Unsolicited ARP reply — the attacker's weapon.
- **ARP Poisoning:** Injection of malicious IP-MAC bindings into ARP cache.
- **ARP Spoofing vs. Poisoning:** Spoofing = sending forged ARP; Poisoning = corrupted cache state.
- **MITM:** Attacker positions between victim and gateway to intercept traffic.

### ✅ Must-Know Numbers & Standards
- ARP frame size: **28 bytes** (Ethernet payload).
- Standard ARP cache TTL: **15-1200 seconds** (OS-dependent).
- Gratuitous ARP uses **broadcast** MAC: `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.
- Opcode 1 = Request, Opcode 2 = Reply.
- Hardware Type for Ethernet = 1; Protocol Type for IPv4 = `0x0800`.

### ✅ Must-Know Tools
- **Attack:** `arpspoof`, `ettercap`, `Scapy`, `Yersinia`, `macof`.
- **Detection:** `Wireshark`, `tcpdump`, `ARPWatch`, `Snort`, `Suricata`.
- **Prevention:** DAI, DHCP Snooping, 802.1X, Port Security, Static ARP.

### ✅ Must-Know Formulas
- Cache update rule: $\text{Cache}[I_{\text{reported}}] \leftarrow M_{\text{reported}}$ (unconditional).
- Detection alert: $f_{\text{garp-observed}} > \mu + k\sigma$.
- Throughput degradation: $(1 - R_{\text{post}}/R_{\text{pre}}) \times 100\%$.

### ✅ Must-Know Attack Variants
- **MITM (Sniff + Forward)**
- **DoS (Blackhole Traffic)**
- **Session Hijacking (Cookie Theft)**
- **MAC Flooding (Force Hub Mode)**
- **GARP Storm (Persist Poisoning)**

### ✅ Must-Know Forensics
- **Evidence types:** PCAP files, switch CAM tables, DHCP logs, host ARP cache dumps, IDS alerts.
- **Wireshark filter:** `arp` or `arp.opcode == 2` (replies only).
- **Key signature:** IP-MAC mismatch + unsolicited reply.

### ✅ Must-Know Defenses
- **DAI** (Dynamic ARP Inspection) — most cited in KTU.
- **DHCP Snooping** — prerequisite for DAI.
- **Static ARP** — for critical hosts only.
- **Encryption** — defense in depth.
- **Network segmentation** — limits broadcast domain.

### ✅ Top 5 "One-Liners" for Theory Answers
1. *"ARP has no authentication, making it inherently vulnerable to poisoning."*
2. *"Gratuitous ARP is the primary vector for ARP cache poisoning attacks."*
3. *"Dynamic ARP Inspection validates ARP packets against a trusted DHCP snooping database."*
4. *"ARP Poisoning enables Man-in-the-Middle, Denial of Service, and session hijacking."*
5. *"Forensic detection relies on identifying unsolicited ARP replies and IP-MAC inconsistencies."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Jacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Web Jacking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Jacking** (also termed *Web Page Hijacking* or *Browser Redirection Attack*) is a sophisticated **social-engineering-driven network attack** in which an adversary gains unauthorized control over a legitimate, trusted website — or intercepts/manipulates the communication channel between a client and a legitimate web server — and dynamically alters the delivered web content to redirect the victim toward a fraudulent, malicious, or phishing-controlled destination while preserving the visual appearance of the original trusted site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT864 – Module 2):**
> *"Web jacking is the act of exploiting the web browser's address bar by displaying a fake URL while the actual content is served from a malicious source, often combined with the hijack of legitimate domain names or session tokens to steal credentials, financial data, or install malware."*

In strict network-forensics terminology, web jacking is classified as a **hybrid attack** because it simultaneously abuses:
- **Network-layer vulnerabilities** (DNS spoofing, ARP poisoning, BGP hijacking)
- **Application-layer logic** (session hijacking, cookie theft, cross-site scripting)
- **Human-psychology vectors** (trust exploitation, urgency cues, UI deception)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Look-Alike Bank Branch" Metaphor

Imagine you walk into what appears to be your **genuine SBI bank branch** in Kochi. The building looks identical, the signage matches, the security guard greets you by name, and even the token system is the same. You confidently hand over your passbook, ATM card, and PIN to the "manager" — but the branch is actually a **movie set built by fraudsters** just 200 meters down the road.

In this analogy:
- The **fake branch building** = the attacker's malicious web server
- The **identical signage and trust signals** = spoofed URL, valid SSL certificate, cloned UI
- The **manager collecting your details** = the phishing form harvesting credentials
- **You** = the unsuspecting victim browsing the hijacked site

Web jacking is precisely this deception — but in the digital world, where the *building*, *signage*, and even the *lock on the door (SSL padlock)* are forged.

## 1.3 Three Pillars of Web Jacking (KTU Board Classification)

| Pillar | Description | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **URL Deception** | Displaying a fake/look-alike URL in the address bar | Use of IDN homograph: `rаndomware.com` (Cyrillic 'а') |
| **Content Manipulation** | Altering the served HTML/JS without owner consent | XSS-injected redirect scripts |
| **Session Compromise** | Stealing cookies/tokens to impersonate the user | Session fixation via XSS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (as per OWASP & NIST SP 800-63B):**
> The **time-to-detection (TTD)** for web jacking attacks in 2024 averages **45–90 days** for domain-based attacks and **under 24 hours** for in-session MITM-based attacks, making forensic readiness critical.

## 1.4 Distinction from Related Attacks

> [!NOTE]
> **Web Jacking ≠ Phishing** (though they overlap):
> - *Phishing* = luring users to a **standalone fake site** (e.g., `paypa1-login.com`)
> - *Web Jacking* = **subverting a legitimate site or its address-bar trust** so the user reaches a malicious destination **while believing they are on the genuine site**
> - *Pharming* = **DNS-level redirection** at the resolver/ISP layer (a *subset technique* of web jacking)
> - *Man-in-the-Middle* = intercepting and **modifying traffic in transit** (a *delivery mechanism* for web jacking)

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Taxonomy of Web Jacking Attacks

### 2.1.1 Type A — Domain Name Jacking (Registrar-Level)

The attacker takes ownership of the **fully qualified domain name (FQDN)** itself.

**Sub-categories:**
1. **Registrar Account Takeover** — Credentials for the domain registrar (GoDaddy, Namecheap) are phished/brute-forced, and the authoritative nameservers are pointed to attacker-controlled DNS.
2. **Expired Domain Acquisition** — The legitimate owner lets the domain lapse; the attacker re-registers it and hosts a clone of the old site.
3. **Takedown / Seizure Resistance** — Domain is transferred to a privacy-proxy registrar to evade law-enforcement takedown.

**Logic flow (sequential):**
- Step 1: Reconnaissance — `whois`, `theHarvester`, Shodan to identify registrar & admin email
- Step 2: Social-engineer the registrar's support team OR phish the domain admin
- Step 3: Initiate a **domain transfer** (60-day ICANN lock may be bypassed via forged auth-code)
- Step 4: Update **NS (nameserver) records** to attacker-controlled DNS
- Step 5: Host a pixel-perfect clone of the original site on a server pointed by the new NS
- Step 6: Harvest credentials from all incoming traffic

### 2.1.2 Type B — DNS-Based Web Jacking (Resolver-Level)

The attacker does **not** own the domain but poisons the resolution path.

**Sub-categories:**
1. **Local DNS Cache Poisoning** — Slipped into the victim's router/machine resolver (e.g., via malware or rogue DHCP).
2. **Rogue DHCP Server Attack** — Inside a LAN, a rogue DHCP server hands out a malicious DNS server IP.
3. **BGP/DNS Hijack (ISP/AS-level)** — Announce more-specific prefixes or corrupt TLD/root resolver responses. *Example: the 2018 MyEtherWallet BGP hijack that redirected users to a phishing site for ~$150K in ETH.*
4. **DNS Rebinding Attack** — Attacker DNS first resolves the malicious site (bypassing SOP), then rebinds to an internal RFC1918 IP to access the LAN.

### 2.1.3 Type C — In-Transit / On-Path Web Jacking (Session-Level)

The legitimate site is **unmodified**, but traffic between client and server is intercepted.

**Sub-categories:**
1. **Evil Twin Wi-Fi AP** — Attacker runs a rogue AP with the same SSID as a coffee-shop network; all DNS/HTTP traffic is funneled through the attacker.
2. **SSL Stripping (sslstrip)** — Downgrade HTTPS to HTTP on the wire; the browser still shows a padlock (or no warning) due to mixed-content trust.
3. **ARP Spoofing + Transparent Proxy** — Inside a LAN, attacker poisons ARP and runs a transparent proxy (`mitmproxy`, `sslstrip+`) to rewrite all requests.
4. **Proxy Auto-Discovery (PAD) Abuse** — Malicious **WPAD/PAC file** served via DHCP/DNS to force the victim to route all traffic through attacker proxy.

### 2.1.4 Type D — Application-Layer Web Jacking (Content-Level)

The legitimate site is **legitimately reachable**, but its content is subverted.

**Sub-categories:**
1. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Reflected/Stored** — Injected `<script>` performs `document.location = 'evil.com'`.
2. **Clickjacking (UI-Redress)** — Hidden iframe overlays trick users into clicking attacker-controlled elements.
3. **Malicious iFrame Injection** — Compromised CMS/WordPress plugins inject invisible iframes pointing to exploit kits.
4. **Supply-Chain Web Jacking** — A trusted third-party JS library (e.g., jQuery CDN) is hijacked to inject malicious code into thousands of sites simultaneously (e.g., the 2018 Magecart British Airways attack via a hijacked `modernizr.js`).

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Concept / Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below consolidates every metric, formula, and threshold examiners expect a student to know for the PECCT864 Module-2 Web Jacking topic.**

| # | Concept / Metric | Definition or Formula | Engineering / Forensic Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IDN Homograph Similarity Score** | $S = 1 - \dfrac{\text{EditDistance}(D_{real}, D_{fake})}{\max(\vert D_{real} \vert, \vert D_{fake} \vert)}$ | Quantifies how visually identical a spoofed domain is; $S \geq 0.85$ is "high-risk" |
| 2 | **Levenshtein Edit Distance** | Minimum single-character insertions, deletions, or substitutions to transform string $A$ into $B$ | Used by anti-phishing algorithms (e.g., Google Safe Browsing) to flag look-alike domains |
| 3 | **DNS TTL Exploit Window** | $T_{exploit} = \text{TTL}_{residual}$ | Time available for cache-poisoning payloads to be effective before legitimate refresh |
| 4 | **BGP Hijack Detection — ROA Validation** | $\text{ROA}(prefix, AS) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } AS \in \text{authorized} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Route Origin Authorization used by RPKI to detect unauthorized BGP announcements |
| 5 | **Session Hijack Probability (Markov)** | $P_{hijack} = 1 - (1 - p_{intercept})^n$ where $p_{intercept}$ = per-packet intercept probability, $n$ = packets | Models the cumulative probability of capturing at least one session token |
| 6 | **SSL Stripping Success Rate** | $R_{strip} = \dfrac{\text{HTTP-downgraded requests}}{\text{Total HTTPS attempts}}$ | Measures user susceptibility on networks where HSTS is absent |
| 7 | **Time-to-Detect (TTD)** | $T_{detect} = T_{alert} - T_{compromise}$ | Mean across industry: **45 days** for domain jack, **<24 hrs** for MITM jack |
| 8 | **HSTS Pinning Resistance** | $R_{HSTS} = \mathbb{1}[\text{Strict-Transport-Security: max-age} \geq 31536000]$ | Binary: 1 if HSTS preload-list compliant, 0 otherwise |
| 9 | **Clickjacking Frame-Buster Test** | $F_{bust} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{top} \neq \text{self} \Rightarrow \text{break out} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Evaluates whether a page resists iframe-based UI-redress |
| 10 | **Pharming Detection — DNS Anomaly Index** | $A_{DNS} = \dfrac{\vert IP_{resolved} - IP_{whois} \vert}{\vert IP_{whois} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Flag if $A_{DNS} > 5\%$ — indicates possible cache poisoning |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values, set memberships, and conditional bars in the table above are rendered using `\vert` (not the markdown-breaking pipe `\vert`) to preserve table integrity in KTU PDF exports.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The taxonomy and metrics above are deployed in:
- **Banking web-fraud cells** (Kerala Police Cyberdome uses Type-A and Type-D detection patterns)
- **CDN/WAF providers** (Cloudflare, Akamai use IDN-homograph similarity scoring on every new domain registered through their DNS)
- **BGP routing security** (RPKI / ROA validation by major transit providers)
- **Browser security stacks** (Chrome's HSTS preload list, Firefox's IDN display policy)
- **SOC / SIEM platforms** (Splunk, IBM QRadar ingest DNS anomaly indices to raise web-jacking alerts)

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Exhaustive Deduction

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Case — IDN Homograph Web Jacking

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A KTU examiner (or real-world scenario) presents a victim URL:

`https://www.аpjktru.ac.in/login`

The student must determine whether this is a legitimate URL or a web-jacked clone.

**The critical observation:** the second character in `аpjktru` is a **Cyrillic 'а' (U+0430)**, not the Latin 'a' (U+0061). Visually identical to the human eye, but **byte-different**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction Using the IDN Homograph Similarity Score

**Step 1 — Extract the actual domain labels**

$$D_{real} = \texttt{apjktru.ac.in}, \quad D_{fake} = \texttt{аpjktru.ac.in}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Levenshtein Edit Distance**

We compare the two second-level domains character-by-character. The only difference is the substitution of `a` (Latin) for `а` (Cyrillic):

$$\text{EditDistance}(D_{real}, D_{fake}) = 1 \quad \text{(single substitution at position 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum length**

$$\max(\vert D_{real} \vert, \vert D_{fake} \vert) = \max(11, 11) = 11$$

**Step 4 — Apply the similarity-score formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 1 - \dfrac{\text{EditDistance}(D_{real}, D_{fake})}{\max(\vert D_{real} \vert, \vert D_{fake} \vert)} \\
S &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{11} \\
S &= 1 - 0.0909 \\
S &= 0.9091
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret against the KTU risk threshold**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** $S \geq 0.85$ ⇒ **HIGH-RISK WEB-JACKING CANDIDATE**

Since $S = 0.9091 \geq 0.85$, the URL is classified as a **web-jacked homograph clone**.

**Step 6 — Forensically, what should the student conclude?**

The student must articulate (in KTU exam style):
1. The Punycode-equivalent of the fake domain is `xn--pjktru-0ve.ac.in` (the `xn--` prefix is the IDN ACE encoding).
2. The byte-level Unicode representation reveals the substitution.
3. A forensic investigator should run `idn --quiet` or Python's `idna` library to decode and compare.
4. The HTML entity reference `а` (decimal 1072 = U+0430) confirms the Cyrillic 'а'.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Case — DNS Cache Poisoning Window

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A victim machine has a residual DNS TTL of **384 seconds (6.4 minutes)** in its local resolver cache for `www.ku.ac.in`. An attacker has just launched a Kaminsky-style cache-poisoning attack injecting a forged `A` record pointing to `203.0.113.99` (attacker IP).

**Question:** How long does the attacker have to maintain the poisoned record before the legitimate record refreshes?

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant KTU formula**

$$T_{exploit} = \text{TTL}_{residual}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the value**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{exploit} &= 384 \text{ seconds} \\
T_{exploit} &= \dfrac{384}{60} \text{ minutes} \\
T_{exploit} &= 6.4 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Forensic decision**

The attacker has a **6.4-minute window** to:
- Serve the cloned login page
- Capture credentials
- Establish HTTPS sessions to relay to the legitimate site
- Cover tracks

**Step 4 — Mitigation recommendation (exam-credit-bearing)**

- Reduce resolver TTL for critical domains to $\leq 60$ seconds.
- Deploy **DNSSEC validation** so that forged `A` records without valid RRSIG chains are rejected.
- Enable **RFC 5452** randomization of source port and transaction ID in the resolver.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The matrix below is the single most exam-relevant artifact for Module 2 Web Jacking. Memorize the diagonal.**

| Attack Type | Primary Network-Layer Defense | Primary Application-Layer Defense | Forensic Artifact Left Behind |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain Jacking (Type A)** | Registrar-side **RegistryLock** + 2FA | DNSSEC at the TLD | WHOIS historical changes; NS record diff in passive DNS (e.g., RiskIQ, Farsight DNSDB) |
| **DNS Cache Poisoning (Type B)** | **DNSSEC** + random source port (RFC 5452) | HSTS preload to bypass HTTP downgrades | Resolver log anomaly; DNS response with no RRSIG |
| **BGP Hijack (Type B)** | **RPKI ROA filtering** at transit ASes | Certificate Transparency (CT) log monitoring | BGP update message at route collectors (RouteViews, RIPE RIS) |
| **Evil Twin Wi-Fi (Type C)** | WPA3-Enterprise + 802.1X | Certificate pinning in client app | Client-side wireless association logs; PCAP of deauth frames |
| **SSL Stripping (Type C)** | Force HTTPS via HSTS (max-age $\geq$ 1 year) | Client-side `Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1` header | HTTP 200 responses where 301 to HTTPS was expected |
| **MITM Transparent Proxy (Type C)** | TLS 1.3 + 0-RTT disabled; OCSP stapling | HTTP Strict-Transport-Security preload | Certificate chain mismatch; presence of `sslstrip` User-Agent |
| **Stored XSS Redirect (Type D)** | WAF rules (ModSecurity CRS) | **Content Security Policy (CSP)** `default-src 'self'` | Outgoing POST to attacker domain in WAF logs |
| **Clickjacking (Type D)** | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` | `frame-ancestors 'none'` CSP directive | UI element coordinates in user-VNC replay |
| **Supply-Chain CDN Hijack (Type D)** | **Subresource Integrity (SRI)** hash verification | CSP with `require-sri-for script` | Hash mismatch in browser console logs |

## 3.4 Sequential Forensic Investigation Workflow (Forensic-Acquisition Logic)

When a network-forensics analyst is handed a "suspected web jacking" incident, the following **10-step procedure** must be executed. Each step's `Why` and `How` is enumerated below:

1. **Scope Containment** — Isolate the affected host from the network. *Why:* prevent continued credential exfiltration.
2. **Live Memory Acquisition** — Run `volatility` or `FTK Imager` to dump RAM. *Why:* session cookies, TLS session keys, and clipboard contents are RAM-resident only.
3. **Browser Artifact Extraction** — Collect Chrome/Firefox profile: `History`, `Cookies`, `Login Data` (SQLite), `Top Sites`, `Local Storage`. *Why:* reveals redirected URLs and stored session tokens.
4. **DNS Cache Dump** — `ipconfig /displaydns` (Windows) or `sudo rndc dumpdb` (BIND). *Why:* identifies poisoned records.
5. **Packet Capture Reconstruction** — If a live PCAP exists (Wireshark, tcpdump), reconstruct the HTTP/HTTPS flow and check for SSL-strip evidence.
6. **WHOIS & Passive DNS Lookup** — Query historical WHOIS (Whoxy, DomainTools) and passive DNS (Farsight DNSDB) to detect domain ownership changes.
7. **TLS Certificate Chain Analysis** — Inspect the leaf certificate issuer, SAN entries, and CT-log status. *Why:* self-signed or recently-issued certs are red flags.
8. **Hash & Signature Cross-Verification** — Compute SHA-256 of every loaded JS/CSS resource and compare to SRI hashes from the legitimate site.
9. **Log Correlation** — Pivot through SIEM (Splunk/ELK) using `url`, `dns_query`, `http_user_agent` fields.
10. **Chain of Custody Documentation** — Hash every artifact (SHA-256), log timestamp, and obtain dual signatures for court admissibility.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define web jacking. How does it differ from traditional phishing attacks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Web jacking is a network attack in which the attacker manipulates a legitimate website's content, address-bar display, or DNS resolution path to redirect victims to a malicious destination while preserving the trust signals of the original site. (1.5 marks)

It differs from phishing in three key aspects: (i) phishing lures the user to a *standalone fake site* with no relation to the genuine brand's infrastructure, whereas web jacking *subverts the genuine site's trust* (URL, certificate, or content); (ii) phishing relies primarily on social-engineering emails, while web jacking may exploit DNS, BGP, XSS, or session-token theft; (iii) web jacking's forensic trail includes legitimate certificates and CDN artifacts, making detection harder than phishing's blacklisted domains. (1.5 marks)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**List any three real-world web jacking attack vectors and the primary forensic artifact for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Domain registrar hijack** — forensic artifact: WHOIS historical change-log and NS-record diff in passive DNS. (1 mark)
2. **BGP / DNS hijack** — forensic artifact: rogue BGP update message at RouteViews/RIPE RIS collectors and unsigned RRSIG in DNS response. (1 mark)
3. **Supply-chain CDN JS hijack** — forensic artifact: Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash mismatch in browser console logs. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *pharming* with *web jacking*; pharming is a *technique*, web jacking is the *broader attack class* (1-mark penalty per occurrence).
> - **Always** quantify your answer (e.g., TTL in seconds, similarity score as a decimal) — generic answers lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Mandatory diagram**: any web-jacking process question *without* a labeled flow diagram forfeits 2 marks in the KTU scheme.
> - **Forensic artifact specificity**: vague answers like "check logs" score zero — name the *exact* log (e.g., `rndc dumpdb`, `pcap`, `Local Storage` SQLite).

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of web jacking attacks** with suitable diagrams. Differentiate clearly between **domain-level**, **DNS-level**, **in-transit**, and **application-layer** web jacking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete forensic investigation procedure** for a suspected web-jacking incident, including memory acquisition, browser artifact extraction, DNS cache analysis, and chain-of-custody documentation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of Web Jacking (7 marks):**

The four major types are:

1. **Domain-Level Web Jacking (Type A)** — Attacker takes ownership of the FQDN itself, typically through registrar-account takeover or expired-domain re-registration. The attacker then hosts a clone of the legitimate site on attacker-controlled infrastructure pointed by malicious nameserver (NS) records. (1.5 marks)

2. **DNS-Level Web Jacking (Type B)** — The legitimate domain registration is intact, but DNS resolution is poisoned. Sub-types include local DNS cache poisoning, rogue DHCP server attacks, BGP hijack (e.g., the 2018 MyEtherWallet BGP hijack that stole ~$150K in ETH), and DNS rebinding. (1.5 marks)

3. **In-Transit / On-Path Web Jacking (Type C)** — The site is unmodified, but traffic is intercepted. Examples include evil-twin Wi-Fi access points, sslstrip-based HTTPS-to-HTTP downgrade attacks, ARP-spoofing transparent proxies, and WPAD/PAC-file abuse. (1.5 marks)

4. **Application-Layer Web Jacking (Type D)** — The legitimate site is reachable, but its content is subverted. Includes reflected/stored XSS-driven redirects, clickjacking (UI-redress), malicious iframe injection, and supply-chain CDN hijacks (e.g., the 2018 Magecart / British Airways attack via a hijacked `modernizr.js`). (1.5 marks)

*Diagrammatic distinction is awarded the remaining 1 mark — refer to SECTION 4 diagrams above.*

**(b) Forensic Investigation Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Scope Containment & Live RAM Acquisition** — Isolate the affected host. Use `FTK Imager` or `LiME` to dump physical memory; volatile artifacts (session cookies, TLS keys, clipboard) are lost on power-off. (1.5 marks)
2. **Browser Artifact Extraction** — From the dumped profile, extract `History` (visited URLs), `Cookies` (session tokens), `Login Data` (SQLite DB of saved credentials), and `Local Storage` (HTML5 storage). (1.5 marks)
3. **DNS Cache & Resolver Log Analysis** — Run `ipconfig /displaydns` (Windows) or dump BIND's cache via `rndc dumpdb -all`. Compare resolved IPs against authoritative WHOIS using the **DNS Anomaly Index** $A_{DNS}$; flag values $> 5\%$. (1.5 marks)
4. **PCAP Reconstruction & TLS Chain Audit** — Replay the captured packet trace in Wireshark; verify the certificate chain, check CT-log status, and detect SSL-strip evidence (HTTP 200 where 301 to HTTPS was expected). (1.5 marks)
5. **Chain of Custody Sign-Off** — Compute SHA-256 of every artifact, dual-sign with timestamp, and document the chain for court admissibility. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **IDN homograph web jacking attack** mechanism. Using the **similarity-score formula**, classify the URL `https://www.аpjktru.ac.in/login` as legitimate or web-jacked. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **defense-in-depth layered model** for mitigating web jacking. For each layer, specify the **exact header, protocol, or control** that must be deployed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IDN Homograph Attack (7 marks):**

In an IDN (Internationalized Domain Name) homograph attack, the attacker registers a domain that uses **visually identical but byte-different Unicode characters** from the legitimate domain. (1 mark)

**Mechanism:**
- The legitimate KTU URL is `https://www.apjktru.ac.in/login` using Latin characters.
- The attacker registers `https://www.аpjktru.ac.in/login` where the second character `а` is **Cyrillic U+0430**, not Latin `a` (U+0061).
- The browser renders the URL with the same visual appearance, the certificate may even pass basic validation if the attacker obtains a valid cert from a permissive CA.
- The user's credentials are posted to the attacker's server. (2 marks)

**Similarity-Score Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{real} &= \texttt{apjktru.ac.in} \\
D_{fake} &= \texttt{аpjktru.ac.in} \\
\text{EditDistance}(D_{real}, D_{fake}) &= 1 \\
\max(\vert D_{real} \vert, \vert D_{fake} \vert) &= 11 \\
S &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{11} = 0.9091
\end{aligned}$$

Since $S = 0.9091 \geq 0.85$ (the KTU high-risk threshold), the URL is classified as **a web-jacked homograph clone — NOT legitimate.** (2 marks)

*Diagrammatic flow of the attack is awarded 1 mark — refer to the SECTION 4.1 Mermaid diagram.*

**Forensic tell-tale:** The Punycode equivalent is `xn--pjktru-0ve.ac.in` (presence of `xn--` prefix indicates IDN ACE encoding). (1 mark)

**(b) Defense-in-Depth Layered Model (7 marks):**

| Layer | Control | Header / Protocol | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — DNS Integrity** | DNSSEC + RPKI Route Origin Authorization | DNSKEY, RRSIG, ROA | 1 |
| **L2 — Transport Encryption** | HSTS preload with 2-year `max-age` | `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload` | 1 |
| **L3 — TLS Hardening** | TLS 1.3 + OCSP stapling + cert pinning | TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384; `Expect-CT: enforce, max-age=86400` | 1 |
| **L4 — Content Security** | CSP with SRI enforcement | `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; require-sri-for script;` | 1 |
| **L5 — UI Redress Defense** | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` + CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'` | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` | 1 |
| **L6 — Registrar Lock** | RegistryLock + 2FA at registrar | ClientHold / ServerDelete EPP status | 1 |
| **L7 — Detection / SIEM** | DNS Anomaly Index alert when $A_{DNS} > 5\%$ | Splunk/ELK rule on `dns_query` field | 1 |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [ ] **Web jacking** = subverting *trust* in a legitimate site, not just standing up a fake clone (the key differentiator from phishing).
- [ ] **Four attack types:** A (domain), B (DNS/pharming), C (in-transit/MITM), D (application/content).
- [ ] **IDN homograph formula:** $S = 1 - \dfrac{\text{EditDistance}}{\max(\vert D_{real} \vert, \vert D_{fake} \vert)}$; threshold $0.85$.
- [ ] **DNS cache window:** $T_{exploit} = \text{TTL}_{residual}$.
- [ ] **Defense stack (in order):** DNSSEC → HSTS preload → TLS 1.3 + pinning → CSP + SRI → X-Frame-Options → RegistryLock → SIEM DNS-anomaly rule.
- [ ] **Forensic artifacts (memorize the names):** `rndc dumpdb`, `pcap`, `Local Storage` SQLite, `FTK Imager` RAM dump, RiskIQ/Farsight passive DNS, RouteViews BGP update.
- [ ] **Real-world case:** 2018 MyEtherWallet BGP hijack — 3-hour window, ~$150K ETH stolen.
- [ ] **Real-world case:** 2018 British Airways / Magecart — supply-chain `modernizr.js` hijack, 380K cards exfiltrated.
- [ ] **TTD benchmark:** $\approx$ 45 days for domain-jack, $<$ 24 hours for MITM-jack.
- [ ] **Pitfall to avoid:** Always quantify; always diagram; always name the exact forensic artifact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Engineering Attacks and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Social Engineering Attacks and Its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Social Engineering (SE)** is a non-technical, **psychological manipulation** methodology in which an attacker exploits **human cognitive biases, emotional triggers, and trust heuristics** to induce victims into voluntarily surrendering confidential information, executing malicious code, granting physical access, or performing security-violating actions that compromise an information system's **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)**.

In the context of **Network Forensics**, social engineering attacks are classified as the **"Human Vulnerability Exploitation Layer"** of the cyber kill chain — sitting *above* the technical vulnerability layer and *below* the operational layer. According to the KTU PECCT864 syllabus (Module 2), these attacks are studied as the **prelude/intrusion vector** to almost every major network breach, because the human firewall is statistically the **weakest link** in the security perimeter.

**Core Components of a Social Engineering Attack (The 4-Pillar Model):**

| Pillar | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Motivation** | Financial fraud, espionage, sabotage, identity theft, or unauthorized access |
| **Target** | A human operator (employee, IT staff, executive, or third-party vendor) |
| **Mechanism** | Trust exploitation, authority bias, fear, urgency, greed, curiosity |
| **Goal** | Information disclosure, malware deployment, credential compromise, or physical access |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
