| **TCP Retransmission Rate** | $Retr\,\% = \dfrac{N_{retrans}}{N_{total\,TCP\,segments}} \times 100$ | percent | Indicates network congestion |
| **Round Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = t_{ACK} - t_{SYN-ACK\,echo}$ | milliseconds | From SEQ/ACK Analysis |
| **Goodput** | $Goodput = \dfrac{L7\,payload\,bytes}{Total\,time}$ | Bps | Excludes protocol overhead |
| **Bandwidth Utilization** | $Util\,\% = \dfrac{Measured\,Throughput}{Link\,Capacity} \times 100$ | percent | e.g., $Util = \dfrac{85\,Mbps}{100\,Mbps} \times 100 = 85\%$ |
| **Channel Capacity (Shannon)** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | Theoretical sniffable ceiling |

> [!NOTE]  
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In KTU answer sheets, always express throughput in **bits per second (bps)** and file size in **bytes (B)** — examiners deduct marks for unit confusion. Use $\vert x \vert$ notation (NOT the pipe symbol) when writing absolute values inside tables to avoid markdown breakage.

## 2.4 Wireshark Architecture — A Layered View

Wireshark is built on a **modular, layered architecture**:

- **Capture Layer** → `dumpcap` (privileged binary) interacts with libpcap/Npcap.
- **File Layer** → Handles `.pcap` / `.pcapng` reading/writing.
- **Core Engine (Glib-based)** → Packet lifecycle, protocol tree, byte-view.
- **Dissector Layer** → Plugin-based protocol decoders (each protocol = a `.so`/`.dll`).
- **GUI Layer (Qt)** → Live capture window, packet list pane, details pane, bytes pane.
- **Filter Engine** → Two separate engines: BPF (capture) and Wireshark Display Filter (Wireshark-specific syntax).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Storage for a Full PCAP Capture

A KTU lab scenario: A forensic investigator captures traffic on a **100 Mbps** link. The average packet size is **800 bytes** and the investigation window is **45 minutes**. Calculate the **raw (uncompressed) pcap file size** in MB, assuming pcap adds a **24-byte** per-packet header.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Link Capacity } (R) &= 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s} \\
\text{Avg Packet Size } (P) &= 800 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{pcap Overhead } (H) &= 24 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Capture Duration } (T) &= 45 \text{ min} = 45 \times 60 = 2700 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective bytes on wire per packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective per packet} &= P + H \\
&= 800 + 24 \\
&= 824 \text{ bytes per packet}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **total forensic artifact size** that must be written to disk per packet.

**Step 3 — Compute the number of packets per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPS &= \dfrac{R}{P \times 8} \\
&= \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{800 \times 8} \\
&= \dfrac{100{,}000{,}000}{6400} \\
&= 15{,}625 \text{ packets per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total packets captured in 2700 s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= PPS \times T \\
&= 15{,}625 \times 2700 \\
&= 42{,}187{,}500 \text{ packets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total capture file size in bytes, then convert to MB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}_{bytes} &= N \times (P + H) \\
&= 42{,}187{,}500 \times 824 \\
&= 34{,}762{,}500{,}000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}_{MB} &= \dfrac{34{,}762{,}500{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \\
&\approx 33{,}153.5 \text{ MB} \\
&\approx 32.38 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This derivation demonstrates why **capture filters and ring buffers** are mandatory in real investigations — capturing *all* traffic on a busy 1 Gbps link can fill terabytes within minutes. The most common mistake students make is forgetting to multiply by 8 when converting *bytes* to *bits* for throughput.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TCP Retransmission Rate from a PCAP

A Wireshark capture contains **18,400 total TCP segments**, of which **276 segments are flagged as retransmissions** by the Expert Info engine. Calculate the **TCP retransmission rate** and classify the link health using the RFC-standard thresholds.

**Step 1 — Apply the retransmission rate formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Retr\,\% &= \dfrac{N_{retrans}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{276}{18{,}400} \times 100 \\
&= 1.5\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify the link health.**

| Retransmission % | Health Classification (Cisco / Wireshark) |
|---|---|
| $Retr\,\% < 1\%$ | **Excellent** — Healthy link |
| $1\% \le Retr\,\% < 3\%$ | **Acceptable** — Minor congestion |
| $3\% \le Retr\,\% < 7\%$ | **Degraded** — Investigate |
| $Retr\,\% \ge 7\%$ | **Critical** — Link failure imminent |

Since $Retr\,\% = 1.5\%$, the link is **Acceptable** — minor congestion, forensic investigator should correlate with I/O graph spikes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth Utilization on a 1 Gbps Backbone

Wireshark I/O Graph reports an **average measured throughput** of **432 Mbps** on a **1 Gbps** backbone link during a 30-minute capture window. Compute utilization and identify the headroom available.

**Step 1 — Apply the utilization formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Util\,\% &= \dfrac{\text{Measured Throughput}}{\text{Link Capacity}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{432 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{9}} \times 100 \\
&= 43.2\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute available headroom.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Headroom\,\% &= 100 - Util\,\% \\
&= 100 - 43.2 \\
&= 56.8\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> For forensic purposes, a sustained utilization above **70%** typically indicates that the link is a candidate for **DPI (Deep Packet Inspection)** bottlenecks and may be the source of dropped evidence packets.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Wireshark Display Filter Quick Reference

| Forensic Goal | Display Filter Expression |
|---|---|
| Show only HTTP requests | `http.request` |
| Show all DNS queries | `dns.flags.response == 0` |
| Show all DNS responses | `dns.flags.response == 1` |
| Show TCP retransmissions | `tcp.analysis.retransmission` |
| Show TCP resets | `tcp.flags.reset == 1` |
| Show traffic to/from an IP | `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10` |
| Show traffic on a specific port | `tcp.port == 443` |
| Show follow TCP stream | right-click → *Follow* → *TCP Stream* |
| Exclude ARP noise | `not arp` |
| Show slow HTTP responses (>1s) | `http.time > 1.0` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Packet Sniffing Forensic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Medium - Hub or SPAN Port] --> B[NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
    B --> C[Kernel Packet Capture - libpcap or Npcap]
    C --> D[BPF Capture Filter]
    D --> E[Ring Buffer in RAM]
    E --> F[Dumpcap Privileged Writer]
    F --> G[Pcap or Pcapng File on Disk]
    G --> H[Wireshark Dissector Chain]
    H --> I[Protocol Tree Decoded]
    I --> J[Display Filter Engine]
    J --> K[Forensic Analyst Review]
    K --> L[Statistics and I-O Graphs]
    K --> M[Follow TCP Stream]
    K --> N[Expert Info Anomalies]
    L --> O[Export Evidence - CSV, JSON, TXT]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The flow moves from *physical interception* (A→B) → *kernel-level capture* (C→D) → *persistent storage* (E→G) → *logical dissection* (H→J) → *forensic interpretation* (K→O). Each transition is a potential **chain-of-custody checkpoint** in a court-admissible investigation.

---

## 4.2 Wireshark Three-Pane GUI Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GUI[Main Wireshark Window] --> P1[Pane 1 - Packet List Pane]
    GUI --> P2[Pane 2 - Packet Details Pane]
    GUI --> P3[Pane 3 - Packet Bytes Pane]
    P1 --> F1[Filter Bar - Display Filter]
    P1 --> F2[Capture Filter Bar]
    P1 --> F3[Status Bar - Capture Statistics]
    P1 --> S1[Statistics Menu]
    P1 --> S2[Expert Information]
    S1 --> SG1[I-O Graphs]
    S1 --> SG2[Conversations]
    S1 --> SG3[Endpoints]
    S1 --> SG4[Protocol Hierarchy]
```

| Pane | Purpose | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Packet List Pane** | Shows time, source, destination, protocol, length, info | Quick triage and filtering |
| **Packet Details Pane** | Decoded protocol tree (expandable) | Header-level field analysis |
| **Packet Bytes Pane** | Raw hex + ASCII dump | Payload-level forensic extraction |

---

## 4.3 Attack vs. Defense Sniffing Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Legitimate_Sniffing
        V1[Forensic Investigator] --> SPAN[Switch SPAN Port]
        SPAN --> IDS[IDS or NMS Sensor]
    end
    subgraph Malicious_Sniffing
        V2[Attacker] --> ARP[ARP Spoof to Victim]
        ARP --> MITM[Man in the Middle Position]
        MITM --> CAP[Capture all Victim Traffic]
    end
    subgraph Defensive_Countermeasures
        D1[Static ARP Entries]
        D2[Port Security]
        D3[Dynamic ARP Inspection]
        D4[802.1X Authentication]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define packet sniffing. Differentiate between passive and active sniffing in the context of network forensics.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Packet Sniffing** is the process of intercepting, capturing, and decoding data packets traversing a computer network using a software or hardware tool called a *packet sniffer*, with the NIC placed in **promiscuous mode** (for wired) or **monitor mode** (for wireless).

| Aspect | Passive Sniffing | Active Sniffing |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Silently listens on shared media | Injects packets (ARP / MAC flood) to redirect traffic |
| Visibility | Only works on hubs / Wi-Fi | Works on switched networks |
| Detectability | Very low — no traffic mutation | Detectable via ARP table anomalies or CAM overflow alerts |
| Forensic Use | Legitimate (SPAN port, IDS) | Generally an attack — admissible only with authorization |

*(1 mark — definition, 1.5 marks — table, 0.5 mark — example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any three display filters used in Wireshark for forensic packet analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **`http.request`** — Filters only HTTP request packets (GET, POST). Used to identify web-based exfiltration attempts. *(1 mark)*
2. **`tcp.analysis.retransmission`** — Shows only TCP segments flagged as retransmissions by Wireshark's expert engine. Used to detect network congestion or packet drops. *(1 mark)*
3. **`dns.flags.response == 0`** — Filters only DNS *queries* (not responses). Used to map a victim's web activity timeline. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse **Capture Filters** (BPF syntax, e.g., `tcp port 80`) with **Display Filters** (Wireshark syntax, e.g., `tcp.port == 80`). Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark for this swap. **BPF uses no `==` and no keywords** — it's a reduced instruction set.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Wireshark** and the function of each layer in the packet dissection pipeline. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A network forensics investigator captures traffic on a **500 Mbps** link. The average packet size is **1200 bytes** and the capture window is **1 hour**. Compute the **raw uncompressed pcap file size in GB**, given a **24-byte pcap header per packet**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture of Wireshark (5 layers):**

1. **Capture Layer — `dumpcap`** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Privileged binary that interfaces with `libpcap` (Linux) or `Npcap` (Windows).
   - Pulls raw frames from the kernel and writes them to a `.pcap` / `.pcapng` file.
   - Applies **BPF capture filters** at this stage to drop unwanted traffic at the kernel level.

2. **File Layer** *(1 mark)*
   - Reads/writes capture files in standard formats (pcap, pcapng).
   - Supports merging and splitting of trace files.

3. **Core Engine (Epan)** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Manages the packet lifecycle: tap → dissect → display.
   - Builds the protocol tree, byte view, and time-stamping.

4. **Dissector Layer** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Plugin-based protocol parsers for **3000+ protocols**.
   - Each protocol has its own dissector; lower-layer dissectors hand off payload to upper-layer dissectors (e.g., Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP).

5. **GUI Layer (Qt)** *(1 mark)*
   - The three-pane interface: Packet List, Packet Details, Packet Bytes.
   - Statistics menus: I/O Graphs, Conversations, Endpoints, Protocol Hierarchy, Expert Info.

**Diagram (must be drawn in answer):** Refer to **Section 4.2** for the three-pane architecture.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Parameters.** *(0.5 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 500 \text{ Mbps} = 500 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s} \\
P &= 1200 \text{ bytes} \\
H &= 24 \text{ bytes} \\
T &= 1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Packets per second (PPS).** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPS &= \dfrac{500 \times 10^{6}}{1200 \times 8} \\
&= \dfrac{500{,}000{,}000}{9600} \\
&= 52{,}083.33 \text{ packets/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total packets in 3600 s.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 52{,}083.33 \times 3600 \\
&= 187{,}500{,}000 \text{ packets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total bytes (with pcap header).** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{bytes} &= N \times (P + H) \\
&= 187{,}500{,}000 \times (1200 + 24) \\
&= 187{,}500{,}000 \times 1224 \\
&= 229{,}500{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to GB.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{GB} &= \dfrac{229{,}500{,}000{,}000}{1024^{3}} \\
&= \dfrac{229{,}500{,}000{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \\
&\approx 213.74 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** *(0.5 mark)*

> The raw uncompressed pcap file size is **≈ 213.74 GB**, justifying the forensic need for capture filters, ring buffers, and compression during long-term forensic captures.

**Cross-check with binary vs decimal units:** If the examiner uses decimal (1 GB = $10^9$ bytes), the answer becomes **229.5 GB**. KTU typically uses **binary (1024³)**, but always state the assumption.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **difference between capture filters and display filters** in Wireshark with suitable syntax examples. Why are display filters preferred during post-capture forensic analysis? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a forensic investigation, a Wireshark capture shows **12,500 total TCP segments** and **875 retransmissions**. Compute the **retransmission rate** and classify the link health. Justify your classification with reference to industry-standard thresholds. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | Capture Filter (BPF) | Display Filter (Wireshark) |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Applied *during* capture (kernel) | Applied *after* capture (userspace) |
| Syntax | BPF — keyword-based | Wireshark — field-based |
| Example | `tcp port 80` | `tcp.port == 80` |
| Operators | `and`, `or`, `not` | `&&`, `\|\|`, `!` or `and`, `or`, `not` |
| Resources | Saves CPU, RAM, disk | Wastes disk on unwanted traffic |
| Modifiable later | **No** — must recapture | **Yes** — instant |

**Why display filters are preferred in post-capture forensic analysis:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Non-destructive** — Original pcap is untouched; only the *view* is filtered.
2. **Richer syntax** — Supports protocol-specific field references (e.g., `http.request.uri contains "login"`).
3. **Re-runnable** — Filters can be saved, shared, and applied to multiple captures.
4. **Forensic integrity** — Original evidence is preserved (chain-of-custody safe).

**Examples to include in answer:**
- `http.request.method == POST` — to find form submissions.
- `ip.src == 10.0.0.5 && tcp.dstport == 22` — to find SSH attempts from a specific host.
- `dns.qry.name contains "bank"` — to find potential phishing-related DNS queries.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply the retransmission formula.** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Retr\,\% &= \dfrac{N_{retrans}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{875}{12{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 7.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify using industry-standard thresholds.** *(2.5 marks)*

| Retr % | Health |
|---|---|
| $< 1\%$ | Excellent |
| $1\% \le x < 3\%$ | Acceptable |
| $3\% \le x < 7\%$ | Degraded |
| $\ge 7\%$ | **Critical** |

Since $Retr\,\% = 7.0\%$, the link is classified as **Critical** — link failure is imminent.

**Step 3 — Forensic Justification.** *(1.5 marks)*

> A 7% retransmission rate implies severe packet loss, possibly due to a faulty NIC, duplex mismatch, congested uplink, or active attack (e.g., ARP flooding). The investigator must correlate this with the **I/O Graph** for bursty loss patterns and check the **Expert Info** panel for further classification (e.g., *Out-of-Order*, *Fast Retransmission*, *Spurious Retransmission*).

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**  
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing **bits** and **bytes** in throughput calculations costs **2 marks** minimum. Always explicitly write "× 8" when converting.  
> 2. **Filter Mix-up:** Confusing BPF capture filter syntax with Wireshark display filter syntax — examiners *immediately* deduct **1 mark**.  
> 3. **Missing `× 100`:** Forgetting to convert ratio to percentage in the retransmission formula is a classic **0.5 mark** loss.  
> 4. **Decimal vs Binary GB:** Always state your assumption (1024³ vs 10⁹) when converting bytes to GB.  
> 5. **No Diagram for 7-mark "Explain" Questions:** KTU mandates a *neat labeled diagram* for any "explain" question worth ≥ 7 marks. Skipping the diagram costs **1.5–2 marks**.  
> 6. **Pcap Header Omission:** In file-size derivations, forgetting the 24-byte pcap header per packet is a guaranteed **1 mark** loss.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Packet sniffing = intercepting packets** using a NIC in **promiscuous mode** (wired) or **monitor mode** (wireless).
- ✅ **Wireshark** = the *de facto* open-source packet analyzer; uses **dumpcap + libpcap/Npcap** at the kernel level.
- ✅ **Passive sniffing** = non-intrusive; **Active sniffing** = uses ARP/MAC flooding — admissible only with legal authorization.
- ✅ **Capture filters** (BPF syntax: `tcp port 80`) apply *before* storage → **save resources**.
- ✅ **Display filters** (Wireshark syntax: `tcp.port == 80`) apply *after* storage → **preserve evidence**.
- ✅ **Three-pane GUI** = Packet List, Packet Details, Packet Bytes.
- ✅ **Expert Info** = Wireshark's built-in anomaly detector (retransmissions, resets, malformed packets).
- ✅ **Statistics menus** = I/O Graphs, Conversations, Endpoints, Protocol Hierarchy — all are KTU-favorite topics.
- ✅ **Key file formats** = `.pcap` (legacy) and `.pcapng` (modern, supports metadata + comments).
- ✅ **Pcap header overhead** = **24 bytes/packet** (do not forget in size derivations).
- ✅ **Throughput unit** = **bits per second (bps)**, but capture file size = **bytes (B)**.
- ✅ **Retransmission thresholds** = < 1% Excellent | 1–3% Acceptable | 3–7% Degraded | ≥ 7% Critical.
- ✅ **Forensic admissibility** requires proper **chain of custody**, **hash verification (MD5/SHA-256)** of pcap files, and **authorized access**.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite display filters:** `http.request`, `dns.flags.response == 0`, `tcp.analysis.retransmission`, `ip.addr == x`, `tcp.port == y`.
- ✅ **Defense mechanisms** = Static ARP entries, Port Security, DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection), 802.1X.
- ✅ **Wireshark architecture** = 5 layers → Capture (`dumpcap`) → File → Core Engine (Epan) → Dissector → GUI (Qt).

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw a **neat, labeled block diagram** for any 7-mark "Explain" question. KTU examiners award **1.5–2 marks** purely for a clear architectural diagram, even if textual explanation is partial.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packet Sniffing and Analysis Using NetworkMiner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packet Sniffing and Analysis Using NetworkMiner

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Packet Sniffing** is the process of monitoring, intercepting, and decoding network traffic traversing a digital network by capturing data packets as they flow across a communication channel. In the context of **Network Forensics**, packet sniffing is the foundational acquisition technique used to gather evidence of network-based incidents, intrusions, data exfiltration, or policy violations.

**NetworkMiner** is a premier **Open-Source Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT)** developed originally by Erik Hjelmvik of Netresec AB (Sweden). It is categorized as a *passive network sniffer* and *packet capturing tool* designed specifically for the forensic analysis of live network traffic and offline **PCAP (Packet Capture)** files. NetworkMiner operates predominantly on the **Windows** and **Linux** platforms (with a commercial NetworkMiner Professional edition adding advanced reporting).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, NetworkMiner is positioned as a **Host-Centric Network Forensics Tool** because it reassembles and presents forensic data organized **per host** (IP/MAC based), rather than strictly chronologically as Wireshark does. This makes it exceptionally useful for reconstructing digital events, identifying compromised endpoints, and extracting transmitted artifacts (files, images, credentials) directly from raw packet data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** NetworkMiner falls under the *Live Acquisition & Passive Sniffing* paradigm of network forensics. Unlike active scanners (e.g., Nmap), NetworkMiner does **NOT** inject any traffic into the network. It silently listens, which is legally and operationally critical for forensic admissibility.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large post office sorting facility** (this is your network). Thousands of letters and parcels (network packets) pass through it every second, each addressed to a specific house (destination IP) and sent from a specific person (source IP).

- **Normal Mail Sorting** = Standard packet routing (routers/switches just forward them).
- **A Detective Standing in the Sorting Room, Reading Every Envelope Without Opening the Seals** = This is exactly what **NetworkMiner** does. It reads the **outside label** of every packet (the headers) — the sender, receiver, time, and declared contents — **without actively disturbing the flow of mail**.

Now, imagine a more advanced detective who can also:
- **Photocopy the contents** of every opened letter it sees (file extraction from FTP/SMB/HTTP).
- **Note down names and faces** it spots in the letters (hostname fingerprinting).
- **Write down the secret passwords** people accidentally scribble on postcards (cleartext protocol credential capture).

That advanced detective is your forensic analyst using **NetworkMiner**. Because NetworkMiner is **passive**, the sender and receiver have **no idea** they are being observed, making it perfect for covert forensic investigation.

---

## 1.3 Key Technical Metrics & Standard Constants

The following metrics are standard reference points when evaluating any packet sniffing tool for KTU examinations:

| Metric | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Default Port for Packet Capture** | Promiscuous mode on Network Interface Card (NIC) |
| **Standard Capture File Format** | **PCAP** (`.pcap` / `.pcapng`) and **libpcap** |
| **Default MTU (Ethernet)** | **1500 bytes** |
| **OSI Layer Primarily Examined** | Layers 2–7 (Data Link → Application) |
| **Maximum Reassembly Buffer** | Configurable (default ~1 MB per session) |
| **Standard Cleartext Protocols Parsed** | FTP, HTTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, Telnet, SMB, DNS, TFTP |
| **Encrypted Protocol Visibility** | TLS/SSL — only metadata, no payload decryption without keys |
| **Memory Footprint (NetworkMiner 2.x)** | ~150–300 MB RAM during active sniffing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept to Memorize:** NetworkMiner works in **promiscuous mode** of the NIC. A normal NIC ignores frames not destined to its MAC address. In promiscuous mode, the NIC accepts *every* frame on the wire segment, enabling passive collection. This is governed by the IEEE **802.3** Ethernet standard.

---

## 1.4 Why NetworkMiner Matters in KTU Forensics Curriculum

Network forensics investigations in the KTU 2024 syllabus context follow a defined lifecycle: **Identification → Preservation → Collection → Analysis → Examination → Presentation**. NetworkMiner directly serves the **Collection and Analysis** stages. It allows investigators to:

1. Identify **all hosts** that participated in a network conversation.
2. Extract **transmitted files** (images, documents, executables) directly from the wire.
3. Reconstruct **user sessions** including login credentials for unencrypted protocols.
4. Generate **forensic-grade reports** (in NetworkMiner Professional) for court-admissible evidence.

These align with KTU Course Outcomes **CO3** (Apply forensic tools to analyze network traffic) and **CO4** (Interpret forensic findings for incident reconstruction).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of NetworkMiner

NetworkMiner is engineered using a modular architecture. Understanding this architecture is critical for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

### 2.1.1 Core Engine Modules

1. **Packet Parser Engine (libpcap/Npcap based)**
   - Receives raw bytes from the NIC or from a pre-recorded PCAP file.
   - Decodes frames according to the protocol stack (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application).
   - Implemented in C# .NET with native bindings to **WinPcap/Npcap** drivers on Windows and **libpcap** on Linux.

2. **Session Reassembler (TCP Stream Reconstruction)**
   - Reconstructs bidirectional TCP streams from individual packets.
   - Groups related packets into **sessions** identified by the 4-tuple: (Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port).
   - This is essential because a single file transfer (e.g., an image over HTTP) may be split across hundreds of packets.

3. **Protocol Decoders (Application-Layer Parsers)**
   - Decodes the payload of each application-layer protocol.
   - For **HTTP**: extracts URLs, User-Agent, referrer, cookies, requested files.
   - For **FTP**: extracts usernames, passwords, transferred files.
   - For **SMB/CIFS**: extracts file names and contents.
   - For **DNS**: extracts queried hostnames and responses (A, AAAA, MX records).
   - For **SMTP/POP/IMAP**: extracts email metadata, attachments, and sometimes cleartext credentials.

4. **Host Fingerprinting Engine (OS Detection)**
   - Uses the **TCP/IP stack fingerprinting** technique (similar to p0f).
   - Examines fields like **TTL (Time To Live)**, **Window Size**, **Don't Fragment (DF) flag**, and **TCP Options** to identify the operating system of the remote host.
   - Maintains a fingerprint database mapping these signatures to known OS families (Windows, Linux, iOS, etc.).

5. **File Carving & Extraction Module**
   - Reassembles files transferred over the network and saves them to disk.
   - Operates on multiple parallel streams simultaneously.
   - Detects file types by **magic bytes** (file signatures) rather than just MIME type.

6. **GUI / CLI Front-End**
   - Tab-based interface organizing forensic data by: **Hosts, Frames, Files, Images, Messages, Credentials, Sessions, DNS, Parameters, Keywords, Anomaly**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps (How NetworkMiner Actually Works)

The packet sniffing and analysis workflow can be broken down into seven structured steps. Each step is examinable in KTU theory questions.

**Step 1 — Interface Selection and Promiscuous Mode Activation**
- NetworkMiner selects a NIC and instructs the OS/driver to place it into **promiscuous mode**.
- This requires administrative/root privileges.

**Step 2 — Raw Frame Capture**
- The libpcap/Npcap library buffers incoming frames from the wire (or from a PCAP file).
- Each frame is timestamped with microsecond precision.

**Step 3 — Protocol Decoding**
- The parser walks through the frame structure, decoding Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application-Layer protocol.

**Step 4 — Host Database Population**
- Every unique IP-MAC pair encountered is registered as a **Host** in the GUI.
- Hostnames (from DHCP, NetBIOS, HTTP) are correlated with the IP.

**Step 5 — Session & Stream Reassembly**
- Related packets are grouped into sessions and reassembled into byte-streams.

**Step 6 — Artifact Extraction**
- The reassembled streams are searched for files, credentials, messages, and keywords.

**Step 7 — Forensic Presentation**
- The investigator reviews the categorized data via tabs and exports reports.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** When asked "Explain the working of NetworkMiner," students must mention **promiscuous mode**, **passive sniffing**, **session reassembly**, and **host-centric organization** to score full marks.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

While NetworkMiner is a tool rather than a formula-heavy topic, the following structured table consolidates the technical terms, parameters, and engineering relationships you must memorize for the KTU examination.

| Concept | Symbol / Term | Definition / Engineering Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| Capture File Format | `.pcap` / `.pcapng` | Packet Capture Next Generation; binary format storing raw frames |
| Promiscuous Mode | $P_{mode}$ | NIC state where all frames are accepted regardless of destination MAC |
| Time To Live | $TTL$ | Decremented per hop; initial value reveals OS (e.g., **64** = Linux/macOS, **128** = Windows, **255** = Network Devices) |
| TCP Window Size | $W_{TCP}$ | Bytes receiver can buffer; fingerprinting parameter for OS detection |
| Session Tuple | $(IP_s, P_s, IP_d, P_d)$ | Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port uniquely defines a session |
| MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) | $MTU = 1500$ bytes | Standard Ethernet payload size |
| Frame Size | $F_{size}$ | Total bytes on wire = $Header_{eth}(14) + Header_{IP}(20) + Header_{TCP}(20) + Payload + Trailer$ |
| Capture Rate | $R_{cap}$ | Packets per second the tool can sustain; depends on CPU and disk I/O |
| File Extraction Confidence | $C_{file}$ | Likelihood that a reassembled stream is a complete file (0 to 1, output of magic byte detection) |
| DNS Query Type | $Q_{type}$ | A, AAAA, MX, NS, TXT, CNAME, SOA, PTR |
| HTTP Status Codes | $H_{code}$ | 200 (OK), 301/302 (Redirect), 401 (Unauthorized), 403 (Forbidden), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Server Error) |
| Cleartext Protocols | $\mathcal{P}_{clear}$ | Set of protocols whose payload is unencrypted: $\{HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, SNMPv1, TFTP\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markdown Table Safeguard:** Notice the use of `\vert` / explicit symbol expressions (e.g., $(IP_s, P_s, IP_d, P_d)$) instead of vertical pipe characters to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. This is a strict KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering requirement.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

NetworkMiner and similar NFATs are deployed in:

- **Incident Response (IR) Teams** — reconstructing the timeline of a data breach.
- **SOC (Security Operations Centers)** — analyzing anomalous traffic captures from sensors.
- **Law Enforcement** — extracting evidence of criminal communications from network logs.
- **Enterprise Forensics** — investigating insider threats, data leakage, and policy violations.
- **Academic / Training** — teaching network protocol behavior using real captures.
- **Threat Hunting** — searching PCAPs for Indicators of Compromise (IoCs).

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic maps directly to **CO3 (Apply forensic tools and techniques for network traffic analysis)** and is typically assessed via a 14-mark analytical question in **Module 3**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The 11 Tabs of NetworkMiner

NetworkMiner's forensic power lies in its tabbed analytical interface. Each tab is a dedicated analysis view. The table below provides a **deep analytical breakdown** of every tab — this is highly examinable in KTU 14-mark questions.

| Tab | Function | Forensic Insight | Example Finding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hosts** | Lists all observed endpoints (IP, MAC, hostname, OS) | Identifies rogue devices, unauthorized hosts | An unknown host with hostname "attacker-pc" joined the network |
| **Frames** | Chronological list of every captured packet | Reconstructs timeline of events | First packet from attacker IP at 02:14 AM |
| **Files** | Reassembled files extracted from protocols | Locates data exfiltration or malware transfer | `malware.exe` extracted from SMB session |
| **Images** | Auto-extracted image files (JPG, PNG, GIF) | Detects inappropriate content, leaked screenshots | Screenshots of confidential dashboards found in HTTP traffic |
| **Messages** | Email messages (SMTP/POP/IMAP) | Reviews email-based data leakage | Confidential invoice forwarded to external email |
| **Credentials** | Cleartext usernames and passwords | Identifies credential exposure over plaintext | FTP login `admin:password123` captured |
| **Sessions** | Lists all TCP/UDP sessions with statistics | Volume analysis, long-lived sessions | 2-hour SMB session transferring 1.2 GB |
| **DNS** | Captures DNS queries and responses | Detects DNS tunneling, beaconing, malicious domains | Repeated queries to `malicious-c2.xyz` every 30 seconds |
| **Parameters** | HTTP GET/POST parameters and cookies | Detects SQL injection, XSS payloads, session hijacking | `id=1' OR '1'='1` in URL parameters |
| **Keywords** | User-defined keyword search across traffic | Targeted forensic hunting | Searching for "confidential" reveals 14 matching packets |
| **Anomalies** | Detected protocol violations and unusual events | Highlights suspicious behavior | TCP connection without proper handshake |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Forensic Analysis Workflow Using NetworkMiner

The following exhaustive procedure is what a KTU examiner expects when a 14-mark question asks: *"Explain with a step-by-step procedure how NetworkMiner is used for packet sniffing and analysis."*

### **Step 1: Tool Acquisition and Installation**

The investigator downloads NetworkMiner from the official Netresec website. Two editions exist:

- **NetworkMiner Free / Open Source** — for individuals and basic use.
- **NetworkMiner Professional** — commercial edition with PCAP-over-IP, reporting, and case management.

On **Windows**, the prerequisite is the **Npcap** driver (the modern successor to WinPcap). On **Linux**, the equivalent is **libpcap**. Installation is straightforward; the tool runs as a managed .NET application.

### **Step 2: Selection of Capture Source**

NetworkMiner offers two capture modes:

$$
C_{source} = \begin{cases} \text{Live Network Interface} & \text{(Real-time passive sniffing)} \\ \text{PCAP File Input} & \text{(Offline forensic analysis)} \end{cases}
$$

For live sniffing, the analyst selects the NIC. For offline analysis, the analyst loads a pre-recorded `.pcap` file from a TAP (Test Access Point) or SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port.

### **Step 3: Initiation of Capture and Promiscuous Mode**

The analyst clicks **Start** to begin sniffing. The NIC is placed in promiscuous mode, and the libpcap/Npcap library begins buffering incoming frames. The number of captured frames and the unique host count update in real-time.

### **Step 4: Host Discovery and OS Fingerprinting**

As frames arrive, NetworkMiner populates the **Hosts** tab. For each host:

- **MAC Address** is extracted from the Ethernet header.
- **IP Address** is extracted from the IP header.
- **Hostname** is resolved from DHCP Option 12, NetBIOS name, or HTTP `Host` header.
- **Operating System** is fingerprinted using TCP/IP stack parameters.

The TTL-based heuristic is given by:

$$
OS_{guess} = \mathcal{F}(TTL_{initial}, W_{TCP}, DF, TOS)
$$

Where $\mathcal{F}$ is the fingerprinting function comparing the observed values to a signature database.

> **Engineering Insight:** A host returning TTL = **64** with Window Size = **65535** and DF = **1** is almost certainly a Linux/macOS system. A TTL = **128** with Window Size = **65535** and DF = **1** indicates Microsoft Windows.

### **Step 5: Application-Layer Protocol Decoding**

Each frame's payload is passed to the appropriate protocol parser. For example, if port 80 is detected, the HTTP parser is invoked. The HTTP parser extracts:

- Request Method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- Requested URI.
- User-Agent string.
- Referer header.
- Cookies.
- Server response code.

### **Step 6: File Carving and Extraction**

When a complete file transfer is detected (e.g., an HTTP `200 OK` response containing an image), NetworkMiner reassembles the TCP stream and saves the file to the case folder. File type detection uses the magic-byte technique:

$$
\text{FileType} = \text{match}(\text{FirstNBytes}, \mathcal{M}_{signatures})
$$

Where $\mathcal{M}_{signatures}$ is the set of known file signatures (e.g., `\xFF\xD8\xFF` for JPEG, `%PDF` for PDF, `PK\x03\x04` for ZIP).

### **Step 7: Credential Harvesting from Cleartext Protocols**

For protocols like FTP, Telnet, SMTP AUTH, HTTP Basic Auth, and POP3, NetworkMiner inspects the stream for credentials sent in plain text. The detected credentials appear in the **Credentials** tab with the source/target host and the protocol used.

### **Step 8: Session and Volume Analysis**

The **Sessions** tab displays bidirectional communication channels sorted by data volume. This helps identify:

- Large data transfers (potential exfiltration).
- Long-lived sessions (potential C2 beaconing).
- Unusual port usage (potential covert channels).

### **Step 9: DNS Forensics**

The **DNS** tab reveals every hostname queried and resolved. Investigators search for:

- Suspicious domains (e.g., DGA-generated names).
- High entropy subdomains.
- DNS tunneling patterns (long TXT queries, high query rate).

### **Step 10: Keyword Search and Anomaly Detection**

Investigators define custom keywords (employee names, project codenames, "confidential", "password", etc.) and search the entire capture. The **Anomalies** tab highlights protocol violations such as:

- TCP connections with no handshake.
- Malformed DNS packets.
- Unusual HTTP methods.

### **Step 11: Export and Reporting**

The investigator exports extracted files, hosts lists, and session data. In NetworkMiner Professional, a **case report** can be generated in HTML or CSV format suitable for legal proceedings.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Foundations: Capture Rate & Storage Estimation

Although NetworkMiner is a tool, KTU examiners may ask students to estimate capture infrastructure requirements. The relevant relationships are:

### **3.3.1 Raw Capture Bit Rate**

For a fully saturated 1 Gbps link:

$$
R_{capture} = \frac{L_{packet} \times 8}{T_{packet}}
$$

Where $L_{packet}$ is the average packet length in bytes and $T_{packet}$ is the inter-packet time.

### **3.3.2 Storage Requirement**

$$
S_{storage} = R_{capture} \times T_{duration} \times \text{Overhead}_{pcap}
$$

**Worked Example:** A 1 Gbps link with 50% utilization captured for 1 hour.

$$
R_{capture} = 0.5 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/sec} = 500 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
S_{storage} = 500 \times 10^6 \times 3600 = 1.8 \times 10^{12} \text{ bits} = 225 \text{ GB}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Dividing by 8 to convert bits to bytes:

$$
S_{bytes} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{12}}{8} = 2.25 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} = 225 \text{ GB}
$$

This illustrates why forensic capture in real environments demands substantial disk provisioning.

### **3.3.3 Loss Probability in High-Speed Capture**

If the disk write speed $W_{disk}$ is less than the incoming rate $R_{capture}$, packets are dropped:

$$
P_{loss} = \max\left(0, 1 - \frac{W_{disk}}{R_{capture}}\right)
$$

Forensic admissibility requires $P_{loss} \approx 0$.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: NetworkMiner vs. Wireshark vs. Tcpdump

This is a frequent KTU question. The structured comparison is:

| Feature | NetworkMiner | Wireshark | Tcpdump |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Paradigm** | Host-centric forensic | Packet-centric analysis | CLI packet capture |
| **GUI** | Yes (rich, tab-based) | Yes (rich, packet-list) | No (command-line) |
| **File Extraction** | Automatic from network traffic | Manual (Export Objects) | Manual (no GUI) |
| **OS Fingerprinting** | Built-in (p0f-style) | Limited | No |
| **Credential Capture** | Automatic for cleartext | Manual inspection | Manual inspection |
| **Live Capture** | Yes (passive) | Yes | Yes |
| **PCAP Analysis** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Best Use Case** | Incident response, forensic | Protocol analysis, debugging | Server-side headless capture |
| **Cost** | Free / Professional (paid) | Free | Free |

> **Key Distinction for KTU:** **Wireshark is packet-centric** (you browse individual packets). **NetworkMiner is host-centric** (you browse by host, then drill into sessions). This paradigm shift is what makes NetworkMiner uniquely suited for forensic reconstruction.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level NetworkMiner Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the internal functional architecture of NetworkMiner, illustrating how data flows from raw frame acquisition to forensic output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Network Wire / PCAP File] --> B[Npcap or libpcap Driver Layer]
    B --> C[Raw Frame Buffer]
    C --> D[Frame Parser Engine]
    D --> E[Protocol Stack Decoder]
    E --> F1[TCP UDP Stream Reassembler]
    E --> F2[Application Layer Parser Module]
    F1 --> G[Session Database]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H1[Host Fingerprint Engine]
    G --> H2[File Carving Module]
    G --> H3[Credential Extractor]
    G --> H4[DNS Analyzer]
    H1 --> I[Forensic Analysis Tabs GUI]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Forensic Report Export]
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- **A → B:** Raw bytes are acquired either from the live wire (via Npcap on Windows or libpcap on Linux) or from a pre-recorded PCAP file.
- **B → C → D:** Frames are buffered and then handed to the parser engine, which walks the protocol stack.
- **D → E → F1/F2:** The application layer is separated into stream-level reassembly and protocol-specific parsing.
- **F1/F2 → G:** Both branches feed a unified **Session Database** keyed by the 4-tuple $(IP_s, P_s, IP_d, P_d)$.
- **G → H1–H4:** Specialized analyzers operate on the session data in parallel.
- **H1–H4 → I → J:** Results are presented in the analyst's GUI and can be exported as forensic reports.

---

## 4.2 Forensic Investigation Workflow Diagram

The following diagram shows the end-to-end workflow a forensic investigator follows when using NetworkMiner during an incident response.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Network Scope] --> S2[Step 2: Deploy TAP or SPAN Port]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Capture Traffic to PCAP]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Load PCAP into NetworkMiner]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Identify Hosts and OS]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Review Sessions]
    S6 --> S7{Anomalies Detected?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 7: Extract Files and Credentials]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Step 8: Generate Baseline Report]
    S8 --> S10[Step 9: Correlate with Threat Intel]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10: Export Court Admissible Report]
    S9 --> S11
```

**Decision Logic:** The diamond `S7` represents a critical forensic decision point. If no anomalies are observed, the investigator produces a baseline characterization of the network. If anomalies are present, the workflow branches into artifact extraction, threat intelligence correlation, and finally the export of an evidentiary report.

---

## 4.3 Host-Centric Data Organization Concept

The following diagram illustrates how NetworkMiner organizes forensic data **by host**, as opposed to Wireshark's packet-centric view.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NetworkMiner_HostView["NETWORKMINER - HOST CENTRIC VIEW"]
        H1[Host 192.168.1.10] --> HS1[Sessions: 14]
        H1 --> HF1[Files Sent: 23]
        H1 --> HC1[Credentials: 0]
        H1 --> HD1[DNS Queries: 87]
    end
    subgraph Wireshark_PacketView["WIRESHARK - PACKET CENTRIC VIEW"]
        P1[Packet 1 at 10:00:01] --> P2[Packet 2 at 10:00:01]
        P2 --> P3[Packet 3 at 10:00:02]
        P3 --> P4[Packet N ...]
    end
    NetworkMiner_HostView --- Wireshark_PacketView
```

**Pedagogical Insight:** The contrast between the two views highlights the fundamental difference in analytical philosophy. NetworkMiner answers the question: *"What did this host do?"* — a typical forensic question. Wireshark answers: *"What did this packet contain?"* — a typical troubleshooting question. For KTU examinations, this distinction is a guaranteed 3–4 mark question.

---

## 4.4 Data Flow: From Frame to Extracted File

This diagram traces how a single transferred file (e.g., a PDF exfiltrated over HTTP) is reconstructed by NetworkMiner from raw frames.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender Host
    participant Wire as Network Wire
    participant NM as NetworkMiner
    participant FS as File System

    Sender->>Wire: Packet 1 (TCP SYN) - Frame 1
    Sender->>Wire: Packet 2 (TCP SYN-ACK) - Frame 2
    Sender->>Wire: Packet 3 (TCP ACK) - Frame 3
    Sender->>Wire: Packet 4 (HTTP GET /report.pdf) - Frame 4
    Sender->>Wire: Packet 5-N (PDF byte stream split across frames) - Frames 5 to N
    Note over Wire,NM: NetworkMiner captures ALL frames in promiscuous mode
    Wire->>NM: Stream of Frames 1 to N
    NM->>NM: Reassemble TCP Stream
    NM->>NM: Parse HTTP Response Headers
    NM->>NM: Detect Magic Bytes (25 50 44 46 for PDF)
    NM->>FS: Save report.pdf to case folder
    FS-->>NM: File saved with SHA256 hash
```

**Analytical Value:** This sequence diagram demonstrates that file carving is a multi-step process involving: (1) connection observation, (2) request identification, (3) stream reassembly, (4) application-layer parsing, (5) magic-byte verification, and (6) integrity hashing. Each step is potentially examined in KTU assessments.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Define packet sniffing. Explain the role of promiscuous mode in packet sniffing tools.**

**Model Answer:**

Packet sniffing is the process of capturing and analyzing network traffic as it traverses a communication medium. It is the foundational technique in network forensics for acquiring evidence of network-based events.

**Promiscuous Mode** is a special operational mode of a Network Interface Card (NIC) in which the NIC accepts **all** frames passing through the wire, regardless of whether the destination MAC address matches its own. Under normal operation, a NIC discards frames not addressed to it. In promiscuous mode, every frame on the segment becomes visible to the sniffing software, enabling passive full-segment visibility. This mode is essential for forensic tools like NetworkMiner and Wireshark, and requires administrative/root privileges to activate, as defined by the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet standard.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**List any six tabs available in the NetworkMiner GUI and state the forensic purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Tab Name | Forensic Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hosts** | Lists all observed IP-MAC endpoints with hostname and OS fingerprint; identifies rogue devices |
| 2 | **Files** | Reassembled files extracted from network traffic; detects data exfiltration or malware transfer |
| 3 | **Credentials** | Cleartext usernames and passwords captured from unencrypted protocols; identifies credential leaks |
| 4 | **DNS** | Captures DNS queries and responses; detects DNS tunneling, beaconing, and malicious domains |
| 5 | **Sessions** | Lists all TCP/UDP sessions with data volume; identifies large transfers and covert channels |
| 6 | **Anomalies** | Detected protocol violations and unusual events; flags suspicious traffic behavior |
