## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** describe NetworkMiner as an "active scanner." It is **passive**. Saying otherwise will cost 2 marks.
> - **Always** mention *promiscuous mode*, *host-centric organization*, and *file carving* when describing NetworkMiner. Omission of these keywords costs 3–4 marks.
> - In comparison questions with Wireshark, students often incorrectly state Wireshark does file extraction automatically — it does NOT. NetworkMiner does it automatically; Wireshark requires manual "Export Objects."
> - Students frequently confuse "credential capture" with "decryption." NetworkMiner **does NOT decrypt** TLS/SSL. It only captures credentials sent in **cleartext protocols** (FTP, HTTP Basic, Telnet, etc.). This distinction is critical.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Module 3 ESE Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the architecture and working of **NetworkMiner** as a Network Forensic Analysis Tool. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the procedure for **extracting files and credentials** from a PCAP capture using NetworkMiner. What forensic insights can be drawn from such artifacts? (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

NetworkMiner is an open-source Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT) developed by Netresec AB. Its architecture is modular, comprising six major engines:

1. **Packet Parser Engine** — Built on Npcap (Windows) or libpcap (Linux), it captures raw frames either from a live NIC in promiscuous mode or from a pre-recorded PCAP file. Each frame is timestamped with microsecond precision.

2. **Protocol Stack Decoder** — Walks through the frame structure from Layer 2 (Ethernet) to Layer 7 (Application), identifying each protocol header.

3. **Session Reassembler** — Groups related packets into bidirectional sessions using the 4-tuple $(IP_s, P_s, IP_d, P_d)$ and reconstructs continuous byte streams from fragmented TCP payloads.

4. **Application-Layer Parsers** — Decodes the content of specific protocols. For HTTP, it extracts URLs, headers, cookies, and User-Agents. For FTP, it extracts credentials and files. For DNS, it records queries and responses.

5. **Host Fingerprinting Engine** — Examines TCP/IP parameters such as TTL, Window Size, DF flag, and TCP options to identify the operating system using a signature database (p0f-style fingerprints).

6. **GUI Front-End** — Presents the forensic data in 11 tabs: Hosts, Frames, Files, Images, Messages, Credentials, Sessions, DNS, Parameters, Keywords, and Anomalies.

**Working Principle:** NetworkMiner operates in **passive** mode. The investigator loads a PCAP or selects a live NIC. Frames are buffered, parsed, and categorized by host. The reconstructed sessions are scanned for files (using magic-byte detection), credentials (from cleartext protocols), and metadata. The investigator reviews the tabbed output to reconstruct the timeline of events.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The procedure for file and credential extraction is as follows:

**Step 1:** Open NetworkMiner and select the input PCAP file via *File → Open* or by dragging the file into the GUI.

**Step 2:** NetworkMiner automatically processes the capture and populates all 11 tabs. No manual configuration is required for basic extraction.

**Step 3:** Navigate to the **Files** tab. This tab lists every file NetworkMiner has reassembled from observed network streams. Each entry shows: filename (if derivable), source host, destination host, protocol used, file size, and a SHA256 hash.

**Step 4:** Right-click any file entry and select **"Open Folder"** to view the extracted file in the case directory, or **"Copy file"** to export it elsewhere for further analysis (e.g., sandboxing, antivirus scanning).

**Step 5:** Navigate to the **Credentials** tab. This tab lists every cleartext username-password pair observed in the capture, along with the source IP, destination IP, and protocol. Common examples include:
   - HTTP Basic Authentication headers: `Authorization: Basic base64(user:pass)`
   - FTP USER and PASS commands
   - SMTP AUTH PLAIN/LOGIN
   - Telnet session streams
   - HTTP POST form submissions

**Step 6:** Cross-reference extracted credentials with the **Sessions** tab to identify which hosts exchanged them and the volume of data transferred in those sessions.

**Forensic Insights Drawn:**

- **Data Exfiltration Evidence:** Files transmitted from corporate hosts to external IPs may indicate unauthorized data leakage.
- **Malware Distribution:** Executables (`.exe`, `.dll`, `.js`) transferred over HTTP/SMB can be flagged for malware analysis.
- **Credential Compromise:** Cleartext credentials indicate a serious security policy violation; the affected accounts must be immediately rotated.
- **Insider Threat Identification:** Files containing confidential documents (PDFs, spreadsheets) sent to personal email accounts reveal insider activity.
- **Lateral Movement:** SMB file transfers between internal hosts may indicate an attacker pivoting through the network.

The extracted artifacts, combined with their SHA256 hashes, form **forensically sound evidence** that can be admitted in legal proceedings provided the chain of custody on the original PCAP is maintained.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Module 3 ESE Alternative]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **NetworkMiner** with **Wireshark** as network forensics tools. Highlight at least six points of difference. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain how NetworkMiner performs **OS fingerprinting** and **DNS analysis**. What forensic conclusions can an investigator draw from these analyses? (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

| # | Aspect | NetworkMiner | Wireshark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Analytical Paradigm** | Host-centric — organizes data by endpoint | Packet-centric — organizes data chronologically by packet |
| 2 | **File Extraction** | Automatic for HTTP, FTP, SMB, TFTP, SMTP | Manual (File → Export Objects → HTTP, etc.) |
| 3 | **OS Fingerprinting** | Built-in p0f-style engine | Not built-in (limited via TCP options inspection) |
| 4 | **Credential Capture** | Automatic for cleartext protocols (FTP, HTTP Basic, Telnet) | Manual via stream-following and string search |
| 5 | **Primary Use Case** | Incident response, forensic reconstruction | Protocol debugging, deep packet inspection |
| 6 | **Keyword/Anomaly Detection** | Built-in keyword search and anomaly tab | Not native (relies on display filters) |
| 7 | **Live Capture** | Yes (passive) | Yes (passive and active) |
| 8 | **Reporting** | NetworkMiner Professional has built-in HTML reporting | No native reporting; manual export required |
| 9 | **Learning Curve** | Moderate (forensic-oriented) | Steep (protocol-detail-oriented) |
| 10 | **Filter Capability** | Limited filtering (by host) | Powerful display filters (BPF syntax) |

**Synthesis:** NetworkMiner is the **forensic investigator's tool** — designed for "what happened and who did it." Wireshark is the **network engineer's tool** — designed for "why is this connection failing." In a mature SOC, both are used complementarily.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**OS Fingerprinting in NetworkMiner:**

NetworkMiner uses **passive OS fingerprinting** based on the TCP/IP stack characteristics of observed traffic. The key parameters inspected are:

- **Initial TTL:** Set by the sender's OS. Common values: **64** (Linux, macOS, BSD), **128** (Microsoft Windows), **255** (Network devices like routers, Cisco IOS).
- **TCP Window Size:** Reflects the receive buffer size of the sender.
- **Don't Fragment (DF) Flag:** Indicates the OS's path-MTU behavior.
- **TCP Options Order:** The sequence of TCP options (MSS, Window Scale, Timestamp, SACK) is highly OS-specific.

The fingerprinting function is given by:

$$
OS_{guess} = \mathcal{F}(TTL_{initial}, W_{TCP}, DF, \text{OptionsOrder}, \text{OptionsValues})
$$

NetworkMiner maintains a signature database mapping these parameters to known OS families, similar to the p0f tool by Michal Zalewski.

**Forensic Insights from OS Fingerprinting:**
- Identification of unauthorized OS types (e.g., Linux host on a Windows-only network).
- Detection of virtual machines or sandboxed environments.
- Attribution of attack traffic to specific OS-based malware families.

**DNS Analysis in NetworkMiner:**

The DNS tab captures every DNS query and its corresponding response, including:

- **Queried Domain Name** (QNAME).
- **Query Type** (A, AAAA, MX, NS, TXT, CNAME).
- **Response IP Addresses** (A/AAAA records).
- **Authoritative Name Server** (NS records).

**Forensic Insights from DNS Analysis:**
- **C2 Beaconing Detection:** Regular queries to a single suspicious domain at fixed intervals indicate command-and-control communication.
- **DNS Tunneling Identification:** Abnormally long TXT queries with high entropy subdomains suggest data exfiltration via DNS.
- **DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) Detection:** High volume of NXDOMAIN responses to seemingly random names indicates malware using DGAs.
- **Phishing Infrastructure Mapping:** DNS lookups to newly registered or low-reputation domains may indicate user engagement with phishing sites.
- **Lateral Movement Tracking:** Internal DNS queries for administrative shares or sensitive hostnames may reveal reconnaissance activity.

Together, OS fingerprinting and DNS analysis provide a high-level behavioral profile of every host on the network, enabling the investigator to focus on **anomalous hosts** for deeper packet-level inspection using Wireshark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — NetworkMiner**

### **Core Concepts**
- ☐ **NetworkMiner** is a *passive*, *host-centric* Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT).
- ☐ It is developed by **Netresec AB (Erik Hjelmvik)** and is available in Free and Professional editions.
- ☐ It supports both **live sniffing** (via Npcap/libpcap) and **offline PCAP analysis**.
- ☐ The NIC must operate in **promiscuous mode** to capture all frames on the wire.
- ☐ NetworkMiner does **NOT** inject any traffic into the network (passive paradigm).
- ☐ It does **NOT** decrypt TLS/SSL traffic; it only captures cleartext protocol data.

### **Architecture**
- ☐ Six engines: Parser, Decoder, Reassembler, Fingerprinter, Carving, GUI.
- ☐ Sessions are identified by the 4-tuple $(IP_s, P_s, IP_d, P_d)$.
- ☐ File carving uses **magic-byte** detection to identify file types.

### **GUI Tabs (11 Total)**
- ☐ Hosts | Frames | Files | Images | Messages | Credentials | Sessions | DNS | Parameters | Keywords | Anomalies.

### **OS Fingerprinting Heuristics (Must Memorize)**
- ☐ TTL = **64** → Linux / macOS / BSD
- ☐ TTL = **128** → Microsoft Windows
- ☐ TTL = **255** → Network devices (routers, switches)
- ☐ Window Size = **65535** is common for both Linux and Windows but is a fingerprinting parameter.

### **Cleartext Protocols Whose Credentials NetworkMiner Can Capture**
- ☐ HTTP (Basic Auth, POST form data)
- ☐ FTP (USER and PASS commands)
- ☐ Telnet (entire session stream)
- ☐ SMTP (AUTH PLAIN/LOGIN)
- ☐ POP3 (USER/PASS)
- ☐ IMAP (LOGIN)
- ☐ SNMPv1/v2 (community strings)

### **Forensic Use Cases**
- ☐ Data exfiltration detection via file carving.
- ☐ Insider threat identification via message/image extraction.
- ☐ Malware C2 detection via DNS analysis and session analysis.
- ☐ Credential leak identification via cleartext protocol parsing.
- ☐ Rogue host/OS detection via fingerprinting.

### **Key Comparisons to Remember**
- ☐ **NetworkMiner vs. Wireshark:** Host-centric vs. Packet-centric. Auto-extraction vs. Manual extraction. Built-in fingerprinting vs. Limited.
- ☐ **NetworkMiner vs. Tcpdump:** GUI vs. CLI. Forensic reporting vs. Raw capture only.
- ☐ **NetworkMiner vs. Nmap:** Passive vs. Active. No traffic injection vs. Active probing.

### **Common Examination Traps**
- ☐ Do NOT call NetworkMiner an "active scanner."
- ☐ Do NOT claim it decrypts TLS/SSL.
- ☐ Do NOT confuse "session reassembly" with "file carving" — reassembly is the prerequisite step; carving is the artifact extraction.
- ☐ Do NOT forget to mention **promiscuous mode** — it is the operational foundation of all packet sniffers.

### **Quick Formula Recap**
- ☐ Session Tuple: $(IP_s, P_s, IP_d, P_d)$
- ☐ Storage Estimate: $S_{storage} = R_{capture} \times T_{duration} \times \text{Overhead}_{pcap}$
- ☐ Loss Probability: $P_{loss} = \max(0, 1 - W_{disk}/R_{capture})$
- ☐ OS Guess: $OS = \mathcal{F}(TTL, W_{TCP}, DF, \text{Options})$
- ☐ File Type: $\text{Type} = \text{match}(\text{FirstNBytes}, \mathcal{M}_{signatures})$

> **Final KTU Preparation Tip:** For Module 3, focus on the **practical workflow** of NetworkMiner (how to load a PCAP, navigate tabs, extract artifacts) and the **comparative analysis** with Wireshark. These two areas account for approximately 70% of past KTU questions on this topic. Always anchor your answers with the four pillars: **passive**, **promiscuous**, **host-centric**, and **cleartext-only**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Wireless (IEEE 802.11) Communication and Security Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Wireless (IEEE 802.11) Communication and Security Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — IEEE 802.11 Wireless Communication

**IEEE 802.11** is a family of wireless local area network (WLAN) standards, commercially branded as **Wi-Fi**, that defines the **Physical (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sub-layers of the OSI Data Link Layer for communicating over unlicensed radio frequency (RF) spectrum bands — primarily the **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and **6 GHz** Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) bands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Network Forensics*, IEEE 802.11 is examined not as a mere communication protocol, but as a **forensically significant attack surface** where RF signals, MAC frames, authentication handshakes, and management frames become primary sources of *evidentiary artefacts* during an investigation.

The most operationally relevant amendments within the 802.11 family are:

| Amendment | Operational Band | Max Link Rate | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **802.11b** | 2.4 GHz | 11 Mbps | Legacy sniffing and WEP cracking |
| **802.11a** | 5 GHz | 54 Mbps | Less congested band analysis |
| **802.11g** | 2.4 GHz | 54 Mbps | Backward-compatible attack surface |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 600 Mbps | MIMO-based signal forensics |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 5 GHz | 6.93 Gbps | Wide-channel monitoring (80/160 MHz) |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 9.6 Gbps | OFDMA-driven traffic analysis |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Invisible Postal System

Imagine a crowded **town square (the air medium)** where dozens of people want to talk to specific others without shouting over each other. The **IEEE 802.11 standard** is essentially a strict *etiquette rulebook* the townspeople agreed upon:
- Each speaker (**station**) waits for silence (**Carrier Sense**), then politely announces a random waiting period (**DCF Backoff**) before speaking, to avoid collisions.
- A **central town crier (Access Point)** regulates the most formal conversations.
- For privacy, every message can be sealed in an envelope (encryption keys: WEP/WPA/WPA2/WPA3).

In *Network Forensics*, the investigator is the **detective standing in the square with a parabolic microphone** (e.g., a packet capture tool like Wireshark + monitor-mode adapter), *recording* every whisper, sealed envelope, and handshake. Security challenges arise because:
1. The "square" has **no physical walls** — signals leak far beyond the building.
2. The "envelopes" (encryption) can be *forged* or *weakened* (WEP, KRACK).
3. The "speakers" can be *impersonated* (Evil Twin, MAC Spoofing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Wireless networks trade *physical security* (cables behind locked doors) for *mobility* — and that trade-off is the foundational reason wireless security challenges exist.

### 1.3 Core Operational Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Stations (STAs):** Any device equipped with an 802.11 wireless network interface (laptop, smartphone, IoT sensor).
>
> **Access Point (AP):** A special STA that acts as a bridge between the wireless medium and a Distribution System (DS), typically Ethernet.
>
> **Basic Service Set (BSS):** The fundamental building block — a set of STAs that communicate with each other. Identified by a **BSSID** (the AP's MAC address).
>
> **Extended Service Set (ESS):** A collection of one or more BSSs interconnected via a Distribution System, presented to upper layers as a single LAN, identified by an **ESSID (network name)**.
>
> **Independent BSS (IBSS / Ad-Hoc):** A peer-to-peer network with no AP — directly relevant to *forensic analysis of covert or post-disaster networks*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 802.11 Architecture — Logical Building Blocks

The 802.11 architecture is structured as a hierarchy of services layered upon a shared radio medium:

1. **Station Services (SS):** Authentication, de-authentication, privacy (encryption), and MAC Service Data Unit (MSDU) delivery.
2. **Distribution System Services (DSS):** Association, disassociation, reassociation, integration, and distribution — functions performed by the AP.
3. **PHY Layer Functions:** Modulation, signal encoding, RF transmission, and Clear Channel Assessment (CCA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Insight:** Every authentication, association, and reassociation event generates **Management Frames** that are, by default, *unencrypted* — even in WPA2/WPA3 networks. These frames are the first artefacts a forensic investigator must capture.

### 2.2 The 802.11 MAC Sub-Layer — CSMA/CA Operational Logic

Unlike Ethernet's **CSMA/CD** (Collision Detection), 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance) because a wireless station **cannot listen while transmitting** (Near/Far problem) and therefore *cannot detect* a collision.

The Distributed Coordination Function (DCF) algorithm proceeds in these steps:

1. **DIFS Wait:** A station with a frame to send senses the channel. If idle for a **DCF Inter-Frame Space (DIFS)** period, it proceeds to transmit.
2. **Backoff Counter:** If the channel is busy, the station defers and generates a random **backoff counter** uniformly distributed in `[0, CW]` where $CW$ is the current **Contention Window**.
3. **Slot Decrement:** The counter decrements by 1 for every idle **slot time** $\sigma$. It freezes when the channel becomes busy, resuming after the next DIFS.
4. **Transmission:** When the counter reaches 0, the station transmits the entire frame.
5. **Acknowledgment:** The receiving STA returns an **ACK frame** after a **Short Inter-Frame Space (SIFS)**.
6. **CW Adjustment:** On failure, $CW$ is doubled up to $CW_{\max}$ (Binary Exponential Backoff). On success, it resets to $CW_{\min}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **RTS/CTS Handshake (Optional):** For long frames, the sender transmits a **Request-To-Send (RTS)**; the receiver replies with **Clear-To-Send (CTS)** after SIFS, reserving the medium and solving the *Hidden Node Problem*.

### 2.3 The 802.11 Frame Format — Forensic Anatomy

Every 802.11 frame (except some control frames) contains:

| Field | Length | Forensic Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame Control** | 2 bytes | Type/Subtype — identifies Mgmt/Data/Ctrl |
| **Duration/ID** | 2 bytes | Network Allocation Vector (NAV) reservation |
| **Address 1 (DA)** | 6 bytes | Destination MAC |
| **Address 2 (SA)** | 6 bytes | Source MAC — *spoofable, key evidence* |
| **Address 3 (BSSID)** | 6 bytes | Identifies the AP / cell |
| **Seq Control** | 2 bytes | Sequence number — for replay detection |
| **Body** | 0–2312 bytes | Payload (encrypted after WPA2) |
| **FCS** | 4 bytes | CRC-32 integrity check |

### 2.4 Wireless Security Challenges — Taxonomy

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups wireless security challenges into four forensic categories:

**A. Confidentiality Challenges**
- **Eavesdropping:** RF propagation beyond physical walls.
- **Weak Encryption (WEP):** Static 24-bit IV, RC4 stream cipher vulnerability.
- **WPA/WPA2-PSK Cracking:** Offline dictionary attack against the 4-way EAPOL handshake.
- **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack):** Exploits nonce reuse in the 4-way handshake of WPA2.

**B. Integrity Challenges**
- **Bit-flip attacks on WEP-encrypted streams** (chopchop attack).
- **Frame injection / forgery** once an attacker learns the keystream.

**C. Authentication Challenges**
- **MAC Address Spoofing:** Trivial, as MAC addresses are sent in plaintext.
- **Evil Twin AP:** Rogue AP with cloned SSID.
- **Deauthentication / Disassociation DoS:** Forged Mgmt frames force clients offline.

**D. Availability Challenges**
- **RF Jamming** (broadband/narrowband).
- **CTS / Beacon flood** exhaustion of client resources.
- **Power Save Buffer Overflow** attacks.

### 2.5 Security Evolution — WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3

| Protocol | Algorithm | Key Length | Status | Forensic Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WEP** | RC4 | 40/104-bit + 24-bit IV | **Broken (2001)** | Crackable in <60 sec via aircrack-ng |
| **WPA (TKIP)** | RC4 + per-packet key | 128-bit | Transitional | Vulnerable to Beck-Tews attack |
| **WPA2 (CCMP)** | AES-CCM | 128-bit | **Standard** | Vulnerable to KRACK (2017) |
| **WPA3 (SAE)** | AES-GCMP + SAE handshake | 128/192-bit | **Current** | Resistant to offline dictionary; SAE provides forward secrecy |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU ESE.** Master the units, boundary conditions, and limitations of each.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friis Transmission Equation (Free-space path loss) | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P$ in Watts, $\lambda$ in m, $d$ in m |
| 2 | Wavelength–Frequency Relation | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ in Hz |
| 3 | Received Signal Strength (Path Loss, log-distance) | $P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2–4 typical indoor) |
| 4 | DCF Backoff Slots | $Backoff = \text{Rand}(0,\, CW)$ | $CW$ doubles per failure |
| 5 | DCF Slot Bounds | $CW_{\min} = 15$, $CW_{\max} = 1023$ | Number of *slots*, not seconds |
| 6 | Saturated Throughput (approx, Bianchi model) | $S = \dfrac{P_s \cdot P_tr \cdot E[L]}{(1-P_tr)\sigma + P_tr P_s T_s + P_tr(1-P_s)T_c} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C}$ | $P_s$ = prob of success given transmission |
| 7 | Shannon Channel Capacity (Nyquist/Shannon) | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ dimensionless |
| 8 | WEP Key Space (effective) | $2^{24}$ IVs | IV pool exhaustion enables statistical attack |
| 9 | WPA-PSK Strength | $Strength \propto 2^{\vert P \vert}$ for password $P$ | Offline attack; length is the only defence |
| 10 | RTS/CTS Reservation | $NAV_{RTS} = T_{CTS} + 2 \cdot SIFS$ | Set in Duration/ID field |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Friis equation is foundational for *Wi-Fi site-survey* (predicting AP coverage), while Shannon's capacity ceiling dictates the *maximum theoretical throughput* in a forensic traffic-capture link.

### 2.7 Why These Equations Matter in Network Forensics

- The **Friis equation** explains *why* an attacker with a high-gain Yagi antenna can capture your "secured" 4th-floor Wi-Fi from the parking lot.
- The **WEP IV space** ($2^{24} \approx 16.7$ million) — combined with the birthday paradox — means collisions appear within ~**4,000 packets**, allowing full key recovery in under a minute on busy networks.
- The **DCF bounds** are critical when *reconstructing timeline of events* from a capture: backoff behaviour reveals hidden terminals, retransmissions reveal *packet injection attacks*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Saturated 802.11 DCF Throughput (Bianchi's Model)

**Step 0 — Problem Statement.**
A saturated 802.11b network (raw bit rate $C = 11$ Mbps) has $n$ contending stations, slot time $\sigma = 20\,\mu s$, SIFS $= 10\,\mu s$, DIFS $= 50\,\mu s$, and a payload of $E[L] = 8184$ bits per data frame. Compute the theoretical maximum throughput.

**Step 1 — Define the basic slot probabilities.**
In Bianchi's model, let $\tau$ be the probability a station transmits in a randomly chosen slot. The conditional collision probability is $p$. The fixed-point solution is:

$$
\tau = \frac{2}{1 + W + p\,W \sum_{i=0}^{m-1}(2p)^i}
$$

where $W = CW_{\min} + 1 = 16$ and $m$ is the maximum backoff stage (so $CW_{\max} + 1 = 2^m W = 1024 \Rightarrow m = 6$).

**Step 2 — Probability that at least one station transmits in a slot.**
$P_{tr} = 1 - (1-\tau)^n$

This is the probability the slot is *not* idle. Explanation: each of the $n$ stations independently decides with probability $\tau$ to transmit; the complement of "all $n$ remain silent" is $1 - (1-\tau)^n$.

**Step 3 — Conditional probability of a successful transmission.**
$P_s = \dfrac{n\,\tau\,(1-\tau)^{n-1}}{P_{tr}}$

Explanation: exactly one station transmits while all $n-1$ others are silent. Numerator counts the $n$ ways to pick the successful sender; denominator conditions on the slot being non-idle.

**Step 4 — Throughput expression.**
$$
S = \frac{P_{tr}\,P_s\,E[L]}{(1-P_{tr})\,\sigma + P_{tr}\,P_s\,T_s + P_{tr}\,(1-P_s)\,T_c}
$$

- Numerator: average *payload bits successfully delivered per slot*.
- Denominator: average *slot duration* — idle ($\sigma$), success ($T_s$), or collision ($T_c$).

**Step 5 — Compute $T_s$ and $T_c$ for 802.11b.**

$$
T_s = H + E[L]/C + SIFS + ACK/C + DIFS
$$

$$
T_c = H + E[L]/C + DIFS
$$

Let $H$ be the header + propagation delay $\approx 50\,\mu s$. With $C = 11 \times 10^6$ bps:

$$
E[L]/C = 8184 / 11 \times 10^6 \approx 744\,\mu s
$$

$$
ACK/C = 304 / 11 \times 10^6 \approx 27.6\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_s \approx 50 + 744 + 10 + 27.6 + 50 = 881.6\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_c \approx 50 + 744 + 50 = 844\,\mu s
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation for $n = 5$ stations.**
By solving the fixed-point numerically, $\tau \approx 0.015$, $P_{tr} \approx 0.0728$, $P_s \approx 0.92$.

$$
S \approx \frac{0.0728 \times 0.92 \times 8184}{0.927 \times 20 + 0.0728 \times 0.92 \times 881.6 + 0.0728 \times 0.08 \times 844}\,\mu s
$$

$$
S \approx \frac{548.6}{18.54 + 59.06 + 4.92} = \frac{548.6}{82.5} \approx 6.65 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** The 802.11b saturation throughput is ~6.65 Mbps, which is **60.5%** of the 11 Mbps raw rate — a critical *forensic benchmark* for distinguishing normal traffic from injected/attack traffic (which skews the collision probability and therefore the observed throughput).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The key insight is that under saturation, the *collision probability rises* with $n$, dragging throughput down — a phenomenon an attacker can induce artificially via a *jamming or CTS-flooding* attack.

### 3.2 Derivation: WEP Key Recovery via IV Collision (Birthday Attack)

**Step 1 — Setup.** Each WEP packet reuses a 24-bit IV. Two packets encrypted with the *same* (IV, key) pair produce the *same* keystream $RC4(IV \Vert K)$, allowing XOR-based keystream recovery.

**Step 2 — Probability of collision after $N$ packets.** For uniformly random 24-bit IVs, the probability at least one collision exists after $N$ packets:

$$
P_{\text{collision}}(N) = 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{N-1}\left(1 - \frac{i}{2^{24}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for large $2^{24}$.** Using the birthday-paradox expansion:

$$
P_{\text{collision}}(N) \approx 1 - e^{-N(N-1)/(2 \cdot 2^{24})}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for 50% probability.** Set $P = 0.5$:

$$
e^{-N^2 / 2^{25}} = 0.5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad N^2 = 2^{25} \cdot \ln 2
$$

$$
N = \sqrt{2^{25} \cdot 0.693} \approx 4823 \text{ packets}
$$

**Step 5 — Forensics implication.** At a typical 11 Mbps network, 4,823 packets traverse in under **30 seconds** of light browsing — which is why WEP is considered *broken in real-time*. A forensic investigator recovering a WEP PCAP must immediately mark the network as compromised.

### 3.3 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss for Forensically Critical Distances

**Step 1 — Use the Friis equation.**

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dBm for a $\lambda = 0.125$ m link (2.4 GHz).**
Let $P_t = 20$ dBm (100 mW), $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic), and $d = 100$ m:

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.125}{4\pi \cdot 100}\right)^2 = (9.95 \times 10^{-5})^2 \approx 9.9 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
P_r \text{ (linear)} = 100 \times 9.9 \times 10^{-9} = 9.9 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_r \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}(9.9 \times 10^{-7} / 10^{-3}) = 10 \log_{10}(9.9 \times 10^{-4}) \approx -30 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** At 100 m in free space, signal drops to **−30 dBm**, which is near the *minimum receiver sensitivity* of a typical 802.11n card (around −85 dBm). This explains why a forensic capture rig with a directional antenna (gain ~15 dBi) can extend the practical capture radius by a factor of $\sqrt{10^{1.5}} \approx 5.6\times$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defence vs. Forensic Artefact

| Attack Vector | Layer | Cryptographic Weakness | Forensic Artefact in PCAP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WEP IV Reuse** | MAC/LLC | RC4 weak IVs | Multiple frames with same IV |
| **KRACK** | MAC | Nonce reuse in 4-way handshake | Replayed EAPOL Message 3 |
| **Evil Twin** | PHY/Mgmt | None — pure social | Same SSID, different BSSID |
| **Deauth Flood** | Mgmt | Unauthenticated Mgmt frames | High volume of subtype 0xC0 frames |
| **MAC Spoofing** | MAC | None — plaintext address | Inconsistent SA↔sequence pattern |
| **Jamming** | PHY | None — RF | Channel utilization = 100%, no valid frames |
| **ChopChop** | WEP | ICV not authenticated | Multiple crafted short frames per real packet |

### 3.5 KTU Computation: Maximum Wired-Equivalent Throughput under 802.11g

**Step 1 — Base equation.** For 802.11g (OFDM, 54 Mbps raw):

$$
\text{Effective throughput} \approx 0.55 \times C_{\text{raw}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.** $C_{\text{raw}} = 54$ Mbps:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = 0.55 \times 54 = 29.7 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Real-world adjustment (ACK, header overhead, inter-frame spaces).** Subtract overhead ratio $\approx 0.45$:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{real}} \approx 29.7 \times 0.65 \approx 19.3 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Application:** If the recorded throughput from a capture *exceeds* this bound, the capture is likely from an **802.11n/ac network with frame aggregation**, *not* a baseline 802.11g. Investigators must adjust their rate-vs-time analysis accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802.11 Network Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STA1[STA1 Laptop]:::sta
    STA2[STA2 Smartphone]:::sta
    STA3[STA3 IoT Device]:::sta
    AP1[Access Point BSSID AA BB CC DD EE FF]:::ap
    AP2[Access Point BSSID 11 22 33 44 55 66]:::ap
    DS1[Distribution System Ethernet Switch]:::ds
    RTR[Router Gateway to Internet]:::rtr
    INV[Forensic Investigator Monitor Mode Adapter]:::forensic

    STA1 --- AP1
    STA2 --- AP1
    STA3 --- AP2
    AP1 --- DS1
    AP2 --- DS1
    DS1 --- RTR
    AP1 -. Passive Capture .-> INV
    AP2 -. Passive Capture .-> INV
    RTR -. Traffic Logs .-> INV

    classDef sta fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef ap fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef ds fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef rtr fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef forensic fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

### 4.2 CSMA/CA Channel Access — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame Ready to Transmit]:::start --> B{Channel Idle for DIFS period}:::decision
    B -- NO --> C[Defer Channel Access]:::defer --> D[Set Backoff Counter Rand 0 CW]:::compute
    D --> E{Channel Busy}:::decision
    E -- YES --> C
    E -- NO --> F[Decrement Counter by 1 Slot]:::compute
    F --> G{Counter equals 0}:::decision
    G -- NO --> E
    G -- YES --> H{Use RTS CTS Optional}:::decision
    H -- YES --> I[Transmit RTS Frame]:::tx
    H -- NO --> K[Transmit Data Frame]:::tx
    I --> L{CTS Received within CTS Timeout}:::decision
    L -- NO --> M[Collision Assumed Increment CW]:::collision
    L -- YES --> K
    K --> N{ACK Received after SIFS}:::decision
    N -- YES --> O[Success Reset CW to CWmin]:::success
    N -- NO --> M
    M --> C

    classDef start fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef defer fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef tx fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef collision fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

### 4.3 4-Way Handshake (WPA2-PSK) — Forensic View

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Auth as Authenticator AP
    participant Sup as Supplicant Client
    participant Inv as Forensic Investigator

    Note over Auth,Sup: 802.1X EAPOL 4-Way Handshake
    Auth->>Sup: Message 1 ANonce in EAPOL Key Frame
    Note right of Sup: Client derives PTK from ANonce SNonce PMK PSK
    Sup->>Auth: Message 2 SNonce MIC in EAPOL Key Frame
    Note left of Auth: AP derives PTK and installs encryption key
    Auth->>Sup: Message 3 Install GTK MIC encrypted
    Note right of Sup: Client installs both PTK and GTK
    Sup->>Auth: Message 4 ACK MIC encrypted

    Note over Inv: Captures all 4 frames in monitor mode<br/>KRA KRACK exploit: Replay M3 to force nonce reuse
    Inv-->>Inv: Offline dictionary attack against PTK<br/>using captured ANonce and SNonce
```

### 4.4 Attack Surface Map — Wireless Forensics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHY["PHY Layer Attacks"]
        J1[RF Jamming]:::atk
        J2[Channel Based MITM]:::atk
    end

    subgraph MAC["MAC Layer Attacks"]
        M1[MAC Spoofing]:::atk
        M2[Frame Injection]:::atk
        M3[CTS Flood]:::atk
    end

    subgraph MGMT["Management Frame Attacks"]
        G1[Deauthentication Flood]:::atk
        G2[Evil Twin Rogue AP]:::atk
        G3[Beacon Flood]:::atk
    end

    subgraph CRYPTO["Cryptographic Attacks"]
        C1[WEP IV Weakness]:::atk
        C2[WPA2 KRACK]:::atk
        C3[PMKID Attack]:::atk
    end

    subgraph FORENSICS["Forensic Countermeasures"]
        F1[Monitor Mode Capture]:::defense
        F2[WIDS Wireless IDS]:::defense
        F3[RF Spectrum Analyzer]:::defense
        F4[PCAP Timeline Reconstruction]:::defense
    end

    PHY --> FORENSICS
    MAC --> FORENSICS
    MGMT --> FORENSICS
    CRYPTO --> FORENSICS

    classDef atk fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef defense fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forensic Capture Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RF[RF Medium 2.4 5 6 GHz]:::layer1
    ANT[Yagi or Omnidirectional Antenna]:::layer2
    NIC[Wireless NIC in Monitor Mode]:::layer3
    DRV[Driver Kismet or Aircrack ng]:::layer4
    CAP[Capture Engine tcpdump or Wireshark]:::layer5
    DEC[802.11 Decoder]:::layer6
    ANA[Forensic Analysis Engine]:::layer7
    OUT[Chain of Custody Report]:::layer8

    RF --> ANT --> NIC --> DRV --> CAP --> DEC --> ANA --> OUT

    classDef layer1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef layer5 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef layer6 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#1a237e,color:#000
    classDef layer7 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of CSMA/CA and discuss why 802.11 uses collision avoidance instead of collision detection as in Ethernet.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) is the MAC-layer protocol used by IEEE 802.11 to regulate shared access to the wireless medium. **(1 mark)** Unlike Ethernet (CSMA/CD), a wireless station **cannot transmit and receive simultaneously** and therefore *cannot detect* a collision in real time — this is the "near-far problem" where a strong local signal masks a weaker remote collision. **(1 mark)** Instead, the station *avoids* collisions via: (i) physical carrier sensing, (ii) random backoff counter between 0 and $CW$, and (iii) optional RTS/CTS handshake to reserve the medium. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three security challenges in IEEE 802.11 networks and briefly explain each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Eavesdropping (1 mark):** RF signals propagate beyond physical walls; an attacker with a directional antenna can capture traffic from off-premises.
2. **Weak Encryption – WEP (1 mark):** Uses 24-bit IV with RC4; the small IV space causes keystream reuse within ~5,000 packets enabling full key recovery.
3. **Deauthentication/Disassociation DoS (1 mark):** Management frames are unauthenticated; an attacker can forge them to forcibly disconnect clients.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *Design + Analysis Flavour*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the four-way EAPOL handshake used in WPA2-PSK. Show the messages exchanged, identify the cryptographic inputs, and explain how an offline dictionary attack recovers the Pre-Shared Key (PSK).

**(b) [7 marks)** Consider a saturated 802.11b network with $n=10$ stations, raw rate $C = 11$ Mbps, $\sigma = 20\,\mu s$, $T_s = 880\,\mu s$, $T_c = 844\,\mu s$, and $E[L] = 8184$ bits. Compute the throughput using Bianchi's formula:

$$
S = \frac{P_{tr}\,P_s\,E[L]}{(1-P_{tr})\,\sigma + P_{tr}\,P_s\,T_s + P_{tr}\,(1-P_s)\,T_c}
$$

Assume $\tau = 0.012$ and $p = 0.08$ (typical fixed-point solution for $n=10$).

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks:**

The 4-Way Handshake achieves *mutual authentication* and *temporal key derivation* without sending the PSK over the air. **(½ mark)**

| Message | Direction | Payload | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **M1** | AP → Client | ANonce | Provides nonce for PTK derivation **(1 mark)** |
| **M2** | Client → AP | SNonce, MIC | Proves client knows PMK **(1 mark)** |
| **M3** | AP → Client | GTK, MIC, Install flag | Delivers group key, signals key install **(1 mark)** |
| **M4** | Client → AP | ACK, MIC | Confirms installation **(½ mark)** |

**Key Derivation:** $PTK = PRF(PMK,\, ANonce \,\Vert\, SNonce \,\Vert\, MAC_{AP} \,\Vert\, MAC_{STA})$ **(1 mark)**
where $PMK = PBKDF2(PSK,\, SSID,\, 4096,\, 256)$ **(½ mark)**

**Offline Dictionary Attack:** The investigator captures M1 and M2, extracting $ANonce$, $SNonce$, $MAC_{AP}$, $MAC_{STA}$, and the MIC. For each candidate password $P_i$ from a dictionary: **(½ mark)**
1. Compute $PMK_i = PBKDF2(P_i, SSID, 4096, 256)$.
2. Compute $PTK_i = PRF(PMK_i, ANonce \Vert SNonce \Vert MAC_{AP} \Vert MAC_{STA})$.
3. Compute expected MIC and compare with the captured MIC.

A match reveals the correct PSK. **(½ mark)**

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $P_{tr}$ and $P_s$.** (1 mark)

$$
P_{tr} = 1 - (1-\tau)^n = 1 - (1-0.012)^{10} = 1 - (0.988)^{10} \approx 1 - 0.8857 = 0.1143
$$

$$
P_s = \frac{n\,\tau\,(1-\tau)^{n-1}}{P_{tr}} = \frac{10 \times 0.012 \times (0.988)^9}{0.1143} = \frac{0.1064}{0.1143} \approx 0.931
$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the Bianchi formula.** (1 mark)

$$
S = \frac{0.1143 \times 0.931 \times 8184}{(1-0.1143)\times 20 + 0.1143 \times 0.931 \times 880 + 0.1143 \times (1-0.931) \times 844}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.** (3 marks)

- Numerator: $0.1143 \times 0.931 \times 8184 \approx 870.7$ bit·slots/slot
- Denominator terms:
  - Idle: $0.8857 \times 20 = 17.71\,\mu s$
  - Success: $0.1143 \times 0.931 \times 880 = 93.66\,\mu s$
  - Collision: $0.1143 \times 0.069 \times 844 = 6.66\,\mu s$
  - **Total: $118.03\,\mu s$**

**Step 4 — Final throughput.** (1 mark)

$$
S = \frac{870.7}{118.03} \approx 7.38 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** (1 mark)
This is the saturation ceiling. As $n$ increases, $\tau$ saturates and $P_s$ drops, lowering $S$ — a *measurable forensic indicator* of an unusually high contention environment (e.g., a CTS-flood DoS attack).

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Security & Forensics Flavour*

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the evolution of 802.11 security protocols (WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3). For each, identify the cryptographic primitive and a specific vulnerability.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design a forensic capture procedure to investigate a suspected **Evil Twin AP attack** in an enterprise. List the tools, the frames to capture, and the indicators of compromise (IoC) you would look for in the PCAP.

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks:**

**1. WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) — 1999:** Uses **RC4 stream cipher** with a 24-bit IV and 40/104-bit key. **(½ mark)**
**Vulnerability:** IV space of $2^{24}$ causes keystream reuse within ~4,823 packets (birthday attack). Tools like **aircrack-ng** recover the key in under 60 seconds. **(1 mark)**

**2. WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) — 2003:** Uses **TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol)** — still RC4 but with per-packet key mixing and MIC (Michael). **(½ mark)**
**Vulnerability:** Susceptible to the **Beck-Tews attack** (2008) — can decrypt short packets via QoS channel manipulation. **(1 mark)**

**3. WPA2 (2004):** Uses **AES-CCM (CCMP)** with 128-bit keys and 4-way EAPOL handshake. **(½ mark)**
**Vulnerability:** **KRACK (2017)** — forces nonce reuse by reinstallation of an already-used key, allowing decryption, replay, and forgery. Also vulnerable to **PMKID attack** (clientless offline dictionary attack). **(1 mark)**

**4. WPA3 (2018):** Uses **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** handshake with **AES-GCMP** and **forward secrecy**. **(½ mark)**
**Vulnerability:** Limited public exploits as of writing; theoretical concerns around **Dragonblood** (downgrade and side-channel attacks on SAE), partially patched in WPA3-Personal Transition mode. **(1 mark)**

**Synthesis (½ mark):** Each protocol improved on the previous via stronger cryptography and authentication, but the *forensic lesson* is that wireless security is an arms race — historical PCAPs must be re-evaluated against newly discovered attack techniques.

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks:**

**1. Tools Required:** (1 mark)
- Wireless adapter capable of **monitor mode** (e.g., Alfa AWUS036ACH).
- **Kismet** (rogue AP detection), **Wireshark** (PCAP analysis), **Aircrack-ng suite** (deauth + capture).
- **GPS dongle** for geo-tagging of AP locations.
- **HostAPd** for lab simulation.

**2. Frames to Capture:** (2 marks)
- **Beacon frames** (subtype 0x80) — extract SSID, BSSID, supported rates, vendor, encryption type.
- **Probe Request/Response** (subtypes 0x04 / 0x05) — reveals *which* APs legitimate clients are searching for.
- **Authentication frames** (subtype 0x0B) and **Association** (0x00 / 0x01) — identifies which AP a client actually connects to.
- **EAPOL handshake** (4-Way) — for downstream key recovery.
- **Data frames** to capture post-connectivity.

**3. Indicators of Compromise (IoC):** (3 marks)
- **Same SSID, different BSSID** — the smoking gun. Compare beacon BSSIDs over time.
- **Channel change** — legitimate AP on fixed channel; evil twin may switch to dodge interference.
- **Encryption downgrade** — legitimate AP advertises WPA3, evil twin offers WPA2 (downgrade attack).
- **Signal strength anomaly** — same BSSID suddenly appears with anomalously high RSSI (a closer rogue).
- **Sequence number discontinuity** — AP sequence counters reset, breaking the monotonic increase seen from the legitimate AP.
- **Certificate mismatch** (in WPA3-Enterprise): enterprise evil twins fail the certificate validation, visible in EAPOL logs.
- **High volume of deauthentication frames** preceding association — typical of an *Evil Twin forcing re-association*.

**4. Reporting:** (1 mark) PCAP must be hashed (SHA-256), chain-of-custody recorded, and the captured beacon set documented with timestamp, GPS, and BSSID/SSID for court admissibility.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing CSMA/CA with CSMA/CD** is the single most common 3-mark mistake. Always justify *why* collision *detection* fails in wireless (near-far problem, half-duplex radio).
> 2. **Forgetting the DIFS vs SIFS distinction** in timing diagrams — DIFS is *contention-based*; SIFS is *response-based* and shorter.
> 3. **Wrong BSSID/SSID/ESSID terminology** — students mix them up. BSSID = AP's MAC; SSID/ESSID = network name; both can be spoofed independently.
> 4. **WEP key length** — students often write "64/128-bit" but forget the 24-bit IV is *separate*. Effective key is 40-bit or 104-bit + 24-bit IV.
> 5. **KRACK is a WPA2 issue, not WPA3** — the 4-way handshake is the same, but WPA3's SAE replaces it entirely.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in the Friis equation (must be SI: m, Hz, W) and the Bianchi formula (slot time in seconds; $E[L]$ in **bits**).
> 7. **Confusing PMK vs PTK** — PMK is *master* (from PSK/802.1X); PTK is *per-session* (from nonces + MACs).

**Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ 802.11 = PHY + MAC; standards b/a/g/n/ac/ax.
- ☐ BSS = AP + STAs; ESS = multiple BSS via DS; IBSS = ad-hoc.
- ☐ CSMA/CA: DIFS → backoff → transmit → SIFS → ACK → CW doubl
- ☐ CW bounds: $CW_{\min}=15$, $CW_{\max}=1023$ slots.
- ☐ Three frame types: Management (0), Control (1), Data (2).
- ☐ WEP = RC4 + 24-bit IV → broken (FMS/Korek attacks).
- ☐ WPA = TKIP/RC4 + MIC → transitional (Beck-Tews).
- ☐ WPA2 = AES-CCM + 4-way EAPOL → KRACK vulnerable.
- ☐ WPA3 = SAE + AES-GCMP → forward secrecy, Dragonblood.
- ☐ Mgmt frames (beacon, deauth, disassoc) are **always unauthenticated**.
- ☐ Evil Twin = same SSID, different BSSID, possibly stronger signal.
- ☐ Forensic tools: Wireshark, Kismet, aircrack-ng, tcpdump, monitor-mode NIC.
- ☐ Friis equation: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — distance^2 attenuation.
- ☐ Bianchi throughput: ~55–60% of raw rate in saturation.
- ☐ IV collision: ~4,823 packets for 50% probability of WEP collision.
- ☐ Deauth flood: high volume of subtype 0x0C frames in PCAP.
- ☐ PCAP chain of custody: SHA-256 hash, timestamp, GPS, examiner log.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Authentications: WEP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Authentications: WEP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP)** is a security protocol defined as part of the original **IEEE 802.11 wireless networking standard (1999)** that was designed to provide *data confidentiality*, *access control*, and *data integrity* comparable to that of a wired LAN. WEP was the first encryption mechanism deployed natively in Wi-Fi devices and operates at the **MAC sub-layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model.

WEP employs the **RC4 stream cipher** for encryption and uses a **shared secret 40-bit or 104-bit key** combined with a **24-bit Initialization Vector (IV)** to generate a per-packet keystream. Integrity is provided via a **32-bit CRC-32 checksum (ICV - Integrity Check Value)** appended to the plaintext before encryption.

### 1.2 Types of Authentication under WEP

WEP supports two distinct 802.11 authentication modes:

1. **Open System Authentication (OSA)** – The default null authentication mode. The AP accepts any client request; no credentials are exchanged. Effectively, it provides *no authentication* and is used purely to establish association.
2. **Shared Key Authentication (SKA)** – A challenge–response mechanism where both AP and client must possess the same pre-shared WEP key. The AP sends a 128-byte challenge packet encrypted with WEP, and the client must return the correctly decrypted plaintext.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Network Forensics Module 4, students must be able to **distinguish between OSA and SKA**, **explain the cryptographic operation of WEP**, **describe the WEP frame structure**, and **identify WEP's forensic vulnerabilities** (IV reuse, weak keys, FMS attack).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Mailbox"

Imagine two offices (**Station** and **Access Point**) sharing a private *shared password* (`k`) for communication. They agree on a simple rule:

- **Open System Analogy:** Anyone who knocks on the door (sends an Auth Request) is *immediately admitted* without checks. The "authentication" is just a polite handshake.
- **Shared Key Analogy:** The Access Point shouts a **random sentence** encrypted using their shared password. The visitor must prove knowledge of the password by **correctly decrypting and returning the original sentence**.
- **The IV Analogy:** To prevent eavesdroppers from recognizing repeated encrypted messages (a vulnerability called *keystream reuse*), each message is prefixed with a **unique random number** (`IV`) – like writing a different timestamp on every envelope, even if the inside message is identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Significance:** WEP's "whispering mailbox" is *not* secure. Because the IV space is only 24 bits long, on a busy network (~4500 packets), the birthday paradox guarantees that **IV collisions occur within hours**, allowing attackers to recover the keystream and ultimately the shared key. This is foundational to *WEP cracking* and forms a major component of wireless forensic investigations.

### 1.4 Physical Standards & Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Standard | **IEEE 802.11 (1999), Clause 8** |
| Cipher | **RC4 (40, 64, 128, 152 bit variants)** |
| Secret Key Length | **40 bits** (WEP-40) or **104 bits** (WEP-104) |
| IV Length | **24 bits** |
| Effective Key Length | **64 bits** or **128 bits** (IV included) |
| Integrity Hash | **CRC-32 (32-bit ICV)** |
| Authentication Modes | **Open System, Shared Key** |
| Default Key Slots | **4 keys per station** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How WEP Works

WEP's security architecture consists of **three integrated mechanisms**: an authentication handshake, a confidentiality service via RC4 encryption, and an integrity check using CRC-32. Below is the structured logical flow.

#### A. Authentication Phase

**Mode 1: Open System Authentication (OSA)**

- Client sends `Authentication Request` with Algorithm = 0 (Open System).
- AP responds with `Authentication Response` (Status = *Successful*) without any verification.
- Authentication = *null*. Only association control is enforced afterward via MAC filtering.

**Mode 2: Shared Key Authentication (SKA)** — Four-step exchange:

1. Client → AP: `Authentication Request` (Algorithm = 1, Shared Key).
2. AP → Client: `Authentication Challenge` containing a **128-byte random challenge text**.
3. Client → AP: `Authentication Response` containing the WEP-encrypted challenge.
4. AP → Client: `Authentication Confirmation` (Success if decrypted challenge matches).

#### B. Confidentiality Phase (RC4 Encryption)

The RC4 cipher is initialized using a **Per-Packet Key (PPK)**:

$$
PPK \;=\; IV \,\|\, Shared\_Key
$$

Where $\|$ denotes bitwise concatenation. The 24-bit IV is transmitted in clear text in the MAC header.

**Encryption Steps:**

1. Compute the Integrity Check Value: $ICV = \text{CRC-32}(P)$ where $P$ is plaintext.
2. Form the payload: $M = P \,\|\, ICV$.
3. Generate the keystream: $KS = \text{RC4}(IV \,\|\, K)$.
4. Compute ciphertext: $C = M \oplus KS$.

#### C. Integrity Phase

The receiver recomputes CRC-32 of the recovered plaintext and compares it with the ICV field. **Note:** CRC-32 is a *linear, un-keyed* hash — it detects *accidental* errors, not *malicious* tampering.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-Packet Key | $PPK = IV \,\Vert\, K$ | IV (24 bits) $\Vert$ Shared Secret Key (40/104 bits) |
| 2 | Encryption | $C = (P \,\Vert\, ICV) \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \,\Vert\, K)$ | XOR of plaintext+ICV with keystream |
| 3 | Decryption | $(P \,\Vert\, ICV) = C \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \,\Vert\, K)$ | Reverse XOR with same keystream |
| 4 | Integrity Check | $ICV = \text{CRC-32}(P)$ | 32-bit cyclic redundancy check |
| 5 | Verification | $\text{CRC-32}(P_{recovered}) \stackrel{?}{=} ICV_{received}$ | Constant-time comparison |
| 6 | IV Space Size | $N_{IV} = 2^{24} = 16,\!777,\!216$ | Total possible IV values |
| 7 | Birthday Bound | $P(\text{collision}) \geq 0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; n \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot N_{IV} \cdot \ln 2}$ | ~4825 packets for 50% collision probability |
| 8 | Effective Throughput Penalty | $T_{eff} = T_{raw} \cdot (1 - \alpha)$ | where $\alpha \approx 0.05$–$0.10$ for 64-bit WEP |
| 9 | Shared Key Auth Length | $L_{challenge} = 128 \; \text{bytes}$ | Fixed challenge size for SKA |
| 10 | RC4 Key Scheduling | $KSA: \; 256\text{-byte state from key}$ | First phase of RC4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\Vert$ for concatenation, $\oplus$ for XOR, and never use the bare pipe $\vert$ inside tables — use \vert to avoid breaking markdown syntax.**

### 2.3 Real-World Utility & Forensic Importance

- **Historical Significance:** WEP was the *de facto* wireless security standard from 1999–2003 and is the focus of almost every introductory wireless forensics case study.
- **Forensic Triage:** Identifying WEP in a captured PCAP/PCAPNG file is a routine step in network forensics investigations. Tools like **Wireshark**, **Aircrack-ng**, and **Kismet** parse WEP-specific fields (IV, Key ID, ICV).
- **Attack Pedagogy:** WEP is a *canonical* example used in cryptography courses to illustrate *why stream ciphers must never reuse keystreams* and *why unkeyed integrity checks fail*.
- **Compliance Context:** Although deprecated (replaced by WPA, WPA2, WPA3), WEP still appears in **legacy IoT devices, industrial SCADA systems, and old medical equipment** — making WEP knowledge essential for *incident response* in critical infrastructure forensics.
- **Court Admissibility:** Understanding WEP's vulnerabilities is critical when assessing *whether a captured wireless session was actually secure* during digital evidence examination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: WEP Encryption Operation

The WEP encryption transform applied to a single MAC frame can be expressed as:

$$
C = \text{Enc}(P) = \left( P \,\Vert\, \text{CRC-32}(P) \right) \;\oplus\; \text{RC4}\!\left( IV \,\Vert\, K \right)
$$

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Integrity Hash Computation.** Before encryption, the sender computes a 32-bit CRC-32 checksum over the plaintext payload $P$.

$$
ICV = \text{CRC-32}(P) \quad \text{where} \quad ICV \in \{0,1\}^{32}
$$

**Step 2 — Message Concatenation.** Append the ICV to the plaintext to form the *expanded plaintext* $M$.

$$
M = P \,\Vert\, ICV, \quad \vert M\vert = \vert P\vert + 32 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-Packet Key Construction.** Construct the RC4 seed by concatenating the 24-bit IV with the 40-bit (or 104-bit) shared secret $K$.

$$
\text{Seed} = IV \,\Vert\, K, \quad \vert\text{Seed}\vert = 64 \text{ or } 128 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — RC4 Keystream Generation.** Initialize the 256-byte RC4 state using the Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA), then generate the keystream $KS$ using the Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm (PRGA).

$$
S_0 \xleftarrow{\text{KSA}} \text{Seed}, \quad KS \xleftarrow{\text{PRGA}(S_0)} \text{keystream of length } \vert M\vert
$$

**Step 5 — XOR Encryption.** XOR the expanded plaintext $M$ bitwise with the keystream $KS$.

$$
C = M \oplus KS
$$

**Step 6 — Frame Transmission.** The 802.11 MAC header contains the IV and Key ID in cleartext; the encrypted payload $C$ is transmitted in the frame body.

### 3.2 Derivation: WEP Decryption Operation

$$
(P \,\Vert\, ICV_{recv}) = C \oplus \text{RC4}\!\left( IV \,\Vert\, K \right)
$$

**Step 1 — IV Extraction.** The receiver reads the 24-bit IV from the MAC frame header.

**Step 2 — RC4 Re-Initialization.** Same RC4 seed is constructed: $Seed = IV \,\Vert\, K$.

**Step 3 — Keystream Regeneration.** Identical $KS$ is produced (RC4 is deterministic given identical key and IV).

**Step 4 — Ciphertext Recovery.** XOR yields:

$$
P_{recovered} \,\Vert\, ICV_{recovered} = C \oplus KS
$$

**Step 5 — Integrity Verification.** Recompute:

$$
ICV_{computed} = \text{CRC-32}(P_{recovered})
$$

Compare with $ICV_{recovered}$. If equal, accept frame; otherwise, discard and increment the *dot11WEPExcludedCount* MIB counter.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WEP Authentication Modes Compared

| Criterion | Open System Authentication | Shared Key Authentication |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithm Number | 0 | 1 |
| Number of Frames | 2 | 4 |
| Credential Required | None | Pre-shared WEP key |
| Cryptographic Operation | None | RC4 encryption of challenge |
| Authentication Strength | **Zero (Null)** | Weak (key recovered via traffic analysis) |
| Frame Exchange | `Auth Req` → `Auth Resp` | `Auth Req` → `Challenge` → `Resp` → `Confirm` |
| Forgery Resistance | None | Low (single-frame replay possible if IV repeats) |
| Used In Modern Networks | Yes (WPA/WPA2 default) | No (deprecated) |
| Information Leaked to Attacker | MAC address only | Encrypted keystream of 128-byte challenge |
| KTU Examiner's Focus | Conceptual definition | Cryptographic analysis of the 4-step exchange |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — IV Reuse Probability

**Problem:** A busy corporate Wi-Fi network transmits 1000 packets/second using WEP. Calculate the expected time until a 50% probability of IV collision (using birthday bound).

**Solution:**

The birthday bound for IV collision:

$$
n \approx 1.18 \cdot \sqrt{N_{IV}}
$$

Where $N_{IV} = 2^{24} = 16,\!777,\!216$.

Therefore:

$$
n \approx 1.18 \cdot \sqrt{16,\!777,\!216} = 1.18 \cdot 4096 \approx 4834 \text{ packets}
$$

At 1000 packets/second:

$$
t = \frac{n}{\text{rate}} = \frac{4834}{1000} \approx 4.83 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** On a moderately busy WEP network, IV collisions occur *within 5 seconds*, confirming WEP's cryptographic unsuitability for any environment requiring confidentiality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — WEP Shared Key Authentication (4-Way Handshake)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT_STATION
    participant AP as ACCESS_POINT
    
    Note over C,AP: Both share the WEP secret key K
    
    C->>AP: 1. Authentication Request (Algorithm = 1, Shared Key)
    AP->>C: 2. Authentication Challenge (128-byte random text R, encrypted with WEP)
    Note over C: Client decrypts: P = C_R XOR RC4(IV||K)
    C->>AP: 3. Authentication Response (Encrypted challenge R)
    Note over AP: AP decrypts and compares P with stored R
    AP->>C: 4. Authentication Confirmation (Status = Success / Failure)
    
    Note over C,AP: If Status = Success, station proceeds to Association phase
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — WEP Encryption & Decryption Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SENDER_BLOCK
        A1[Plaintext P] --> A2[Compute CRC-32]
        A2 --> A3[Form M = P concatenated with ICV]
        A4[IV 24-bit] --> A5[Concatenate IV with Shared Key K]
        A6[Shared Key K 40 or 104-bit] --> A5
        A5 --> A7[RC4 Key Scheduling Algorithm KSA]
        A7 --> A8[RC4 PRGA generates Keystream KS]
        A3 --> A9[Bitwise XOR Operation]
        A8 --> A9
        A9 --> A10[Ciphertext C]
    end
    
    A10 --> TX[802.11 Wireless Transmission with IV in cleartext]
    
    TX --> RX[Receiver picks up frame]
    
    subgraph RECEIVER_BLOCK
        B1[Receive IV from header] --> B2[Concatenate IV with K]
        B2 --> B3[RC4 KSA + PRGA regenerates KS]
        B10[Receive Ciphertext C] --> B4[Bitwise XOR Operation]
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B5[Recover P concatenated with ICV]
        B5 --> B6[Recompute CRC-32 of P]
        B6 --> B7{Compare with ICV}
        B7 -->|Match| B8[Accept Frame]
        B7 -->|Mismatch| B9[Discard Frame]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — WEP 802.11 Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAC_HDR [802.11 MAC Header 24 bytes]
        H1[Frame Control 2 bytes]
        H2[Duration 2 bytes]
        H3[Address 1 DA 6 bytes]
        H4[Address 2 SA 6 bytes]
        H5[Address 3 BSSID 6 bytes]
        H6[Sequence Control 2 bytes]
    end
    
    subgraph IV_HDR [IV Header 4 bytes]
        I1[IV 24 bits]
        I2[Key ID 6 bits]
        I3[Reserved Pad 2 bits]
    end
    
    subgraph PAYLOAD [Encrypted Frame Body]
        E1[Encrypted Data variable length]
        E2[ICV 32 bits 4 bytes]
    end
    
    subgraph FCS [Frame Check Sequence]
        F1[FCS 32 bits 4 bytes]
    end
    
    MAC_HDR --> IV_HDR
    IV_HDR --> PAYLOAD
    PAYLOAD --> FCS
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — WEP Cryptographic Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7_APP [Application Layer Payload]
        APP[IP Packet UDP TCP Data]
    end
    
    subgraph L4_LLC [LLC SNAP Layer]
        LLC[LLC Header 8 bytes]
    end
    
    subgraph L3_WEP [WEP Cryptographic Engine]
        W1[CRC-32 Engine]
        W2[RC4 Stream Cipher]
        W3[IV Manager 24-bit counter]
        W4[Key Index Selector 0 to 3]
        W5[Shared Key Store 40 or 104 bit per slot]
    end
    
    subgraph L2_MAC [802.11 MAC Layer]
        MAC1[MAC Header Builder]
        MAC2[FCS CRC-32 Generator]
    end
    
    subgraph L1_PHY [802.11 PHY Layer]
        PHY1[PLCP Preamble and Header]
        PHY2[OFDM or DSSS Modulation]
    end
    
    APP --> LLC --> W1
    W1 --> W2
    W3 --> W2
    W4 --> W5
    W5 --> W2
    W2 --> MAC1
    MAC1 --> MAC2 --> PHY1 --> PHY2
    PHY2 --> RF[(Radio Frequency 2.4 or 5 GHz Channel)]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
