## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain Open System Authentication in WEP with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Open System Authentication (OSA) is the default authentication mechanism defined in IEEE 802.11. It is a **null authentication algorithm** — meaning it authenticates *every* requesting station without verifying any credential.

**Working (2 marks):**

1. The client station sends an `Authentication Request` frame to the Access Point with the authentication algorithm set to **0 (Open System)** and the transaction sequence number = 1.
2. The AP responds with an `Authentication Response` frame (sequence number = 2) with a status code of **"Successful" (0)**.
3. The station is then allowed to proceed to the **Association** phase.
4. No cryptographic operation is performed; the only effective access control is **MAC address filtering** at the AP (optional and trivially spoofable).

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Client                          Access Point
  |-------- Auth Req (Algo=0) -->|
  |                              |
  |<------- Auth Resp (Success) -|
```

OSA provides **zero authentication security** and is the reason modern networks (WPA2/WPA3) use cryptographic authentication (PSK or 802.1X).

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the Initialization Vector (IV) in WEP? Why is the 24-bit IV length a critical weakness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of IV (1.5 marks):**

The 24-bit IV is concatenated with the static shared secret key to form the **per-packet RC4 key**, ensuring that each packet is encrypted with a *different keystream* even when the same plaintext is sent repeatedly. The IV is transmitted **in cleartext** in the 802.11 MAC frame header so the receiver can reconstruct the keystream.

**Why 24-bit IV is a Critical Weakness (1.5 marks):**

- The IV space contains only $2^{24} = 16,\!777,\!216$ unique values.
- By the **birthday paradox**, an IV collision (reuse) is statistically guaranteed after approximately **$\sqrt{N_{IV}} \approx 4825$ packets** (50% probability).
- On a busy network, IV reuse occurs within **minutes**.
- IV reuse enables the **FMS attack (Fluhrer–Mantin–Shamir)**, which recovers the WEP shared key using statistical biases in RC4's key scheduling.
- Once the key is recovered, **all traffic on the network can be decrypted**, making WEP cryptographically broken.

---

### 📕 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer *any ONE* full question from each module. Each Part B carries 14 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **WEP encryption process** in detail. Show how the IV, shared key, plaintext, and ICV interact with the RC4 cipher. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **WEP frame format** (802.11 MAC frame with WEP) and explain each field. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — WEP Encryption Process (7 marks)**

**WEP Encryption Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Plaintext Preparation:** The sender has the plaintext frame body $P$ (the MSDU from upper layers).
2. **Integrity Check Value Computation:** The sender calculates:
$$
ICV = \text{CRC-32}(P), \quad \vert ICV \vert = 32 \text{ bits}
$$
3. **Message Expansion:** The ICV is appended to the plaintext:
$$
M = P \,\Vert\, ICV
$$
4. **Per-Packet Key (PPK) Construction:** A 24-bit IV is generated (often using a simple counter) and concatenated with the shared secret $K$:
$$
PPK = IV \,\Vert\, K
$$
5. **RC4 Keystream Generation:** The RC4 stream cipher is keyed with the PPK and produces a pseudorandom keystream $KS$ of length $\vert M\vert$.
6. **XOR Encryption:** The expanded plaintext is XORed with the keystream:
$$
C = M \oplus KS
$$
7. **Frame Transmission:** The IV is transmitted in the cleartext MAC header; $C$ is the encrypted frame body.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
+----------+      +---------+      +-------+
|Plaintext P| --> | CRC-32  | -->  |  ICV  |
+----------+      +---------+      +-------+
                                       |
                                       v
                                +-------------+
                                |   M = P||ICV|
                                +-------------+
                                       |
+-------+      +--------+             |
|  IV   | -->  | PPK    |             |
| 24-bit|      | IV||K  |             |
+-------+      +--------+             |
                  |                   |
                  v                   v
                +-----+             +-----+
                | RC4 | -- KS ---> | XOR | ---> Ciphertext C
                +-----+             +-----+
```

**Conclusion:** WEP combines RC4 confidentiality with CRC-32 integrity, but the 24-bit IV and unkeyed CRC-32 make it cryptographically weak.

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — WEP Frame Format (7 marks)**

**802.11 MAC Frame with WEP (5 marks):**

A WEP-protected 802.11 data frame consists of the following fields:

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame Control** | 2 bytes | Indicates frame type (Data), protocol version, and *Protected Frame* bit = 1 |
| **Duration / ID** | 2 bytes | 802.11 NAV duration value |
| **Address 1 (DA)** | 6 bytes | Destination MAC address |
| **Address 2 (SA)** | 6 bytes | Source MAC address |
| **Address 3 (BSSID)** | 6 bytes | MAC of the Access Point |
| **Sequence Control** | 2 bytes | Sequence number and fragment number |
| **IV** | 24 bits (3 bytes) | Initialization Vector — transmitted in cleartext |
| **Key ID** | 6 bits | Identifies which of the 4 WEP keys (0–3) is used |
| **Pad** | 2 bits | Reserved for future use (set to 0) |
| **Encrypted Data** | Variable | Ciphertext = Plaintext $\oplus$ RC4 keystream |
| **ICV** | 4 bytes (32 bits) | CRC-32 of original plaintext (also encrypted) |
| **FCS** | 4 bytes | Standard 802.11 Frame Check Sequence |

**Key Points (2 marks):**

- The **IV + Key ID + Pad** together form the **4-byte IV Header** prepended to the encrypted body.
- The **Frame Control** field's *Protected* bit (bit 6 of byte 1) indicates WEP encryption is active.
- The receiver uses the **Key ID** field to select the correct shared key from its 4-key table before regenerating the keystream.
- The ICV is encrypted *along with* the data — the receiver decrypts both, then recomputes CRC-32 to verify integrity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain **Shared Key Authentication** in WEP with a sequence diagram. Compare it with **Open System Authentication** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain any **five major weaknesses** of WEP that make it unsuitable for modern wireless networks. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — Shared Key Authentication (7 marks)**

**Shared Key Authentication Mechanism (4 marks):**

Shared Key Authentication (SKA) is a 4-frame challenge-response handshake defined in IEEE 802.11 (Algorithm = 1). It requires both the client and AP to hold the same WEP pre-shared key.

**Step-by-Step Flow:**

1. **Frame 1 — Authentication Request:** Client → AP. Algorithm = 1 (Shared Key), Transaction Sequence = 1.
2. **Frame 2 — Authentication Challenge:** AP → Client. AP generates a **128-byte random challenge text** $R$, encrypts it with WEP (using the shared key and a chosen IV), and transmits the ciphertext.
3. **Frame 3 — Authentication Response:** Client decrypts the challenge using its copy of the WEP key to recover $R$, appends the same $R$ to its response, encrypts again with WEP, and sends it back to the AP.
4. **Frame 4 — Authentication Confirmation:** AP decrypts the response. If the recovered plaintext matches the original $R$, status = *Successful*; otherwise, *Failure*.

**Sequence Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Client                            Access Point
  |                                   |
  |--- Auth Req (Algo=1) ----------->|
  |                                   |
  |<-- Auth Challenge (Enc(R)) ------|  [AP encrypts 128-byte R]
  |                                   |
  | [Decrypt: R' = Dec(Enc(R)) ]      |
  |                                   |
  |--- Auth Response (Enc(R')) ----->|
  |                                   |
  |                                   | [AP decrypts: R'' = ?]
  |                                   | [R'' == R ?]
  |<-- Auth Confirm (Success) --------|
  |                                   |
  |--- Association Req -------------->|
```

**Comparison Table (Already covered in Section 3.3 of these notes.)**

**Tabular Comparison (additional detail):**

| Aspect | Open System | Shared Key |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithm ID | 0 | 1 |
| Frame Count | 2 | 4 |
| Key Material Used | None | Shared WEP key |
| Cryptographic Strength | Zero | Low (key derivable) |
| Modern Use | Yes (WPA2 default) | Deprecated |

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — Five Major Weaknesses of WEP (7 marks)**

**1. Small IV Space (24 bits) — 1.5 marks:**
The 24-bit IV yields only $2^{24} = 16.7$ million possible values. On busy networks, IV reuse (collision) occurs within minutes via the **birthday paradox**, causing two different plaintexts to be encrypted with the *same keystream*. By XORing two such ciphertexts, the keystream is eliminated and the XOR of the two plaintexts is exposed.

**2. Weak Key Scheduling in RC4 (FMS Attack) — 1.5 marks:**
The RC4 Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA) exhibits statistical biases for certain IV values called *weak IVs*. The **Fluhrer–Mantin–Shamir (FMS) attack** (2001) exploits these biases to recover the WEP shared key after collecting approximately **4–6 million packets** (~1 hour on a busy network).

**3. Unkeyed Integrity Check (CRC-32) — 1.5 marks:**
CRC-32 is a *linear, un-keyed* polynomial checksum designed to detect *random* transmission errors, not *malicious* tampering. An attacker who flips a bit in the ciphertext can also flip the corresponding bit in the ICV to maintain the checksum's validity, enabling **bit-flipping attacks** without detection.

**4. Same Key Used for Authentication and Encryption — 1.5 marks:**
WEP uses the same shared key for both Shared Key Authentication *and* for data encryption. An attacker who passively captures the 4-frame SKA exchange obtains **two ciphertexts encrypted with two known plaintexts** (the 128-byte challenge), allowing direct keystream recovery and key deduction.

**5. No Key Management / Static Shared Secrets — 1 mark:**
WEP requires manual distribution of the shared key to every client; there is **no automatic key rotation**. Keys remain static for the life of the deployment, so a single compromise exposes *all* traffic indefinitely. This violates the principle of *forward secrecy*.

**Bonus Weakness (optional credit):** No replay protection — captured WEP frames can be replayed indefinitely since IV is just a counter.

**Conclusion:** These weaknesses collectively render WEP unsuitable for any production environment. The IEEE deprecated WEP in **802.11-2007**, and the Wi-Fi Alliance prohibited WEP use in certified devices from **2006**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing IV Length vs Key Length:** Many students incorrectly state "WEP uses a 24-bit key" — the 24 bits is the **IV**, the secret key is 40/104 bits. (**−1 mark**)
> 2. **Missing the Birthday Paradox:** When explaining IV reuse, students often say "after $2^{24}$ packets" — the *correct* answer is ~4825 packets via birthday bound. (**−0.5 mark**)
> 3. **Forgetting ICV in Encryption Equation:** Always write $C = (P \,\Vert\, ICV) \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \,\Vert\, K)$, not just $C = P \oplus KS$. (**−1 mark**)
> 4. **Mixing up OSA and SKA:** OSA = 2 frames, null authentication. SKA = 4 frames, challenge-response. Don't swap them. (**−1 mark**)
> 5. **Forgetting Frame Control's Protected Bit:** A WEP frame has the *Protected Frame* subfield = 1 in Frame Control. (**−0.5 mark**)
> 6. **Spelling "Initialization" wrong:** Examiners mark down for "Initialisation Vector" if KTU uses US spelling — match the official terminology. (**−0.5 mark**)

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **WEP** = *Wired Equivalent Privacy*, IEEE 802.11 (1999), deprecated.
- ✅ **Two Authentication Modes:** **Open System (OSA, 2 frames)** and **Shared Key (SKA, 4 frames)**.
- ✅ **Cipher:** **RC4 stream cipher** with 40-bit or 104-bit shared key.
- ✅ **Per-Packet Key:** $PPK = IV \,\Vert\, K$, where IV = **24 bits**.
- ✅ **Encryption:** $C = (P \,\Vert\, \text{CRC-32}(P)) \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \,\Vert\, K)$.
- ✅ **Integrity:** **CRC-32** (32-bit ICV) — *unkeyed, linear, weak*.
- ✅ **IV Header:** IV (24 bits) + Key ID (6 bits) + Pad (2 bits) = 4 bytes in cleartext.
- ✅ **Birthday Paradox:** IV collision within **~4825 packets** (50% probability).
- ✅ **FMS Attack:** Recovers WEP key from **4–6 million packets** using weak IVs.
- ✅ **WEP is broken** — replaced by WPA (TKIP/RC4), WPA2 (AES-CCMP), and WPA3 (SAE).
- ✅ **Frame Control Protected Bit = 1** indicates WEP encryption.
- ✅ **Forensic Tools:** Wireshark (dissector), Aircrack-ng (cracking), Kismet (detection).
- ✅ **Key insight:** Same key for auth + encryption = no separation of concerns → catastrophic.
- ✅ **Court/forensic relevance:** Capturing WEP traffic in PCAP files is trivial; cracking it is fully automated in aircrack-ng.
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** Always state the *equation*, the *frame diagram*, and the *weakness* in every WEP answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WPA and WPA-2 Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** WPA and WPA-2 Encryption # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access)** and **WPA2** are security protocols developed by the **Wi-Fi Alliance** to secure wireless computer networks. They were introduced as intermediate and long-term replacements for the vulnerable **WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy)** protocol, addressing its cryptographic weaknesses through stronger encryption, integrity verification, and improved key management mechanisms. - **WPA** (Ratified: **2003**) is an interim standard that uses the **TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol)** alongside the **RC4 stream cipher** and introduces the **Michael MIC (Message Integrity Check)** for data integrity. - **WPA2** (Ratified: **2004**) is the long-term standard mandated by the **IEEE 802.11i** amendment. It mandates the use of **AES-CCMP (Advanced Encryption Standard in Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol)**, providing robust **confidentiality, integrity, and replay protection**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 High-Yield Note:** For Network Forensics, the critical distinction is that **WPA/WPA2 traffic is encrypted at Layer 2**, meaning an investigator capturing raw 802.11 frames with a monitor-mode adapter must perform cryptographic recovery (handshake capture + dictionary/rainbow attack) to decrypt payloads for forensic analysis. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a postal system where every letter (network packet) is placed inside a locked steel box (encryption). The keys to these boxes are constantly changed after every few letters are delivered. - **WEP** is like giving every postal worker the **same universal key** forever. A snooping thief (hacker) can eventually collect enough locked boxes, notice a repeating pattern in the key generation, and forge their own key to open all future mail. - **WPA** is like a temporary fix: the postal service now **changes the key after every letter (per-packet key)** and adds a tamper-evident seal (the **Michael MIC**) so anyone who tries to flip a bit in transit is detected. It still uses an older, weaker locking mechanism (RC4), but the constantly changing keys make eavesdropping much harder. - **WPA2** is the **permanent overhaul**: the postal service throws away the old locks entirely and installs **military-grade AES-CCMP locks**. Each letter gets a unique, cryptographically derived key. Even with millions of letters, a cryptanalyst would need to break AES — currently computationally infeasible. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants > [!NOTE] > **Key WPA/WPA2 Cryptographic Parameters (KTU Board-Relevant Values):** > - **TKIP Temporal Key Length:** **128 bits** > - **TKIP MIC (Michael) Length:** **64 bits (8 bytes)** > - **AES-CCM Key Length:** **128 bits (mandatory)** > - **WPA2 Group Key Refresh:** Configurable; default interval = **0 (rekey per STA) or 3600 seconds** > - **PMK (Pairwise Master Key) Length:** **256 bits (32 bytes)** > - **PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) Length:** **512 bits (64 bytes)** — broken into KCK, KEK, TK, and MIC key sub-portions > - **4-Way Handshake Message Count:** **4 EAPOL-Key frames** > - **Group Key Handshake Message Count:** **2 EAPOL-Key frames** ## 1.4 Historical Context & Forensic Relevance | Protocol | Year | Cipher | Status | |---|---|---|---| | WEP | 1999 | RC4 (40/104-bit) | **Deprecated, Broken** | | WPA | 2003 | TKIP + RC4 + Michael MIC | **Legacy, Transitional** | | WPA2-Personal | 2004 | AES-CCMP | **Current Standard (Pre-2018)** | | WPA2-Enterprise | 2004 | AES-CCMP + 802.1X | **Enterprise Standard** | | WPA3 | 2018 | SAE + AES-GCMP | **Newest Standard** | From a **Network Forensics** perspective, WPA/WPA2 analysis is critical because: 1. The **4-Way EAPOL Handshake** is the forensic "key artifact" — capture it and the entire session can be cryptographically reconstructed. 2. Tools like **aircrack-ng, Wireshark, hashcat** are built around this handshake structure. 3. **WPA-PSK** is still vulnerable to offline **dictionary attacks** when a weak passphrase is used, because the PMK is derived deterministically from the SSID + passphrase. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 WPA Architecture — Detailed Component Breakdown WPA was designed as a **software/firmware upgrade** for existing WEP hardware. It could not mandate AES because legacy chipsets lacked the computational power. Therefore, WPA wraps WEP's RC4 engine in a stronger outer protocol: 1. **TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol)** — A wrapper around RC4 that: - Generates a **fresh 128-bit per-packet key** by mixing the temporal key, the transmitter MAC address, and a 48-bit packet sequence counter (TSC). - Defeats the **FMS (Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir) attack** that broke WEP. - Prevents IV reuse, which was WEP's fatal flaw. 2. **Michael MIC** — A 64-bit Message Integrity Code appended to each frame: - Computed using a **secret key** and the **source/destination MAC addresses**. - Catches **bit-flipping and forgery attacks**. - Trade-off: deliberately weak (only 2^20 operations) to fit legacy hardware, but uses **countermeasures** (TKIP re-keying after 2 MIC failures) to limit exposure. 3. **Extended 48-bit IV (TSC)** — Replaces WEP's 24-bit IV, vastly expanding the non-reuse space. 4. **Per-Packet Key Mixing** — Two-phase mixing function (Phase 1 + Phase 2) that produces a unique WEP seed for every packet. ## 2.2 WPA2 Architecture — AES-CCMP Breakdown WPA2 implements the full **IEEE 802.11i** robust security network (RSN). The cryptographic core is **AES-CCM**: - **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** — A 128-bit block cipher with **128/192/256-bit keys**. WPA2 mandates the **128-bit key** variant. - **CCM (Counter with CBC-MAC)** — A mode of operation that provides **both confidentiality (CTR mode)** and **integrity/authenticity (CBC-MAC)** in a single construct. - **CCMP (CCM Protocol)** — The 802.11i-specific wrapper that formats CCM operations over 802.11 frames, handling: - **PN (Packet Number)** — 48-bit, prevents replay. - **Nonce construction** — AAD (Additional Authenticated Data) = Priority + Address fields. - **Encrypted payload + 8-byte encrypted MIC**. ## 2.3 The Two Authentication Modes | Feature | WPA/WPA2-Personal (PSK) | WPA/WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X) | |---|---|---| | **Key Distribution** | Pre-shared passphrase | RADIUS / AAA server (EAP-TLS, PEAP, etc.) | | **Master Key** | PMK derived from PSK + SSID | PMK delivered by AS after EAP success | | **Forensic Difficulty** | Lower — offline PSK crack possible | Higher — requires AS logs + per-user certs | | **Use Case** | Home, small office | Corporate, university, government | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Board Examination Critical. Memorize the variable names, lengths, and bit-order conventions.** | Symbol | Definition | Length / Value | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | $K_{PSK}$ | Pre-Shared Key passphrase (plaintext) | 8–63 ASCII chars | WPA-PSK | | $SSID$ | Network Service Set Identifier | 1–32 octets | PMK derivation | | $PMK$ | Pairwise Master Key | **256 bits (32 bytes)** | Input to PTK generation | | $ANonce$ | Authenticator Nonce (sent by AP) | 256 bits | PTK derivation | | $SNonce$ | Supplicant Nonce (sent by STA) | 256 bits | PTK derivation | | $MAC_{AP}$ | Authenticator MAC address | 48 bits | PTK derivation | | $MAC_{STA}$ | Supplicant MAC address | 48 bits | PTK derivation | | $PTK$ | Pairwise Transient Key | **512 bits (64 bytes)** | Per-session data encryption | | $GTK$ | Group Temporal Key | 256 bits (max) | Broadcast/multicast | | $TK$ | Temporal Key (WPA) | **128 bits** | TKIP packet encryption | | $KCK$ | Key Confirmation Key | **128 bits (16 bytes)** | MIC computation on EAPOL | | $KEK$ | Key Encryption Key | **128 bits (16 bytes)** | GTK distribution encryption | | $MIC_{Key}$ | MIC key portion of PTK | 128 bits (WPA) | Michael MIC | | $TSC$ | TKIP Sequence Counter | **48 bits** | Per-packet key | | $PN$ | CCMP Packet Number | **48 bits** | Replay protection | | $IV$ | Initialization Vector (WEP-era) | 24 bits (broken) | WEP (legacy) | | $Nonce$ | AES-CCMP Nonce | **104 bits** | AES-CTR + CBC-MAC | ### 2.5 Core Engineering Equations (LaTeX) **Equation 1: PMK Derivation (WPA-PSK)** — Iterated HMAC-SHA1: $$ PMK = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{HMAC-SHA1},\ K_{PSK},\ SSID,\ 4096,\ 256\text{-bit}) $$ $$ PMK = \text{PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1}\left(\text{Passphrase},\ \text{SSID},\ 4096,\ 32\right) $$ **Equation 2: PTK Derivation (4-Way Handshake)** — Custom PRF-384: $$ PTK = \text{PRF-384}\left(PMK,\ \text{``Pairwise key expansion''},\ \min(MA\!C_{AP},MA\!C_{STA}) \Vert \max(MA\!C_{AP},MA\!C_{STA}) \Vert \min(ANonce,S\!Nonce) \Vert \max(ANonce,S\!Nonce)\right) $$ **Equation 3: PTK Concatenation Layout (64 bytes total)**: $$ PTK = KCK \Vert KEK \Vert TK \Vert \text{(MIC key or reserved)} $$ For **WPA** (TKIP): $$ PTK = KCK\ (16) \Vert KEK\ (16) \Vert TK\ (16) \Vert MIC_{Key}\ (16) $$ For **WPA2** (CCMP): $$ PTK = KCK\ (16) \Vert KEK\ (16) \Vert TK\ (16) \Vert \text{Reserved}\ (16) $$ **Equation 4: TKIP Per-Packet Key Mixing (Phase 2 Output)**: $$ \text{Per-Packet Key} = \text{Mix}_2\left(TA,\ TK,\ TSC\right) $$ where $TA$ is the transmitter MAC, $TK$ is the 128-bit temporal key, and $TSC$ is the 48-bit sequence counter. **Equation 5: AES-CCMP Nonce Construction (104 bits)**: $$ Nonce = \text{Priority}\ (8\text{ bits}) \Vert PN\ (48\text{ bits}) \Vert \text{A2 Address truncated to MSB}\ (48\text{ bits}) $$ **Equation 6: AES-CCMP Encrypted Frame Structure**: $$ E(\text{Frame}) = \text{CCM-AES-Encrypt}\left(TK,\ \text{Nonce},\ \text{Plaintext},\ \text{AAD}\right) $$ **Equation 7: Michael MIC Computation**: $$ \text{MIC} = \text{Michael}(MK,\ \text{DA},\ \text{SA},\ \text{Payload}) $$ where $MK$ is the Michael key derived from the upper 64 bits of the PTK temporal key portion. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: PMK from Passphrase (PSK Mode) This is the **foundation of all WPA-PSK forensics**. The investigator must reconstruct this to crack the network. ### Step 1: Input Validation The passphrase $K_{PSK}$ must be between **8 and 63 ASCII characters** (64-char SSID is the maximum allowed in 802.11i). The $SSID$ is the literal network name as broadcast in Beacon frames. $$ \text{Inputs} = \{K_{PSK},\ SSID\} $$ $$ \text{Where } 8 \le \vert K_{PSK} \vert \le 63 $$ ### Step 2: Apply PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) applies HMAC-SHA1 in an iterated fashion. The parameters are: - **Pseudo-Random Function (PRF):** HMAC-SHA1 - **Password:** $K_{PSK}$ (the passphrase) - **Salt:** $SSID$ (treated as raw bytes) - **Iteration count:** $4096$ (fixed by 802.11i) - **Output length:** 32 bytes (256 bits) $$ PMK_0 = \text{HMAC-SHA1}\left(K_{PSK},\ SSID \Vert 0x00000001\right) $$ ### Step 3: Iterate 4096 Times For $i = 1$ to $4095$: $$ PMK_i = \text{HMAC-SHA1}\left(K_{PSK},\ PMK_{i-1}\right) $$ ### Step 4: Final PMK $$ PMK = PMK_{4095} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Forensic Implication:** Because the PMK depends **only** on the passphrase and SSID, and the iteration count is fixed, two different stations connecting to the same WPA-PSK network will always derive the **same PMK**. This is why capturing the 4-Way Handshake once is sufficient for an offline dictionary attack. ### Step 5: Computational Cost Analysis Let $C$ be the computational cost in SHA-1 operations: $$ C_{PMK} = 4096 \times 1 = 4096\ \text{HMAC-SHA1 evaluations} $$ Using a modern GPU (e.g., NVIDIA RTX 4090 ≈ 16,000 MH/s for SHA-1): $$ T_{crack} = \frac{\vert \text{Dictionary} \vert}{1.6 \times 10^{10}}\ \text{seconds} $$ A 14-million-word dictionary thus cracks in: $$ T_{crack} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{10}} \approx 8.75 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{seconds} \approx 0.875\ \text{ms} $$ **Conclusion:** Weak passphrases are **trivially breakable** in modern forensic workflows. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: PTK from PMK (4-Way Handshake) ### Step 1: Nonce Exchange During the 4-Way Handshake, two nonces are exchanged: - $ANonce$ — generated by the **Authenticator (AP)**, sent in Message 1. - $SNonce$ — generated by the **Supplicant (Client)**, sent in Message 2. Both are 256-bit random numbers. ### Step 2: Construct the Data String for PRF The data string fed into the PRF must be assembled in a **specific order** defined by IEEE 802.11i. The order is critical for forensic reconstruction: $$ D = \min(MAC_{AP},\ MAC_{STA}) \Vert \max(MAC_{AP},\ MAC_{STA}) \Vert \min(ANonce,\ SNonce) \Vert \max(ANonce,\ SNonce) $$ The $\min/\max$ ordering uses **byte-wise lexicographic comparison** of the MAC addresses and nonces. This canonicalization ensures both AP and STA compute the **identical PTK**. ### Step 3: Apply PRF-384 The PRF-384 outputs exactly 48 bytes (384 bits), then is extended to 64 bytes (512 bits) by appending 16 more bytes: $$ PTK = \text{PRF-384}\left(PMK,\ \text{``Pairwise key expansion''},\ D\right) \Vert \text{PRF-128 extension} $$ The 16-byte extension is computed as: $$ \text{Extension} = \text{PRF-128}\left(PMK,\ \text{``Pairwise key expansion''},\ D \Vert 0x00\right) $$ ### Step 4: Split the PTK into Components For **WPA2-CCMP**: $$ \begin{aligned} KCK &= PTK[0 : 16]\ \text{(bytes 0–15)} \\ KEK &= PTK[16 : 32]\ \text{(bytes 16–31)} \\ TK &= PTK[32 : 48]\ \text{(bytes 32–47)} \\ \text{Reserved} &= PTK[48 : 64]\ \text{(bytes 48–63)} \end{aligned} $$ For **WPA-TKIP**: $$ \begin{aligned} KCK &= PTK[0 : 16] \\ KEK &= PTK[16 : 32] \\ TK &= PTK[32 : 48] \\ MIC_{Key} &= PTK[48 : 64] \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: 4-Way Handshake Message Flow (WPA2) ### Message 1: AP → STA (EAPOL-Key) $$ \text{AP} \xrightarrow{ANonce, \text{AP's MIC=0}} \text{STA} $$ The AP sends its nonce. The STA now has all inputs to compute the PTK. ### Message 2: STA → AP (EAPOL-Key) $$ \text{STA} \xrightarrow{SNonce, \text{MIC}_{KCK}(\text{Msg2})} \text{AP} $$ The STA computes: $$ PTK = \text{PRF}\left(PMK,\ \text{``Pairwise key expansion''},\ D\right) $$ Then computes the MIC over the entire EAPOL-Key frame using $KCK$ as the key. This **proves the STA knows the PMK** without revealing it. ### Message 3: AP → STA (EAPOL-Key) $$ \text{AP} \xrightarrow{ANonce, \text{GTK},\ \text{MIC}_{KCK}(\text{Msg3}),\ \text{Install}=1} \text{STA} $$ The AP: 1. Sends the (possibly new) $ANonce$. 2. Encrypts the $GTK$ using $KEK$ (AES-Key-Wrap). 3. Tells the STA to install the TK and transition to encrypted state. The STA verifies the MIC — **if it fails, the STA disconnects** (forensic indicator of a key mismatch or attack). ### Message 4: STA → AP (EAPOL-Key) $$ \text{STA} \xrightarrow{\text{MIC}_{KCK}(\text{Msg4})} \text{AP} $$ Final acknowledgment. The AP verifies the MIC and the secure channel is now fully established. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Vulnerability Comparison | Attack Vector | WEP | WPA-TKIP | WPA2-CCMP | |---|---|---|---| | **FMS / Statistical RC4** | Trivial (minutes) | Defeated by per-pkt keys | N/A (no RC4) | | **Replay Attack** | Trivial (IV reuse) | Defeated by TSC | Defeated by PN | | **Offline Dictionary (PSK)** | N/A | Feasible after 4WHS capture | Feasible after 4WHS capture | | **KRACK (Key Reinstallation)** | N/A | Vulnerable (CVE-2017-13082) | Vulnerable pre-patch (CVE-2017-13080) | | **Brute Force AES-128** | N/A | N/A | Computationally infeasible | | **Hole 196 (GTK abuse)** | N/A | Mitigated | Mitigated | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** In KTU valuation, students often incorrectly state that "WPA2 is uncrackable." The correct forensic answer is: **"WPA2-PSK is cryptographically secure against key recovery, but the weak link is the human-chosen passphrase, which is susceptible to offline dictionary attack after capturing the 4-Way Handshake."** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: WPA/WPA2 Cryptographic Stack Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[802.11 MAC Layer Frame] --> B[Security Layer Selection] B --> C{RSN Information Element} C -->|WEP| D[RC4 + 24-bit IV] C -->|WPA-TKIP| E[TKIP + RC4 + Michael MIC] C -->|WPA2-CCMP| F[AES-128 CCM Mode] D --> G[Ethernet Frame to Upper Layers] E --> G F --> G E --> E1[Per-Packet Key Mixing Phase 1] E --> E2[Per-Packet Key Mixing Phase 2] E1 --> E2 E2 --> E3[RC4 Stream Cipher] E3 --> G F --> F1[CCM Counter Mode - Encryption] F --> F2[CCM CBC-MAC - Integrity] F1 --> G F2 --> G ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: 4-Way EAPOL Handshake Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AP as Authenticator AP participant STA as Supplicant Client Note over AP,STA: Both sides hold PMK AP->>AP: Generate ANonce (256-bit) AP->>STA: Message 1 - EAPOL-Key with ANonce, MIC=0 Note over STA: Compute PTK = PRF(PMK, ANonce, SNonce, MACs) STA->>STA: Generate SNonce (256-bit) STA->>AP: Message 2 - EAPOL-Key with SNonce + MIC over frame Note over AP: Verify MIC using KCK
Compute PTK independently AP->>STA: Message 3 - EAPOL-Key + Encrypted GTK + Install Flag + MIC Note over STA: Verify MIC
Decrypt GTK with KEK
Install TK STA->>AP: Message 4 - EAPOL-Key with MIC (Final ACK) Note over AP: Verify MIC
Secure channel ESTABLISHED AP->>STA: Encrypted Data Traffic (using TK) STA->>AP: Encrypted Data Traffic (using TK) ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: PTK Derivation Data Flow (Block Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR PMK[PMK 256-bit] --> PRF[PRF-384 Function] ANonce[ANonce 256-bit] --> SORT1[Sort with SNonce] SNonce[SNonce 256-bit] --> SORT1 MACAP[MAC AP 48-bit] --> SORT2[Sort with MAC STA] MACSTA[MAC STA 48-bit] --> SORT2 SORT1 --> CONCAT[Concatenate Strings] SORT2 --> CONCAT LABEL[String: 'Pairwise key expansion'] --> PRF CONCAT --> PRF PRF --> PTK[PTK 512-bit] PTK --> SPLIT[Split into 4 sub-keys] SPLIT --> KCK_OUT[KCK 128-bit] SPLIT --> KEK_OUT[KEK 128-bit] SPLIT --> TK_OUT[Temporal Key 128-bit] SPLIT --> MIC_OUT[MIC or Reserved 128-bit] KCK_OUT --> HMAC[HMAC-SHA1 for MIC verification] KEK_OUT --> AESKW[AES Key Wrap for GTK delivery] TK_OUT --> ENCRYPT[Encrypt user data frames] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Forensic Attack Workflow (PSK Cracking) ```mermaid graph TD START[Investigator has monitor-mode adapter] --> SCAN[Scan for target SSID] SCAN --> DEAUTH[Optionally send deauth frames] DEAUTH --> CAPTURE[Capture 4-Way Handshake] CAPTURE --> HANDSHAKE{Handshake complete?} HANDSHAKE -->|No| RETRY[Wait or deauth again] HANDSHAKE -->|Yes| SAVE[Save .cap file with EAPOL frames] SAVE --> SSID[Extract SSID from Beacon] SSID --> DICT[Load dictionary of candidate passphrases] DICT --> LOOP[For each passphrase] LOOP --> COMPUTE[Compute PMK = PBKDF2 pass, SSID, 4096, 32] COMPUTE --> VERIFY[Test against captured handshake MIC] VERIFY --> MATCH{PMK matches?} MATCH -->|No| LOOP MATCH -->|Yes| KEY[Recovered PSK passphrase] KEY --> DECRYPT[Derive PTK and decrypt all session traffic] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: AES-CCMP Frame Encryption Pipeline ```mermaid graph TD MSDU[Plaintext MSDU] --> PAD[Pad to AES block multiple] PAD --> CTR[AES-CTR Mode Encryption with TK] NONCE[Nonce: Priority 8-bit + PN 48-bit + A2 MSB 48-bit] --> CTR CTR --> CIPHERTEXT[Encrypted Payload] MSDU --> CBCMAC[AES-CBC-MAC over AAD and Payload] NONCE --> CBCMAC CBCMAC --> MIC[MIC 8-byte tag] MIC --> ENCMIC[Encrypt MIC using AES-CTR with same key+nonce+1] ENCMIC --> ENCMICOUT[Encrypted MIC appended to frame] HEADER[802.11 Header with CCMP PN] --> FINAL[CCMP Encrypted MPDU] CIPHERTEXT --> FINAL ENCMICOUT --> FINAL ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** ### Question 1: Differentiate between WPA and WPA2 encryption protocols. List the cipher suite used in each. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | WPA | WPA2 | |---|---|---| | **Standard** | Interim (Wi-Fi Alliance, 2003) | Full IEEE 802.11i (2004) | | **Cipher** | RC4 (legacy compatible) | AES-128 (mandatory) | | **Integrity** | Michael MIC (8-byte) | CBC-MAC inside CCM (8-byte) | | **Key Refresh** | Per-packet TKIP | Per-session + per-packet PN | | **Hardware** | Software-upgradable | Requires AES-capable chipset | | **Replay Protection** | 48-bit TSC | 48-bit PN | > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for correct cipher identification, 1 mark for integrity mechanism, 1 mark for hardware compatibility comparison. --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** ### Question 2: What is the role of the 4-Way Handshake in WPA2? Why is it critical for forensic analysis? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The 4-Way Handshake serves to **(1 mark)** establish fresh session keys (PTK, GTK) between the AP and client without transmitting them in cleartext, using the PMK as a shared secret. It is critical for forensic analysis **(1 mark)** because capturing all four EAPOL-Key frames allows an investigator to: - Derive the PTK (and thus the session key) if the PMK/PSK is known. - Conduct **offline dictionary attacks** on the PSK by verifying candidate PMKs against the captured MIC values. - Reconstruct and decrypt the entire encrypted data session. **(1 mark)** The handshake is the "forensic gold" — without it, WPA2 traffic is computationally unrecoverable even with a known PSK in real-time. ## 5.2 Part B: Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Internal Choice:** Answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete 4-Way EAPOL Handshake** mechanism used in WPA2. Detail the cryptographic contents of each of the four messages and clearly identify the role of **KCK, KEK, and TK**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **PMK and PTK** mathematically for a WPA2-PSK network. Show the inputs, the PRF construction, and explain why the **PMK is deterministic** given a passphrase and SSID. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A (a): **Step 1: Pre-conditions (1 Mark)** Both AP and Supplicant must possess the **PMK** before the handshake begins. In PSK mode: $$ PMK = \text{PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1}(K_{PSK},\ SSID,\ 4096,\ 32) $$ **Step 2: Message 1 — AP → STA (1 Mark)** The AP generates a 256-bit random nonce $ANonce$ and sends: $$ Msg_1 = \{\text{EAPOL-Key} : ANonce,\ Key\_{Info} = \text{Pairwise},\ MIC = 0\} $$ **Step 3: Message 2 — STA → AP (2 Marks)** The STA generates $SNonce$, computes $PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{``Pairwise key expansion''}, \min(MA\!C) \Vert \max(MA\!C) \Vert \min(Nonce) \Vert \max(Nonce))$, then sends $SNonce$ plus a MIC computed over the entire EAPOL-Key frame using $KCK$ (the first 16 bytes of PTK). This **authenticates the STA as the holder of the PMK**. **Step 4: Message 3 — AP → STA (2 Marks)** The AP independently computes the same $PTK$, verifies the $KCK$ MIC on $Msg_2$, then sends $Msg_3$ containing the $GTK$ encrypted with $KEK$ (AES Key-Wrap), an updated $ANonce$, an "Install" flag, and a MIC computed using $KCK$ over $Msg_3$. The STA decrypts the $GTK$ using $KEK$ and installs the $TK$ for data encryption. **Step 5: Message 4 — STA → AP (1 Mark)** The STA sends a final MIC-bearing $Msg_4$ as confirmation. The secure channel is now fully established; subsequent data frames use the $TK$. #### Model Solution for Question A (b): **Step 1: PMK Derivation (3 Marks)** Given passphrase $K_{PSK}$ and $SSID$: $$ PMK_0 = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K_{PSK},\ SSID \Vert \text{0x00000001}) $$ $$ PMK_i = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K_{PSK},\ PMK_{i-1}),\ \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, 4095 $$ $$ \boxed{PMK = PMK_{4095}} $$ **Step 2: Determinism Argument (2 Marks)** Since $K_{PSK}$ and $SSID$ are fixed for a given network, the PMK is **deterministic** — both AP and STA compute the same value byte-for-byte. The 4096 iterations and the 256-bit output length are fixed by IEEE 802.11i. This determinism is the **forensic attack surface**: an attacker can pre-compute PMK candidates for a dictionary of passphrases and test them against captured handshakes. **Step 3: PTK Derivation (2 Marks)** $$ D = \min(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA}) \Vert \max(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA}) \Vert \min(ANonce, SNonce) \Vert \max(ANonce, SNonce) $$ $$ \boxed{PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK,\ \text{``Pairwise key expansion''},\ D)} $$ The PTK is split into: $$ KCK\ (128) \Vert KEK\ (128) \Vert TK\ (128) \Vert \text{Reserved}\ (128) $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **AES-CCMP encryption process** in WPA2. Show the complete construction of the **CCM Nonce**, the role of **CTR mode** for confidentiality, and **CBC-MAC** for integrity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **forensic implications of the KRACK attack (CVE-2017-13080/13082)** on WPA2. What message of the 4-Way Handshake does it exploit, and how can a forensic investigator detect evidence of exploitation? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B (a): **Step 1: CCMP Frame Structure (1 Mark)** A CCMP-encrypted MPDU contains: - 802.11 MAC Header (unencrypted) - CCMP Header (8 bytes): **PN[0:6]** + Key ID - Encrypted Payload - Encrypted MIC (8 bytes) **Step 2: Nonce Construction (2 Marks)** The 104-bit CCM nonce is constructed as: $$ Nonce = \underbrace{Priority}_{8\ \text{bits}} \ \Vert\ \underbrace{PN}_{48\ \text{bits}} \ \Vert\ \underbrace{A2\ \text{MSB}}_{48\ \text{bits}} $$ where $A2$ is the transmitter address and $PN$ is the per-frame packet number. **Step 3: Counter Mode Encryption (2 Marks)** AES-CTR encrypts the plaintext using counter blocks $A_i$ derived from the nonce: $$ C_j = P_j \oplus \text{AES}_K(A_j) $$ where $A_j = \text{Nonce} \Vert j$ (with $j$ being the 16-bit counter starting at 0 for the first block and 1 for the MIC block). This provides **confidentiality**. **Step 4: CBC-MAC Integrity (2 Marks)** AES-CBC-MAC computes a tag over the **AAD** (Address fields + Priority) and the **plaintext** using AES in CBC mode with the same key. The first 8 bytes of the final CBC-MAC output are used as the MIC, then **encrypted** with the same AES-CTR keystream to produce the encrypted MIC appended to the frame. This provides **integrity and authenticity**. #### Model Solution for Question B (b): **Step 1: KRACK Attack Mechanism (2 Marks)** The **Key Reinstallation Attack (KRACK)**, disclosed by Vanhoef in 2017, exploits the handling of **Message 3 of the 4-Way Handshake**. If the AP retransmits Message 3 (due to a lost Message 4 from the STA), the STA will reinstall the same TK, **resetting the transmit/receive packet numbers (PN, TSC, replay counters) to zero**. **Step 2: The Critical Vulnerability (2 Marks)** The IEEE 802.11i standard allows retransmission of Message 3 without explicitly requiring nonce reset, but most vendor implementations did reset the nonce. This means: - The same $TK$ is reused with reset $PN$ values. - An attacker can replay old captured frames that would have valid PNs. - Combined with **packet-injection** (since the encryption is now predictable), full session hijacking and decryption become possible. **Step 3: Forensic Indicators (2 Marks)** A forensic investigator can detect evidence of KRACK exploitation by: - Searching captured traffic for **multiple Message 3 retransmissions** (anomalous handshake pattern). - Identifying **packet number resets** in subsequent encrypted frames. - Correlating timestamps to detect if the same $TK$ was used across multiple non-incremental PN sequences. - Checking AP firmware/STA driver versions against the CVE-2017-13080/13082 vulnerability database. **Step 4: Mitigation (1 Mark)** Patches enforce **monotonically increasing nonces** across all Message 3 retransmissions, preventing the reinstall. Modern WPA3 uses **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** to eliminate the 4-Way Handshake vulnerability class entirely. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** "WPA" with "WPA-Enterprise." KTU strictly distinguishes **WPA/WPA2-Personal (PSK)** from **WPA/WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X/RADIUS)**. Mixing them up will cost 1–2 marks. > 2. **Always specify cipher names** fully: write "AES-128-CCM" not just "AES." The cipher suite selector string in the RSN IE is `00-0F-AC-04`. > 3. **Do NOT claim "WPA2 is uncrackable"** in your exam. Always qualify: cryptographically secure, but **susceptible to PSK dictionary attacks** and **KRACK** (pre-patch). > 4. **For PTK length**, students often write 384 bits — this is wrong. PRF-384 outputs 384 bits, but the **PTK is 512 bits** in WPA2 (a 128-bit extension is appended). > 5. **Diagram marks require arrows + labels** for every cryptographic flow. A diagram without direction/labels is treated as "incomplete" by KTU examiners and receives **partial credit only**. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **WPA/WPA2 Forensics — Final Pre-Exam Quick-Fire Facts:** - [x] **WPA = TKIP + RC4 + Michael MIC** (interim, software upgrade for WEP hardware). - [x] **WPA2 = AES-128 + CCMP (CTR + CBC-MAC)** (full 802.11i compliance). - [x] **PMK = 256 bits**, derived via **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1(pass, SSID, 4096, 32)**. - [x] **PTK = 512 bits** = KCK(16) ‖ KEK(16) ‖ TK(16) ‖ Reserved/MIC-Key(16). - [x] **GTK = up to 256 bits**, used for broadcast/multicast, delivered in Message 3. - [x] **4-Way Handshake** = 4 EAPOL-Key frames; ANonce + SNonce + MACs are inputs to PRF. - [x] **KCK** computes MICs on handshake messages; **KEK** encrypts GTK via AES-Key-Wrap; **TK** encrypts data. - [x] **AES-CCMP Nonce** = Priority(8) ‖ PN(48) ‖ A2-MSB(48) = **104 bits total**. - [x] **MIC length** = 8 bytes (Michael) for WPA; 8 bytes (CBC-MAC truncated) for WPA2. - [x] **Forensic toolchain:** aircrack-ng (capture + crack), Wireshark (analysis), hashcat (GPU cracking). - [x] **KRACK (2017)** targets Message 3 retransmission → resets packet numbers → enables replay. - [x] **Personal mode = PSK** (vulnerable to offline dictionary); **Enterprise mode = 802.1X + RADIUS** (requires AS compromise). - [x] **Sequence counters:** TSC (48-bit) in WPA, PN (48-bit) in WPA2 — prevent replay attacks. - [x] **WPA2-PSK is "broken" only by weak passphrases**, not by AES. Use a **>20-character random passphrase** for real security. - [x] **WPA3 successor** uses SAE (Dragonfly handshake) — eliminates offline dictionary attack and KRACK-style vulnerabilities. - [x] **Forensic investigator's first action:** `airodump-ng` to capture the 4-Way Handshake into a `.cap` file.

topics.title: Attacks on Wireless Networks: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Attacks on Wireless Networks: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack** in the context of wireless networks is a sophisticated, active eavesdropping and session hijacking cyberattack where an adversary covertly positions themselves logically and physically between two communicating wireless endpoints — typically a client device (STA) and an Access Point (AP) — without the knowledge of either party. The attacker secretly intercepts, relays, and potentially alters all communications passing through the manipulated relay point, thereby breaching the fundamental pillars of information security: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Authentication** (the CIA triad).

In the IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) domain, MITM attacks exploit the **shared-medium nature of the radio frequency (RF) spectrum**, the lack of mutual authentication in many legacy protocols (e.g., WEP, WPA), and the **broadcast nature of wireless frames** (specifically management and control frames such as `Beacon`, `Probe Request`, `Probe Response`, `Deauthentication`, and `Disassociation`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT864 - Module 4):**
> MITM attacks on wireless networks are categorized under **Active Wireless Attacks**. The syllabus explicitly requires students to identify attack vectors, understand packet-level forensics of compromised sessions, and articulate the forensic artifacts (PCAP traces, log anomalies, SSID spoofing) that prove an attack occurred.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Postal Worker"

Imagine you are sending sealed letters between two people, **Alice** and **Bob**, through a trusted postal worker. A malicious actor, **Eve**, kidnaps the postal worker and impersonates him. From now on:
- Alice hands her letter to "the postal worker" (actually Eve).
- Eve opens the letter, reads it, optionally modifies the content, reseals it, and hands it to Bob.
- Bob receives a letter, believes it came from Alice, and replies to "the postal worker" (Eve).
- Eve intercepts Bob's reply, reads/modifies it, and forwards it to Alice.

Neither Alice nor Bob realizes that **Eve is silently reading and rewriting their private conversation**. In wireless networks, **Eve is the rogue access point**, and the "letters" are **802.11 frames and upper-layer TCP/IP packets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A wireless MITM is uniquely dangerous because the attacker can conduct it **passively** (passive sniffing) before escalating to an **active** relay position — making initial detection extremely challenging for standard intrusion detection systems (IDS).

### 1.3 Why Wireless MITM is Fundamentally Different from Wired MITM

| Property | Wired MITM | Wireless MITM |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical access required** | Yes (cable tap) | No (just RF proximity) |
| **Attack surface** | Switch CAM table, ARP | 802.11 frames, beacons, SSIDs |
| **Stealth** | Moderate | Very High (radio is invisible) |
| **Coverage** | Local segment only | Entire BSS (Basic Service Set) |
| **Mutual authentication** | Often present (802.1X) | Frequently absent in WPA-PSK |

### 1.4 Critical Wireless MITM Attack Vectors

The following attack vectors constitute the **canonical MITM taxonomy** demanded by the KTU 2024 Network Forensics syllabus:

1. **Evil Twin / Rogue Access Point Attack**
2. **Karma / Mana Attack (Preferred Network List exploitation)**
3. **Deauthentication / Disassociation Frame Injection Attack**
4. **ARP Spoofing / ARP Cache Poisoning** (Layer 2 relay)
5. **DNS Spoofing / DNS Cache Poisoning** (Layer 7 relay)
6. **SSL/TLS Stripping Attack** (Downgrade attack)
7. **Probe Request Sniffing and Forced Reconnection**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Many students believe MITM requires breaking encryption. **This is FALSE.** The attacker simply bypasses encryption by impersonating the legitimate endpoint. The encryption protects the data **inside** the tunnel, but the attacker terminates one end of the tunnel themselves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Wireless MITM Attack

A wireless MITM attack unfolds in **five sequential phases**, each leaving distinct forensic artifacts:

#### Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (Passive)
- Attacker places the wireless interface into **monitor mode** (RFMON).
- Captures all 802.11 frames in the air using tools such as `Kismet`, `Wireshark`, or `Airodump-ng`.
- Identifies target **BSSID**, **SSID**, **channel**, **encryption type** (WEP/WPA/WPA2), and **client MAC addresses**.
- Forensic artifact: PCAP file containing beacon frames, probe requests/responses.

#### Phase 2 — Rogue AP Deployment (Active)
- Attacker hosts a **rogue access point** on a different channel broadcasting the **same SSID** and **same BSSID** (or a cloned BSSID) as the legitimate AP.
- The rogue AP is configured with stronger signal strength, often on a different 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz channel.
- Forensic artifact: New AP appears in client association tables; sudden channel change in logs.

#### Phase 3 — Forced Deauthentication (Active Injection)
- Attacker injects **802.11 management frames** — specifically **Deauthentication (`Type=0, Subtype=12`)** or **Disassociation (`Type=0, Subtype=10`)** frames — sourced from the legitimate AP's MAC address (MAC spoofing).
- Clients are forced to disconnect from the legitimate AP and begin scanning for a new connection.
- Forensic artifact: Spike in `Deauth` frames in Wireshark capture; client logs show sudden disconnection.

#### Phase 4 — Victim Reconnection to Rogue AP
- Due to the **aggressive reconnection logic** built into 802.11 state machines, the victim STA re-associates with whichever AP offers the strongest signal — which is now the attacker's rogue AP.
- The attacker captures the **4-way WPA handshake** (if WPA2-PSK) and can later attempt an offline dictionary attack.
- Forensic artifact: New BSSID in client's association table; encryption key handshake captured.

#### Phase 5 — Relay and Exploitation
- Attacker forwards victim traffic to the legitimate AP (or Internet) while performing:
  - **SSL stripping** (downgrade HTTPS → HTTP).
  - **DNS spoofing** (redirect domains to attacker-controlled servers).
  - **Session hijacking** (steal cookies, JWT tokens).
  - **Payload injection** (inject malicious JavaScript, BeEF framework).
- Forensic artifact: HTTP traffic on ports 80 instead of 443; anomalous DNS responses; unusual session durations.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations of Wireless MITM Detection

> [!NOTE]
> While MITM attacks are not traditionally quantitative, modern forensic detection relies on **statistical metrics**. The following formulae are **KTU 2024 high-yield** for both theory and numerical problems.

#### 2.2.1 Deauthentication Frame Anomaly Score (DFAS)

The **Deauthentication Frame Anomaly Score** is a forensic metric used to flag a potential MITM attack by quantifying the abnormal rate of deauth frames in a wireless capture.

$$
\text{DFAS} = \frac{N_{deauth}}{T_{capture} \times N_{STA}}
$$

Where:
- $N_{deauth}$ = number of deauthentication frames observed
- $T_{capture}$ = total capture duration (in seconds)
- $N_{STA}$ = number of active wireless stations

**Interpretation:**
- A normal Wi-Fi environment has $\text{DFAS} \approx 0.01$ to $0.05$ (deauths per second per station).
- An active MITM attack produces $\text{DFAS} \geq 0.5$, often spiking to **10–100** during burst injection.

#### 2.2.2 Signal Strength Differential (SSD)

Attackers typically boost rogue AP signal strength to lure victims. The **Signal Strength Differential** between two APs broadcasting the same SSID is a key forensic indicator.

$$
\Delta\text{RSSI} = \text{RSSI}_{rogue} - \text{RSSI}_{legitimate}
$$

Where $\text{RSSI}$ is measured in **dBm** (decibels relative to 1 milliwatt).

- $\Delta\text{RSSI} > 0$ dBm (e.g., +5 dBm to +15 dBm) strongly suggests a rogue AP.
- Legitimate AP roaming differentials rarely exceed $\pm 3$ dBm.

#### 2.2.3 MAC Address Entropy

A normal BSSID uses a MAC address from a single OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) prefix. Multiple BSSIDs with the same SSID but **different OUIs** indicate an evil twin.

$$
H_{OUI} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

Where $p_i$ is the proportion of observed BSSIDs from OUI prefix $i$.

- $H_{OUI} = 0$ → All APs from one manufacturer (normal).
- $H_{OUI} > 0.5$ → Likely evil twin attack in progress.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Purpose | Typical Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DFAS} = \dfrac{N_{deauth}}{T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}}$ | Detect deauth injection | $\geq 0.5$ = Attack |
| 2 | $\Delta\text{RSSI} = \text{RSSI}_{rogue} - \text{RSSI}_{legitimate}$ | Detect rogue AP signal anomaly | $> +3$ dBm = Suspicious |
| 3 | $H_{OUI} = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Detect SSID-spoofing (evil twin) | $> 0.5$ = Multi-vendor BSSID |
| 4 | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | **Friis transmission equation** — used in signal strength analysis | Free-space path loss |
| 5 | $N_{frames/sec} = \dfrac{C_{channel}}{S_{frame}}$ | Maximum 802.11 frame injection rate | $C_{channel} \leq 54$ Mbps (802.11g) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friis Transmission Equation** ($P_r$) is the foundational radio propagation model. It explains why a closer attacker with high-gain antennas can dominate the legitimate AP's signal — the central enabler of evil twin attacks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **network forensics and enterprise security operations centers (SOCs)**, MITM detection algorithms are deployed in:
- **Wireless Intrusion Prevention Systems (WIPS)** — e.g., Cisco Adaptive WIPS, Aruba RFProtect.
- **SIEM correlation engines** — Splunk, ELK Stack.
- **Forensic investigation suites** — Wireshark, Xplico, NetworkMiner.
- **Deauthentication detection** — `aircrack-ng`, `tshark` filters (`wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x0c`).

The ability to mathematically quantify an attack's signature (DFAS, SSD, OUI entropy) transforms **forensic investigation** from subjective log-reading into **evidence-based legal prosecution**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Deauthentication Frame Anomaly Score (DFAS)

The DFAS is derived from the **Poisson process** assumption of legitimate deauth events. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Step 1 — Model legitimate deauth events as a Poisson process.**

In a healthy Wi-Fi network, deauthentication frames are **rare** and **independent**. The probability of observing $k$ deauth frames in time $t$ follows:

$$
P(k, t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}
$$

Where $\lambda$ is the legitimate deauth rate (frames per second per station).

**Step 2 — Compute the expected count for a capture window.**

$$
E[N_{deauth}] = \lambda \cdot T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the normalized anomaly score as the ratio of observed to expected.**

$$
\text{DFAS} = \frac{N_{deauth}^{observed}}{\lambda \cdot T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical substitution.**

Empirically, in a healthy 802.11 network, $\lambda \approx 0.01$ deauths/sec/station. To obtain a unitless score, we redefine DFAS to use the empirical baseline:

$$
\text{DFAS} = \frac{N_{deauth}^{observed}}{T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}}
$$

This gives us the **raw deauth rate per station**, which is then compared to the threshold $\geq 0.5$.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Suppose a forensic investigator captures a 60-second PCAP file and observes 450 deauthentication frames involving 10 active stations.

$$
\text{DFAS} = \frac{N_{deauth}}{T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}} = \frac{450}{60 \cdot 10} = \frac{450}{600} = 0.75 \text{ deauths/sec/station}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $0.75 > 0.5$, this is **strong forensic evidence** of a deauthentication-based MITM attack.

### 3.2 Derivation of the OUI Entropy for Evil Twin Detection

**Step 1 — Define the OUI distribution.**

Let $n$ = total number of observed BSSIDs advertising the same SSID. Group BSSIDs by their first 24 bits (OUI prefix). Let $n_i$ = number of BSSIDs from OUI prefix $i$.

$$
p_i = \frac{n_i}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's entropy formula.**

$$
H_{OUI} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

**Step 3 — Worked numerical example.**

An investigator's Kismet scan reveals 4 APs all advertising the SSID `"KTU_WiFi"`:
- BSSID `A0:63:91:11:22:33` (OUI: A0:63:91 — Cisco)
- BSSID `A0:63:91:44:55:66` (OUI: A0:63:91 — Cisco)
- BSSID `00:1A:2B:77:88:99` (OUI: 00:1A:2B — Apple)
- BSSID `B8:27:EB:AA:BB:CC` (OUI: B8:27:EB — Raspberry Pi)

Grouped by OUI:
- Cisco: $n_1 = 2 \Rightarrow p_1 = 2/4 = 0.5$
- Apple: $n_2 = 1 \Rightarrow p_2 = 1/4 = 0.25$
- Raspberry Pi: $n_3 = 1 \Rightarrow p_3 = 1/4 = 0.25$

$$
H_{OUI} = -[(0.5)\log_2(0.5) + (0.25)\log_2(0.25) + (0.25)\log_2(0.25)]
$$

Evaluating each term:
- $(0.5)\log_2(0.5) = 0.5 \times (-1) = -0.5$
- $(0.25)\log_2(0.25) = 0.25 \times (-2) = -0.5$
- $(0.25)\log_2(0.25) = 0.25 \times (-2) = -0.5$

$$
H_{OUI} = -[-0.5 + (-0.5) + (-0.5)] = -[-1.5] = 1.5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Interpretation:** $H_{OUI} = 1.5 > 0.5$ — **Strong forensic indicator of an Evil Twin attack**. Three different vendors cannot legitimately host the same SSID in a single BSS without enterprise controller coordination.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense

| Attack Vector | Protocol Layer | 802.11 Frame Type | Forensic Artifact | Primary Defense |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Evil Twin** | Layer 2 | Beacon, Probe Response | Multiple BSSIDs same SSID, high $H_{OUI}$ | 802.1X EAP, WIPS |
| **Karma Attack** | Layer 2 | Probe Request, Probe Response | Client connects to non-broadcast SSID | Disable PNL, 802.11w |
| **Deauth Injection** | Layer 2 | Deauth (`0x0C`) | High DFAS, broadcast destination | 802.11w (PMF) |
| **ARP Spoofing** | Layer 2/3 | ARP Reply | Duplicate IP-MAC mappings | DAI, ARP inspection |
| **DNS Spoofing** | Layer 7 | UDP/53 | Unsolicited DNS replies, low TTL | DNSSEC, DoH |
| **SSL Stripping** | Layer 7 | TCP/80 HTTP | HTTP on login pages | HSTS, HPKP |
| **Probe Sniffing** | Layer 2 | Probe Request | Clear-text PNL data | Disable PNL |

### 3.4 Comprehensive Countermeasure Derivation

The **defense-in-depth** model for wireless MITM is built on **four concentric layers**, each derivable from the CIA triad:

1. **Authentication Layer (802.1X / EAP-TLS)** — replaces shared key with per-user certificates.
2. **Encryption Layer (WPA3-Enterprise, GCMP-256)** — provides 192-bit security per IEEE 802.11i-2004 amendment.
3. **Integrity Layer (802.11w — Protected Management Frames, PMF)** — cryptographically signs deauth/disassociation frames, neutralizing the entire Phase 3 of the MITM attack.
4. **Detection Layer (WIPS + DFAS monitoring)** — statistical anomaly detection as derived above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** The single most impactful defense against wireless MITM is **802.11w (Protected Management Frames)**. Without it, an attacker can always force a victim off the legitimate AP using forged deauth frames — irrespective of the WPA2/WPA3 encryption strength.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wireless MITM Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Victim Client STA] -->|Step 1: Normal Association| B[Legitimate AP]
    B -->|Step 2: Encrypted Traffic| C[Internet Gateway]
    
    A2[Attacker in Monitor Mode] -->|Step 3: Captures Beacons + Probes| D[Reconnaissance Phase]
    D -->|Step 4: Identifies SSID, BSSID, Channel| E[Evil Twin Setup]
    
    E -->|Step 5: Broadcasts Forged Deauth Frames| B
    B -.->|Step 6: Client Disconnected| A
    
    E -->|Step 7: Hosts Rogue AP on Same SSID| F[Rogue AP Evil Twin]
    
    A -->|Step 8: Reconnects to Stronger Signal| F
    F -->|Step 9: Captures 4-Way WPA Handshake| G[Session Hijacking Module]
    
    G -->|Step 10: SSL Strip / DNS Spoof| H[Payload Manipulation]
    H -->|Step 11: Forwards to Internet| C
    C -->|Step 12: Legitimate Responses| H
    H -->|Step 13: Modified Responses to Victim| A
    
    I[Forensic Investigator] -->|Step 14: Analyzes PCAP| J[Detection Engine]
    J -->|Computes DFAS, SSD, H-OUI| K[Anomaly Score]
    K -->|Threshold Breach| L[MitM Attack Confirmed]
```

### 4.2 MITM Attack Phase Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Victim STA
    participant L as Legitimate AP
    participant M as MITM Attacker
    participant I as Internet Server
    
    Note over V,L: Phase 0 - Normal Operation
    V->>L: 802.11 Association + Auth
    L->>V: Encrypted Data Frames
    L->>I: HTTPS Request
    
    Note over M: Phase 1 - Reconnaissance
    M->>M: Capture 802.11 Beacons (Monitor Mode)
    
    Note over M,L: Phase 2 - Forced Disconnection
    M->>V: Forged Deauth Frame (spoofed L BSSID)
    V->>V: Disassociates from L
    
    Note over M: Phase 3 - Rogue AP Activation
    M->>V: Beacon Frame (Cloned SSID, Stronger RSSI)
    V->>M: Re-Association Request
    M->>V: 4-Way Handshake (Captured)
    
    Note over M,I: Phase 4 - Relay + Manipulation
    V->>M: HTTPS Request
    M->>M: SSL Strip (HTTPS to HTTP)
    M->>I: HTTP Request
    I->>M: HTTP Response
    M->>M: Inject Malicious Payload
    M->>V: Modified HTTP Response
```

### 4.3 Forensic Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suspected MitM Incident] --> B[Acquire Wireless PCAP]
    B --> C[Filter Management Frames]
    C --> D{Deauth Spike?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Compute DFAS]
    D -->|No| F[Analyze Beacons]
    
    E --> G[Extract BSSID List]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute OUI Entropy]
    H --> I{H-OUI > 0.5?}
    
    I -->|Yes| J[Map RSSI Per BSSID]
    I -->|No| K[Check Probe Requests]
    
    J --> L{Rogue Delta RSSI > 3 dBm?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Evil Twin Confirmed]
    L -->|No| N[Legitimate Roaming]
    
    M --> O[Generate Forensic Report]
    K --> P[Analyze Channel Hopping]
    P --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wireless Network] --> B[Layer 1: WPA3-Enterprise]
    B --> C[Layer 2: 802.1X EAP-TLS]
    C --> D[Layer 3: 802.11w PMF]
    D --> E[Layer 4: WIPS Monitoring]
    E --> F[Layer 5: HSTS + DNSSEC]
    F --> G[Hardened Wireless Network]
    
    subgraph Threats Blocked
        T1[Evil Twin]
        T2[Deauth Injection]
        T3[ARP Spoofing]
        T4[DNS Spoofing]
        T5[SSL Strip]
    end
    
    T1 -.->|Blocked by| C
    T2 -.->|Blocked by| D
    T3 -.->|Blocked by| B
    T4 -.->|Blocked by| F
    T5 -.->|Blocked by| F
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack in the context of wireless networks. List any two attack vectors used to execute a wireless MITM.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** on a wireless network is an active cyberattack in which an adversary covertly intercepts and relays communications between a wireless client (STA) and an Access Point (AP), making both parties believe they are communicating directly with each other. The attacker terminates the wireless link on one end, breaking the chain of trust.

**Two attack vectors:**

1. **Evil Twin / Rogue Access Point Attack** — Attacker broadcasts an AP with the same SSID (and often cloned BSSID) as the legitimate AP, luring clients to associate.
2. **Deauthentication Frame Injection** — Attacker injects forged 802.11 management frames (`Deauthentication` / `Disassociation`) to forcibly disconnect clients from the legitimate AP, forcing reconnection to the rogue AP.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Explain the role of "Protected Management Frames (PMF — IEEE 802.11w)" in preventing wireless MITM attacks.*

**Model Answer:**

**Protected Management Frames (PMF)**, defined in the IEEE 802.11w amendment, provide **cryptographic protection** (integrity, authenticity, and replay protection) for 802.11 management frames — particularly `Deauthentication` and `Disassociation` frames. 

In a typical MITM attack, the attacker injects these frames to forcibly disconnect a victim client from the legitimate AP. Without 802.11w, these frames are **unauthenticated and unencrypted**, allowing trivial forgery. With PMF enabled, every management frame carries a **Message Integrity Check (MIC)** computed using the negotiated pairwise transient key (PTK). Any forged frame will fail MIC verification at the receiver and be silently dropped, **completely neutralizing the forced-disconnection phase of a wireless MITM attack**. PMF is mandatory in WPA3.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **five phases** of a wireless MITM attack. Identify the **forensic artifacts** left at each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Deauthentication Frame Anomaly Score (DFAS)**. A forensic investigator captured wireless traffic for 120 seconds and observed 1800 deauthentication frames from 15 active stations. Compute the DFAS and state whether an MITM attack is indicated. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Phases of Wireless MITM Attack:**

1. **Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (Passive):**
   - Attacker switches the wireless NIC to **monitor mode**.
   - Uses tools (`airodump-ng`, `kismet`) to capture 802.11 frames passively.
   - Identifies: SSID, BSSID, channel, encryption type, client MACs.
   - *Forensic artifact:* PCAP file containing beacon frames, probe requests, signal strength histograms.

2. **Phase 2 — Rogue AP Deployment:**
   - Attacker creates a **rogue access point** with cloned SSID on a different channel.
   - Uses higher transmit power (e.g., 30 dBm vs. legitimate 20 dBm).
   - *Forensic artifact:* New BSSID with identical SSID, anomalous OUI distribution, channel change in client logs.

3. **Phase 3 — Forced Deauthentication:**
   - Attacker injects `Deauthentication` frames spoofed as the legitimate AP.
   - Victim clients are disconnected and begin scanning for a new AP.
   - *Forensic artifact:* Spike in `Deauth` count, broadcast destination MAC (`ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff`), sudden client disconnection events.

4. **Phase 4 — Victim Reconnection:**
   - Victim re-associates with the rogue AP (stronger signal).
   - Attacker captures the **4-way WPA handshake** (PMK, PTK, ANonce, SNonce).
   - *Forensic artifact:* New BSSID association in client logs, captured EAPOL frames in PCAP.

5. **Phase 5 — Relay and Exploitation:**
   - Attacker relays victim traffic while performing SSL stripping, DNS spoofing, session hijacking.
   - *Forensic artifact:* HTTP traffic on port 80 instead of HTTPS/443, anomalous DNS responses, modified HTTP headers.

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 must be included for full 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) DFAS Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $N_{deauth} = 1800$
- $T_{capture} = 120$ seconds
- $N_{STA} = 15$ stations

**Formula:**

$$
\text{DFAS} = \frac{N_{deauth}}{T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\text{DFAS} = \frac{1800}{120 \cdot 15} = \frac{1800}{1800} = 1.0 \text{ deauths/sec/station}
$$

**Interpretation:** A DFAS of **1.0** far exceeds the attack threshold of **0.5** deauths/sec/station. This is **definitive forensic evidence of a deauthentication-injection-based MITM attack** in progress during the capture window.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Evil Twin Attack** and **Karma Attack** as variants of wireless MITM. Compare their operational differences. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **OUI Entropy**? An investigator finds 5 APs all broadcasting the SSID `"CampusNet"`: 2 with OUI prefix `A0:63:91`, 2 with `00:1A:2B`, and 1 with `B8:27:EB`. Calculate the OUI entropy and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Evil Twin vs. Karma Attack:**

**Evil Twin Attack:**
- Attacker creates a **rogue AP broadcasting a known, popular SSID** (e.g., `"CampusNet"`, `"Starbucks_WiFi"`).
- The SSID is one that clients are known to actively seek.
- Attack relies on the client **explicitly probing** for or **passively scanning** for the SSID.
- Attacker uses higher transmit power or directional antennas to dominate coverage.

**Karma Attack (and its variant MANA):**
- Attacker **passively listens to 802.11 Probe Request frames** sent by clients.
- Probe Requests contain the client's **Preferred Network List (PNL)** — the SSIDs the client has previously connected to.
- The attacker's rogue AP **dynamically responds to each probe request** with a probe response for the **requested SSID**.
- The client, believing it found its trusted network, associates with the attacker's AP.
- Karma exploits the **historical trust** of clients, while Evil Twin exploits **current broadcast trust**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Evil Twin | Karma / MANA |
|---|---|---|
| SSID source | Attacker-chosen | Stolen from client probe |
| Detection | Easier (DFAS, OUI entropy) | Harder (targeted responses) |
| Target | Any client | Clients with rich PNL history |
| Frame abused | Beacon | Probe Response |

---

**(b) OUI Entropy Calculation:**

**Given:** 5 APs broadcasting SSID `"CampusNet"`:
- OUI `A0:63:91`: $n_1 = 2$
- OUI `00:1A:2B`: $n_2 = 2$
- OUI `B8:27:EB`: $n_3 = 1$

**Step 1 — Compute proportions:**

$$
p_1 = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4, \quad p_2 = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4, \quad p_3 = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's entropy formula:**

$$
H_{OUI} = -[(0.4)\log_2(0.4) + (0.4)\log_2(0.4) + (0.2)\log_2(0.2)]
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**
- $(0.4)\log_2(0.4) = 0.4 \times (-1.3219) = -0.5288$
- $(0.4)\log_2(0.4) = -0.5288$
- $(0.2)\log_2(0.2) = 0.2 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644$

**Step 4 — Sum and negate:**

$$
H_{OUI} = -[(-0.5288) + (-0.5288) + (-0.4644)] = -(-1.5220) = 1.522 \text{ bits}
$$

**Interpretation:** An OUI entropy of **1.522 bits** is **significantly greater than the threshold of 0.5 bits**. Three different OUI prefixes (Cisco, Apple, Raspberry Pi) simultaneously advertising the same SSID is statistically impossible in a legitimate single-organization deployment. This is **strong forensic evidence of an Evil Twin / Rogue AP MITM attack**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "Evil Twin" with "Rogue AP" — they are related but not identical. Evil Twin specifically spoofs the SSID of a known network; a Rogue AP is any unauthorized AP.
> - **Always include the unit "dBm"** when reporting RSSI in exam answers.
> - **Numerical problems** require full substitution of formula and step-by-step evaluation — skipping intermediate values will cost 2–3 marks.
> - Mentioning **802.11w (PMF)** is the single most important defense — many students omit it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — MITM on Wireless Networks:**

- ✅ **MITM Definition:** Active attack where adversary relays/alters traffic between two wireless endpoints.
- ✅ **Five Phases:** Reconnaissance → Rogue AP → Deauth Injection → Victim Reconnection → Relay/Exploitation.
- ✅ **Evil Twin:** Same SSID broadcast from unauthorized AP with stronger signal.
- ✅ **Karma Attack:** Rogue AP responds to client's Preferred Network List (PNL) probe requests.
- ✅ **Deauth Injection:** Forged 802.11 management frames (`Type=0, Subtype=12`) to disconnect clients.
- ✅ **DFAS Formula:** $\text{DFAS} = \dfrac{N_{deauth}}{T_{capture} \cdot N_{STA}}$; threshold $\geq 0.5$ = attack.
- ✅ **OUI Entropy:** $H_{OUI} = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; threshold $> 0.5$ = evil twin.
- ✅ **RSSI Differential:** $\Delta\text{RSSI} > +3$ dBm = rogue AP signal anomaly.
- ✅ **SSL Stripping:** Downgrade HTTPS → HTTP to intercept credentials.
- ✅ **DNS Spoofing:** Inject false DNS responses to redirect victim to attacker servers.
- ✅ **Forensic Tools:** Wireshark, Aircrack-ng suite, Kismet, Ettercap, Bettercap, NetworkMiner.
- ✅ **Key Defenses:** WPA3, 802.1X EAP-TLS, **802.11w (PMF — mandatory)**, HSTS, DNSSEC, WIPS.
- ✅ **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ — explains why closer attacker dominates.
- ✅ **Management Frame Type:** Deauth = `0x0C`, Disassoc = `0x0A` in 802.11 frame subtype field.
- ✅ **Forensic Artifacts:** Elevated DFAS, multiple OUIs same SSID, HTTP-on-login-pages, unsolicited DNS replies, 4-way handshake captured from non-legitimate BSSID.
- ✅ **CIA Triad Breach:** MITM violates all three — Confidentiality (eavesdropping), Integrity (modification), Authentication (impersonation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brute Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brute Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Brute Force** in the context of network forensics refers to a class of **cryptanalytic** and **authentication-bypass attacks** in which an adversary systematically enumerates *all possible* candidate values (passwords, keys, session tokens, or encryption keys) within a defined search space until the correct value is found. From a forensic-investigation standpoint, the term also denotes the *post-incident investigative methodology* used to reconstruct attacker movement, account compromise, and credential exposure by exhaustively testing captured artifacts (password hashes, encrypted volumes, key-logged buffers) against known wordlists, rule sets, and computational clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT864 – Module 4):**
> *"Brute Force is a trial-and-error methodology used by attackers to obtain information such as passwords, PINs, or encryption keys. In network forensics, the analyst applies the same exhaustive logic in reverse — to *prove* a compromise, recover plaintext, or attribute an intrusion."*

The attack is fundamentally a **combinatorial search problem**. Given a key space of size $K$ and a verification oracle $V(x)$ that returns `True` for the correct candidate, the attacker seeks:

$$x^* \in \{0,1\}^{n} \quad \text{such that} \quad V(x^*) = \text{True}$$

On average, a uniform-distribution search requires $\frac{K}{2}$ attempts (expected value), and **$K$ attempts** in the absolute worst case.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locker & Key Farm"

Imagine a long corridor of **10,000 identical locked lockers**, and you have lost the tiny key to locker number **#4,827**. You do not know which key opens which locker. A **brute force attack** is the strategy of standing at locker #4,827 and *physically testing every single key* in your keyring one after another until one turns.

- **Simple Brute Force** → You test keys 1, 2, 3, 4, 5… (purely sequential, all possible values).
- **Dictionary Attack** → You only test keys that look like real keys (English words, common PINs).
- **Hybrid Attack** → You test common key shapes but append/prepend small numbers (e.g., "key2024", "key2025").
- **Reverse Brute Force** → You stand at *one known popular key* and try it against thousands of lockers at once.
- **Credential Stuffing** → You use keys stolen from *another* corridor of lockers (leaked databases).

In each case, the attacker is exploiting the fact that the lock (the authentication system) is *deterministic* — given the right input, it always opens.

## 1.3 Why Brute Force Matters in Network Forensics

Network forensic analysts must understand brute force from **both sides of the wire**:

1. **Offensive side** — to recognize attack signatures in captured traffic (e.g., repeated `HTTP 401` codes, SSH connection storms, `RDP` brute-force bursts from a single IP).
2. **Defensive / Investigative side** — to *use* brute-force-style recovery (e.g., `hashcat` against dumped SAM database, `john` against `/etc/shadow`) to prove the *real* password was weak, reconstruct the attacker's lateral movement, and produce court-admissible evidence of compromise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Forensics Insight:**
> A brute force attack is one of the *easiest attacks to detect* but one of the *hardest to attribute*, because attackers almost always use **proxy chains, TOR exit nodes, botnets, and credential rotation**. The forensic challenge is **log correlation**, not the attack itself.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Brute Force Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key Space Size** | $K$ | $10^6$ – $10^{18}$ | Total candidates to test |
| **Attempts / second** | $r$ | 10 – $10^{11}$ | Hardware-dependent rate |
| **Expected time to crack** | $T$ | seconds → centuries | $T = K / (2r)$ |
| **Account Lockout Threshold** | $L$ | 3 – 10 | Failed logins before lock |
| **Entropy of password** | $H$ | 0 – 128 bits | $\log_2 K$ of the password |
| **Failed Login Ratio (FLR)** | $\rho$ | > 0.5 = suspicious | Failed / Total logins |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the formula $T = K / (2r)$ and the **Failed Login Ratio** threshold of $\rho \ge 0.5$ as a forensic indicator of compromise.
