# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Brute Force Attack

A brute force campaign, when dissected from a forensic standpoint, follows a **five-stage operational pipeline**:

1. **Reconnaissance (Footprinting)**
   - Identify target service: `SSH (22)`, `RDP (3389)`, `SMB (445)`, `FTP (21)`, `HTTP-Auth (80/443)`, `SMTP (25)`, `Telnet (23)`, `MySQL (3306)`.
   - Enumerate valid usernames (via OSINT, LinkedIn, `/etc/passwd` leak, or "username enumeration" via differential response timing).

2. **Tool Selection & Wordlist Curation**
   - Tools: `Hydra`, `Medusa`, `Ncrack`, `Patator`, `Aircrack-ng` (Wi-Fi), `Hashcat` / `John the Ripper` (offline hashes).
   - Wordlists: `rockyou.txt` (14 million), `SecLists`, custom dumps from previous breaches.

3. **Execution Phase (The "Spray")**
   - Generate high-rate authentication attempts, often distributed across botnet nodes to evade IP-based rate-limiting.
   - Use of **slow-burn tactics** (e.g., 1 attempt every 30 seconds) to stay under `fail2ban` thresholds.

4. **Detection Avoidance / Obfuscation**
   - Source IP rotation via **TOR**, **residential proxies**, **AWS/GCP free-tier abuse**.
   - User-Agent spoofing, source port randomization, jitter, and legitimate-looking JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprints.

5. **Post-Exploitation & Lateral Movement**
   - Successful login → token theft, persistence (SSH key injection, scheduled tasks), pivot to internal services.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Brute Force Variants

| Variant | Search Strategy | Typical Targets | Forensically-Detectable Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exhaustive / Pure Brute Force** | Tries every character combination | Short PINs, 4-digit tokens | Volume of failures, uniform distribution |
| **Dictionary Attack** | Tests only known words | Web logins, WPA2-PSK | Specific wordlist fingerprint |
| **Hybrid Attack** | Word + suffix/prefix rules | "Summer2024!", "Admin@123" | Wordlist + small mutation pattern |
| **Reverse Brute Force** | One password → many accounts | Credential leaks, "password" spray | Single password, many usernames |
| **Credential Stuffing** | Stolen pairs from previous breaches | Multi-site reuse | Unusual geographic / UA variance |
| **Password Spraying** | Few passwords → many accounts (low-and-slow) | Office 365, Azure AD | Distributed failures across user-base |
| **Rainbow Table** | Pre-computed hash → plaintext | LM hash, unsalted MD5 | Look-up speed anomaly |
| **Distributed Brute Force** | Botnet-parallelized | High-value targets (banks) | Source IP diversity graph |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. All quantities are **SI / SI-derived** unless otherwise stated.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Key Space Size** | $K = S^L$ | $S$ = symbol set size, $L$ = password length | $S \in \{26, 52, 62, 95\}$ |
| 2 | **Entropy** | $H = \log_2 K = L \cdot \log_2 S$ | bits | $H < 28$ ⇒ weak (NIST) |
| 3 | **Expected Time to Crack (Online)** | $T_{on} = \dfrac{K}{2 r_{on}}$ | $r_{on}$ = attempts/sec (online, throttled) | $r_{on} \approx 1$–$100$ |
| 4 | **Expected Time to Crack (Offline)** | $T_{off} = \dfrac{K}{2 r_{off}}$ | $r_{off}$ = hash/sec (GPU cluster) | $r_{off} \approx 10^{10}$–$10^{14}$ |
| 5 | **Failed Login Ratio (FLR)** | $\rho = \dfrac{F}{F + S}$ | $F$ = failures, $S$ = successes | $\rho \ge 0.5$ ⇒ suspicious |
| 6 | **Lock-out Time Penalty** | $T_{eff} = T_{on} \cdot L$ | $L$ = lockout threshold | $L = 3$ ⇒ $3\times$ slower |
| 7 | **Bit-strength to Time (linear)** | $\log_2 T = H - \log_2(2r)$ | bits | — |
| 8 | **Probability of Match in N tries** | $P(\text{success} \le N) = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{K}\right)^{N}$ | dimensionless | $N = K \Rightarrow P \approx 0.632$ |
| 9 | **Work Factor Reduction (Salting)** | $K_{salted} = K \cdot S_{salt}$ | $S_{salt}$ = salt alphabet size | $S_{salt} = 2^{128}$ for 128-bit salt |
| 10 | **Rate-Limit Detection Threshold** | $R_{alert} = \mu_{baseline} + 3\sigma$ | baseline mean & std-dev | 3-sigma rule (Wireshark, Splunk) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Bar Convention:** In KTU answer sheets and in this table, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is rendered with `\vert`, never `|`, to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penetration Testing** — Brute force modules are part of the `PTES` (Penetration Testing Execution Standard) and `OWASP` testing guides; ethical hackers use them to *prove* weakness to a client.
- **Incident Response** — SOC analysts apply reverse logic to confirm whether an exposed hash was crackable (and therefore whether the breach had *real* impact).
- **Digital Forensics & e-Discovery** — Recovering encrypted evidence (e.g., BitLocker, VeraCrypt) when the key is partially known uses the same combinatorial framework.
- **Compliance Auditing** — `PCI-DSS 8.3`, `NIST 800-63B`, and `ISO 27001` all mandate brute-force resistance testing as a control objective.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When asked *"how long will this attack take?"*, always quote both the **online** (network-throttled) and **offline** (hash-cracking) time, because they differ by **8–10 orders of magnitude**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Time to Crack (Online Attack)

We start from first principles. Assume:

- $K$ = total number of candidate passwords.
- $r$ = attempts per second (constant rate).
- The attacker tests candidates **uniformly at random without replacement**.

> **Step 1 — Modelling the search.**
> Let $X$ be the discrete random variable denoting the *position* (1-indexed) at which the correct password is found. Because the correct password is equally likely to be in any of the $K$ positions:
> 
> $$P(X = i) = \frac{1}{K}, \quad i \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$$

> **Step 2 — Expected position of the correct password.**
> The expected value of a discrete uniform distribution is:
> 
> $$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{K} i \cdot P(X = i) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} i$$
> 
> Using the closed form $\sum_{i=1}^{K} i = \frac{K(K+1)}{2}$:
> 
> $$E[X] = \frac{1}{K} \cdot \frac{K(K+1)}{2} = \frac{K+1}{2}$$

> **Step 3 — Worst case.**
> The attacker may be extraordinarily unlucky:
> 
> $$X_{worst} = K$$

> **Step 4 — Converting attempts to wall-clock time.**
> If the attacker can test $r$ candidates per second, the expected time in seconds is:
> 
> $$T = \frac{E[X]}{r} = \frac{K+1}{2r} \approx \frac{K}{2r} \quad (\text{since } K \gg 1)$$

> **Step 5 — Units and sanity check.**
> $K$ is dimensionless, $r$ has units $\text{attempts} \cdot s^{-1}$, so $T$ has units of seconds. ✓

> **Step 6 — Worked numerical example.**
> Suppose a 4-digit PIN ($K = 10^4$), online rate $r = 5$ attempts/sec.
> 
> $$T = \frac{10^4}{2 \times 5} = \frac{10^4}{10} = 10^3 \text{ seconds} \approx 16.67 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Step 7 — Lockout penalty.**
> With a lockout threshold of $L = 5$ failed attempts followed by a 30-second lockout, the effective rate becomes:
> 
> $$r_{eff} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{r} + \frac{T_{lock}}{L}} \approx \frac{L}{T_{lock}} = \frac{5}{30} = \frac{1}{6} \text{ attempts/sec}$$
> 
> Therefore:
> 
> $$T_{eff} = \frac{K}{2 r_{eff}} = \frac{10^4 \times 6}{2} = 30{,}000 \text{ s} \approx 8.33 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Password Entropy & NIST Compliance

> **Step 1 — Definition of entropy.**
> For a password of length $L$ drawn uniformly from an alphabet of $S$ symbols:
> 
> $$H = L \cdot \log_2 S \quad \text{bits}$$

> **Step 2 — Derivation from the Boltzmann/Shannon relation.**
> A uniform distribution over $K = S^L$ outcomes carries the Shannon entropy:
> 
> $$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i \log_2 p_i = -K \cdot \frac{1}{K} \log_2 \frac{1}{K} = \log_2 K = \log_2(S^L) = L \log_2 S$$

> **Step 3 — Typical alphabet sizes.**
> 
> $$S_{digits} = 10,\ \log_2 S = 3.32 \text{ bits/char}$$
> $$S_{lower} = 26,\ \log_2 S = 4.70 \text{ bits/char}$$
> $$S_{alnum} = 62,\ \log_2 S = 5.95 \text{ bits/char}$$
> $$S_{full} = 95,\ \log_2 S = 6.57 \text{ bits/char}$$

> **Step 4 — Numerical example.**
> An 8-character password from a 95-symbol set:
> 
> $$H = 8 \times 6.57 = 52.56 \text{ bits}$$
> 
> Against a $10^{10}$ hash/sec offline attack:
> 
> $$T = \frac{2^{52.56}}{2 \times 10^{10}} = \frac{6.27 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} = 3.13 \times 10^{5} \text{ s} \approx 3.6 \text{ days}$$

> **Step 5 — NIST 800-63B threshold.**
> NIST deems passwords with $H < 30$ bits as *memorized secrets of low entropy* and rejects them in federal systems. Therefore an 8-character alphanumeric password is **borderline acceptable**, while a 12-character one ($H = 71.4$ bits) is **strongly compliant**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Compromise Under Rate-Limited Defense

> **Step 1 — Setting up the geometric-style model.**
> Each attempt is an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability $p = 1/K$. After $N$ attempts:
> 
> $$P(\text{at least one success}) = 1 - P(\text{all failures}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{K}\right)^N$$

> **Step 2 — Taylor approximation for large $K$.**
> For $K \gg 1$ and $N$ moderate:
> 
> $$\left(1 - \frac{1}{K}\right)^N \approx e^{-N/K}$$
> 
> Hence:
> 
> $$P \approx 1 - e^{-N/K}$$

> **Step 3 — Numerical sample.**
> $K = 10^9$ (1 billion possible passwords), $N = 10^4$ attempts before lockout:
> 
> $$P \approx 1 - e^{-10^4 / 10^9} = 1 - e^{-10^{-5}} \approx 10^{-5} = 0.001\%$$

> **Step 4 — Forensic interpretation.**
> Even a *small* lockout window ($N = 10$) reduces the per-attacker success probability by **6 orders of magnitude** — which is why the **Failed Login Ratio** $\rho$ is the single most useful live forensic indicator.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defensive Control Effectiveness

| Defense Layer | Reduces What? | Effective Rate Reduction | Forensic Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Account Lockout ($L = 3$)** | $r_{on}$ | $r \to r/L$ | High (event log) |
| **Captcha / reCAPTCHA v3** | Automation | $r \to 0$ for bots | Medium (challenge-response log) |
| **MFA / TOTP** | Successful login | $P(\text{success}) \to 0$ even if $x^* = x$ | Very High (auth-method log) |
| **Rate-limit (e.g., 5/min)** | $r$ | $r \to 5/\text{min}$ | High (firewall log) |
| **Geo-blocking** | Source IP space | $K_{\text{effective}}$ reduced | High (WAF log) |
| **Salting (128-bit)** | Rainbow tables | $K \to K \cdot 2^{128}$ | None (server-side) |
| **bcrypt / Argon2 (slow KDF)** | $r_{off}$ | $r \to 10^{-3}$ of MD5 | None (offline timing) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Brute Force Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Attacker Initiates Campaign]) --> B[Reconnaissance\nService & Username Enumeration]
    B --> C[Wordlist Curation\nrockyou.txt / SecLists / Custom Dump]
    C --> D[Tool Selection\nHydra / Medusa / Hashcat / Aircrack]
    D --> E[Execution\nOnline: SSH/RDP/HTTP-Auth]
    D --> F[Execution\nOffline: Hash Cracking]
    E --> G{Response Code\n401 / Timeout / Lockout}
    F --> G
    G -->|Failure| H[Log Iteration\nBackoff + Jitter]
    H --> D
    G -->|Success| I[Credential Captured\nToken / Session Hijack]
    I --> J[Persistence\nSSH Key / Cron / Scheduled Task]
    J --> K([Lateral Movement\nPivot to Internal Services])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Forensic Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A([SIEM Alert:\nHigh FLR Detected]) --> B[Capture Live Traffic\nWireshark / tcpdump]
    B --> C[Extract Source IPs\nfrom NetFlow / Firewall Logs]
    C --> D[Correlate with\nAuthentication Logs\nWindows EVTX / Linux auth.log]
    D --> E{Forensic Triage}
    E -->|Online Attack| F[Replay Attack\nin Sandbox with Hydra]
    E -->|Offline Hash| G[Recover Hash\nSAM / NTDS.dit / shadow]
    F --> H[Identify Tool Signature\nUser-Agent / Packet Size]
    G --> I[Hashcat / John the Ripper\nwith rule-based attack]
    H --> J[Timeline Reconstruction]
    I --> J
    J --> K([Court-Admissible Report\nChain of Custody])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Detection Logic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Login Event Received]) --> Count{FLR > 0.5\nin 5 min window?}
    Count -->|No| Normal([Mark as Normal Traffic])
    Count -->|Yes| Rate{Attempts/sec\n> 3-sigma of baseline?}
    Rate -->|No| Watch([Flag for SOC Review])
    Rate -->|Yes| Geo{Same Source\nacross >5 Countries?}
    Geo -->|No| Botnet([Classify: Botnet-Driven\nBrute Force])
    Geo -->|Yes| Dist([Classify: Distributed\nCredential Stuffing])
    Botnet --> Action[Trigger IP Block + Honeypot]
    Dist --> Action
    Watch --> Action
    Action --> End([Incident Ticket Created])
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Architecture of a Brute-Force-Resilient Auth System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client([User Client]) --> WAF[Web Application Firewall\nRate Limiter + Geo Filter]
    WAF --> LB[Load Balancer\nTLS Termination]
    LB --> Auth[Authentication Service\nAuth0 / Keycloak / Custom]
    Auth --> MFA{MFA Module\nTOTP / WebAuthn}
    MFA -->|Success| Token[JWT / Session Token]
    MFA -->|Fail| Lockout[Lockout Counter\n5 fails in 10 min]
    Lockout --> Audit[Audit Log Sink\nELK / Splunk / SIEM]
    Auth --> Audit
    Token --> App[Application Backend]
    Audit --> SIEM([SIEM + SOC Alerting])
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. Define Brute Force Attack. Mention any two tools commonly used for it.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A *brute force attack* is a cryptanalytic technique in which an adversary systematically enumerates **all possible candidates** of a secret value (password, key, PIN) within its key space $K$ until the verification oracle returns a `True` response. The expected number of trials is $K/2$.
>
> Two widely used tools are:
> 1. **Hydra (THC-Hydra)** — parallelized network login cracker supporting SSH, FTP, HTTP, RDP, SMB, etc.
> 2. **Hashcat / John the Ripper** — high-speed offline hash crackers leveraging CPU and GPU acceleration.
>
> *(3 marks: definition 1, tool names 1, brief role 1)*

> **Q2. What is the Failed Login Ratio (FLR) and why is it a critical forensic indicator for detecting brute force activity?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
>
> **Model Answer:**
> $$\rho = \frac{F}{F + S}$$
> where $F$ = number of failed logins and $S$ = number of successful logins in a defined time window.
>
> **Forensic importance:**
> - A **legitimate user** has $\rho \to 0$ (one success, no failures).
> - A **brute-force campaign** drives $\rho \to 1$ (many failures, no successes).
> - An empirical threshold of $\rho \ge 0.5$ inside a 5-minute window is treated as a high-confidence indicator of compromise by SIEM platforms.
>
> *(3 marks: formula 1, threshold 1, forensic significance 1)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the operational lifecycle of a brute force attack with a neatly labeled diagram. Discuss **three** distinct variants (dictionary, hybrid, credential stuffing) and their forensic signatures. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 6-character password is constructed from a 26-character lowercase alphabet. An attacker has an offline GPU cluster capable of $10^{11}$ hash computations per second.
>     (i) Calculate the key space $K$ and the entropy $H$ in bits.
>     (ii) Estimate the **expected time** to crack.
>     (iii) Recommend **two** cryptographic or policy controls that would defeat this attack, with justification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The brute force attack lifecycle consists of **five phases**:

1. **Reconnaissance** — Identify open ports and live services (`nmap -p- target`). Enumerate usernames by exploiting differential responses (e.g., *“Invalid username”* vs *“Invalid password”*).

2. **Wordlist & Tool Curation** — The attacker selects or crafts a wordlist (`rockyou.txt`, custom breach corpora) and a tool tuned to the protocol (`Hydra` for online, `Hashcat` for offline).

3. **Execution** — Parallel authentication attempts are launched, often distributed across a botnet to defeat IP-based throttling. Packet inspection reveals *uniformly small inter-arrival times* and *identical packet sizes* — a key forensic signature.

4. **Detection Evasion** — Source IP rotation via TOR / residential proxies; legitimate-looking User-Agents; throttling to stay below `fail2ban` thresholds.

5. **Post-Exploitation** — On success, the attacker injects persistence (SSH keys, scheduled tasks) and pivots laterally.

**Three variants and their forensic signatures:**

| Variant | Mechanism | Forensic Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Dictionary Attack** | Tests only known words | Single wordlist fingerprint in packet payload; uniform distribution of attempts per source |
| **Hybrid Attack** | Word + mutation rules (e.g., append digits) | Patterns like `password1`, `password123` visible in failed-auth payloads; rule-engine correlation |
| **Credential Stuffing** | Stolen pairs from prior breach | Unusual *geographic* and *UA* variance per user; correlation to known breach dumps (HIBP) |

*(7 marks: lifecycle 2, diagram 1, three variants 2, forensic signatures 2)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given:** $L = 6$ characters, $S = 26$ (lowercase), $r = 10^{11}$ hash/s.

> **(i) Key space and entropy:**
> 
> $$K = S^L = 26^6 = 308{,}915{,}776 \approx 3.09 \times 10^{8}$$
> 
> $$H = \log_2 K = 6 \times \log_2 26 = 6 \times 4.7004 = 28.20 \text{ bits}$$

> **(ii) Expected time to crack:**
> 
> $$T = \frac{K}{2r} = \frac{3.09 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 1.545 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 1.55 \text{ ms}$$
> 
> **Interpretation:** This password is **broken in under 2 milliseconds** — a complete failure against offline attack.

> **(iii) Two recommended controls:**
> 
> 1. **Length increase to $L = 12$:** $H = 12 \times 4.7 = 56.4$ bits, raising crack time to $T \approx 4.4 \times 10^{5}$ s $\approx 5$ days. Adding uppercase + digits + symbols ($S = 95$) gives $H = 78.7$ bits — crack time exceeds the age of the universe.
> 
> 2. **Adopt a slow KDF (Argon2id / bcrypt cost 12):** Reduces effective $r$ from $10^{11}$ to $\sim 10^{1}$–$10^{3}$ hash/s, raising crack time by **8–10 orders of magnitude**, while remaining transparent to the user.

*(7 marks: i-2, ii-2, iii-3)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**
>
> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **forensic investigation workflow** after a brute force attack has been detected by a SIEM. Discuss at least **four** sources of log evidence the analyst must correlate. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A network administrator observes 800 failed SSH logins from IP `203.0.113.45` within 60 seconds, followed by 1 successful login. Compute:
>     (i) The Failed Login Ratio $\rho$.
>     (ii) The probability that a *legitimate* user from this IP could produce this pattern (justify your assumption).
>     (iii) Outline **three** immediate incident-response steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Forensic Investigation Workflow** (matches SECTION-4.2 diagram):

1. **Alert Triage** — SIEM raises a high-FLR alert; SOC analyst opens an incident ticket.
2. **Live Traffic Capture** — Begin `tcpdump` or Wireshark capture on the affected segment to preserve volatile evidence (volatile = memory-resident, lost on reboot).
3. **Log Correlation** — Pull and time-align:
   - **Authentication logs** (`/var/log/auth.log` on Linux, `Security.evtx` on Windows).
   - **Firewall / NetFlow logs** (source IP, port, byte count).
   - **Web-server access logs** (`/var/log/apache2/access.log` for HTTP brute force).
   - **IDS/IPS alerts** (Snort, Suricata rule SID for SSH brute force, e.g., `1:2001218`).
   - **Process / EDR telemetry** (unexpected `sshd` child processes, anomalous cron entries).
4. **Hash & Artifact Recovery** — Pull SAM / NTDS.dit / `/etc/shadow` and run `hashcat` in evidence mode.
5. **Timeline Reconstruction** — Use **Plaso / Timesketch** to align attacker actions to a single timeline.
6. **Chain of Custody & Reporting** — Hash the evidence files (SHA-256), write to write-blocker-mounted media, sign the chain-of-custody form.

*(7 marks: workflow 3, four log sources 2, chain-of-custody 2)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given:** $F = 800$ failures, $S = 1$ success, time window = 60 s.

> **(i) Failed Login Ratio:**
> 
> $$\rho = \frac{F}{F + S} = \frac{800}{801} = 0.9988 \approx 99.88\%$$
> 
> This is **far above the 0.5 threshold** — high-confidence indicator of brute force.

> **(ii) Probability that a legitimate user produced this pattern:**
> 
> A reasonable human typing speed is ~1 keystroke per 200 ms, plus username entry and 2FA. Even an extremely fast typist would need:
> 
> $$\frac{800 \text{ attempts}}{60 \text{ s}} \approx 13.3 \text{ attempts/sec}$$
> 
> This exceeds the **maximum possible human-typing rate** ($\sim 1$–$2$ attempts/sec including latency) by an order of magnitude. Combined with the single-success tail, the pattern is **computationally indistinguishable from an automated attack**. $P(\text{legit}) \approx 0$.

> **(iii) Three immediate incident-response steps:**
> 
> 1. **Block source IP at perimeter firewall / WAF** (`iptables -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.45 -j DROP`) and add to global block-list.
> 2. **Force password rotation + revoke active sessions** for the compromised user account. Audit `last -f /var/log/wtmp` and `lastlog` for post-compromise activity.
> 3. **Enable / tighten rate-limiting and account lockout** (`fail2ban` with `maxretry = 3`, `findtime = 600`, `bantime = 3600`) and deploy MFA for all remote-access users.

*(7 marks: i-2, ii-2, iii-3)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $T = K/(2r)$** — many students write $K/r$ and lose a full mark.
> 2. **Conflating online vs offline rates** — examiners *will* ask "if the same password is in an online vs an offline setting, which cracks first and by how many orders of magnitude?"
> 3. **Omitting chain-of-custody** in forensic reports — even perfect technical work scores zero if the evidence is not court-admissible.
> 4. **Using $L$ as both "lockout" and "length"** — always subscript: $L_{lock}$, $L_{len}$.
> 5. **Ignoring dictionary vs exhaustive distinction** — the entropy formula is for *uniform* distributions; English-language passwords carry far less entropy than $L \log_2 S$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Brute Force (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Exhaustive search of key space $K$ for matching secret; expected trials = $K/2$.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $T = K / (2r)$; entropy $H = L \log_2 S$; FLR $\rho = F/(F+S)$.
- ✅ **Online rate** $r \approx 1$–$100$ /s; **offline rate** $r \approx 10^{10}$–$10^{14}$ /s — they differ by **8–10 orders of magnitude**.
- ✅ **NIST threshold:** $H \ge 30$ bits for memorized secrets; modern recommendation is $H \ge 60$ bits.
- ✅ **Variants:** Exhaustive, Dictionary, Hybrid, Reverse, Credential Stuffing, Password Spraying, Rainbow Table, Distributed.
- ✅ **Forensic signatures:** High FLR, uniform inter-arrival times, identical packet sizes, single-IP / single-UA clusters, time-correlated failures across many users.
- ✅ **Key tools:** Hydra (online), Hashcat & John the Ripper (offline), Medusa, Aircrack-ng (Wi-Fi), fail2ban (defender).
- ✅ **Top defenses:** Account lockout, rate-limiting, MFA, slow KDFs (bcrypt, Argon2id), salting, geo-blocking, CAPTCHA.
- ✅ **FLR threshold:** $\rho \ge 0.5$ in a 5-minute window → high-confidence brute-force alert.
- ✅ **Detection rule:** Rate $> \mu_{baseline} + 3\sigma$ → trigger SIEM rule.
- ✅ **Forensic chain of custody:** Hash evidence (SHA-256), use write-blockers, sign chain-of-custody form, preserve volatile memory.
- ✅ **Legal:** Brute force against systems you don't own is illegal under **IT Act 2000 §66** (India) and **CFAA** (USA).
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Brute force fails on **L**ength, **A**lphabet, **S**alting, **M**FA, **L**ockout."* — **LASML**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evil Twin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evil Twin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: WIRELESS NETWORK FORENSICS — EVIL TWIN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Evil Twin** (also termed a *Rogue Access Point Attack* or *Wi-Fi Impersonation Attack*) is a hostile wireless access point (AP) that masquerades as a legitimate, trusted AP by broadcasting an identical **Service Set Identifier (SSID)** and **BSSID (MAC address)** spoofing profile. The attacker positions the rogue AP within the radio frequency (RF) coverage area of a genuine Wi-Fi network, exploiting the client's roaming and re-association behavior to silently redirect traffic through a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) proxy.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An Evil Twin attack is a wireless-layer social engineering exploit in which a forensic attacker (or forensic investigator in a controlled test) sets up an unauthorized AP that duplicates the SSID, security configuration, and RF signature of a legitimate network, capturing authentication handshakes, plaintext traffic, and credential material for subsequent forensic analysis.

> [!NOTE] **Key Forensically Relevant Parameters**
> - **SSID Spoofing**: Identical network name as the legitimate AP.
> - **BSSID Spoofing (MAC Cloning)**: Identical 48-bit MAC address.
> - **Channel Co-location**: Operates on the same 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz / 6 GHz channel.
> - **Signal Supremacy**: Transmit power (Tx) deliberately elevated to dominate probe-response contention.
> - **Deauthentication Injection**: Forced disassociation using 802.11 management frames.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Impostor Coffee Shop"

Imagine a busy airport terminal. A real Starbucks café (legitimate AP named *"Starbucks_Free_WiFi"*) is at Gate B. An attacker sets up a *look-alike booth* with the same logo, same name, and a louder, brighter sign right outside Gate B. Tired travelers, scanning for free Wi-Fi, see two identical networks and connect to the louder one without a second thought. Once seated, the attacker notes down everything they order (credentials, browsing history, payment info) and only later forwards the order to the real café to avoid suspicion.

In this analogy:
- The **traveler** = Wi-Fi client/supplicant.
- The **real café** = Legitimate Access Point.
- The **impostor booth** = Evil Twin AP.
- The **loud sign** = Higher RSSI / TX power beacon.
- The **"note-taking"** = Packet capture and credential harvesting.

This makes the *trust-based* nature of Wi-Fi association immediately intuitive.

### 1.3 Why Evil Twin Matters in Network Forensics

From a forensic-investigation standpoint, Evil Twin attacks are studied for **three reasons**:

1. **Incident Response**: To attribute breaches where user credentials leaked over open/captive-portal Wi-Fi.
2. **Red-Teaming & Penetration Testing**: Authorized simulation of wireless attacks to harden enterprise WLANs.
3. **Digital Evidence Reconstruction**: To recognize Evil Twin artifacts (duplicate BSSID, anomalous beacon intervals, deauth floods) in captured `.pcap` evidence files.

> [!NOTE] **Forensic Value:** A correctly identified Evil Twin trace in a `.pcap` file is *direct corroborating evidence* of wireless-layer compromise and frequently forms the crux of KTU case-study questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of an Evil Twin Attack

The attack is typically executed in **five structured phases**. Each phase is associated with distinct forensic artifacts that an investigator can hunt for in captured traffic.

### Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (Passive Capture)
- Attacker places the wireless interface in **monitor mode**.
- Tool: *Airodump-ng*, *Kismet*, *Wireshark* (with promiscuous capture).
- Captures: **Beacon frames**, **Probe Request/Response frames**, **802.11 management traffic**.
- The attacker enumerates target SSIDs, encryption ciphers, channel utilization, and client MAC addresses.

### Phase 2 — Deauthentication (DoS Trigger)
- The attacker broadcasts **802.11 deauthentication frames** (Type 0, Subtype 12) spoofed with the legitimate AP's BSSID.
- Legitimate clients are forcefully disassociated.
- Clients immediately attempt re-association, scanning all channels for the target SSID.
- This creates the "window of opportunity" required for Phase 3.

### Phase 3 — Rogue AP Activation
- Attacker broadcasts a new AP with the **same SSID** but **higher RSSI** (typically +5 dBm to +15 dBm above the legitimate AP).
- The rogue AP is often configured as an **Open Network** or with a *captive portal* mimicking the original authentication page.
- WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise Evil Twins use a *Rogue RADIUS server* to harvest MS-CHAPv2 challenge-response pairs.

### Phase 4 — Association & Handshake Capture
- Victim devices auto-connect based on:
  - **Preferred Network List (PNL) ranking**.
  - **Signal strength dominance**.
  - **Probe-response timing**.
- A full **4-way EAPOL handshake** is captured if the rogue AP clones WPA2-PSK credentials.
- Tool: *airodump-ng* (writes to `.cap` files), *Bettercap*, *Eaphammer*.

### Phase 5 — Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) & Exfiltration
- The Evil Twin forwards traffic to the legitimate AP (acting as a transparent bridge) so the victim experiences "normal" connectivity.
- All traffic is **mirrored** through attacker's host, enabling:
  - DNS spoofing.
  - HTTP session hijacking (e.g., `sslstrip` downgrades HTTPS → HTTP).
  - Credential harvesting via captive-portal cloning.
  - Malicious payload injection (drive-by JavaScript, malicious APKs).

## 2.2 Theoretical Model — Client Association Decision

Wi-Fi client association is governed by a **scoring function** that the supplicant computes for every candidate AP. The Evil Twin wins by maximizing this score.

Let:
- $R_i$ = RSSI (dBm) of candidate AP *i*.
- $P_i$ = TX power (dBm) of candidate AP *i*.
- $S_i$ = SSID match score (binary: 1 for match, 0 otherwise).
- $C_i$ = Channel preference weighting.
- $T_i$ = Time-since-last-association penalty.

A simplified **association desirability score** is:

$$
D_i = \alpha \cdot \frac{R_i}{-100} + \beta \cdot S_i + \gamma \cdot C_i - \delta \cdot T_i
$$

where $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, $\delta$ are **supplicant-specific weighting coefficients** (vendor-defined), and the RSSI term is normalized to the range $[0, 1]$ using the practical floor of $-100$ dBm.

The Evil Twin attacker artificially inflates $R_i$ (and thus $D_i$) by:
- Increasing $P_i$ (transmit power).
- Repositioning physically closer to the victim.
- Selecting a less congested channel ($C_i$ boost).

## 2.3 RSSI vs. Distance — Friis Transmission Equation

The **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** at the victim client is modeled using the **log-distance path loss model**:

$$
\text{PL}(d) \;=\; \text{PL}(d_0) + 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}
$$

where:
- $\text{PL}(d)$ = path loss (dB) at distance $d$ (meters).
- $\text{PL}(d_0)$ = path loss at the reference distance $d_0$ (typically **1 meter**).
- $n$ = path-loss exponent (e.g., **2.0** for free space, **3.0–4.0** for indoor office).
- $X_{\sigma}$ = zero-mean Gaussian random variable (shadow fading, **$\sigma \approx 4$ dB** indoors).

The received power is then:

$$
P_r \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL}(d)
$$

where $P_t$ is the transmit power, $G_t$ and $G_r$ are the transmit and receive antenna gains (in dBi). For the Evil Twin to dominate, the attacker solves:

$$
P_t^{\text{rogue}} + G_t^{\text{rogue}} - \text{PL}(d_{\text{rogue}}) \;>\; P_t^{\text{legit}} + G_t^{\text{legit}} - \text{PL}(d_{\text{legit}})
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Evil Twin Wireless Metrics

| Metric / Symbol | Definition | Unit / Range | Forensic Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SSID** | Service Set Identifier (network name) | String, 0–32 octets | Match against legitimate AP list |
| **BSSID** | AP MAC address (48-bit) | Hexadecimal | Detect MAC cloning anomalies |
| **RSSI** | Received Signal Strength Indicator | dBm, typically $-30$ to $-90$ | Identify abnormally strong rogue AP |
| **TX Power ($P_t$)** | Transmit power at antenna feedpoint | dBm, typically $1$ to $30$ | Distinguish legitimate vs. rogue via regulatory caps |
| **Beacon Interval** | Time between AP beacon frames | ms, typically $100$ | Detect non-standard intervals (rogue indicator) |
| **Channel** | Operating RF channel | 1–13 (2.4 GHz), 36–165 (5 GHz) | Identify co-channel duplication |
| **Path-Loss Exponent ($n$)** | Free-space coefficient | $2.0$ (free), $3$–$4$ (indoor) | Estimate rogue AP physical distance |
| **EAPOL 4-Way** | WPA2 handshake frames | 4 message exchange | Confirm handshake capture |
| **Deauth Frame** | 802.11 deauthentication | Type 0, Subtype 12 | Detect forced-disassociation attempts |
| **PNL Ranking** | Preferred Network List order | Vendor-specific | Predict likely victim device |
| **Dwell Time** | Time victim stays on rogue AP | seconds | Estimate data exfiltration window |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Board Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When asked to identify an Evil Twin in a `.pcap`, examiners expect you to mention **at least two** of the following markers: (1) duplicate BSSID on a different channel, (2) abnormally high beacon-rate, (3) deauthentication burst preceding client association, (4) EAPOL handshake with non-standard cipher suite.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evil Twin analysis is critical across multiple professional domains:

- **Enterprise Security Audits**: Companies like Cisco, Aruba, and Fortinet employ Evil Twin emulation modules in their WIPS (Wireless Intrusion Prevention Systems) — e.g., *Cisco Adaptive Wireless IPS* detects **honeypot/Evil-Twin signatures** in real time.
- **Law Enforcement & DFIR**: Agencies use forensic tools like *WiFi Pineapple Mark VII* (by Hak5) during **authorized penetration tests**.
- **Forensic Tool Development**: Open-source projects like *Wireshark*, *Kismet*, and *tcpdump* include Evil-Twin detection filters (e.g., Wireshark filter `wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x0c` for deauth frames).
- **Standards Bodies**: The IEEE 802.11w-2009 amendment (Protected Management Frames, **PMF**) was introduced specifically to mitigate deauth-based Evil Twin attacks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Rogue TX Power to Dominate a Legitimate AP

**Problem Setup.**
A legitimate AP transmits at $P_t^{\text{legit}} = 20$ dBm from a distance $d_{\text{legit}} = 15$ m. The Evil Twin attacker is at $d_{\text{rogue}} = 3$ m from the victim. Assume:
- Free-space propagation: $n = 2.0$, $d_0 = 1$ m, $\text{PL}(d_0) = 40$ dB.
- Antenna gains: $G_t^{\text{legit}} = G_t^{\text{rogue}} = 2$ dBi.
- Shadow fading $X_{\sigma} = 0$ (best-case, deterministic).
- To dominate, the rogue AP must deliver **at least +5 dB more** received power than the legitimate AP.

**Step 1 — Compute the path loss from the legitimate AP.**

Using the log-distance model:

$$
\text{PL}(15) \;=\; \text{PL}(1) + 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{15}{1}\right)
$$

$$
\text{PL}(15) \;=\; 40 + 10 \cdot 2.0 \cdot \log_{10}(15)
$$

$$
\text{PL}(15) \;=\; 40 + 20 \cdot 1.1761
$$

$$
\text{PL}(15) \;=\; 40 + 23.52
$$

$$
\text{PL}(15) \;=\; 63.52 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the received power from the legitimate AP.**

$$
P_r^{\text{legit}} \;=\; P_t^{\text{legit}} + G_t^{\text{legit}} - \text{PL}(15)
$$

$$
P_r^{\text{legit}} \;=\; 20 + 2 - 63.52
$$

$$
P_r^{\text{legit}} \;=\; -41.52 \; \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required received power from the rogue AP.**

For dominance, the rogue AP must achieve:

$$
P_r^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; P_r^{\text{legit}} + 5
$$

$$
P_r^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; -41.52 + 5
$$

$$
P_r^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; -36.52 \; \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the path loss from the rogue AP (at 3 m).**

$$
\text{PL}(3) \;=\; 40 + 10 \cdot 2.0 \cdot \log_{10}(3)
$$

$$
\text{PL}(3) \;=\; 40 + 20 \cdot 0.4771
$$

$$
\text{PL}(3) \;=\; 40 + 9.54
$$

$$
\text{PL}(3) \;=\; 49.54 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the required rogue TX power.**

$$
P_t^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; P_r^{\text{rogue}} - G_t^{\text{rogue}} + \text{PL}(3)
$$

$$
P_t^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; -36.52 - 2 + 49.54
$$

$$
P_t^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; 11.02 \; \text{dBm}
$$

$$
P_t^{\text{rogue}} \;\geq\; 11.02 \; \text{dBm} \quad (\text{i.e., } \approx 12.6 \; \text{mW})
$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Interpretation:** The attacker needs only **~12.6 mW** of transmit power (well within typical Wi-Fi adapter specs of 30–200 mW) to dominate a legitimate 100 mW AP, simply by being 5× closer to the victim. This is why Evil Twin attacks are *practical even with low-cost hardware*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forensic Estimation of Rogue AP Distance from RSSI

**Problem Setup.**
A forensic investigator captures an Evil Twin beacon at $P_r = -55$ dBm. The known TX power of the attacker's hardware is $P_t = 18$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, $\text{PL}(d_0) = 40$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ m, $n = 3.0$ (indoor office environment).

**Step 1 — Rearrange the path loss equation to solve for $d$.**

$$
P_r \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL}(d_0) - 10n \log_{10}(d)
$$

$$
\text{PL}(d) \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - P_r
$$

$$
40 + 10 \cdot 3 \cdot \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 18 + 2 + 2 - (-55)
$$

$$
40 + 30 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 77
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the log term.**

$$
30 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 77 - 40
$$

$$
30 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 37
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) \;=\; \frac{37}{30} \;=\; 1.2333
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to find $d$.**

$$
d \;=\; 10^{1.2333} \;\approx\; 17.1 \; \text{meters}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Forensic Use Case:** Investigators use this calculation to **triangulate** the rogue AP position by collecting RSSI samples from multiple vantage points. This becomes a KTU-favorite 7-mark derivation problem.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detection Threshold Using Beacon-Interval Anomaly

A reliable forensic signature is a deviation in the **beacon interval** of the rogue AP. Let $\mu$ be the standard beacon interval (typically $102.4$ ms, transmitted every 100 TU). A rogue AP may use non-standard intervals like $50$ ms (faster handshakes) or $200$ ms (energy-saving).

Define the **Z-score** for a captured beacon interval $\hat{T}$ as:

$$
Z \;=\; \frac{\hat{T} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation across the network. A rogue AP is flagged when $\vert Z \vert > 3$ (i.e., lies beyond $3\sigma$ from the mean).

**Worked Example.**
Suppose $\mu = 102.4$ ms, $\sigma = 2$ ms. A captured Evil Twin emits beacons at $\hat{T} = 90$ ms.

$$
Z \;=\; \frac{90 - 102.4}{2} \;=\; \frac{-12.4}{2} \;=\; -6.2
$$

Since $\vert Z \vert = 6.2 > 3$, the beacon source is **statistically anomalous** and flagged as a probable rogue AP. This forms the basis of commercial WIPS detection engines.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Forensics Table

| Forensic Vector | Legitimate AP | Evil Twin AP | Detection Difficulty |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SSID | Unique or branded | Duplicated exactly | Low (trivial to spoof) |
| BSSID | Vendor-OUI registered | Cloned or random OUI | Medium (OUI DB lookup) |
| RSSI distribution | Gaussian, stable | Skewed, very high near attacker | Medium (requires RF survey) |
| Beacon interval | 100 TU (102.4 ms) | Variable (50–200 ms) | High (statistical baseline needed) |
| Encryption advertised | WPA2-Enterprise / WPA3 | Open / WPA2-PSS / Rogue RADIUS | Low (mismatch is obvious) |
| Deauth burst | Rare / legitimate roaming | Frequent, anomalous pattern | Medium (Wireshark `wlan.fc.type_subtype == 12` filter) |
| Probe-response timing | Standard backoff | Aggressive (preemptively answers) | High (microsecond timing analysis) |
| Channel width | 20/40/80 MHz (standardized) | 5/10/20 MHz (atypical) | Medium |
| EAPOL handshake | Hardware-bound | Captured/replayed | High (nonce analysis) |
| MAC sequence number | Monotonically increasing | Reset / discontinuous | High (forensic-grade) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Evil Twin Attack

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the Evil Twin attack lifecycle, showing how the attacker's components interact with the victim client, the legitimate AP, and upstream services. This is a *block-level functional architecture flow* (per the fallback guidance) rather than a physical RF propagation diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Victim Wi-Fi Client] -->|1. Probe Request SSID| B[Legitimate AP]
    A -->|2. Forced Deauth Burst| C[Evil Twin Attack Host]
    C -->|3. Spoofed Beacon Higher RSSI| A
    A -->|4. Open Auth / WPA2 Re-Association| C
    C -->|5. EAPOL Handshake Capture| A
    C -->|6. Traffic Mirroring via Bridge| D[DHCP / DNS Spoofing Module]
    C -->|7. Transparent Forwarding| B
    B -->|8. Internet Path| E[Upstream Services]
    C -->|9. Credential Harvesting| F[Forensic Evidence Store]
    C -->|10. sslstrip HTTPS Downgrade| F
    F -->|11. pcap Export| G[Investigator Analysis]
    G -->|12. Z-score Beacon Analysis| H[Rogue AP Confirmed]
    G -->|13. RSSI Triangulation| I[Physical Location Estimate]
    G -->|14. BSSID OUI Lookup| J[Hardware Attribution]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Nodes A & B** represent the legitimate Wi-Fi ecosystem.
- **Node C** is the *attacker host* running a *WiFi Pineapple* or *hostapd-mana* suite.
- **Node F** is the *forensic data sink* where evidence accumulates.
- **Nodes H, I, J** represent the *investigator's analytical outputs* derived from the captured `.pcap`.

## 4.2 Forensics Investigator Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DETECT [Detection Phase]
        K1[Capture pcap with Monitor Mode] --> K2[Filter Deauth Bursts]
        K2 --> K3[Identify Duplicate BSSID]
        K3 --> K4[Compute Beacon Z-score]
    end
    subgraph ANALYZE [Analysis Phase]
        K4 --> L1[RSSI Triangulation]
        K4 --> L2[EAPOL Handshake Diff]
        K4 --> L3[OUI Vendor Lookup]
    end
    subgraph REPORT [Reporting Phase]
        L1 --> M1[Geolocation Map]
        L2 --> M2[Credential Exposure Log]
        L3 --> M3[Hardware Attribution Report]
    end
    M1 --> N[Court-Admissible Forensic Report]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
```

## 4.3 Conceptual State Transition of the Victim Client

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ConnectedLegit : Initial State
    ConnectedLegit --> Disassociated : Deauth Flood Received
    Disassociated --> Scanning : Client Rescans Channels
    Scanning --> Probing : SSID Match Found
    Probing --> EvilTwinAuth : Higher RSSI Selected
    EvilTwinAuth --> HandshakeComplete : EAPOL Captured
    HandshakeComplete --> MitMActive : sslstrip / DNS Spoof
    MitMActive --> DataExfil : Credentials Harvested
    DataExfil --> [*]
    MitMActive --> ReconnectsLegit : User Detects Anomaly
    ReconnectsLegit --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define the term "Evil Twin" in the context of wireless network security. List any three forensic indicators that can be used to detect an Evil Twin attack from a captured `.pcap` file.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
An Evil Twin is a rogue wireless access point that impersonates a legitimate AP by broadcasting the same SSID (and often the same BSSID) on the same RF channel, in order to trick wireless clients into associating with it. Once associated, the attacker can intercept, modify, or exfiltrate the victim's traffic, effectively performing a Man-in-the-Middle attack at Layer 2.

**Forensic Indicators (2 Marks — any three of the following, 1 mark for 2 correct, 2 marks for 3 correct):**
1. **Duplicate BSSID on multiple channels** — Indicates MAC cloning/spoofing of a legitimate AP.
2. **Burst of 802.11 deauthentication frames** (`wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x0c`) preceding client re-association — Indicates forced disassociation.
3. **Anomalous beacon interval** (Z-score $\vert Z \vert > 3$ from the network mean of 102.4 ms) — Indicates non-standard AP behavior.
4. **Open encryption advertised on a "secured" SSID** — Indicates a downgrade attack.
5. **Abnormally high RSSI** at the capture point relative to the legitimate AP — Indicates physical proximity of a rogue transmitter.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Explain the role of a "Deauthentication Flood" in launching an Evil Twin attack. Why is the IEEE 802.11w (PMF) standard important in mitigating this?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of Deauthentication Flood (2 Marks):**
A deauthentication flood is the preparatory step that disconnects legitimate clients from the genuine AP by broadcasting spoofed 802.11 management frames (Type 0, Subtype 12) that impersonate the legitimate AP. Since the original 802.11 standard did not authenticate these management frames, any client receiving a deauth frame is forced to immediately terminate its session. The clients then re-scan all Wi-Fi channels searching for the known SSID. During this "re-association window," the Evil Twin (with higher RSSI) accepts the client's re-association request, completing the takeover. Without this DoS trigger, the client would remain on the legitimate AP and never attempt to connect to the rogue.

**Importance of 802.11w / PMF (1 Mark):**
The IEEE 802.11w amendment, also called **Protected Management Frames (PMF)**, cryptographically signs deauthentication and disassociation frames using the existing PTK (Pairwise Transient Key). This makes it impossible for an attacker to forge a valid deauth frame, neutralizing the deauth-flood trigger. PMF is mandatory in **WPA3** and recommended in WPA2.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the five operational phases of an Evil Twin attack. For each phase, identify the specific forensic artifact that an investigator should look for in a captured wireless traffic dump.

**Model Answer:**

| Phase | Attacker Action | Forensic Artifact in `.pcap` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Reconnaissance** | Monitor mode capture of all SSIDs, BSSIDs, channels, and clients using tools like *Airodump-ng* or *Kismet*. | Captured **Beacon frames** listing SSIDs in the vicinity. Channel utilization statistics. |
| **2. Deauthentication** | Broadcast spoofed 802.11 deauth frames (Subtype 12) targeting all clients of the target BSSID. | A **burst of deauthentication frames** with a single source BSSID and multiple destination client MACs, occurring within milliseconds. |
| **3. Rogue AP Activation** | Stand up a new AP on the same SSID, same channel, with TX power +5 to +15 dBm higher. | **Two beacon frames** advertising the same SSID but different BSSIDs, with one BSSID having significantly higher RSSI. |
| **4. Association & Handshake Capture** | Victim client re-associates with the rogue; attacker initiates a 4-way EAPOL handshake (or Open Auth). | **4-way EAPOL exchange** OR **Open Authentication success** on a previously-encrypted SSID. |
| **5. MitM & Exfiltration** | Bridge traffic to the legitimate AP; mirror all packets; run *sslstrip*, *bettercap*, *dnsmasq* for DNS spoofing. | **DNS responses with mismatched IPs**, **HTTP requests where HTTPS was expected** (downgrade), large outbound flows to attacker-controlled IPs. |

**Forensic Signature Summary (1 mark for framework + 1 mark per phase):**
The composite forensic fingerprint is a *triplet*: (deauth burst) → (duplicate BSSID beacon with elevated RSSI) → (unencrypted/downgraded traffic flow).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] In a wireless forensic investigation, a victim device is found to have associated with an Evil Twin broadcasting SSID *"CampusWiFi"* with BSSID `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`. The captured RSSI of the rogue AP at the victim's location is **−48 dBm**, while the legitimate AP on the same SSID is received at **−72 dBm**. Given that the legitimate AP transmits at 20 dBm with 2 dBi gain from a distance of 20 m, and the indoor path-loss exponent is **n = 3.5** with reference loss PL(1 m) = 40 dB, compute:
  (i) the received power difference between rogue and legitimate AP, and
  (ii) the approximate distance of the rogue AP from the victim.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Verify the legitimate AP's received power matches the captured value.**

$$
P_r^{\text{legit}} \;=\; P_t + G_t - \text{PL}(20)
$$

$$
\text{PL}(20) \;=\; 40 + 10 \cdot 3.5 \cdot \log_{10}(20)
$$

$$
\text{PL}(20) \;=\; 40 + 35 \cdot 1.3010 \;=\; 40 + 45.54 \;=\; 85.54 \; \text{dB}
$$

$$
P_r^{\text{legit}} \;=\; 20 + 2 - 85.54 \;=\; -63.54 \; \text{dBm}
$$

(Close to the reported −72 dBm; the residual ~8 dB is explained by shadow fading and antenna orientation.)

**Step 2 — Compute the received power difference (Part i).**

$$
\Delta P_r \;=\; P_r^{\text{rogue}} - P_r^{\text{legit}} \;=\; -48 - (-72)
$$

$$
\Delta P_r \;=\; +24 \; \text{dB}
$$

The rogue AP is delivering **24 dB more received power** — an enormous dominance margin (~250× in linear power), making the rogue AP the unambiguous association target for any supplicant.

**Step 3 — Compute the rogue AP's distance (Part ii).**

For the rogue AP, assume the same hardware profile ($P_t^{\text{rogue}} = 20$ dBm, $G_t = 2$ dBi) and use:

$$
P_r^{\text{rogue}} \;=\; P_t^{\text{rogue}} + G_t - \text{PL}(d_{\text{rogue}})
$$

$$
-48 \;=\; 20 + 2 - \left[ 40 + 35 \cdot \log_{10}(d_{\text{rogue}}) \right]
$$

$$
-48 \;=\; 22 - 40 - 35 \log_{10}(d_{\text{rogue}})
$$

$$
-48 \;=\; -18 - 35 \log_{10}(d_{\text{rogue}})
$$

$$
35 \log_{10}(d_{\text{rogue}}) \;=\; -18 - (-48) \;=\; 30
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\text{rogue}}) \;=\; \frac{30}{35} \;=\; 0.857
$$

$$
d_{\text{rogue}} \;=\; 10^{0.857} \;\approx\; 7.2 \; \text{meters}
$$

**Forensic Conclusion (1 mark):** The rogue AP is located approximately **7.2 meters** from the victim — well within the immediate vicinity, strongly suggesting an *insider threat* or a *personally-targeted attack*. Investigators should focus CCTV review and access logs on a **~14-meter diameter zone** centered on the victim.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative to Question A*

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an Evil Twin attack. Label all components and data flows.

**Model Answer:**

Refer to **Section 4.1 (Block-Level Functional Architecture)** above for the complete Mermaid block diagram.

**Component-by-Component Explanation (7 marks allocated as follows):**

1. **Victim Wi-Fi Client (1 mark):** Wireless device with stored Preferred Network List (PNL). Sends probe requests on every channel after deauth.
2. **Legitimate AP (1 mark):** The original, trusted access point — usually enterprise-managed with WPA2-Enterprise / 802.1X.
3. **Evil Twin Attack Host (2 marks):** The attacker's laptop running *hostapd-mana*, *WiFi Pineapple*, or *Bettercap*. Contains three sub-modules:
   - **Rogue AP daemon** (broadcasts spoofed beacons).
   - **Deauth injector** (sends spoofed 802.11 management frames).
   - **MitM proxy** (forwards traffic while mirroring).
4. **DHCP / DNS Spoofing Module (1 mark):** Issues fake DHCP leases and resolves victim DNS queries to attacker-controlled IPs.
5. **Forensic Evidence Store (1 mark):** Stores the captured `.pcap`, harvested credentials, and HTTP session tokens. Investigators extract this in court-admissible form.
6. **Investigator Analysis Tools (1 mark):** Wireshark, Kismet, inSSIDer — used to confirm Evil Twin signatures and triangulate location.

**Key Data Flows (must be labeled in the answer):**
- *Probe Request / Response* — victim ↔ AP negotiation.
- *Deauth frames* — attacker → victim (forced disassociation).
- *EAPOL handshake* — victim → attacker (credential exposure).
- *Mirrored traffic* — attacker → forensic store.
- *Bridged traffic* — attacker ↔ legitimate AP (concealment).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the forensic detection techniques that can be used to identify an Evil Twin in a wireless capture. Include in your answer: (i) beacon interval analysis, (ii) BSSID OUI lookup, and (iii) RSSI-based triangulation.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Beacon Interval Analysis [2 Marks]:**
The IEEE 802.11 standard recommends a beacon interval of **100 time units (TU)** = 102.4 ms. An attacker may deviate from this to optimize re-association speed or to fit within power constraints. The investigator computes the Z-score:

$$
Z \;=\; \frac{\hat{T} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

For $\mu = 102.4$ ms, $\sigma = 2$ ms, any beacon with $\vert Z \vert > 3$ (i.e., outside ~95.6–109.2 ms) is statistically anomalous. Commercial WIPS engines like Cisco Adaptive WIPS use this technique.

**(ii) BSSID OUI Lookup [2 Marks]:**
The first 24 bits of a BSSID identify the **Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI)** of the manufacturer. Investigators cross-reference the rogue BSSID against a database of legitimate enterprise AP OUIs. A mismatch — e.g., a "Cisco" SSID being broadcast by a *Realtek* OUI — is a strong forensic indicator of a rogue AP. Tools: *Wireshark* (Statistics → Endpoints → IEEE 802.11), or the IEEE OUI registry.

**(iii) RSSI-Based Triangulation [3 Marks]:**
Using the log-distance path loss model with the Friis transmission equation:

$$
P_r \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL}(d_0) - 10n \log_{10}(d)
$$

The investigator captures RSSI samples from **at least 3 known vantage points**. Solving the model for $d$ at each vantage point yields three distance estimates. Geometrically intersecting the three circles on a floor plan locates the rogue AP within **±2–3 meters** of accuracy. This technique is widely used in law enforcement Wi-Fi forensics (e.g., in identifying *"Wi-Fi sniffer"* locations in industrial espionage cases).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams**
> 1. **Confusing Evil Twin with Rogue AP:** A *Rogue AP* is *any* unauthorized AP connected to the corporate LAN (e.g., a staff member's personal hotspot). An *Evil Twin* specifically **impersonates an existing legitimate SSID**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Omitting the deauth phase:** Many students describe the rogue AP setup but skip the deauthentication flood. This is worth **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question and is the *most-skipped* forensic phase in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Forgetting the log-distance equation:** In numerical questions, students often give the formula $P_r = P_t - \text{PL}(d)$ without the antenna gain terms $G_t$ and $G_r$. Always include them.
> 4. **Units confusion:** RSSI is in **dBm** (power, relative to 1 mW), not **dB** (dimensionless ratio). Examiners will deduct marks for "−48 dB" instead of "−48 dBm".
> 5. **Forgetting Beacon Interval Standard:** The default beacon interval is **100 TU = 102.4 ms**, not 100 ms. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 6. **Skipping the Mitigation Slide:** Even when not asked, mentioning **802.11w / PMF** or **WPA3** as a countermeasure adds 1 mark of examiner goodwill on theory questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Evil Twin** = Rogue AP impersonating a legitimate SSID + BSSID on the same channel.
- ✅ **Five Phases**: Recon → Deauth Flood → Rogue AP Activation → Association/Handshake → MitM/Exfiltration.
- ✅ **Deauthentication Frame**: 802.11 Type 0, Subtype **0x0c** (12 decimal).
- ✅ **Default Beacon Interval**: **100 TU = 102.4 ms**.
- ✅ **Default Beacon Frame Type/Subtype**: Type 0, Subtype **0x08** (8 decimal).
- ✅ **RSSI Unit**: dBm (NOT dB).
- ✅ **Path-Loss Model**: $\text{PL}(d) = \text{PL}(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$.
- ✅ **Free-space n = 2.0**, indoor office n = 3–4.
- ✅ **Friis Equation**: $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL}(d)$.
- ✅ **Z-score for beacon anomaly**: $\vert Z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow$ Rogue AP candidate.
- ✅ **Mitigation Standard**: IEEE **802.11w** (Protected Management Frames / PMF) — mandatory in WPA3.
- ✅ **Forensic Tools**: Wireshark, Kismet, Airodump-ng, WiFi Pineapple, Bettercap.
- ✅ **Wireshark Filters**: `wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x0c` (deauth), `wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x08` (beacon), `eapol` (handshake).
- ✅ **Distinguish carefully**: Evil Twin ≠ Rogue AP ≠ Ad-Hoc Network ≠ Wi-Fi Pineapple (the *tool*).
- ✅ **Forensic Triangulation** requires minimum **3 RSSI samples** from known locations.
- ✅ **OUI Lookup** is a quick, low-cost forensic check for hardware attribution.

> **End of Module 4 — Evil Twin Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rogue Access Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rogue Access Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Rogue Access Point (Rogue AP)** is an unauthorized wireless access point installed on a secured network—either by an insider (deliberately or accidentally) or by an external attacker—that has not been provisioned, approved, or administratively controlled by the network's IT/Security Operations Center (SOC). It violates the network's security policy, bypasses the centralized authentication infrastructure (RADIUS/AAA), and creates a backdoor channel into the protected Local Area Network (LAN).

According to the **IEEE 802.11-2020** standard and **NIST SP 800-153 (Guidelines for Securing Wireless Local Area Networks)**, rogue APs are formally categorized as **"unauthorized wireless devices operating within the physical or logical coverage area of an authorized WLAN."**

### 1.2 Extended Taxonomy of Rogue APs (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Sub-Class | Technical Distinction | Threat Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Rogue AP** | Connected to the wired network; bridges wireless to corporate LAN | **CRITICAL** |
| **Unauthorized AP** | Detected AP not present in the inventory, but not necessarily connected to LAN | HIGH |
| **Evil Twin** | AP with same SSID as legitimate AP, used for MITM attacks | **CRITICAL** |
| **Soft AP** | Software-emulated AP on a laptop/PC (e.g., hosted network) | MEDIUM |
| **Ad-hoc Network Node** | Peer-to-peer 802.11 connection outside infrastructure mode | MEDIUM |
| **Misconfigured AP** | Authorized device, but with weak/default configuration | LOW-MEDIUM |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Impersonator Guard"

> [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Analogy**
> Imagine a high-security corporate building with one official entry gate (the **Authorized AP**), guarded by a receptionist who checks employee IDs (WPA2-Enterprise / 802.1X). A **Rogue AP** is like a back door left open by a careless employee who installed their own wireless router in their cubicle "for convenience." Now anyone outside the building (an attacker) can connect through that back door and walk straight into the office—bypassing the front desk entirely. The receptionist (RADIUS) never sees them.

In the **Evil Twin** variant, the analogy becomes sharper: imagine a fraudster standing outside the building, holding up a sign that says **"Official Entrance →"**—visitors connect to *him* first, and he relays all their traffic while harvesting credentials.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Mandate)

The following standard values are routinely referenced in KTU question papers for wireless forensics:

- **2.4 GHz band:** Operates across **11 (US) / 13 (EU/India) channels**, 22 MHz each, non-overlapping channels = **1, 6, 11**
- **5 GHz band:** UNII-1 through UNII-4, up to **25 non-overlapping 20 MHz channels**
- **Typical rogue AP transmission power:** **20 dBm (100 mW)**
- **Standard RSSI threshold for "out of range" detection:** **−80 dBm**
- **SNR minimum for stable association:** **≥ 25 dB**
- **802.11 beacon interval:** **100 ms** (default)
- **Maximum association rate (802.11n/ac/ax):** up to **9.6 Gbps** (theoretical, 802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Rogue AP Attack — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Rogue" is a Forensic Gold Mine**
> Every rogue AP leaves a **digital exhaust trail**: beacon frames (Layer 2), DHCP lease logs (Layer 3), MAC address artifacts (OUI fingerprints), and timing anomalies. Forensics investigators reconstruct attack timelines from these artifacts.

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance (Pre-deployment)**
- Attacker uses tools (e.g., **Kismet**, **NetStumbler**, **Airodump-ng**) to enumerate legitimate APs
- Captures **BSSID**, **SSID**, **channel**, **encryption type**, **vendor OUI**, and **signal strength**
- Goal: identify high-traffic, weakly-secured SSIDs to impersonate

**Stage 2 — AP Provisioning**
- Attacker configures a hardware AP (e.g., **Raspberry Pi + Alfa AWUS036ACH** adapter) or a software AP
- Sets identical **SSID**, similar **BSSID** (often MAC-spoofed to match vendor OUI), and a higher **beacon signal strength** to attract clients

**Stage 3 — Luring / Association**
- Victim device (using MAC randomisation or persistent SSID memory) auto-associates with the rogue AP based on RSSI priority
- Attacker may use **deauthentication frames (802.11 management type=12, subtype=0)** to force a "reconnect" — clients drop from legitimate AP and re-associate with the rogue

**Stage 4 — MITM / Traffic Capture**
- Rogue AP forwards traffic to a real Internet gateway (if connected) while **logging all packets**
- Captures credentials, session cookies, DNS queries, and unencrypted payload data
- May inject malware via captive portal

**Stage 5 — Lateral Pivot (Corporate Rogue Case)**
- If the rogue AP is plugged into the corporate LAN, attacker tunnels through it into the internal network
- Bypasses **802.1X port-based authentication** if the rogue AP's Ethernet port is not configured for it

**Stage 6 — Cover-up**
- Attacker may change MAC, change channel, or use directional antennas to evade detection
- The physical AP may be disguised (e.g., inside a printer, behind a desk)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] > **Master Reference Table — Equations Used in Rogue AP Detection & Forensics**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$ | Distance estimation from signal strength | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz, result in dB |
| 2 | **Received Signal Strength (RSSI)** | $$RSSI = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$$ | Theoretical signal at receiver | dBm |
| 3 | **Link Budget Equation** | $$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} - L_{fading}$$ | Receiver sensitivity planning | dBm |
| 4 | **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** | $$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ | Theoretical max throughput for given SNR | bits/sec |
| 5 | **SNR Calculation** | $$SNR_{dB} = P_{signal} - P_{noise}$$ | Link quality indicator | dB |
| 6 | **Round-Trip-Time (RTT) for location** | $$d = \frac{RTT \cdot c}{2}$$ | Coarse geolocation from timing | meters, $c=3\times10^8$ m/s |
| 7 | **Probability of Detection (Baye's posterior)** | $$P(D \vert S) = \frac{P(S \vert D) \cdot P(D)}{P(S)}$$ | Statistical detection confidence | dimensionless $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | **Kismet Channel Hop Time** | $$T_{hop} = \frac{N_{channels}}{C_{dwell}}$$ | Time to scan full spectrum | seconds |

> [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for KTU Board Papers**
> Always write the unit of $dB$ or $dBm$ explicitly. Examiners **deduct 0.5 marks** if units are missing in numerical answers. **Power is in dBm; ratios/gains/losses are in dB.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production enterprise networks** (banks, hospitals, defense), rogue AP detection is a **mandatory control** under **PCI-DSS 11.1**, **HIPAA Security Rule §164.312**, and **ISO 27001 Annex A.13.1**. Forensic readiness requires:
- **Wireless Intrusion Prevention Systems (WIPS)** such as Cisco Adaptive WIPS, Aruba RFProtect
- **Spectrum analyzers** for non-802.4 RF threats
- **Periodic site surveys** (active and passive)
- **Centralized SIEM ingestion** of all 802.11 management frame logs

In **forensic post-incident analysis**, the rogue AP is itself **"the scene of the crime"** — investigators image its flash storage, recover configuration backups, and correlate DHCP/ARP logs to reconstruct the attack window.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Distance Estimation of a Rogue AP using FSPL

> [!NOTE] > **Why we need this:** During forensic investigation, locating the *physical* position of a rogue AP is critical. Using the captured beacon RSSI from three or more sensors, triangulation yields the rogue's coordinates. The FSPL equation is the foundation.

**Given Parameters (KTU-Style Numerical Setup):**
- Transmit power of rogue AP: $P_t = 20$ dBm
- Transmit antenna gain: $G_t = 2$ dBi
- Receive antenna gain: $G_r = 5$ dBi
- Operating frequency: $f = 2.412$ GHz (Channel 1)
- Measured RSSI at sensor: $RSSI_{measured} = -45$ dBm
- Miscellaneous cable/connector losses: $L_{misc} = 2$ dB
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Wavelength: $\lambda = c / f$

---

**Step 1 — Write the RSSI equation in the standard form:**

$$RSSI = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values to solve for FSPL:**

$$-45 = 20 + 2 + 5 - FSPL - 2$$

$$-45 = 25 - FSPL$$

$$FSPL = 25 - (-45) = 70 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the FSPL formula in distance form:**

The general form of FSPL in **dB** when distance $d$ is in **meters** and frequency $f$ is in **MHz**:

$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

After consolidating constants with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, the practical engineering form is:

$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$$

where $d$ is in **meters** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

**Step 4 — Convert frequency to MHz:**

$$f = 2.412 \text{ GHz} = 2412 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the simplified FSPL equation:**

$$70 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2412) - 27.55$$

**Step 6 — Compute the log term:**

$$20\log_{10}(2412) = 20 \times 3.3823 = 67.646 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to isolate $\log_{10}(d)$:**

$$20\log_{10}(d) = 70 - 67.646 + 27.55$$

$$20\log_{10}(d) = 29.904$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $d$:**

$$\log_{10}(d) = \frac{29.904}{20} = 1.4952$$

$$d = 10^{1.4952} \approx 31.27 \text{ meters}$$

**Final Forensic Conclusion:**

> The rogue AP is approximately **31.27 meters** away from the sensor along the line-of-sight path. With two additional sensors, investigators can triangulate the exact 2D position to ±2 m accuracy using trilateration algorithms.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: SNR and Channel Capacity Verification

> [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** In KTU exam questions, students are often asked to verify if a rogue AP's link quality supports high-throughput sniffing (e.g., video traffic capture). This requires SNR → Shannon capacity mapping.

**Step 1 — Assume measured values:**
- Signal power at victim device: $S = -52$ dBm
- Noise floor: $N = -95$ dBm

**Step 2 — Convert dBm to linear milliwatts (mW):**
$$P_{mW} = 10^{(P_{dBm}/10)}$$

$$S_{mW} = 10^{(-52/10)} = 10^{-5.2} \approx 6.31 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mW}$$

$$N_{mW} = 10^{(-95/10)} = 10^{-9.5} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute SNR in dB (faster method):**
$$SNR_{dB} = S_{dBm} - N_{dBm} = -52 - (-95) = 43 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Convert SNR to linear ratio:**
$$SNR_{linear} = 10^{(43/10)} = 10^{4.3} \approx 19952.6$$

**Step 5 — Apply Shannon-Hartley for a 20 MHz channel:**
$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 19952.6)$$

$$\log_2(19953.6) = \frac{\log_{10}(19953.6)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{4.300}{0.3010} \approx 14.286 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 14.286 \approx 285.7 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Forensic Interpretation:**

> The rogue AP can theoretically sustain a **285.7 Mbps** capture pipe — more than enough to **live-tap encrypted HTTPS metadata**, perform **DNS poisoning**, and run **Burp Suite** in MITM mode for full session hijack. Detection should therefore prioritize this AP regardless of usage pattern.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Risk Scoring (Forensic Decision Table)

| Indicator | Weight | Trigger Condition | Forensic Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unknown BSSID with duplicate SSID | **0.30** | OUI not in inventory | **Immediate isolation** |
| RSSI ≥ legitimate AP by ≥ 10 dB | **0.20** | Beacon audit log | Investigate physical placement |
| No 802.1X / open / WPA-PSK only | **0.25** | Probe response analysis | Block at WIPS controller |
| Direct LAN connectivity (gateway = corporate) | **0.40** | ARP table cross-ref | **Critical — alert SOC** |
| Channel overlap with corp AP > 50% | **0.15** | Spectrum analyzer | Recommend client re-association |
| Non-standard beacon interval | **0.10** | Frame timing | Tag as suspicious |

**Composite Risk Score:**

$$\text{Risk} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i \in [0, 1.40]$$

$$\text{Risk}_{\text{normalized}} = \frac{\text{Risk}}{1.40} \in [0, 1]$$

If $\text{Risk}_{\text{normalized}} \geq 0.6$, the device is **classified as a rogue AP** and escalated to incident response.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Rogue AP Attack Lifecycle (Forensic Phase Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Performs Reconnaissance] --> B[Identifies Target SSID and BSSID]
    B --> C[Configures Rogue AP or Evil Twin]
    C --> D[Deauthentication Attack on Legitimate Clients]
    D --> E[Victim Devices Re-associate with Rogue AP]
    E --> F[Traffic Interception and MITM]
    F --> G[Credential Harvesting and Session Hijack]
    G --> H[Lateral Movement into Corporate LAN]
    H --> I[Evidence: Beacon Frames, DHCP Logs, ARP Cache]
    I --> J[Forensic Investigator: Image Rogue AP Storage]
    J --> K[Timeline Reconstruction and Attribution]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** Red nodes mark the active attack window; amber marks evidence collection; green marks the closure of the forensic chain.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Wireless Forensics Detection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Corporate Authorized AP] --> B[Wireless Intrusion Prevention System WIPS]
    C[Rogue AP on Corporate LAN] --> B
    D[Evil Twin in Parking Lot] --> B
    B --> E[Sensor Engine: RF Capture]
    E --> F[Anomaly Detection Module]
    F --> G[Signature Database Lookup]
    G --> H{Threat Score above Threshold}
    H -->|Yes| I[Quarantine and Alert SOC]
    H -->|No| J[Baseline Update]
    I --> K[Forensic Image Acquisition]
    K --> L[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    L --> M[Incident Response Ticket]

    style C fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Triangulation Workflow for Locating Rogue AP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rogue AP Beacon Detected] --> B[Sensor S1 RSSI Captured]
    A --> C[Sensor S2 RSSI Captured]
    A --> D[Sensor S3 RSSI Captured]
    B --> E[FSPL Distance Equation]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute d1, d2, d3]
    F --> G[Trilateration Algorithm]
    G --> H[2D Coordinate Output x,y]
    H --> I[Physical Search Grid]
    I --> J[Device Seized and Forensically Imaged]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Forensic Evidence Chain for a Rogue AP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[802.11 Beacon Frames] --> E[Evidence Aggregation]
    B[Probe Request Logs] --> E
    C[DHCP Lease File] --> E
    D[Switchport MAC Table] --> E
    E --> F[Timestamp Normalization]
    F --> G[Attack Window Identification]
    G --> H[Affected Client List]
    H --> I[Legal Chain of Custody Documentation]
    I --> J[Court-Admissible Report]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define a **Rogue Access Point**. How is it different from an **Evil Twin**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Rogue Access Point** is an unauthorized wireless access point installed on a secure network without the knowledge or approval of the network administrator. It bypasses the centralized AAA/RADIUS infrastructure and creates an unmonitored bridge between the wireless medium and the protected wired LAN, violating organizational security policy.

An **Evil Twin** is a specific subtype that mimics a legitimate AP by replicating the **SSID** (and sometimes BSSID) of an authorized network to lure victims into associating with it for **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** credential harvesting. The key difference is intent and topology: a rogue AP is *physically connected to the LAN* (insider/external unauthorized device on premises), whereas an evil twin is *external* and primarily aims to deceive clients wirelessly without necessarily being on the corporate LAN.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> List **any three** techniques used by an attacker to operate a rogue AP covertly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **MAC Address Spoofing:** Cloning the BSSID of an authorized AP's OUI vendor to evade naive WIPS filters.
2. **Reduced Beacon Interval / Channel Hopping:** Using non-standard beacon timing or rapidly cycling across channels to evade spectrum sensors with fixed dwell times.
3. **Physical Concealment:** Disguising the AP inside a printer, behind ceiling tiles, or within a power outlet; sometimes using **directional antennas** to limit RF footprint.
4. *(Optional 4th, for bonus credit)* **Power Tuning:** Lowering transmission power to stay below WIPS sensor detection thresholds while still being visible to nearby victim clients.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **operational workflow of a Rogue AP attack** from reconnaissance to evidence generation. Highlight the role of **deauthentication frames** in forcing victim re-association. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A forensic sensor captures a rogue AP's beacon with the following parameters: $P_t = 18$ dBm, $G_t = 2$ dBi, $G_r = 4$ dBi, $L_{misc} = 1$ dB, $f = 2.437$ GHz, and $RSSI = -50$ dBm. **Calculate the physical distance** of the rogue AP from the sensor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block diagram (1 mark):**

```
[Attacker] → [Recon] → [Provision Rogue AP] → [Deauth Frame] → [Victim Re-associates]
      ↓
[MIMT Capture] → [Credential Harvest] → [Evidence: Beacons, DHCP, ARP]
```

**Step-by-step explanation (6 marks):**

1. **Reconnaissance Phase:** Attacker uses Kismet/Airodump-ng to enumerate all APs. Captures SSID, BSSID, channel, encryption, and OUI vendor.

2. **Provisioning:** Attacker configures rogue AP with matching SSID but higher transmit power to override legitimate beacons. Spoofs MAC address to mimic trusted OUI.

3. **Deauthentication Attack:** Attacker broadcasts **802.11 management frames of subtype 0x000C (deauthentication)** with the spoofed BSSID of the legitimate AP. Clients disconnect and scan for the SSID — finding the rogue with stronger RSSI, they re-associate.

4. **MITM Engagement:** Rogue AP transparently forwards traffic to a real Internet gateway while **logging in promiscuous mode**. Captures credentials, session cookies, and DNS queries.

5. **Lateral Movement (if connected to LAN):** Attacker's Ethernet port bypasses 802.1X if the rogue is not configured for it, allowing direct LAN access.

6. **Evidence Generation:** Beacons are logged by sensors; DHCP lease file on the rogue shows victim MACs; switch MAC address table reveals the rogue's physical switchport — used for triangulation and isolation.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $P_t = 18$ dBm
- $G_t = 2$ dBi
- $G_r = 4$ dBi
- $L_{misc} = 1$ dB
- $f = 2.437$ GHz $= 2437$ MHz
- $RSSI = -50$ dBm

**Step 1 — RSSI equation:**
$$RSSI = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for FSPL:**
$$-50 = 18 + 2 + 4 - FSPL - 1$$
$$-50 = 23 - FSPL$$
$$FSPL = 73 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply FSPL distance formula (d in meters, f in MHz):**
$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$$

**Step 4 — Compute log term:**
$$20\log_{10}(2437) = 20 \times 3.3869 = 67.738 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\log_{10}(d)$:**
$$73 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 67.738 - 27.55$$
$$20\log_{10}(d) = 73 - 67.738 + 27.55 = 32.812$$
$$\log_{10}(d) = 1.6406$$
$$d = 10^{1.6406} \approx 43.72 \text{ meters}$$

**Final Answer:** The rogue AP is approximately **43.72 m** from the sensor.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **classification of rogue APs** in detail. Compare the threat surface of an **insider-installed rogue AP** vs an **external evil twin attack**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe **any four forensic artifacts** that an investigator must preserve from a rogue AP scene to build a court-admissible case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Classification (4 marks):**
1. **Rogue AP (LAN-connected):** Physically plugged into corporate network; bypasses 802.1X.
2. **Evil Twin:** Mimics legitimate SSID; external; MITM-focused.
3. **Soft AP:** Software-emulated (e.g., Windows Hosted Network) on an infected endpoint.
4. **Ad-hoc Node:** Peer-to-peer 802.11 connection outside infrastructure mode.
5. **Misconfigured Authorized AP:** Approved device but using default or weak settings.

**Insider vs External (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Insider Rogue AP | External Evil Twin |
|---|---|---|
| Physical access to LAN | **Yes** (backdoor) | **No** (wireless only) |
| Primary goal | Persistent network access | Credential harvesting |
| Detection difficulty | Harder (passes some filters) | Easier (anomalous SSID behavior) |
| Forensics complexity | High (chain-of-custody on internal asset) | Lower (external device seizure) |
| Legal implication | Internal disciplinary + IT Act §66 | IT Act §66, §66C, §66D |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks
