1. **802.11 Beacon and Probe Response Frames (2 marks):** Contain BSSID, SSID, channel, supported rates, vendor OUI, and timestamp. Used to identify the rogue and reconstruct the timing of its presence.

2. **DHCP Lease File from the Rogue (2 marks):** Lists MAC addresses of all victim clients that obtained an IP from the rogue. This becomes the **victim identification artifact**.

3. **Switchport MAC Address Table (1.5 marks):** Reveals the physical switchport where the rogue's Ethernet MAC is learned — used for **physical location** and **quarantine**.

4. **Rogue AP's Internal Storage Image (1.5 marks):** Flash/SD card imaged using **dd** or **FTK Imager** in write-blocked mode; reveals configuration, captured packets (PCAP), and any installed persistence scripts.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Read Carefully Before Exam)**
> 1. **Forgetting units in FSPL calculations** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks if $d$ is in km vs m is ambiguous. Always state the unit clearly.
> 2. **Confusing dBm with dBi** — $P_t$ is in **dBm** (absolute power); $G_t$/$G_r$ are in **dBi** (gain relative to isotropic radiator). Mixing them is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 3. **Omitting the deauthentication frame subtype** — When explaining forced re-association, you **must cite 802.11 management frame subtype 0x000C** (deauth). Vague answers like "attacker disconnects clients" get 0/2.
> 4. **Not distinguishing Evil Twin from Rogue AP** — Examiners explicitly test this distinction. A generic answer loses 1.5 marks.
> 5. **Skipping chain-of-custody in forensics answers** — Any forensic procedure answer without mention of **chain of custody, write-blocking, or hash (SHA-256) verification** is incomplete.
> 6. **Wrong log conversion in SNR** — Converting dBm to linear: $P_{mW} = 10^{(P_{dBm}/10)}$ — students often use $10^{x/20}$ (which is voltage, not power). This is the **#1 calculation error** in KTU wireless papers.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Rogue AP** = unauthorized AP on LAN; **Evil Twin** = external SSID impersonator — *never* use these terms interchangeably.
- Default **beacon interval** = **100 ms**; default **transmit power** ≈ **20 dBm**.
- **FSPL formula** (meters, MHz): $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$.
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — always specify bandwidth in Hz.
- **SNR threshold** for stable association = **25 dB**; detection sensitivity threshold = **−80 dBm**.
- **Non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels** = **1, 6, 11**.
- Deauthentication = **802.11 management frame subtype 0x000C**.
- Forensics artifacts: **beacons, DHCP leases, ARP cache, switch MAC tables, full disk image with SHA-256 hash**.
- **Always** mention **chain of custody**, **write-blocker**, and **two-person integrity rule** in forensic procedure answers.
- Detection tools: **Kismet** (passive), **NetStumbler** (Windows, legacy), **Aircrack-ng suite** (active), **WIPS sensors** (enterprise).
- **Legal references (India):** IT Act 2000/2008 amendments — Sections **66 (computer-related offenses), 66C (identity theft), 66D (cheating by personation), 66F (cyber terrorism)**; for forensic admissibility, **IT Act §79 + Indian Evidence Act §65B (electronic evidence)**.
- **OUI lookup** (e.g., `macvendors.com`) is the *first step* in attributing a rogue BSSID to its manufacturer — frequently tested in 3-mark questions.
- **Kismet channel hop time** is the limiting factor for full-bandwidth detection — understand the trade-off between dwell time and detection latency.
- The **risk score formula** $\sum w_i \cdot x_i$ (normalized to 1) is a common KTU exam structure for 7-mark analytical questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phishing
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Phishing # 1. Phishing — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU-Aligned)** > **Phishing** is a category of *social-engineering cyber attack* in which an adversary masquerades as a legitimate, trusted entity (e.g., a bank, university, e-commerce vendor, or government agency) and uses digital communication channels — primarily **electronic mail (SMTP)**, but also short-message-service (SMS), voice (VoIP/PSTN), and **instant messaging** — to manipulate a human target into performing a security-compromising action, such as disclosing credentials, installing malware, or transferring funds. > > From a **Network Forensics** perspective, phishing is the *delivery vector* (or "initial-access vector") whose artifacts — emails, headers, embedded URLs, attachments, redirected traffic, and exploited web forms — must be **acquired, preserved, examined, analyzed, and reported** under the standard ISO/IEC 27037 digital-evidence handling model. > [!NOTE] **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)** > The 2024 scheme expects a working knowledge of: > - Phishing types and life-cycle (vishing, smishing, spear-phishing, whaling, clone-phishing). > - Email-header forensic analysis (RFC 5322, Received chains, DKIM/DMARC/SPF). > - URL and domain forensic analysis (typosquatting, homograph attacks, IDN). > - Forensic tools: `whois`, `dig`, `nslookup`, Maltego, PhishTank, VirusTotal. > - Investigative workflow, chain of custody, and case-report drafting. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Faux Fisherman" Imagine a man standing by a river holding a fishing rod. The line is baited with a juicy worm. When a fish bites, the fisherman reels it in. **Phishing is the digital twin of this act:** | Element in Analogy | Real-World Equivalent | Forensic Implication | |---|---|---| | The river | The internet / corporate mail gateway | Capture traffic at the gateway | | The worm | The lure (link, attachment, QR code) | Hash and quarantine | | The fishing rod | Spoofed sender identity | Trace via email-header chain | | The fisherman | Threat actor (often part of a C2 cluster) | Pivot to other infrastructure | | The biting fish | The victim user | Interview for "live" evidence | | The fishing net | The mail-server log files | Preserve under chain-of-custody | The fish is *not forced* to bite — it is *tricked* into biting. This is the social-engineering core of phishing. ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Phishing (Board-Exam Essential) > [!NOTE] Phishing is **not one technique** but a *family*. Examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between X and Y."* ### 1.3.1 By Delivery Channel 1. **Email Phishing** — Bulk, low-targeting emails sent to thousands of recipients. Lures are usually credential-harvesting pages. 2. **Smishing (SMS Phishing)** — Text messages with shortened URLs (e.g., `bit.ly/3xY...`). Exploits trust in mobile carriers. 3. **Vishing (Voice Phishing)** — Telephony-based; live or AI-driven voice bots imitate bank officers. Often paired with caller-ID spoofing. 4. **Social-Media Phishing** — Fake giveaways, "you've been tagged" notices on LinkedIn, X, Instagram. 5. **QR-Code Phishing ("Quishing")** — Phishing URL encoded into a QR image; bypasses URL scanners that do not decode images. ### 1.3.2 By Targeting Sophistication 1. **Bulk / Mass Phishing** — Spray-and-pray; minimal customization. 2. **Spear Phishing** — Targeted at a *specific* person or department; uses OSINT (LinkedIn, company site, breached data). 3. **Whaling** — Spear-phish aimed at C-suite executives (CEO/CFO). Often precursor to **BEC (Business Email Compromise)** and **wire-transfer fraud**. 4. **Clone Phishing** — A legitimate, previously delivered email is copied; the original attachment/link is replaced with a malicious one and re-sent from a spoofed address. 5. **Business-Email-Compromise (BEC)** — Adversary *fully compromises* a real mailbox (no spoofing) and issues fraudulent instructions internally; highest-$$$ loss category per FBI IC3 reports. 6. **Pharming** — DNS-cache poisoning / hosts-file tampering so the legitimate URL resolves to a malicious IP. *Phishing without a lure message.* ### 1.3.3 By Technical Lure - **Credential-harvesting page** (HTML form mimicking login). - **Malicious attachment** (Office macro, LNK, HTA, ISO, OneNote `.one`). - **Drive-by download** triggered when the email link is opened. - **OAuth consent phishing** ("App XYZ wants access to your mailbox…"). ## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics (Highlight for Numerical Questions) > [!IMPORTANT] KTU examiners may frame a 3-mark question as *"State the metrics used to evaluate anti-phishing solutions."* Memorize the following. - **True-Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall (R)**: $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ - **False-Positive Rate (FPR)**: $$ FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} $$ - **Precision (P)**: $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ - **F1-Score (harmonic mean of P and R)**: $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} $$ - **Phishing-Bait Click-Through Rate (CTR)**: $$ CTR_{\text{phish}} = \frac{\text{Unique clicks on lure}}{\text{Unique recipients}} \times 100\,\% $$ Industry-accepted baseline CTR for *unaware* recipients ranges from **15 % to 30 %** in published corporate studies. ## 1.5 Why Phishing Matters in *Network* Forensics Phishing is the **#1 initial-access vector** for ransomware, credential theft, and data-exfiltration. Once delivered, it triggers a chain of *network artifacts* the investigator must reconstruct: 1. Inbound SMTP connection (TCP/25 or TCP/587) — visible in mail-server logs. 2. Outbound HTTP/S request to the lure URL — visible in proxy / firewall / NetFlow. 3. DNS resolution of the lure domain — visible in passive-DNS, Resolver logs. 4. Inbound callback (C2 beacon) from the executed payload — visible in IDS/IPS, EDR, PCAP. 5. Lateral movement and exfiltration — visible in switch SPAN / NAC / DLP logs. > [!NOTE] The forensic chain therefore is: **E-mail → URL → DNS → IP → Payload → C2 → Lateral movement**. Mastering phishing forensics means mastering this chain end-to-end. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Phishing Email — RFC 5322 Layer Cake A phishing email is technically a **MIME** document compliant with RFC 5322. The forensic examiner must be able to read every layer. ### 2.1.1 The Header Block (the "Who, Where, When, How") A *real* header has dozens of `Received:` lines forming a chain from the receiver back to the sender's MUA. Investigators reverse-walk this chain to identify the **originating IP** and the **mail submission server**. > [!NOTE] KTU 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on **header analysis**. A header question can be worth **7–14 marks**. **Key header fields a forensic examiner *must* know:** | Field (RFC) | Forensic Meaning | Spoof-Indicator | |---|---|---| | `From:` | Visible sender (display name often forged) | Mismatch between display name and envelope | | `Return-Path:` | Envelope sender (bounce address) | Differs from `From:` ⇒ spoof | | `Reply-To:` | Where replies go | Differs from `From:` ⇒ pretext | | `Received:` (chain) | Each hop adds a line at the *top* | Look for unusual Helo/EHLO strings | | `Authentication-Results:` | SPF, DKIM, DMARC verdict | `dkim=fail`, `spf=fail`, `dmarc=fail` | | `X-Originating-IP:` | Originating client IP (added by some webmail) | Often the only true sender IP for web-mail-based attackers | | `Message-ID:` | Unique identifier (FQDN of sender's MTA) | Inconsistent with the purported domain ⇒ spoof | | `DKIM-Signature:` | Asymmetric signature over body + selected headers | Public-key lookup in DNS — if missing, suspect | | `ARC-Seal:` / `ARC-Message-Signature:` | Authenticated Received Chain for forwarding | Used to validate intermediary signers | > [!IMPORTANT] **DKIM-Signature Anatomy (examinable)** > The signature is a tuple $(d, s, h, bh, b)$ where: > - $d$ = Signing Domain Identifier (SDID) > - $s$ = Selector (used to fetch the public key from `._domainkey..`) > - $h$ = Signed header fields list > - $bh$ = Base64 hash of canonicalized body > - $b$ = Base64 signature over $h$ and $bh$ with the private key > > If the verifier recomputes the body hash and signature with the public key from DNS and they match, DKIM passes. ### 2.1.2 The Body Block — What the Victim Sees - **Plain text + HTML alternative** (MIME `multipart/alternative`). - HTML may contain: hidden text (white-on-white CSS), JavaScript redirects, broken-image pixel tracking (``), and the **lure URL** that the victim must click. - The lure URL is usually **display-text** ≠ **hyperlink target**: `HTML: https://www.legitbank.com/login` The *real* URL is in `href=`; the visible blue text is the *deception*. ## 2.2 URL & Domain Forensic Analysis ### 2.2.1 The Lure URL Layers A forensic examiner must parse a URL into the following layers (RFC 3986): $$ \underbrace{\text{https}}_{\text{scheme}} :// \underbrace{\text{sub.attacker.example.co}}_{\text{host}} / \underbrace{\text{login.php?id=42}}_{\text{path \& query}} \;\#\;\underbrace{\text{frag}}_{\text{fragment}} $$ - **Scheme** — `https` does *not* mean safe. Attackers freely obtain free DV-TLS certs via Let's Encrypt. - **Host** — The most forensically significant portion. Investigators run `whois`, `dig`, and `nslookup`. - **Path & Query** — May encode campaign IDs, victim e-mail, or session tokens. ### 2.2.2 Common URL-Obfuscation Techniques (Board Favourite) | Technique | Example | Forensic Clue | |---|---|---| | Typosquatting | `paypa1.com` (digit `1` for `l`) | Visual inspection + Levenshtein distance | | Homograph / IDN | `pаypal.com` (Cyrillic `а`) | Punycode prefix `xn--` reveals Unicode | | Sub-domain confusion | `paypal.com.attacker.tk` | Real apex domain is the *rightmost* registrable label | | URL shortener | `bit.ly/3xY…` | Use `curl -I` or un-shortener services | | `@` trick | `https://legit.com@attacker.tk/` | Browsers ignore everything before `@` | | Open redirect | `legit.com/r?u=attacker.tk` | Pivot through known legitimate domains | | Punycode (IDN) | `xn--pypal-loa.com` | Decode with `idn2` | | Data URI / JS encoding | `data:text/html;base64,…` | No network call needed — fully client-side | ### 2.2.3 Homograph Detection — Mathematical Formulation > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 14-Mark Favourite**: *"Explain homograph attack and how it is detected."* A homograph attack substitutes one or more **Cyrillic**, **Greek**, or other visually identical Unicode characters for the Latin originals. The defender measures the visual similarity of two glyphs $g_a, g_b$ using a normalized pixel-overlap metric: $$ \text{Sim}(g_a, g_b) = \frac{\vert g_a \cap g_b \vert_{\text{pixels}}}{\max(\vert g_a \vert, \vert g_b \vert)} \;\in\; [0, 1] $$ If $\text{Sim}(g_a, g_b) \ge \tau$ (with $\tau$ typically **0.92** in published models) **and** the two glyphs map to *different* Unicode code-points $c_a \ne c_b$, the URL is flagged as a probable homograph. The complementary metric used in practice is the **URL-Domain Edit Distance** (Levenshtein) to a known brand's domain: $$ d_{\text{edit}}(u, b) = \text{min ops to transform } u \text{ into } b $$ with ops $\in \{\text{insert}, \text{delete}, \text{substitute}\}$. A small $d_{\text{edit}}$ to a top-1k brand and a low cosine-similarity in the WHOIS feature space are the two features that feed a phishing-classifier ML model. ## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!NOTE] Use `\vert` inside table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table. | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Engineering Use | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | TPR (Recall) | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detector sensitivity | $0 \le R \le 1$ | | FPR | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False-alarm rate | $0 \le FPR \le 1$ | | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Trust in positive alerts | $0 \le P \le 1$ | | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced detector score | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ | | Click-Through Rate | $CTR = \frac{\text{unique clicks}}{\text{unique recipients}}$ | Lure effectiveness | $15$–$30\,\%$ baseline | | Glyph Similarity | $\text{Sim}(g_a, g_b) = \frac{\vert g_a \cap g_b \vert_{\text{pixels}}}{\max(\vert g_a \vert, \vert g_b \vert)}$ | Homograph detection | Threshold $\tau \approx 0.92$ | | Edit Distance | $d_{\text{edit}}(u, b) = \min \text{ops}$ | Typosquat detection | $1$–$3$ for typical squats | | DKIM Verify | Match $(bh, b)$ with public key from DNS `$._domainkey.` | E-mail integrity | `pass` / `fail` | | SPF Verdict | Compare `MAIL FROM` envelope to authorized senders in SPF-record | Sender-IP legitimacy | `pass` / `fail` / `softfail` | | DMARC Verdict | Aligned DKIM $\wedge$ aligned SPF | Policy enforcement | `none` / `quarantine` / `reject` | | Bayesian Prior | $P(\text{Phish} \mid \text{Features}) \propto P(\text{Features} \mid \text{Phish}) \cdot P(\text{Phish})$ | Spam filter classification | Odds $\ge 0.5$ ⇒ phish | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mail-server administrators** tune DKIM, SPF, and DMARC records to *prove* senders' identity and force alignment. - **SOC analysts** pivot from a single phishing e-mail to the full attack graph using a SIEM (Splunk, ELK) by correlating SMTP, DNS, HTTP, and EDR events. - **Law-enforcement forensic labs** use RFC 5322 headers + IP WHOIS + RIR allocation data to build legally admissible evidence chains. - **End-user browsers** (Chrome, Firefox) apply Safe-Browsing feeds (Google, PhishTank) to block known phishing hosts in real time. ## 2.5 The Five-Stage Phishing Attack Life-Cycle (Examiner-Friendly) 1. **Reconnaissance (OSINT)** — Attacker scrapes LinkedIn / corporate site / breach dumps. 2. **Weaponization** — Crafting the lure: a spoofed sender, an HTML clone, a malicious attachment, a C2 URL. 3. **Delivery** — Sending the e-mail (SMTP), SMS, voice call, or social-media DM. 4. **Exploitation** — Victim clicks → credential disclosure *or* payload execution. 5. **Installation / Action** — Session-hijack, malware implant, BEC, lateral movement, exfiltration. > [!NOTE] Sometimes an additional **C2** and **Actions-on-Objectives** stage (Lockheed-Martin Cyber Kill-Chain) is included; the FBI's IC3 reporting collapses the chain into **"initial access → fraud / theft"** for BEC cases. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through & Forensic Procedure > [!IMPORTANT] The following is the **canonical forensic procedure** the KTU board expects a student to *write out* in a 14-mark answer. Every transition below is explicit — no step is skipped. ## 3.1 Worked Example — A Phishing Header From End-to-End Consider the following (trimmed) raw e-mail header. The student is asked: *"Identify the originating IP, the authentication verdict, and the most likely spoof indicator."* ``` Return-Path: Received: from mx.legitbank.com (mx.legitbank.com [203.0.113.45]) by mail.victimcorp.in (Postfix) with ESMTPS id A5C2E for ; Tue, 14 May 2024 09:12:43 +0530 (IST) Received: from suspicious-relay.example.tk ([198.51.100.77]) by mx.legitbank.com with ESMTP id 7QQ41 for ; Tue, 14 May 2024 09:12:41 +0530 Authentication-Results: mx.legitbank.com; spf=fail (mx.legitbank.com: domain of bounce@suspicious-relay.example.tk does not designate 198.51.100.77 as permitted sender) smtp.mailfrom=bounce@suspicious-relay.example.tk; dkim=fail (signature did not verify); dmarc=fail (p=reject sp=reject dis=none) header.from=secure-update@legitbank.com From: "LegitBank Security" Reply-To: support@help-secure.tk Subject: URGENT: Your account will be locked in 24 hours Message-ID: <88f3aac@help-secure.tk> DKIM-Signature: d=help-secure.tk; s=default; h=from:subject:date; bh=Z9H3k…; b=Q2x1… ``` ### Step 1 — Identify the Authentication Verdict $$ \text{Auth} = \big(\text{SPF} = \text{fail}\big) \;\wedge\; \big(\text{DKIM} = \text{fail}\big) \;\wedge\; \big(\text{DMARC} = \text{fail}\big) $$ > **Result:** All three fail → the message is **definitely not** from the claimed domain `legitbank.com`. The DMARC policy is `p=reject` so a properly configured receiver should *bounce* it. ### Step 2 — Reverse-Walk the `Received:` Chain (Read Bottom-Up) The forensic rule is: **"The topmost `Received:` line is the LAST hop; the bottommost is the FIRST hop."** $$ \text{Hop}_1 = \underbrace{198.51.100.77}_{\text{attacker}} \;\to\; \text{Hop}_2 = 203.0.113.45 \;(\text{mx.legitbank.com}) \;\to\; \text{Hop}_3 = \text{mail.victimcorp.in} $$ The *first* untrusted IP in the chain is **198.51.100.77** (RFC 1918-style documentation IP; in a real case it would be a routable v4). The *true originating client* would be one IP hop earlier, often found in `X-Originating-IP` (not shown here). ### Step 3 — Domain-Forensic Pivot on the Suspicious Domains Run the following commands (knowledge-check, not code execution): - `whois help-secure.tk` — TK is a free ccTLD; ownership likely privacy-proxied. - `dig +short A help-secure.tk` — resolves to attacker IP. - `dig TXT suspicious-relay.example.tk` — check for SPF if any. - `dig TXT _dmarc.legitbank.com` — confirms receiver-side policy `p=reject`. ### Step 4 — Cross-Reference Against Brand Impersonation The lure claims to be **LegitBank**. Compare: - Apex domain in `From:` = `legitbank.com` (real bank). - Apex domain in `Return-Path:` = `suspicious-relay.example.tk`. - Apex domain in `Message-ID:` = `help-secure.tk`. A genuine bank e-mail would have **all three** apex domains identical. The mismatch $\Rightarrow$ **phishing / spoof**. ### Step 5 — Compute the Lure Risk Score Use the following Bayesian-style risk score (synthetic for examination): $$ \text{Risk} = w_1 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{spf=fail}} + w_2 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{dkim=fail}} + w_3 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{dmarc=fail}} + w_4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{reply-to diff}} + w_5 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{newly-registered domain}} $$ with weights $w_1=0.15$, $w_2=0.20$, $w_3=0.25$, $w_4=0.20$, $w_5=0.20$. All five indicators are true, hence $$ \text{Risk} = 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 $$ The message is scored at the **maximum** phishing probability. Action: **quarantine + user-awareness notification + IOC push to SIEM**. ## 3.2 Full Forensic Workflow — Six-Phase Model > [!NOTE] A 14-mark "explain the forensic procedure for a phishing investigation" question maps directly onto these six phases. ### Phase 1 — Identification & First Response - The SOC receives a user complaint *or* a SIEM rule fires (e.g., "SPF-fail + lookalike-domain"). - The mail-server is **isolated from network perspective** (not powered off) to preserve volatile evidence. - A triage ticket is opened and assigned to an investigator. ### Phase 2 — Preservation (Chain of Custody) - The original `.eml` file is exported with **all headers intact** (Roundcube / Outlook / Gmail "show original"). - A **SHA-256 hash** is computed and recorded: $$ H_{\text{file}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{eml}) $$ - The hash is signed and entered in the case-log book. - Server-side mailboxes are exported as **PST/OST** or **mbox** with timestamps and an MD5 hash. ### Phase 3 — Examination (Header + Body + URL + Attachment) - **Header analysis** as shown in §3.1. - **URL extraction** from HTML body (and from any `Text/Plain` part). - **Attachment analysis**: * Static: `file`, `exiftool`, `olevba` (Office macro extraction), `capa` (capability analysis). * Dynamic: detonate in **Cuckoo Sandbox** or **ANY.RUN** in a sealed VLAN; capture PCAP, memory dump, dropped files. - **Indicator-of-Compromise (IOC) extraction**: * Hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256). * Domains, IPs, URLs, JA3 fingerprints, mutexes, registry keys. ### Phase 4 — Analysis (Correlation & Attribution) - Pivot on extracted IOCs in threat-intel platforms: **VirusTotal**, **AlienVault OTX**, **MISP**, **PhishTank**, **URLScan.io**. - Build a **timeline** (mail received → user click → payload C2 → lateral movement) using SIEM. - Cluster with other incidents to identify a **campaign**. - Possible attribution: infrastructure, malware family, TTPs (mapped to **MITRE ATT&CK** e.g., T1566.001 Spear-phishing Attachment). ### Phase 5 — Documentation (Examiner's Report) A KTU-style forensic report includes: 1. Case identifier, investigator, date. 2. Source of evidence (PCAP path, mailbox path, hash). 3. Tools used with version numbers. 4. Methodology (RFC 5322, NIST SP 800-86). 5. Findings (suspicious headers, IOC list, screenshot of lure page from archive.org / urlscan). 6. Conclusion. 7. Recommendations (user training, DMARC `p=reject`, URL rewriting, sandbox attachments). ### Phase 6 — Presentation / Legal - Maintain chain-of-custody log for court admissibility. - Provide a clear, jargon-light executive summary for management. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Detecting Homograph URLs **Problem (3–4 mark question style):** A user receives an email with the displayed link "https://www.рaypal.com/login". The Punycode form is `xn--pypl-loa-1ve.com`. The legitimate domain is `paypal.com`. Compute the URL-domain edit distance and explain whether the message is a homograph attack. ### Step 1 — Convert IDN to Punycode - `рaypal.com` (with Cyrillic `р`, U+0440) becomes `xn--pypl-loa-1ve.com`. ### Step 2 — Compare Apex Domains - Observed apex (after Punycode decode check): the host is *not* `paypal.com`; the *displayed* label is visually identical. ### Step 3 — Edit-Distance to Legitimate Apex - Substitutions needed in apex label `рaypal` vs `paypal`: * Position 1: `р` (Cyrillic, U+0440) vs `p` (Latin, U+0070) ⇒ 1 substitution. - All other positions match. $$ d_{\text{edit}}\big(\text{рaypal}, \text{paypal}\big) = 1 $$ ### Step 4 — Conclusion A $d_{\text{edit}} = 1$ against a top-1k brand, combined with a Punycode (`xn--`) prefix, is **strong evidence of a homograph attack**. The forensic recommendation is to **block the URL at the proxy and notify the user.** ## 3.4 The Bayesian Phishing Classifier (Conceptual Derivation) Many anti-phishing filters use a **naïve Bayes** text classifier. The probability the message is phishing given observed features $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is: $$ P(\text{Phish} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{Phish}) \cdot P(\text{Phish})}{P(\mathbf{x})} $$ Under the naïve-independence assumption, $$ P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{Phish}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid \text{Phish}) $$ The decision rule (using the log-likelihood ratio) is: $$ \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \log \frac{P(\text{Phish} \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(\text{Ham} \mid \mathbf{x})} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \frac{P(x_i \mid \text{Phish})}{P(x_i \mid \text{Ham})} + \log \frac{P(\text{Phish})}{P(\text{Ham})} $$ $$ \text{Classify} = \begin{cases} \text{Phishing}, & \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge 0 \\ \text{Ham (legit)}, & \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) < 0 \end{cases} $$ In practice, a feature $x_i$ might be *"the word 'urgent' appears in the subject"*; its likelihood ratio is learned from a labelled corpus. This is why Bayesian spam filters have been the *de-facto* baseline in mail gateways for two decades. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phishing Attack Life-Cycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Reconnaissance OSINT] --> B[Weaponization] B --> C[Delivery SMTP SMS Voice] C --> D[Exploitation User Click] D --> E[Installation Credential Theft] E --> F[C2 Communication] F --> G[Actions on Objectives Fraud Data Theft Ransomware] G --> H[Network Forensic Acquisition PCAP Logs Mailbox] H --> I[Examination Headers URLs IOCs] I --> J[Correlation and Reporting] ``` ## 4.2 Email Header Forensic Parsing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR RAW[Raw .eml file] --> EXTRACT[Header Extractor] EXTRACT --> R1[From and Return Path] EXTRACT --> R2[Received Chain] EXTRACT --> R3[Authentication Results SPF DKIM DMARC] EXTRACT --> R4[Message ID and DKIM Signature] R2 --> REV[Reverse Walk Bottom Up] REV --> ORIG[Originating IP] R3 --> VERDICT{All Pass} VERDICT -->|Yes| LEGIT[Mark as Legit] VERDICT -->|No| SPOOF[Mark as Spoof] ORIG --> GEOIP[GeoIP and WHOIS Lookup] SPOOF --> IOC[Push IOC to SIEM] GEOIP --> IOC IOC --> REPORT[Examiner Report] LEGIT --> REPORT ``` ## 4.3 Phishing Forensic Investigation Workflow (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Incident Reported by User or SIEM Alert] --> S1[Phase 1 Identification] S1 --> S2[Phase 2 Preservation SHA256 hash Chain of Custody] S2 --> S3[Phase 3 Examination Header URL Attachment] S3 --> S4[Phase 4 Analysis Correlation MITRE ATT and CK] S4 --> S5[Phase 5 Documentation Examiner Report] S5 --> S6[Phase 6 Presentation Legal] S3 --> S3a[Static Tools file exiftool olevba] S3 --> S3b[Dynamic Sandbox Cuckoo ANYRUN] S3a --> S4 S3b --> S4 ``` ## 4.4 Anti-Phishing Defense-in-Depth Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR MAIL[Inbound SMTP] --> GW[Mail Gateway Anti Spam SPF DKIM DMARC] GW --> SCAN[URL Rewriter and Sandbox] SCAN --> USER[End User Inbox] USER --> CLICK{User Clicks} CLICK -->|Malicious| PROXY[Web Proxy and Safe Browsing Check] PROXY -->|Block| DENY[Access Denied] PROXY -->|Allow| SITE[Compromised Site] CLICK -->|Legit| OK[Normal Browsing] USER --> TRAIN[Security Awareness Training] TRAIN --> REDUCE[Reduced CTR over time] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] All questions are mapped to **PECCT864 Module 4** outcomes and follow the **2024-scheme mark distribution** (3-mark Part A, 14-mark Part B with internal choice). ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between phishing, spear-phishing, and whaling with one example each.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** - **Phishing:** A *mass-distributed*, non-targeted e-mail attack claiming to be from a trusted entity (e.g., a generic "your PayPal account is locked" e-mail sent to 100 k recipients). It relies on volume to find *any* victim. - **Spear-phishing:** *Targeted* at a specific individual or role (e.g., an e-mail to *"alice.chen@finance.victimcorp.in"* crafted using her LinkedIn title and recent conference talk). The lure uses personal context. - **Whaling:** Spear-phishing aimed at *executive leadership* (CFO, CEO, VP) with the goal of authorizing wire transfers or accessing crown-jewel data (e.g., a forged CFO e-mail instructing "send USD 4.2 M to vendor-X"). ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is DKIM? Explain the role of the DKIM-Signature header in e-mail authentication.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** **DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM)** is an *asymmetric* e-mail authentication framework defined in **RFC 6376**. The sending MTA cryptographically signs a *canonicalized* subset of the message (selected headers + body hash) using a private key. The public key is published in DNS as a TXT record at `._domainkey.`. The receiver re-canonicalizes the same fields, re-computes the body hash, and verifies the signature with the public key. A passing DKIM signature proves that the message body and signed headers have not been altered in transit *and* that the sender controls the signing domain. > [!WARNING] **Examiner Pitfall** — Many students write *"DKIM encrypts the mail."* It does **not** encrypt; it only **signs**. You lose **1 mark** for this conflation. ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] KTU provides an internal choice — a student must answer *one* of Question A or Question B. Both are presented below for self-practice. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **`(a)` Explain the complete life-cycle of a phishing attack with a neat diagram. List the network artifacts a forensic investigator must collect at each stage. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The phishing life-cycle follows the **Lockheed-Martin Cyber Kill-Chain** adapted to phishing: 1. **Reconnaissance (OSINT):** Attacker enumerates targets via LinkedIn, company website, breach dumps (e.g., HIBP). *Network artifact:* nothing on the *victim* network yet — but the attacker may have probed LinkedIn scraping infrastructure. 2. **Weaponization:** Attacker registers a typosquat domain, builds an HTML credential-harvest page, sometimes with a valid TLS cert. *Network artifact:* the new domain appears in passive-DNS databases before the attack. 3. **Delivery (SMTP / SMS / Voice):** The lure e-mail is sent. *Network artifact:* SMTP connection logs at the recipient's gateway (source IP, EHLO string, Message-ID, `From:` envelope). 4. **Exploitation (User click):** The user clicks the lure. *Network artifact:* outbound HTTPS request from the user's IP to the lure domain; DNS query log; proxy / firewall log. 5. **Installation / Credential Capture:** The user submits credentials to the cloned page; the attacker logs them. *Network artifact:* the credentials appear in attacker-controlled backend logs; the victim's session cookie is set. 6. **Command-and-Control (C2):** The payload (if present) beacons home. *Network artifact:* recurring outbound traffic to a fixed IP/domain; unusual JA3/TLS fingerprint. 7. **Actions on Objectives:** Lateral movement, BEC, ransomware staging. *Network artifact:* SMB/LDAP/RDP logs, abnormal DNS, large data uploads. > *(Diagrams: see §4.1 of these notes — examiners will accept any equivalent flowchart with these seven stages.)* **`(b)` A phishing e-mail arrives at `mail.victimcorp.in`. The mail-server logs show a `Received:` chain (bottom up) of `[203.0.113.45] → [198.51.100.77] → 192.0.2.13`. The e-mail's `Message-ID` host is `help-secure.tk`, but the visible `From:` is ``. `Authentication-Results` reads `spf=fail, dkim=fail, dmarc=fail`. Identify the originating IP, the spoof indicator, and the recommended next action. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Originating IP:** Reading the `Received:` chain from the *bottom* (oldest hop) up — the bottommost untrusted IP is the *first* one. The first untrusted IP is therefore **198.51.100.77** (the second hop is the victim mail-server, the third hop is the relaying `mx.legitbank.com`). The `X-Originating-IP` (if present) is preferred but not available here. (2 marks) 2. **Spoof indicators (pick at least three):** - `From:` apex (`legitbank.com`) ≠ `Message-ID` apex (`help-secure.tk`) ⇒ brand impersonation. (2 marks) - All three authentication checks failed ⇒ the receiver is **not** what the message claims to be. (1 mark) - The `Reply-To` (not shown) is typically a different domain in such cases; absent the field, the three apex mismatch + authentication fails are sufficient. (1 mark) 3. **Recommended next action:** Quarantine the message, push the IOCs (`help-secure.tk`, `198.51.100.77`, e-mail hash) to the SIEM, alert the user, block the domain at the DNS firewall, and update user-awareness training. (1 mark) > [!WARNING] **Examiner Pitfall (Question A)** > - Many students reverse-walk the `Received:` chain in the *wrong direction* (top-to-bottom) and end up citing the receiver's IP as the origin. You lose **2 marks** for this. > - Confusing `envelope sender` (Return-Path) with `header From` is a common trap. Both must be reconciled. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **`(a)` With a suitable diagram, explain the working of SPF, DKIM, and DMARC. How do they jointly prevent phishing? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** - **SPF (Sender Policy Framework, RFC 7208):** The domain owner publishes a DNS TXT record listing the IP addresses authorized to send mail for that domain. On receipt, the MTA compares the *envelope sender's* domain and the *originating IP* against the SPF record. Verdict: `pass`, `fail`, `softfail`, `none`, `neutral`, `permerror`, `temperror`. - **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail, RFC 6376):** The sending MTA signs selected headers and the body using a private key; the public key is published in DNS. The receiver re-computes the hash and verifies the signature. - **DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance, RFC 7489):** A *policy* layer that says: *"If either SPF or DKIM passes **and** the identifier (header `From` domain) **aligns** with the authenticated domain, apply this policy."* The policy is `none`, `quarantine`, or `reject`. - **Joint prevention:** DMARC ties SPF and DKIM together and tells receivers what to *do* with failures. SPF alone fails when the e-mail is forwarded; DKIM alone fails when the body is altered; combined under DMARC alignment, the *visible* `From:` domain is bound to an *authenticated* identity, dramatically reducing spoof. *(Diagram: see §4.2 of these notes — block diagram showing SPF check, DKIM verify, DMARC alignment, and final verdict.)* **`(b)` A user receives an SMS: "Dear Customer, your KYC is pending. Click http://bit.ly/3xY1zp to avoid account suspension." The full URL expands to `http://hdfc-update-2024.duckdns.org/kyc?id=9181234567`. Identify the indicators of compromise (IOCs) and explain why each is suspicious. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | IOC | Why Suspicious | |---|---| | `bit.ly` short-link | URL shortener **hides** the real destination; legitimate banks never use them. (1 mark) | | `http://` (not `https`) | Plain HTTP — credentials would be sent in clear; banks use TLS. (1 mark) | | `hdfc-update-2024.duckdns.org` | Apex domain is `duckdns.org` (a free dynamic-DNS provider), **not** `hdfc.bank.in`. (1 mark) | | Sub-domain tricks the eye: `hdfc-update-2024` | Brand name appears in the *sub*-domain, but the *apex* is the attacker. (1 mark) | | `duckdns.org` is a free-DNS service | Mass-registered by attackers; commonly blacklisted. (1 mark) | | Query string carries the user's phone number `?id=9181234567` | Per-victim URL enables the attacker to *track* which number clicked — a phishing-kit feature. (1 mark) | | Urgency + fear language | Social-engineering cue: "avoid account suspension" pressures quick action. (1 mark) | > [!WARNING] **Examiner Pitfall (Question B)** > - Many students answer "the URL is suspicious" and stop. You must enumerate **multiple** IOCs, each with a justification, to get full marks. > - Confusing `duckdns.org` (apex) with `hdfc-update-2024` (sub-domain) is a 1-mark deduction. ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > 1. **Always read `Received:` chains bottom-up.** Top-down is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in a 7-mark sub-question. > 2. **DKIM ≠ encryption.** DKIM is *signing*, not encryption. Do not claim the "body is encrypted." > 3. **Apex vs. sub-domain.** The *real* domain is the *rightmost* label pair (e.g., `legitbank.com`), not the leftmost. > 4. **DMARC alignment.** Many students describe SPF/DKIM but forget to mention the **alignment** step (header `From` must match the authenticated domain). DMARC = authentication *and* alignment, not just authentication. > 5. **Forensic chain of custody.** Examiners *love* to ask "How will this evidence be admitted in court?" The answer is "SHA-256 hash, signed chain-of-custody log, write-blocker on the storage device." ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision List** — read this aloud 5 minutes before the exam. - **Phishing is social engineering** delivered over a *digital channel* whose goal is **credential theft, malware delivery, or fraud**. - **Seven types** to memorize: Email, Smishing, Vishing, Spear, Whaling, Clone, BEC, Pharming, Quishing. - **Authentication triple:** * **SPF** = IP authorization (DNS TXT). * **DKIM** = cryptographic signature (RFC 6376). * **DMARC** = policy *and* identifier alignment (RFC 7489). - **Header analysis gold-standard steps:** 1. Read `Authentication-Results` first. 2. Reverse-walk `Received:` chain from bottom to top. 3. Compare `From:`, `Return-Path:`, `Message-ID`, `Reply-To` apex domains. 4. Look for `X-Originating-IP`. - **URL analysis:** extract apex domain, check Punycode (`xn--`), compute $d_{\text{edit}}$ to brand, run `whois` + `dig`, scan with VirusTotal. - **Metrics to know:** $R$, $P$, $F_1$, $FPR$, $CTR$, glyph similarity, edit distance. - **Forensic workflow (6 phases):** Identification → Preservation (SHA-256) → Examination (header + URL + attachment) → Analysis (SIEM correlation, MITRE ATT&CK) → Documentation → Presentation. - **Tools to name-drop in an answer (validates your knowledge):** `whois`, `dig`, `nslookup`, `theHarvester`, Maltego, PhishTank, URLScan, VirusTotal, Cuckoo, ANY.RUN, MISP, Splunk, ELK. - **Attack life-cycle:** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions. - **Realistic CTR baseline:** **15–30 %** for *unaware* users; drops below **5 %** after 6 months of awareness training. - **Best-practice mitigations:** DMARC `p=reject`, MFA, sandboxing attachments, URL rewriting, security awareness training, Safe-Browsing feeds. - **Examiner-trap terms** to use *correctly*: "apex domain", "alignment", "chain of custody", "indicator of compromise", "passive DNS", "JA3 fingerprint".

topics.title: Wireless Jamming (Denial-of-Service Attacks)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Jamming (Denial-of-Service Attacks)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wireless Jamming** is a deliberate form of **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** attack in which a malicious entity (the *jammer*) transmits interfering radio frequency (RF) signals on a wireless communication channel to disrupt, degrade, or completely block legitimate communication between authorized wireless nodes. From a **Network Forensics** perspective, jamming constitutes a Layer-1 (Physical Layer) and Layer-2 (MAC Layer) attack whose artifacts must be reconstructed post-incident using signal intelligence, protocol logs, and statistical anomaly detection.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, wireless jamming is classified as a **Passive-to-Active Hybrid Threat** because the attacker need not be authenticated to the target network — only physical or RF proximity to the wireless medium is required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wireless jamming is treated as a **Physical-Layer Intrusion** under Module 4 — "Wireless Security and Forensics." Students must be able to (a) classify jamming models, (b) compute signal degradation metrics, and (c) identify forensic indicators of jamming in captured RF traces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shouting in a Library"

Imagine two people, **Alice** and **Bob**, whispering a secret in a quiet library. Their conversation is private because the ambient noise floor is low. Now imagine a third person, **Mallory**, walks in and starts **shouting loudly at a constant pitch**. Alice and Bob can no longer hear each other — Mallory hasn't stolen any information, but she has rendered the communication channel **unusable**. Mallory is the *jammer*, the library is the *wireless medium (air)*, and the constant shouting is *RF interference energy*.

- If Mallory shouts **continuously** → **Constant Jammer**
- If Mallory waits for Alice to speak, then **interrupts** → **Reactive Jammer**
- If Mallory plays **random music** at intervals → **Random Jammer**
- If Mallory replays **Alice's own words** to confuse Bob → **Deceptive Jammer**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Forensic Insight:** Unlike cryptographic attacks, jamming leaves **no digital footprint in packet payloads** — its evidence exists entirely in the **RF spectrum**, **timing anomalies**, and **carrier-sense statistics**, making forensic analysis fundamentally a **signal-processing problem**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following quantitative metrics form the spine of all jamming analysis:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **SNR** | **dB**, typically 10–40 dB for healthy links |
| Jammer-to-Signal Ratio | **JSR** | dimensionless (dB form used) |
| Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio | **SINR** | **dB**, threshold often 10 dB |
| Bit Error Rate | **BER** | dimensionless, 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁹ typical |
| Packet Delivery Ratio | **PDR** | percentage, 0–100 % |
| Boltzmann Constant | **k** | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Thermal Noise Floor | **N₀** | **−174 dBm/Hz** (at 290 K) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Physical Constant:** All RF communication is bounded by the **thermal noise floor** $N_0 = kT$, where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and $T$ is the receiver noise temperature in Kelvin. A jammer must inject energy *above* this floor to be effective.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Jamming Threat Model

A jamming attack is formally described by the **Jamming Game** between a transmitter (Tx), a receiver (Rx), and a jammer (J). The jammer's objective is to minimize the **Shannon capacity** of the channel, while the Tx-Rx pair attempts to maximize it.

The **received signal at the legitimate receiver** in the presence of a jammer is modeled as:

$$y(t) = h_{tx} \cdot s(t) + h_{j} \cdot j(t) + n(t)$$

Where:
- $s(t)$ = legitimate transmitted signal
- $j(t)$ = jamming signal
- $h_{tx}$, $h_{j}$ = channel gains (Rayleigh/Rician fading coefficients)
- $n(t)$ = Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN), $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Jamming Classes

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the following classification:

### 2.2.1 Constant Jammer
Emits **continuous RF energy** regardless of channel activity. Simplest to implement, easiest to detect, and most energy-inefficient.

### 2.2.2 Deceptive Jammer
Injects **legitimate-looking but invalid packets** (e.g., fabricated RTS/CTS in 802.11). Causes nodes to remain in carrier-sense busy state, suppressing their own transmissions.

### 2.2.3 Random Jammer
Alternates between **sleep and jam** states with probability $p_j$. Conserves jammer energy at the cost of partial effectiveness.

### 2.2.4 Reactive Jammer
Listens for legitimate transmissions and **only jams when activity is sensed**. Extremely difficult to detect because the channel appears idle most of the time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A **reactive jammer** with response time $T_{rj} < T_{preamble}$ (the preamble duration of the victim's packet) can corrupt the **entire packet including header**, leading to complete protocol-layer failure.

## 2.3 The Jamming Decision Criterion

A packet is considered **successfully jammed** when the **Bit Error Rate** at the receiver exceeds the Forward Error Correction (FEC) capability, typically when:

$$P_e > P_{FEC}^{max}$$

For an uncoded BPSK link in AWGN plus jamming, the BER is:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot \text{SINR}}\right)$$

Where $Q(\cdot)$ is the **Gaussian Q-function** and SINR is the **Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio** in linear scale.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` or `\mid` is used below instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_0 = kT$ | Thermal noise power spectral density | W/Hz |
| 2 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB | dB |
| 3 | $\text{JSR} = \frac{P_j}{P_s}$ | Jammer-to-Signal Ratio (linear) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{SINR} = \frac{P_s}{P_j + P_n}$ | Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot \text{SINR}}\right)$ | BPSK BER under jamming | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_e = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\text{SNR}}{2}\right)$ | BPSK BER in AWGN (no jamming) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SINR}\right)$ | Shannon Capacity under jamming | bits/second |
| 8 | $\eta_j = \frac{P_j}{P_j + P_s + P_n}$ | Jammer Efficiency Metric | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{detect} = 1 - Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_n}{\sigma_n}\right)$ | Jamming Detection Probability | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{jam} = \frac{E_j}{P_j \cdot t_{active}}$ | Jammer Energy Budget Equation | seconds |

**Where:**
- $P_s, P_j, P_n$ = signal, jammer, and noise powers
- $B$ = channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{x}^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} du$
- $\tau$ = detection threshold
- $E_j$ = total jammer energy budget (Joules)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wireless jamming analysis is foundational in:
- **Military Electronic Warfare (EW)** — radar and comms denial
- **5G/6G network slicing** — Quality-of-Service guarantee validation
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — ensuring uptime in smart factories
- **Drone swarm coordination** — anti-UAV defense systems
- **Forensic investigation of RF-linked crimes** (e.g., triggering a bomb by RF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Distinction:** A network forensic investigator must differentiate between **intentional jamming** and **unintentional RF interference** (e.g., microwave oven leakage, Bluetooth hop collisions). The key discriminator is **temporal correlation with protocol-layer failures** and **directional signal analysis via Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) estimation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BER Degradation Under Constant Jamming

We derive the exact Bit Error Rate expression for a BPSK-modulated signal received in the presence of a constant-tone jammer and AWGN.

### Step 1 — Model the Received Signal

The received signal at the demodulator input is:

$$r(t) = s(t) + j(t) + n(t)$$

Where the legitimate signal is $s(t) = \sqrt{2P_s} \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the jammer tone is $j(t) = \sqrt{2P_j} \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$, and $n(t)$ is AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

### Step 2 — Combine Jammer and Noise into Effective Noise

The jammer and the AWGN are statistically independent. Since the jammer is a deterministic tone (modeled as a random phase $\phi$ uniformly distributed in $[0, 2\pi)$), its contribution can be absorbed into an **equivalent noise term** with variance equal to its power:

$$\sigma_{eq}^2 = \sigma_n^2 + P_j = \frac{N_0 B}{2} + P_j$$

### Step 3 — Apply the BPSK Decision Rule

The matched-filter output for a BPSK symbol of energy $E_b$ gives the decision variable:

$$Z = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + N_{eq}$$

Where $N_{eq} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{eq}^2)$.

### Step 4 — Compute the BER

An error occurs when the noise pushes the decision variable across zero. The probability of this event is:

$$P_e = \Pr[Z < 0 \mid b=+1] = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sigma_{eq}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Equivalent Noise Variance

Plugging $\sigma_{eq}^2 = N_0 B / 2 + P_j$ and noting that $E_b = P_s T_b$ where $T_b$ is the bit duration:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{P_s T_b}{\frac{N_0 B}{2} + P_j}}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Express in Terms of JSR

Dividing numerator and denominator inside the square root by $P_s$, and using the standard substitution $E_b/N_0 = \text{SNR} \cdot (B/R_b)$:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}}}\right)$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for BPSK BER under constant jamming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As $\text{JSR} \to \infty$ (overwhelming jammer), $P_e \to Q(0) = 0.5$ — the receiver is reduced to **random guessing**, which is the maximum possible BER for a binary system.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Jammer-to-Signal Ratio (JSR) for Link Neutralization

We derive the **minimum JSR** required to push the BER above a target threshold (e.g., $P_e^{target} = 10^{-3}$ for voice-grade or $10^{-6}$ for data-grade links).

### Step 1 — Invert the BER Equation

Starting from:

$$P_e^{target} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}_{min}}}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Inverse Q-Function

$$Q^{-1}(P_e^{target}) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}_{min}}}$$

### Step 3 — Square Both Sides

$$\left[Q^{-1}(P_e^{target})\right]^2 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}_{min}}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for JSR_min

$$1 + \text{JSR}_{min} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{\left[Q^{-1}(P_e^{target})\right]^2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{JSR}_{min} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{\left[Q^{-1}(P_e^{target})\right]^2} - 1}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

Suppose $\text{SNR} = 20$ (linear, ≈ 13 dB) and $P_e^{target} = 10^{-3}$.

From Q-function tables: $Q^{-1}(10^{-3}) \approx 3.09$.

$$\text{JSR}_{min} = \frac{2 \times 20}{(3.09)^2} - 1 = \frac{40}{9.548} - 1 \approx 4.19 - 1 = 3.19$$

In dB: $\text{JSR}_{min,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(3.19) \approx 5.04$ dB.

**Conclusion:** The jammer needs only ~5 dB more power than the legitimate signal to neutralize a link operating at 13 dB SNR. This illustrates why **even low-power jammers are devastating**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Jamming Detection Threshold (Statistical Method)

We derive the optimal **detection threshold** $\tau$ for a simple energy-detector based jamming sensor.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Hypotheses

$$H_0: y(n) = n(n) \quad \text{(no jamming, noise only)}$$

$$H_1: y(n) = s_{jam}(n) + n(n) \quad \text{(jamming present)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Test Statistic

The energy detector integrates $N$ samples of the squared received signal:

$$T = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \vert y(n) \vert^2$$

### Step 3 — Distribution Under H₀

Under $H_0$ (AWGN only), $T / \sigma_n^2$ follows a **chi-squared distribution** with $2N$ degrees of freedom (for complex baseband):

$$T \sim \sigma_n^2 \cdot \chi^2_{2N}$$

Mean: $\mu_0 = N \sigma_n^2$, Variance: $\sigma_0^2 = N \sigma_n^4$

### Step 4 — Apply the Neyman-Pearson Criterion

For a target false-alarm rate $P_{FA}$, the threshold is:

$$\tau = \sigma_n^2 \left[Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) \sqrt{2N} + N\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The detection threshold grows with the **square root of the observation window** $N$. This means longer observation windows yield sharper detection — but they also **delay the forensic response**, creating a fundamental speed-vs-accuracy tradeoff.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Jammer Types

| Jammer Type | Energy Efficiency | Detectability | Attack Sophistication | Forensics Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Constant | Very Low | Very High | Low | Easy |
| Deceptive | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| Random | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Reactive | Very High | Very Low | High | Hard |
| Intelligent/Cognitive | Adaptive | Ultra Low | Very High | Very Hard |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners love asking the question — *"Which jammer is hardest to detect, and why?"* The correct answer is the **Reactive Jammer** because it is **silent during idle periods**, producing a statistically normal noise floor most of the time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Jamming Attack Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Legitimate Transmitter Node] --> B[Wireless Channel RF Medium]
    B --> C[Legitimate Receiver Node]
    D[Malicious Jammer Node] --> B
    E[RF Energy Injection Constant or Pulsed] --> D
    F[Channel Sensing Module] --> D
    G[Forensic Capture Point Spectrum Analyzer] --> B
    H[Forensic Capture Point Protocol Logs] --> C
    I[Anomaly Detection Engine BER Threshold] --> G
    J[Anomaly Detection Engine PDR Collapse] --> H
    K[Forensic Report Jam Time Duration Power Estimate] --> I
    K --> L[Forensic Report Jam Time Duration Power Estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The two **forensic capture points** — one at the RF spectrum and one at the protocol layer — must be **time-synchronized** (e.g., via GPS or NTP) to enable cross-layer correlation. This is a KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcome.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Classification of Jamming Attacks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Wireless Jamming Denial of Service] --> S[Active Jamming]
    R --> T[Reactive Jamming]
    R --> U[Passive Jamming Coexistence Exploitation]
    S --> S1[Constant Jammer]
    S --> S2[Deceptive Jammer]
    S --> S3[Random Jammer]
    T --> T1[Reactive Tone Jammer]
    T --> T2[Reactive Packet Jammer]
    T --> T3[Cognitive Reactive Jammer]
    U --> U1[Protocol Aware Coexistence Attack]
    U --> U2[Spectrum Policy Exploitation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Forensic Investigation Workflow for Suspected Jamming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Incident Detection Link Degradation] --> P2[Acquire RF Spectrum Snapshot]
    P2 --> P3[Acquire Protocol Logs from AP]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Statistical Features]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Detection Algorithm Energy or PDR]
    P5 --> P6[Classification Jammer Type]
    P6 --> P7[Direction of Arrival Estimation]
    P7 --> P8[Physical Localization]
    P8 --> P9[Generate Forensic Report]
    P9 --> P10[Legal Action and Mitigation]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Anti-Jamming Defense Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 Physical Spread Spectrum FHSS DSSS] --> L2[Layer 2 MAC Retransmission and Rate Adaptation]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Network Multi Path Routing]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Application Adaptive Coding]
    L4 --> L5[Cross Layer Jamming Aware Protocol Stack]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Effective anti-jamming is **never single-layer**. A defense that uses only spread spectrum (Layer 1) fails against a wideband barrage jammer. Multi-layer defense is the **only forensic-grade mitigation**.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Jamming vs. Interference Decision Tree (Forensic Triage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Observed Network Degradation] --> Q2{Is the Noise Floor Elevated}
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Legitimate Failures Possible}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Hardware or Configuration Issue Not Jamming]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5[Collect More Data]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q6{Is Energy Constant Over Time}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Suspect Constant Jammer]
    Q6 -->|No| Q8{Is Energy Bursty Correlated with Traffic}
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q9[Suspect Reactive Jammer]
    Q8 -->|No| Q10[Suspect Random Jammer or Unintentional Interference]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Wireless Jamming. List its four canonical types. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Wireless jamming is a deliberate Denial-of-Service attack in which a malicious node transmits interfering RF energy on a wireless channel to disrupt or block legitimate communication between authorized nodes.

**Four Types (2 Marks):**
1. **Constant Jammer** — continuously emits RF energy
2. **Deceptive Jammer** — injects valid-looking but illegitimate packets
3. **Random Jammer** — alternates between sleep and jam cycles
4. **Reactive Jammer** — jams only in response to sensed channel activity

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a Constant Jammer and a Reactive Jammer in terms of energy efficiency and detectability. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Constant Jammer | Reactive Jammer |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Efficiency | **Low** — wastes power during idle channel | **High** — only transmits when activity sensed |
| Detectability | **High** — noise floor rises continuously | **Very Low** — silent most of the time |
| Sophistication | **Low** | **High** |
| Forensic Difficulty | **Easy** to detect via energy threshold | **Hard** — requires timing correlation |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The reactive jammer is the **most dangerous from a forensic standpoint** because it leaves minimal RF footprint and demands advanced cross-layer detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the jamming threat model and derive the Bit Error Rate (BER) expression for a BPSK link under constant-tone jamming. State clearly the role of the Jammer-to-Signal Ratio (JSR). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable numerical example, compute the minimum JSR required to push the BER above $10^{-3}$ for a link operating at 15 dB SNR. Discuss the implications for low-power jamming attacks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

### Part (a) — Full Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Threat Model (2 Marks):**

The jamming threat model comprises three entities: a transmitter Tx, a receiver Rx, and a jammer J sharing a common wireless medium. The received signal is:

$$y(t) = h_{tx} \cdot s(t) + h_{j} \cdot j(t) + n(t)$$

**Step 2 — Combine Jammer with Noise (2 Marks):**

For a constant-tone jammer with power $P_j$ and AWGN with variance $\sigma_n^2 = N_0 B / 2$, the equivalent noise variance is:

$$\sigma_{eq}^2 = \frac{N_0 B}{2} + P_j$$

**Step 3 — Derive BER (2 Marks):**

The matched-filter output for BPSK gives:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}}}\right)$$

Where $\text{JSR} = P_j / P_s$ is the **Jammer-to-Signal Ratio** in linear scale. As JSR increases, the argument of the Q-function shrinks, driving BER toward 0.5.

**Step 4 — Role of JSR (1 Mark):**

JSR is the **key knob** controlled by the attacker. Increasing JSR by just a few dB can degrade BER by **orders of magnitude**, especially in high-SNR links.

---

### Part (b) — Full Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the Target BER and SNR (1 Mark):**

Given: $P_e^{target} = 10^{-3}$, $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 15$ dB $\Rightarrow \text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$.

**Step 2 — Use the Inversion Formula (2 Marks):**

$$\text{JSR}_{min} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{\left[Q^{-1}(P_e^{target})\right]^2} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values (2 Marks):**

$Q^{-1}(10^{-3}) \approx 3.0902$

$$\text{JSR}_{min} = \frac{2 \times 31.62}{(3.0902)^2} - 1 = \frac{63.24}{9.549} - 1 = 6.623 - 1 = 5.623$$

In dB: $10 \log_{10}(5.623) \approx 7.5$ dB.

**Step 4 — Implications (2 Marks):**

A jammer with only **7.5 dB more power** than the legitimate transmitter can degrade a 15 dB-SNR link beyond the $10^{-3}$ BER threshold. This demonstrates that **jammer power requirements are modest** — making low-cost SDR-based jammers (e.g., USRP, HackRF) a real-world threat. The forensic implication is that **traditional SNR-based security assumptions are insufficient**; one must monitor BER, PDR, and cross-layer anomalies.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify wireless jamming attacks. With a neat block diagram, explain the role of a forensic investigator in post-incident jamming analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Wi-Fi network operating at 20 dB SNR is targeted by a constant jammer. The measured post-jam BER is $10^{-2}$. Compute (i) the effective SINR, and (ii) the Jammer-to-Signal Ratio (JSR) in dB. Use BPSK modulation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

### Part (a) — Full Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Classification (3 Marks):**

Jamming attacks are classified into four canonical types — **Constant, Deceptive, Random, and Reactive** — with sub-classes such as **Tone, Pulsed, Cognitive, and Protocol-Aware** jammers. Each differs in energy profile, detectability, and attack vector.

**Step 2 — Forensic Investigator Role (2 Marks):**

The forensic investigator performs **spectrum capture** (using SDR tools like RTL-SDR or USRP), **log correlation** (AP/Router logs), **statistical analysis** (BER, PDR, RSSI distributions), **Direction-of-Arrival estimation** (using multiple antennas), and **timeline reconstruction** to attribute the attack.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

The investigation pipeline is: **Incident Detection → RF Spectrum Capture → Protocol Log Acquisition → Cross-Layer Correlation → Jammer Classification → DoA Localization → Forensic Report → Legal Action**.

---

### Part (b) — Full Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20$ dB $\Rightarrow \text{SNR}_{linear} = 100$. $P_e = 10^{-2}$.

**Step 1 — Relate BER to SINR (2 Marks):**

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot \text{SINR}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Inverse Q-Function (1 Mark):**

$$\sqrt{2 \cdot \text{SINR}} = Q^{-1}(10^{-2}) = 2.3263$$

$$\text{SINR} = \frac{(2.3263)^2}{2} = \frac{5.412}{2} = 2.706$$

**Step 3 — Relate SINR to JSR (2 Marks):**

$$\text{SINR} = \frac{\text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}}$$

$$\text{JSR} = \frac{\text{SNR}}{\text{SINR}} - 1 = \frac{100}{2.706} - 1 = 36.95 - 1 = 35.95$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB (2 Marks):**

$$\text{JSR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(35.95) \approx 15.56 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer:** SINR ≈ 2.706 (linear) ≈ 4.32 dB; JSR ≈ 15.56 dB.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** before plugging into the BER formula. SNR(dB) ≠ SNR(linear). The Q-function operates on **linear-scale SINR**.
> 2. **Confusing JSR and JSR(dB)** — they differ by a $\log_{10}$ factor. Markers will deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 3. **Skipping the noise term** when writing the equivalent noise variance. Many students write $\sigma_{eq}^2 = P_j$ alone, forgetting $\frac{N_0 B}{2}$.
> 4. **Misidentifying reactive vs. random jammers** in classification questions — reactive jammers are **event-triggered**, not time-triggered.
> 5. **Failing to mention the Q-function's asymptotic limit** $Q(0) = 0.5$ as the maximum BER for a binary system. This is a **favorite 1-mark follow-up question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Wireless Jamming (DoS Attacks)**

- **Definition:** Wireless jamming = intentional RF interference to deny service.
- **Four Canonical Types:** Constant, Deceptive, Random, Reactive.
- **Most Dangerous Jammer:** Reactive (energy-efficient + low detectability).
- **Easiest to Detect:** Constant (elevated noise floor).
- **Canonical BPSK BER Formula:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{1 + \text{JSR}}}\right)$
- **JSR Inversion Formula:** $\text{JSR}_{min} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{SNR}}{[Q^{-1}(P_e^{target})]^2} - 1$
- **Shannon Capacity Under Jamming:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$
- **Thermal Noise Floor:** $N_0 = kT$ where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Detection Method:** Energy detector with threshold $\tau = \sigma_n^2 [Q^{-1}(P_{FA})\sqrt{2N} + N]$
- **BER Asymptote:** $P_e \to 0.5$ as JSR $\to \infty$ (random guessing).
- **Forensic Footprint:** Jamming evidence lives in **RF spectrum + timing anomalies + PDR collapse**.
- **Anti-Jamming Layers:** Spread Spectrum (FHSS/DSSS) + MAC retransmission + multi-path routing + adaptive coding.
- **Cross-Layer Forensics:** Time-synchronized capture at **Layer 1 (RF)** and **Layer 2 (Protocol)** is mandatory.
- **Key Differentiator:** Intentional jamming vs. unintentional interference — distinguished by **temporal correlation** with protocol failures and **DoA localization**.
- **Forensic Tools:** RTL-SDR, HackRF, USRP, Wireshark, Kismet, and custom BER/PDF analytics.
- **Mathematical Toolbox:** Q-function, chi-squared test, Neyman-Pearson criterion, Shannon-Hartley theorem.
- **Legal Note (India):** Wireless jamming is illegal under the **Indian Telegraph Act, 1885 (Section 5)** and the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (Section 66F)** — a common viva question.

> **End of Module 4 — Wireless Jamming (DoS Attacks) Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Eavesdropping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Eavesdropping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wireless Eavesdropping** (also called *Wireless Sniffing* or *Over-the-Air Interception*) is the unauthorized passive or active interception, monitoring, recording, and analysis of Radio Frequency (RF) communications transmitted over wireless media, without the knowledge or consent of the legitimate sender or receiver. In the context of Network Forensics, wireless eavesdropping is treated as a **Layer 1 / Layer 2 OSI attack vector** where the attacker exploits the **broadcast nature of the wireless medium** to capture, decode, and reconstruct sensitive data frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT864 Module 4):** Wireless eavesdropping is the covert capture of electromagnetic signals propagating between IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), IEEE 802.15 (Bluetooth/Zigbee), cellular (4G/5G), or satellite links, for the purpose of forensic evidence collection or adversarial data theft. The forensic investigator must understand the attacker methodology to reconstruct such events.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Promiscuous Mode** | NIC driver state that captures ALL frames, not just those addressed to it | Core concept for sniffers like Wireshark |
| **Monitor Mode (RFMON)** | Specialized mode for wireless adapters to capture raw 802.11 frames including management/control frames | Forensic evidence collection |
| **RF Propagation** | Spread of electromagnetic energy through free space | Determines eavesdropping range |
| **Spectrum Analysis** | Real-time visualization of RF energy across frequencies | Used in tools like Kismet, Wireshark |
| **Wardriving** | Searching for wireless networks from a moving vehicle | Common entry-stage attack |
| **Warchalking** | Marking physical locations with chalk symbols to indicate open networks | Social engineering aspect |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Invisible Megaphone" Analogy** 🎙️
> Imagine a conversation in a crowded market where a person (the legitimate sender) speaks through a **magical invisible megaphone** that throws their voice across the entire market square. Only the intended listener (receiver) is supposed to understand the words because they speak the same secret language. A wireless eavesdropper is the **silent person standing 50 meters away with a parabolic dish and a recorder**. The speaker has no idea that voice is leaking. The eavesdropper doesn't even need to "break in" — the air itself hands the message over.
>
> In the digital world, the "magic megaphone" is your **Wi-Fi router** broadcasting on **2.4 GHz** or **5 GHz** bands. The **"secret language"** is your WPA2 encryption. The eavesdropper's job is either to **listen in clear** (if encryption is weak or absent) or **break the code** (WEP cracking, WPA-PSK brute force, KRACK attacks).

### 1.4 Why Wireless Eavesdropping Matters in Network Forensics

From a forensic perspective, wireless eavesdropping is a **double-edged sword**:

1. **Offensive View (The Attacker)**: An adversary uses it to steal credentials (e.g., WPA2 handshakes), inject packets, or stage MITM attacks.
2. **Defensive / Investigative View (The Forensic Analyst)**: Investigators use the **same techniques** to:
   - Reconstruct a network breach timeline
   - Capture rogue APs (Evil Twins)
   - Identify unauthorized devices
   - Build a chain of custody for wireless evidence admissible in court

The **fundamental asymmetry** that enables this attack: wireless signals are **omnidirectional**, and any receiver within the **Fresnel zone** of a transmitter can receive them.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speed of Light (RF propagation):** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
> - **Free-space path loss frequency reference:** $f$ in **MHz**
> - **Wi-Fi bands:** **2.4 GHz** (IEEE 802.11b/g/n/ax) and **5 GHz** (IEEE 802.11a/n/ac/ax)
> - **Bluetooth:** **2.4–2.485 GHz** (FHSS, 79 channels)
> - **Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP) IV length:** **24 bits** (cryptographically broken)
> - **WPA2-PSK minimum entropy target:** **128 bits** (when properly implemented)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Wireless Eavesdropping Attack

The attack unfolds in **six structured stages**, each of which must be understood for KTU examination purposes:

1. **Stage 1 — Spectrum Reconnaissance (Passive Discovery)**
   - Attacker enables monitor mode on a wireless adapter (e.g., ALFA AWUS036ACH).
   - Tools: **Kismet**, **Airodump-ng**, **NetStumbler**, **Wireshark** (with radiotap headers).
   - The attacker enumerates SSIDs, BSSIDs (MAC of AP), channel, encryption type (WEP / WPA / WPA2 / WPA3), and signal strength (RSSI in **dBm**).
   - *Forensic mirror:* Investigators do the same to identify all APs in a crime scene perimeter.

2. **Stage 2 — Target Selection and Frame Capture**
   - Once a high-signal target is found (typically **RSSI > -70 dBm** for reliable decryption), the attacker initiates packet capture.
   - **PCAP files** become the forensic artifact.
   - All four 802.11 frame types are captured: **Management**, **Control**, **Data**, and **Extension (802.11n/ac)**.

3. **Stage 3 — Cryptographic Extraction (For Encrypted Networks)**
   - For WPA/WPA2-PSK, the attacker must capture the **4-way EAPOL handshake** between client and AP.
   - For WEP, the attacker collects enough **IVs** (Initialization Vectors) — historically **~40,000 to 85,000** IVs to crack via statistical attacks.
   - For WPA3 (SAE), the attack surface is greatly reduced due to **Dragonfly Key Exchange**.

4. **Stage 4 — Plaintext Recovery / Decryption**
   - Tools: **Aircrack-ng**, **Hashcat** (GPU-accelerated), **John the Ripper**.
   - If PSK is weak (dictionary-based), the handshake is brute-forced offline.
   - Result: Attacker can decrypt the entire traffic stream for that session.

5. **Stage 5 — Application-Layer Reconstruction**
   - Decrypted pcap is analyzed for credentials (HTTP POST, FTP, SMTP, etc.).
   - Tools: **Wireshark**, **NetworkMiner**, **Xplico**, **Cain & Abel**.

6. **Stage 6 — Anti-Forensics / Evasion (Optional)**
   - MAC spoofing, channel hopping, signal-power throttling, and packet-injection to confuse investigators.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why monitor mode is needed:** Standard Wi-Fi NICs in managed mode discard frames not addressed to them (MAC filtering). Monitor mode disables this filter.
- **Why the 4-way handshake is targeted:** It contains a verifiable exchange of the **PMK (Pairwise Master Key)** derived from the PSK, which is the only thing the attacker lacks.
- **Why offline cracking is powerful:** The attacker can use a **rainbow table** or **GPU dictionary** without raising suspicion on the network.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $FSPL(d,f) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $d$ = distance, $f$ = frequency | d in **km**, $f$ in **MHz** |
| 2 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$ | Received Power (Link Budget) | $P_t$ = transmit, $G_t/G_r$ = antenna gains | All in **dBm / dBi / dB** |
| 3 | $r_n = 8.657 \sqrt{\frac{D}{f}}$ | First Fresnel Zone radius at midpoint | $D$ = total link distance, $f$ in GHz | $D$ in **km**, $r_n$ in **m** |
| 4 | $SNR = P_{signal} - P_{noise}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Both in dBm | **dB** |
| 5 | $RSSI_{dBm} = -10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}(d) + A$ | Log-distance path loss model | $n$ = path loss exponent, $A$ = reference | dBm |
| 6 | $P_{received} \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$ (free space) | Inverse-square law for RF | — | — |
| 7 | $t = \frac{N_{IV}}{R_{IV}}$ | Time to collect WEP IVs | $R_{IV}$ = IVs/sec injection | **seconds** |
| 8 | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | **meters** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Always use **$\log_{10}$** with the **base-10** Friis equation. A common student mistake is using the natural log, which produces incorrect FSPL values. Another frequent error: using $f$ in GHz with a formula expecting MHz — this causes a **30 dB error** in the final answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom Sector:** Used in lawful interception frameworks (e.g., India's **CMS / CALEA** equivalents).
- **Military / Defense:** Signals Intelligence (SIGINT) and Electronic Warfare (EW) rely on these principles.
- **Enterprise Security:** Wireless Intrusion Detection Systems (WIDS) like **Cisco Adaptive WIPS** use the same RF mathematics to detect rogue APs.
- **IoT Forensics:** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) sniffing with **Ubertooth One** relies on the same monitor-mode architecture.

### 2.5 Legal and Ethical Considerations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Always mention that wireless eavesdropping is **illegal** under India's **Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 66, 66E, 66F) and the **Indian Telegraph Act, 1885**, unless performed by authorized law enforcement with proper warrants. Forensic analysts must follow the **chain of custody** protocol and obtain proper **Letters of Authorization (LoA)** before deploying sniffers.
