**(b) Ransomware Incident on Radiology Server (7 Marks):**

Given data:
- Affected subjects: $N_{\text{subjects}} = 20{,}000$
- Scans published on leak site: $2{,}000$ (confidentiality breach)
- Lost scans: 8 hours of data (availability breach)
- $T_{\text{detect}} = 03\!:\!00$ UTC
- $T_{\text{contain}} = 11\!:\!30$ UTC

(i) **Time-to-Contain:**

$$ TTC = T_{\text{contain}} - T_{\text{detect}} = 11\!:\!30 - 03\!:\!00 = 8.5 \text{ hours} $$

This exceeds the mature-SOC benchmark of 1 hour, indicating room for improvement in detection automation.

(ii) **Risk Score:**

Assigning values: $L = 5$ (ransomware publication is almost certain to lead to harm), $I = 5$ (medical data leak is catastrophic), $S = 5$ (PHI plus Aadhaar is the highest sensitivity class).

$$ R = L \times I \times S = 5 \times 5 \times 5 = 125 $$

(iii) **Regulatory Notification Decision:**

- $R = 125 \geq 48$ → **High Risk Breach**.
- $E_c = 0$ (ransomware is presumed to have exfiltrated plaintext) → Encryption waiver **not applicable**.
- **Decision:** Mandatory notification to the **Data Protection Board of India within 72 hours** AND to all **20,000 data subjects**, with special outreach to the 2,000 whose scans were published. The hospital must also engage **CERT-In** and initiate a forensic preservation of the ransomware binary and the lateral movement trail.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Personal Data Breach" with "Data Breach" alone.** A personal data breach *must* involve PII. A server outage that affects only system logs (no PII) is NOT a PDB.
> 2. **Always mention the 72-hour clock starts from "awareness"**, not from "occurrence". Many students incorrectly use the time of attack.
> 3. **Encryption waiver is conditional.** Strong encryption ($E_c = 1$) with the key not compromised is required. AES-256 with the key on the same breached server is NOT a waiver.
> 4. **Mention the DPDPA 2023 (India) context** in addition to GDPR. KTU 2024 scheme rewards Indian-context awareness.
> 5. **In risk score computations, do not forget the Sensitivity multiplier $S$.** Many students compute only $L \times I$, losing 2–3 marks per question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Anchor:** Personal Data Breach = breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure, or access to personal data (**GDPR Art. 4(12)**).
- **Three Principal Forms:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- **Three Key Actors:** Data Subject, Data Controller, Data Processor.
- **Risk Formula:** $R = L \times I \times S$, with critical threshold $R_{\text{critical}} = 48$.
- **72-Hour Rule:** Notification to the Data Protection Authority within **72 hours of awareness**.
- **Encryption Waiver:** If $E_c = 1.0$ (data unintelligible to attacker), individual subject notification may be exempted.
- **Penalty Caps:** GDPR = €20M or 4% global turnover; DPDPA India = up to ₹250 Crore per breach.
- **Forensic KPIs:** MTTD, MTTR, TTC — all measured in hours.
- **Indian Context:** **DPDPA 2023** + **CERT-In Directions of 2022** (mandatory 6-hour log retention, incident reporting within 6 hours).
- **Six Kill Chain Stages:** Recon → Initial Access → Persistence → Data Discovery → Exfiltration/Modification → Cover-up.
- **Six Data Source Layers:** Firewall/IDS, Web/App logs, Database audit, EDR, Email gateway, Cloud storage.
- **Final Exam Tip:** Always frame your answer as **Detection → Classification → Risk Scoring → Notification → Containment → Recovery → Lessons Learned** to capture full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need of DPDP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Need of DPDP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP)** refers to the comprehensive legal, technical, and procedural framework mandated by the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA 2023)** of India, designed to govern the **collection, storage, processing, transfer, and disposal** of digital personal data of individuals (Data Principals) by any entity (Data Fiduciary) operating within or outside India when offering goods/services to data principals in India.

In the context of **Network Forensics**, DPDP establishes the **statutory boundaries** within which forensic investigators, incident responders, and SOC analysts must operate while collecting, preserving, and analyzing network-borne evidence that may contain personally identifiable information (PII).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECCT864 explicitly links the "Need of DPDP" to the legal-ethical dimension of network forensic investigations. The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, came into force on **August 11, 2023**, and the Rules were notified on **January 3, 2025** — making it a high-yield, contemporary topic for KTU board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic detective** entering a crime scene. You may collect fingerprints, blood samples, and personal diaries as evidence. However, you **cannot publish the victim's medical records on social media**, nor can you share their personal letters with the media, even if these items helped solve the crime. There must be a **legal cap** on how far you can go with someone's private information.

DPDP works the same way for the **digital world**:

- The **Internet** is the crime scene.
- **Network packets, logs, emails, and browser history** are the "fingerprints."
- The **forensic investigator** is the detective.
- **DPDP is the law** that tells the detective: "You may use this evidence to solve the case, but you **must not misuse, retain longer than necessary, or disclose** the personal data you find inside that evidence."

## 1.3 Why the "Need" of DPDP Arises — Core Drivers

The **necessity** of DPDP is not arbitrary; it emerges from observable, measurable failures in the pre-regulation ecosystem:

| Driver | Observable Problem (Pre-DPDP Era) | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass Surveillance** | ISPs, OTT platforms collecting browsing metadata without consent | Erosion of informational self-determination |
| **Data Brokerage Economy** | Unauthorised sale of user data to third parties | Identity theft, targeted manipulation |
| **Cross-Border Data Flows** | Cloud servers in foreign jurisdictions beyond Indian legal reach | Sovereign data loss |
| **Forensic Over-Collection** | Investigators capturing entire disk images / full packet captures containing PII unrelated to the crime | Privacy violation of bystanders, victims, and suspects |
| **Breach Non-Disclosure** | Companies hiding breaches for years (e.g., the 2018 Aadhaar leak of 1.1 billion records) | Delayed remediation, cascading harm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for Examiners:** The global average cost of a data breach in 2024 was **USD 4.88 million** (per IBM's *Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024*). India specifically averaged **USD 2.55 million** per breach. DPDP's existence is statistically justified by these financial and human-impact figures.

## 1.4 Core Terminology You Must Memorise

The following terms are **board-exam favourites** and appear frequently in KTU 2024 scheme ESE papers:

- **Data Principal**: The individual to whom the personal data relates (the "user," "victim," "subject").
- **Data Fiduciary**: The entity that determines the purpose and means of processing (the "controller" — e.g., a bank, an e-commerce site).
- **Data Processor**: An entity that processes data on behalf of the Data Fiduciary.
- **Personal Data**: Any data about an individual who is identifiable by or in relation to such data.
- **Consent**: A free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous agreement.
- **Data Breach**: Any unauthorised acquisition, sharing, or disclosure of personal data.
- **Significant Data Fiduciary (SDF)**: A class of fiduciaries notified by the Central Government based on volume and sensitivity.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Five-Layer Need Analysis" — Why DPDP is Indispensable for Network Forensics

### Layer 1: Constitutional Foundation (Article 21)
The Indian Supreme Court, in *K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)*, unanimously held that the **Right to Privacy is a fundamental right** under **Article 21** of the Constitution. Any forensic activity that captures, retains, or disseminates personal data without a legal framework violates this right. DPDP operationalises this constitutional mandate.

### Layer 2: Jurisdictional Gap Closure
Pre-DPDP, India had **IT Act 2000 (Sections 43A, 72A)** and the SPDI Rules 2011, but these were:
- **Sector-specific** (only covered sensitive personal data).
- **Consent-based** with weak enforcement.
- **Lacking extraterritorial reach**.

DPDP closes these gaps by applying to **any data fiduciary outside India** if it offers goods/services to data principals in India.

### Layer 3: Proportionality in Evidence Collection
The principle of **data minimisation** demands that forensic investigators collect **only the data strictly necessary** for the investigation. This is the digital equivalent of the Fourth Amendment (US) or Article 20(3) of the Indian Constitution (protection against self-incrimination).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examinable Principle:** *Data Minimisation* states: *"The data collected should be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which it is processed."*

### Layer 4: Breach Notification Obligation
Under **Section 8 of DPDPA 2023**, every Data Fiduciary must notify the **Data Protection Board of India (DPB)** and affected Data Principals of a personal data breach **as soon as possible**, and in any case **within 72 hours**. For a forensic investigator, this means the chain-of-custody documentation must include breach-impact assessment.

### Layer 5: Rights Asymmetry Correction
DPDP grants Data Principals **7 statutory rights**, which directly affect how forensic evidence may be presented:

1. Right to access information about personal data
2. Right to correction and erasure
3. Right to grievance redressal
4. Right to nominate another individual
5. Right to data portability
6. Right to restrict disclosure (in specific cases)
7. Right to avoid automated decision-making

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" in DPDP are **legal-metric** in nature, not engineering equations. They quantify **risk, compliance cost, and impact**.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit / Bound |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **Risk Score (RS)** | $RS = P(\text{breach}) \times I(\text{impact})$ | Probability of breach times impact magnitude | Dimensionless (0–1 normalised) |
| **Consent Validity (CV)** | $CV = \text{Free} \land \text{Specific} \land \text{Informed} \land \text{Unambiguous}$ | Boolean AND of 4 consent conditions | Boolean $\in \{0,1\}$ |
| **Notice Period (NP)** | $NP \leq 72$ | Maximum hours to notify DPB after breach awareness | **hours** |
| **Penalty Ceiling (Penalty)** | $P_{\max} = \text{Rs. } 250 \text{ crore}$ | Maximum financial penalty per breach instance | **INR (₹)** |
| **Child Data Age** | $A < 18 \text{ years}$ | Verifiable parental consent required below this age | **years** |
| **Data Retention (DR)** | $DR \leq T_{\text{necessary}}$ | Retention capped at purpose-fulfilment time | **months / years** |
| **Extraterritorial Scope (ETS)** | $ETS = \text{Offer} \lor \text{Profile} \lor \text{Process}$ | Any of these triggers DPDP applicability | Boolean |
| **Significant Data Fiduciary Threshold** | Volume $\geq V_{t}$ AND Sensitivity $\geq S_{t}$ | Central Government notified thresholds | Qualitative |
| **Anonymisation Quality (AQ)** | $AQ = 1 - \dfrac{\vert \text{Re-identifiable records} \vert}{\vert \text{Total records} \vert}$ | Higher is better | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| **Hash Collision Resistance (HCR)** | $HCR = 2^{n}$ where $n = $ hash length in bits | Minimum 256-bit for forensic hashing | Bit-strength |

> **Table Convention Note:** All absolute value expressions are written using the $\vert$ symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DPDP framework directly impacts the following **network forensic engineering tasks**:

1. **Packet Capture (PCAP) Operations**: PCAP files contain unencrypted payload (HTTP, SMTP, FTP) that may include PII. DPDP mandates that such captures be **stored encrypted, access-logged, and retained only for the duration of the case**.
2. **SIEM / Log Management**: Logs containing usernames, IP addresses, and timestamps are "personal data." Cross-border SIEM providers (Splunk Cloud in US, Sumo Logic, etc.) must demonstrate **contractual safeguards** under DPDP.
3. **Threat Intelligence Sharing**: IOCs (Indicators of Compromise) shared via MISP, STIX/TAXII must be **sanitised** to remove PII before cross-organisation exchange.
4. **Memory Forensics (Volatility Framework)**: Memory dumps contain credentials, session tokens, and chat logs. Post-investigation, these must be **cryptographically erased** if not required as exhibits.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Forensic Compliance Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantifying Forensic Compliance Cost under DPDP

In board examinations, you may be asked to **estimate the penalty exposure** of a forensic investigation that mishandles personal data. The following step-by-step framework is expected by KTU valuators:

### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let:
- $N$ = number of affected data principals (victims of mishandling)
- $S_i$ = sensitivity weight of data category $i$ (e.g., Aadhaar $= 1.0$, email $= 0.2$)
- $D$ = duration of unauthorised retention in days
- $C_e$ = culpability factor (negligent $= 0.5$, wilful $= 1.0$, gross wilful $= 2.0$)

### Step 2: Compute the Personal Data Exposure Index (PDEI)

The Personal Data Exposure Index measures the **cumulative exposure** of sensitive data to the public or unauthorised parties.

$$
\text{PDEI} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \cdot S_i
$$

where $k$ is the number of distinct data categories exposed.

**Conversion logic:** Each record $i$ is weighted by its sensitivity. Aadhaar leakage is weighted ten times more than a generic email leak.

### Step 3: Compute the Compliance Penalty Exposure (CPE)

$$
\text{CPE} = \text{PDEI} \times D \times C_e
$$

**Conversion logic:** The longer the data is exposed and the higher the culpability, the greater the penalty exposure.

### Step 4: Cap Against Statutory Maximum

$$
P_{\text{applicable}} = \min(\text{CPE}, \text{Rs. } 250 \text{ crore})
$$

**Conversion logic:** Regardless of how high the computed exposure becomes, the law caps the penalty at **₹250 crore per instance**.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A forensic vendor's cloud server in Singapore leaked 50,000 Aadhaar numbers ($S = 1.0$) and 200,000 email addresses ($S = 0.2$) for 30 days due to wilful negligence ($C_e = 1.0$). Compute the PDEI, CPE, and applicable penalty.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDEI} &= (50{,}000 \times 1.0) + (200{,}000 \times 0.2) \\
&= 50{,}000 + 40{,}000 \\
&= 90{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPE} &= 90{,}000 \times 30 \times 1.0 \\
&= 2{,}700{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The units are abstract; the statutory cap of **₹250 crore** is the binding ceiling. Hence:

$$
P_{\text{applicable}} = \min(2{,}700{,}000, \, \text{Rs. } 250\text{ crore}) = \text{Rs. } 250 \text{ crore}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** KTU examiners often ask students to demonstrate that they understand the **interaction** between the mathematical model and the statutory cap. Always end with the `min()` notation to show legal awareness.

## 3.2 Forensic Compliance Decision Matrix (Examinable Table)

The following matrix maps **forensic actions** to **DPDP compliance requirements**. Memorise this for 14-mark questions.

| Forensic Action | DPDP Section | Required Safeguard | Penalty Risk |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Capturing live network traffic (Wireshark/tcpdump) | Sec. 4, 6 | Notice + Consent or Legitimate Use | **High** if PII is captured without basis |
| Storing PCAP files in cloud (AWS S3, Azure Blob) | Sec. 8, 16 | Cross-border contract, encryption-at-rest | **Very High** if region is non-compliant |
| Sharing IOCs with peer organisations | Sec. 4(1)(a) | Anonymisation / pseudonymisation | **Medium** |
| Email header forensics in phishing cases | Sec. 6 | Limited retention, purpose limitation | **Low** if headers only, no body |
| Memory dump analysis (RAM forensics) | Sec. 8 | Post-analysis cryptographic erasure | **High** if retained beyond case closure |
| DNS log analysis (passive DNS) | Sec. 6 | Query hashing where feasible | **Low–Medium** |
| Disk image acquisition (EnCase, FTK) | Sec. 6, 8 | Chain-of-custody + access logs | **High** (entire user profile at risk) |
| Presenting PII as court evidence | Sec. 8, 17 | Court-directed disclosure exception | **None** (lawful disclosure) |

## 3.3 Mapping the "Need" to the Five Pillars of Forensics

The "need" of DPDP in network forensics is justified by its alignment with the **five pillars of digital evidence integrity**:

1. **Admissibility** — Evidence collected in violation of DPDP may be challenged under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023).
2. **Authenticity** — Hashing (SHA-256) of evidence must be **DPDP-compliant** when the evidence contains PII.
3. **Chain of Custody** — Must include **DPDP compliance checkpoints** at each transfer point.
4. **Reliability** — Tools used (EnCase, Autopsy, Volatility) must produce forensically sound output that does not incidentally leak PII to vendor clouds.
5. **Non-Interference** — Investigators must not access or copy PII unrelated to the investigation scope.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: Define the term "Data Fiduciary" as per the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023. Why is this role critical in the context of network forensics?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Fiduciary** under Section 2(i) of the DPDPA, 2023 is any person who, alone or in conjunction with other persons, **determines the purpose and means of the processing of personal data**.

In network forensics, the Data Fiduciary is typically the **organisation that has suffered the incident** (e.g., a bank, hospital, or e-commerce platform) and which engages the forensic investigator. Its criticality arises because:

1. The Data Fiduciary bears the **primary statutory liability** for any breach of personal data during forensic handling.
2. The Data Fiduciary must ensure that any **third-party forensic vendor** it engages enters into a valid data processing agreement compliant with DPDP.
3. The Data Fiduciary is the **first responder** to the Data Protection Board in case of a breach notification under Section 8.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for forensic relevance)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: List and briefly explain any three rights granted to a Data Principal under the DPDP Act, 2023.**

**Model Answer:**

The DPDP Act grants Data Principals the following three (of seven) rights:

1. **Right to Access (Section 11)**: A Data Principal may request a summary of her personal data being processed, the processing activities, and the identities of all Data Fiduciaries/Processors with whom data has been shared. *Forensic relevance: Investigators must be prepared to disclose what personal data was extracted during the case.*

2. **Right to Correction and Erasure (Section 12)**: A Data Principal can demand that inaccurate data be corrected and data no longer necessary be erased. *Forensic relevance: Investigators must be ready to redact or expunge PII unrelated to the investigation upon request.*

3. **Right to Grievance Redressal (Section 13)**: Every Data Fiduciary must establish a grievance mechanism and respond within **30 days**. *Forensic relevance: Forensic findings that affect a Data Principal's rights must be communicable in a privacy-respecting manner.*

*(3 marks — 1 mark per right with forensic linkage)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write generic answers about "data protection" without **citing specific Sections** of the DPDP Act 2023. This costs **2–3 marks** per question.
> - Avoid confusing the **IT Act 2000 (Section 43A)** with the **DPDPA 2023**. The IT Act deals with "sensitive personal data or information," whereas DPDP uses the broader term "personal data" with stricter rules.
> - Always link the legal principle to a **forensic engineering artefact** (e.g., PCAP, log, memory dump). Answers that ignore the network forensics angle lose 50% of contextual marks.
> - Do **not** mention **GDPR (EU)** unless the question specifically asks for comparison. KTU prefers Indian-law-centric answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the salient features of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, with specific reference to its applicability in network forensic investigations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the obligations of a Data Fiduciary under DPDPA 2023 when a personal data breach occurs during a network forensic engagement. What is the role of the Data Protection Board of India? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Salient Features & Forensic Applicability:**

The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA), is India's first comprehensive horizontal data protection legislation. Its salient features relevant to network forensics are:

1. **Extraterritorial Applicability (Section 3(b))**: The Act applies to any Data Fiduciary outside India if it offers goods or services to Data Principals in India. For forensic investigators, this means **any cross-border evidence transfer** (e.g., PCAP data sent to a foreign vendor for analysis) is governed by DPDP.

2. **Consent-Based Processing (Section 6)**: Personal data may be processed only for a lawful purpose with explicit consent, or for "certain legitimate uses" (Section 7). Forensic investigators must rely on **legitimate use** grounds, typically framed as *"responding to a cyber security incident"* — a category recognised in the DPDP Rules 2025.

3. **Data Minimisation (Section 4)**: Only data necessary for the purpose may be collected. Forensic acquisition must use **targeted capture** (e.g., specific IP ranges, time windows) rather than blanket full-packet captures when feasible.

4. **Storage Limitation (Section 8(7))**: Personal data must be erased once the purpose is fulfilled, unless retention is mandated by law. Investigators must define a **case-closure-based retention schedule** before data capture begins.

5. **No Criminal Liability on Data Principals**: Unlike the IT Act 2000, the DPDPA 2023 does not penalise data principals — the liability rests entirely on the Data Fiduciary and Processor.

6. **Significant Data Fiduciary Classification**: The Central Government can classify certain fiduciaries (e.g., social media intermediaries, fintechs) as "Significant," imposing additional obligations like DPO appointment, audits, and periodic impact assessments.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per salient feature, with 1 bonus mark for the forensic linkage explanation)*

**Part (b) — Breach Obligations & DPB Role:**

When a personal data breach occurs during a forensic engagement, the following obligations apply:

**Obligations of the Data Fiduciary:**

1. **Breach Notification (Section 8(1))**: Notify the **Data Protection Board of India (DPB)** of the breach **as soon as possible** and in any case **within 72 hours** of awareness.

2. **Notify Affected Data Principals (Section 8(3))**: Inform each affected individual about the breach, the nature of the data exposed, and remedial measures — unless the DPB waives this requirement.

3. **Mitigation Obligation (Section 8(5))**: Take immediate steps to contain the breach, including isolation of affected systems, revocation of compromised credentials, and forensic preservation of memory/disk state.

4. **Record-Keeping**: Maintain internal records of all breaches and submit them to the DPB upon request.

5. **Cooperation with Forensic Investigator**: Provide the forensic team with necessary access, logs, and snapshots while ensuring that **chain-of-custody documentation** includes DPDP compliance checkboxes at each step.

**Role of the Data Protection Board of India (DPB):**

- The DPB is the **adjudicatory body** constituted under **Section 18** of the Act.
- It is headed by a **Chairperson** (a retired judge or eminent person) and includes members with expertise in data protection, law, and IT.
- Its functions include:
  - Receiving and acting on breach notifications
  - Conducting inquiries (Section 19)
  - Imposing penalties (Section 33) up to **₹250 crore per instance**
  - Directing remedial actions
  - Hearing appeals against orders of the Data Fiduciary's grievance officer
  - Issuing directions to Data Fiduciaries and Processors

Forensic investigators may be called upon by the DPB as **technical experts** to explain the forensic findings in plain language, making their reports the primary evidentiary document.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for fiduciary obligations, 4 marks for DPB role with Section 18/19/33 references)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: (a)** "The principle of data minimisation is the single most important compliance lever for network forensic investigators under DPDPA 2023." Critically analyse this statement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the breach notification timelines under the IT Act 2000 (SPDI Rules 2011) and the DPDPA 2023. How does the timeline impact forensic evidence preservation strategy? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Critical Analysis of Data Minimisation in Network Forensics:**

The statement is **substantially correct**, though it requires nuanced qualification.

**Arguments Supporting the Statement:**

1. **Legal Mandate**: Section 4 of DPDPA 2023 explicitly requires that personal data collected be *"limited to what is necessary for the purpose for which it is processed."* In network forensics, "the purpose" is the **incident response or criminal investigation** — not the wholesale harvesting of network traffic.

2. **Operational Discipline**: Data minimisation forces investigators to define a **scope of collection** *before* acquisition — e.g., "TCP/443 traffic from subnet 10.0.0.0/8 between 02:00 IST and 04:00 IST on the incident date." This results in **smaller, faster, more defensible** evidence sets.

3. **Reduced Penalty Exposure**: As derived in Section 3 of these notes, smaller exposure = smaller PDEI = smaller CPE. Data minimisation is a **direct cost-control mechanism** for forensic engagements.

4. **Evidentiary Admissibility**: Evidence that is *over-collected* may be challenged as a "fishing expedition" by the defence or by the Data Principal's counsel. Limited, purposeful collection survives admissibility challenges under **Section 65B / Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam**.

**Counterpoints / Qualifications:**

1. **Technical Reality**: In many incidents (e.g., lateral movement, APT campaigns), the malicious traffic **cannot be pre-scopped** without losing contextual evidence. Investigators may need full-packet captures to reconstruct the attack timeline.

2. **Trade-off with Thoroughness**: Strict data minimisation can hinder the discovery of **unknown unknowns** — i.e., the second-stage malware that the investigator didn't know to look for.

3. **Anonymisation as an Alternative**: Where full capture is unavoidable, the data can be **anonymised in real time** at the capture pipeline (as shown in the 4.4 architecture), satisfying the *spirit* of minimisation even when the *letter* is technically violated.

**Conclusion**: The principle of data minimisation is indeed the most important compliance lever because it is **the only principle that the investigator can actively operationalise** in real time. Consent, retention, and breach notification are mostly ex-post; minimisation is ex-ante.

*(7 marks — 3 for supporting arguments, 2 for counterpoints, 2 for conclusion with forensic engineering linkage)*

**Part (b) — Breach Notification: IT Act 2000 vs DPDPA 2023:**

| Aspect | IT Act 2000 / SPDI Rules 2011 | DPDPA 2023 |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Triggering Event** | Disclosure of "sensitive personal data" | Any unauthorised handling of "personal data" |
| **Timeline to Notify Authority** | Not explicitly mandated by IT Act; SPDI Rules 2011 said "reasonable time" | **Within 72 hours** of awareness (Section 8(2)) |
| **Authority to Notify** | No dedicated body; CERT-In handled advisories | **Data Protection Board of India (DPB)** |
| **Notification to Data Principal** | Not mandatory under IT Act | Mandatory (Section 8(3)), unless waived |
| **Penalty** | Compensation under Section 43A; criminal under Section 72A (max 3 years imprisonment) | **Monetary penalty up to ₹250 crore** (Section 33) |
| **Scope of Data Covered** | Only "sensitive" categories (passwords, financial data, health, sexual orientation, biometrics) | All "personal data" — much broader |

**Impact on Forensic Evidence Preservation Strategy:**

1. **The 72-Hour Clock Starts at Awareness**: Once the Data Fiduciary becomes aware of a breach (often at the moment forensic investigation begins), the clock starts. Investigators must therefore:
   - Have **pre-defined SOPs** for breach notification drafting.
   - Maintain **continuous chain-of-custody** documentation that can be converted into a breach notice within hours.
   - Use **automated timestamping** (NTP-synchronised, RFC 3161 trusted timestamps) on all forensic artefacts.

2. **CERT-In Direction of 2022**: Independently, CERT-In's *Directions of 28 April 2022* require incident reporting within **6 hours** for certain categories — an even tighter window. Forensic tools must therefore have a **rapid-export** feature to produce notification-ready reports.

3. **Volatile Evidence Priority**: The 72-hour notification pressure must not compromise **order of volatility** (RFC 3227). Investigators should:
   - Capture memory and network state first
   - Then proceed to disk and logs
   - Notification drafting can occur in parallel by a separate team member

4. **Retention of Pre-Breach Baseline**: After notification, the DPB may request evidence of pre-breach controls. Investigators must preserve a **"known good" baseline** (e.g., a verified-clean image of an unaffected system) for at least the duration of the DPB inquiry.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for the comparison table, 4 marks for the strategic implications with RFC 3227 and CERT-In references)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Revision — Memorise This for KTU ESE**

- **DPDPA 2023** was enacted on **11 August 2023**; Rules notified on **3 January 2025**.
- The Act applies to **digital personal data** of Data Principals **in India**, with **extraterritorial** reach.
- **7 Data Principal Rights**: Access, Correction/Erasure, Grievance Redressal, Nomination, Portability, Restriction of Disclosure, No Automated Profiling.
- **5 Lawful Grounds for Processing**: Consent (Section 6) + Certain Legitimate Uses (Section 7).
- **72-hour breach notification** to DPB is the most-cited number in KTU papers.
- **Penalty ceiling**: **₹250 crore per instance** — not per breach type, per **instance**.
- **Significant Data Fiduciaries** must appoint a DPO, conduct DPIAs, and undergo periodic audits.
- **Data minimisation** is the most examiner-friendly principle — link it to PCAP scoping, log filtering, and targeted acquisition.
- **Cross-border transfer** is permitted only to countries not specifically restricted by the Central Government (negative list approach, unlike GDPR's whitelist).
- **Children's data** (under 18) requires **verifiable parental consent**; no behavioural tracking or targeted advertising allowed.
- The **Data Protection Board of India (DPB)** is the adjudicatory body; it does **not** sit under the Ministry of Electronics & IT directly — it is statutorily independent.
- Forensic reports must integrate **DPDP compliance checkpoints** into the **chain-of-custody** record; otherwise, evidence may be challenged.
- **Anonymisation** (irreversible) is preferred over **pseudonymisation** (reversible) where feasible, as anonymised data falls **outside** DPDP's scope.
- The **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023** (formerly Indian Evidence Act) replaced Section 65B; forensic certificates must comply with its electronic evidence provisions.
- **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022** work in **tandem** with DPDP — they require 6-hour reporting for certain incident categories and log retention for **180 days**.
- The principle of **purpose limitation** (Section 4) means forensic data collected for incident response **cannot be repurposed** for marketing, profiling, or unrelated internal investigations.
- **Automated decision-making** under Section 17 is restricted — explainable AI requirements may apply if forensic ML tools classify a Data Principal.
- The Act **does not apply** to data of deceased individuals, anonymised data, or data processed for personal/household purposes.

> **Quick Recall Mnemonic (for the 7 Data Principal Rights):**
> **"A-C-E-G-N-P-R"** → **A**ccess, **C**orrection, **E**rasure, **G**rievance, **N**omination, **P**ortability, **R**estriction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Features of DPDP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT864 - NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Key Features of DPDP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** is the primary legislation enacted by the Parliament of India to govern the **processing of digital personal data**. It establishes a framework for protecting the privacy of individuals (referred to as **Data Principals**) while balancing the lawful processing of data by organizations (**Data Fiduciaries**). The Act introduces the **Data Protection Board of India (DPB)** as the adjudicatory authority for disputes and breaches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The DPDP Act 2023 replaced the older IT Act (2000/2008 amendments) provisions related to sensitive personal data and is now the **definitive legal standard** for digital forensics investigations involving personal data in India.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (As Defined in Section 2 of the Act)

| Term | Definition (Section 2(t), 2(i), 2(k)) |
|---|---|
| **Data** | Any representation of information, including electronic, digital, or physical form. |
| **Digital Personal Data** | Data about an individual who can be identified from it, or from it combined with other data. |
| **Data Principal** | The individual to whom the personal data relates (the user/citizen). |
| **Data Fiduciary** | Any person/organization that determines the purpose and means of processing personal data. |
| **Data Processor** | An entity that processes data on behalf of the Data Fiduciary. |
| **Consent** | A free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous agreement to process data. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The House & Guest Rule"**
> Imagine your personal data is a **valuable asset inside your house**. Under DPDP, you (the **Data Principal**) are the homeowner. Companies (the **Data Fiduciary**) are like **guests** who wish to enter. The DPDP Act is the **rulebook** that says:
> 1. Guests must **knock and ask permission** (Consent) before entering.
> 2. Guests must state **why they are visiting** (Purpose Limitation & Notice).
> 3. Guests cannot **damage your belongings** (Data Protection obligations).
> 4. If a guest **misbehaves**, there is a **judge** (Data Protection Board) to punish them.
> 5. A network forensic investigator is like a **detective** — they must operate strictly **inside the boundaries of this rulebook** to ensure the evidence they collect is admissible in court.

### 1.4 Why is DPDP Critical for Network Forensics?

In **network forensics**, investigators often intercept, log, and analyze packets, traffic, and communications — all of which may contain **personal data**. The DPDP Act directly dictates:
- What data a forensic investigator can **legally collect**.
- How **breach evidence** is handled and reported.
- The **penalties** for non-compliance during forensic acquisition.
- The **rights of the accused** (data erasure after case closure).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 7 Key Features of DPDP Act 2023

The DPDP Act is structured around **seven core pillars** that every KTU student must memorize. These are the *most-asked features* in examinations.

#### **Feature 1: Extraterritorial Applicability (Section 3(b))**

The Act applies to:
- Data processing within the **territory of India**.
- Data processing **outside India** if it is related to offering goods or services to Data Principals in India.

> **Forensic Implication:** An MNC with servers in Singapore serving Indian customers is still bound by DPDP.

#### **Feature 2: Consent-Based Framework (Section 6 & 7)**

Personal data can only be processed for a **lawful purpose** with the **free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous consent** of the Data Principal. A notice must be provided **before** or along with the consent request.

#### **Feature 3: Rights of the Data Principal (Section 11–14)**

The Act grants Data Principals the following enforceable rights:
1. **Right to access** a summary of their personal data and processing activities.
2. **Right to correction and erasure** of their personal data.
3. **Right of grievance redressal** — Data Principals can file complaints with the Data Fiduciary.
4. **Right to nominate** another individual to exercise their rights in case of death or incapacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Exam Point:** The **Right to Erasure** directly impacts forensic evidence retention. Investigators must be aware that logs/evidence containing personal data **cannot be retained indefinitely** after the legal purpose is fulfilled.

#### **Feature 4: Obligations of Data Fiduciaries (Section 8, 8A, 9, 10)**

Data Fiduciaries must:
- Process data only for the **purpose consented to**.
- Implement **reasonable security safeguards** to prevent breaches.
- Notify affected Data Principals and the **Data Protection Board** of any **personal data breach** promptly.
- Delete data once the **purpose is fulfilled** or upon withdrawal of consent.
- Publish a **business contact** for grievance redressal.

#### **Feature 5: Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs) (Section 10)**

The Central Government may classify certain Data Fiduciaries as **Significant Data Fiduciaries** based on:
- **Volume and sensitivity** of data processed.
- **Risk to data principals**.
- **Potential impact on the sovereignty and integrity of India**.

**Additional obligations of SDFs:**
- Appoint a **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** based in India.
- Conduct an independent **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)**.
- Conduct periodic **audits**.

#### **Feature 6: Data Protection Board of India (Section 18–25)**

- An **adjudicatory body** established by the Central Government.
- Has the powers of a **civil court** (summons, evidence, discovery).
- Can impose **penalties** on Data Fiduciaries for non-compliance.
- Data Principals can file **complaints** with the Board directly.

#### **Feature 7: Penalties for Non-Compliance (Section 33)**

The Act enforces **monetary penalties** (no criminal prosecution under DPDP). Key penalty tiers include:

| Violation | Maximum Penalty (INR) |
|---|---|
| Failure to take reasonable security safeguards | Up to **₹250 Crore** |
| Failure to notify the Board of a breach | Up to **₹200 Crore** |
| Failure to fulfill Data Principal obligations | Up to **₹50 Crore** |
| Other violations | Up to **₹50 Crore** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Note:** These penalties are significantly **higher** than those under the older IT Act, signaling strong legislative intent.

### 2.2 Exemptions and Notable Provisions

| Provision | Description |
|---|---|
| **Section 17(1)–(2)** | Exemptions for State functions: sovereignty, public order, security, prevention of offences, etc. |
| **Children's Data (Section 9)** | Verifiable parental consent required before processing data of children under **18 years**. |
| **Cross-Border Transfer (Section 16)** | Government may restrict transfer of personal data to **specific countries** via notification. |
| **Deemed Consent (Section 7(b))** | Specific "legitimate uses" allow processing without explicit consent (e.g., medical emergencies, employment). |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Forensic Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While DPDP is a legal framework, the following "operational formula" helps students map forensic actions to legal compliance.

$$
\text{Lawful Processing} = \text{Consent} \lor \text{Legitimate Use} \quad \text{(Section 6, 7)}
$$

$$
\text{Erasure Deadline} = T_{\text{purpose fulfilled}} \quad \text{(Section 8(7))}
$$

$$
\text{SDF Threshold} = f(V, S, R) 
$$
$$
\text{where } V = \text{Volume}, S = \text{Sensitivity}, R = \text{Risk to Sovereignty}
$$

$$
\text{Forensic Admissibility} \propto \text{Compliance with DPDP} \land \text{Chain of Custody}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application of DPDP |
|---|---|
| **SIEM / SOC Tools** | Must redact personal data from logs to comply with consent scope. |
| **Cloud Forensics** | Cross-border data transfer clauses dictate which foreign cloud servers are accessible. |
| **Incident Response** | The **72-hour breach notification** rule (akin to GDPR) drives IR timelines. |
| **e-Discovery** | Right to erasure may compel deletion of certain evidence categories post-investigation. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Forensic Activity → DPDP Compliance

Since DPDP is a regulatory framework, the "derivation" in this context is the **logical flow of obligations and rights** as they apply to a network forensic investigator.

#### **Step 1: Identify the Role of the Organization**

$$
\text{Role} = \begin{cases} \text{Data Fiduciary (DF)} & \text{if purpose } \& \text{ means of processing are determined} \\ \text{Data Processor (DP)} & \text{if processing on behalf of a DF} \\ \text{Not Applicable} & \text{if data is anonymized} \end{cases}
$$

> **Step Logic:** A forensic investigator employed by an organization to analyze the company's own logs is a **Data Processor**. An investigator contracted by an external client to perform independent forensics is a **Data Fiduciary** in the context of the investigation.

#### **Step 2: Verify the Lawful Basis for Collection**

$$
\text{Lawful Basis} = \text{Explicit Consent} \;\lor\; \text{Deemed Consent (Section 7(b))}
$$

$$
\text{If False} \Rightarrow \text{Collection is UNLAWFUL} \Rightarrow \text{Penalty up to } \mathbf{\text{₹250 Crore}}
$$

> **Step Logic:** Network forensic tools like **Wireshark, tcpdump, or Zeek** capture **every packet** by default. To be DPDP-compliant, the investigator must apply a **capture filter** (BPF) that isolates only the **lawfully consented** data flows (e.g., traffic monitored under an AUP or corporate policy).

#### **Step 3: Apply the Purpose Limitation Principle**

$$
\text{Data Usage Scope} = \text{Purpose for which Consent was given}
$$

$$
\text{Excess Data} = \text{Data Collected} - \text{Essential Data for Purpose}
$$

> **Step Logic:** If a network admin collects full packet captures (PCAPs) to investigate a **DDoS attack** but the PCAPs also contain **employee email credentials**, the admin has captured data beyond the DDoS purpose. This is an **over-collection violation**.

#### **Step 4: Apply the Data Retention Policy**

$$
T_{\text{retention}} \leq T_{\text{consent validity}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{erasure}} = T_{\text{purpose fulfilled}} + \Delta t_{\text{legal hold}}
$$

> **Step Logic:** In a forensic investigation, $\Delta t_{\text{legal hold}}$ extends the retention until **all legal proceedings are concluded**. After that, data must be **erased** unless anonymized.

#### **Step 5: Breach Detection and Notification**

$$
T_{\text{notification}} \leq T_{\text{regulatory threshold}} \quad (\text{typically 72 hours})
$$

$$
\text{Penalty} = \begin{cases} \text{₹200 Crore} & \text{if Board is not notified} \\ \text{₹250 Crore} & \text{if security safeguards are inadequate} \end{cases}
$$

> **Step Logic:** A network forensic investigator who detects a **data exfiltration incident** has a **dual obligation** — preserve evidence AND trigger the breach notification workflow.

### 3.2 Comparison Matrix: DPDP vs. IT Act, 2000 (vs. GDPR)

This is a **frequently tested analytical question** in KTU examinations.

| Parameter | **DPDP Act 2023** | **IT Act 2000/2008** | **GDPR (EU)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Digital personal data | All electronic data | All personal data (digital & physical) |
| **Extraterritorial** | Yes (Sec 3(b)) | Limited | Yes (Art. 3) |
| **Consent** | Explicit, informed, unconditional | Implicit under "consent + contract" | Explicit, freely given, specific |
| **Data Fiduciary Registration** | Not required (except SDFs) | Not required | Required (DPO/Representative) |
| **Penalties** | Up to **₹250 Crore** | Up to ₹5 Crore (Sec 43A) | Up to **€20M or 4% of global turnover** |
| **Adjudicatory Body** | Data Protection Board | Adjudicating Officer (Cyber Appellate Tribunal) | National Supervisory Authority (e.g., CNIL) |
| **Right to Erasure** | Yes (Sec 12) | No explicit right | Yes (Art. 17) |
| **Cross-border Transfer** | Government notified list | Unrestricted | Adequacy decision required |

### 3.3 Logical Derivation: Why DPDP Matters to a Forensic Investigator

$$
\boxed{\text{Network Data} \cap \text{Personal Data} \neq \emptyset}
$$

$$
\therefore \; \text{All forensic collection} \subset \text{Data Processing}
$$

$$
\therefore \; \text{Investigator} \in \{\text{Data Fiduciary}, \text{Data Processor}\}
$$

$$
\therefore \; \text{Investigator must comply with DPDP} \quad \text{(Sec 8, 8A, 10)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Every packet capture, every log file, every memory dump is potentially subject to DPDP. The forensic investigator is **legally accountable** for compliance.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DPDP Ecosystem Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete data flow** under DPDP and the role of a **Network Forensic Investigator** within it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Data Principal: The Individual whose data is being processed]
    nodeB[Data Fiduciary: Determines purpose and means of processing e.g. Banks, ISPs, Social Media]
    nodeC[Data Processor: Processes data on behalf of the Data Fiduciary e.g. Cloud Vendor, Forensic Tool Vendor]
    nodeD[Data Protection Board of India: Adjudicatory authority for disputes and penalties]
    nodeE[Central Government: Notifies rules, designates Significant Data Fiduciaries, restricts cross-border transfer]
    nodeF[Network Forensic Investigator: Collects and analyzes digital evidence from networks]

    nodeA -- Provides Explicit Consent and Notice Request --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Issues Notice and Request for Consent --> nodeA
    nodeB -- Engages and Provides Data for Processing --> nodeC
    nodeB -- Files Breach Notification in case of Incident --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Imposes Penalties and Resolves Complaints --> nodeB
    nodeE -- Notifies Cross Border Transfer Restrictions --> nodeB
    nodeE -- Designates Significant Data Fiduciaries --> nodeB
    nodeF -- Performs Forensics Operates within DPDP Boundaries --> nodeB
    nodeF -- Provides Forensic Evidence to --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Issues Subpoenas to --> nodeF
    nodeA -- Files Grievance or Complaint --> nodeD
    nodeA -- Exercises Right to Erasure Correction Access --> nodeB
```

### 4.2 Forensic Investigation Workflow Under DPDP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStep1[Step 1: Identify lawful basis for data collection]
    nodeStep2[Step 2: Apply capture filters to limit data to scope]
    nodeStep3[Step 3: Collect and preserve evidence with chain of custody]
    nodeStep4[Step 4: Analyze evidence within purpose limitation]
    nodeStep5[Step 5: Check for personal data breach]
    nodeStep6[Step 6: Notify Data Protection Board if breach detected]
    nodeStep7[Step 7: Retain only essential data erase rest after investigation]

    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5
    nodeStep5 -- Breach Detected --> nodeStep6
    nodeStep5 -- No Breach --> nodeStep7
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7
```

### 4.3 Rights vs Obligations Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DP_Rights[Rights of Data Principal Section 11 to 14]
        nodeR1[Right to Access Summary of Data]
        nodeR2[Right to Correction and Erasure]
        nodeR3[Right to Grievance Redressal]
        nodeR4[Right to Nominate]
    end

    subgraph DF_Obligations[Obligations of Data Fiduciary Section 8 to 10]
        nodeO1[Issue Notice and Obtain Consent]
        nodeO2[Implement Reasonable Security Safeguards]
        nodeO3[Notify Board of Breach]
        nodeO4[Delete Data upon Purpose Fulfillment]
        nodeO5[Appoint DPO and Conduct DPIA if Significant Data Fiduciary]
    end

    DP_Rights -->|Enforced by| DF_Obligations
    DF_Obligations -->|Reported to| DPB[Data Protection Board]
    DP_Rights -->|Complaints to| DPB
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
Define the term **"Data Fiduciary"** as per the DPDP Act, 2023. How is it different from a **"Data Processor"**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per **Section 2(i)** of the DPDP Act, 2023, a **Data Fiduciary** is any person or entity that, alone or in conjunction with others, **determines the purpose and means of the processing of personal data**.
A **Data Processor** (Section 2(j)), on the other hand, is an entity that **processes personal data on behalf of the Data Fiduciary** without determining the purpose. In short, the **Data Fiduciary decides "why"** the data is processed, while the **Data Processor decides "how"** it is processed under instructions from the Fiduciary.
*(1 + 2 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
List any **three rights of a Data Principal** under the DPDP Act, 2023.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three rights of a Data Principal under the DPDP Act, 2023 are:
1. **Right to access** a summary of personal data and processing activities (Section 11).
2. **Right to correction and erasure** of personal data (Section 12).
3. **Right to nominate** another individual to exercise these rights in case of death or incapacity (Section 14).
*(1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
(a) Explain the **concept of Consent** under the DPDP Act, 2023. What are the key requirements for consent to be legally valid? **(7 marks)**
(b) Discuss the **penalties** prescribed under the DPDP Act, 2023 for **non-compliance** by a Data Fiduciary. Mention at least **three specific violations** and their corresponding penalty tiers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Consent under DPDP Act, 2023 (7 marks):**

As per **Section 6 and 7** of the DPDP Act, personal data may be processed only for a **lawful purpose** and only with the **consent of the Data Principal**.

The Act specifies that valid consent must be:
1. **Free** – obtained without coercion, fraud, or undue influence.
2. **Specific** – tied to a clearly defined purpose, not a blanket approval.
3. **Informed** – the Data Principal must receive a clear **Notice** (in English or any of the 22 languages of the 8th Schedule) describing the personal data being processed and the purpose.
4. **Unconditional** – no hidden conditions or pre-ticked boxes.
5. **Unambiguous** – a clear affirmative action by the Data Principal.

The consent request must include an **itemized notice** describing:
- The personal data and the purpose of processing.
- The manner of exercising the right to withdraw consent.
- The manner of making a complaint to the Data Protection Board.

Consent can be **withdrawn at any time**, and the Data Fiduciary must honor it, with effects being prospective.

*(1 mark for each of the 5 elements + 2 marks for notice and withdrawal provisions = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Penalties under DPDP Act, 2023 (7 marks):**

Unlike the IT Act, the DPDP Act imposes **monetary penalties** (no criminal prosecution). As per **Section 33**, the following key violations carry specific penalties:

| S. No. | Violation | Maximum Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Failure to take **reasonable security safeguards** to prevent personal data breach | Up to **₹250 Crore** |
| 2 | Failure to **notify the Data Protection Board** of a breach | Up to **₹200 Crore** |
| 3 | Failure to fulfill additional obligations as a **Significant Data Fiduciary** (e.g., not appointing DPO, not conducting DPIA) | Up to **₹150 Crore** |
| 4 | Failure to comply with **Data Principal's rights** (access, correction, erasure) | Up to **₹50 Crore** |
| 5 | Other general violations of the Act | Up to **₹50 Crore** |

The **Data Protection Board** determines the actual quantum of penalty based on factors such as the **gravity of the violation**, the **volume of data involved**, and the **previous track record** of the Data Fiduciary.

*(2 marks for framing + 1.5 marks each for three violations = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a) What is a Significant Data Fiduciary (SDF) under the DPDP Act? Discuss the criteria for designation and the additional obligations imposed on SDFs. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the role and powers of the Data Protection Board of India. How does a Data Principal file a complaint with the Board? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Significant Data Fiduciary (7 marks):**

As per **Section 10** of the DPDP Act, the **Central Government** may designate certain Data Fiduciaries as **Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs)** based on:
1. The **volume and sensitivity** of personal data processed.
2. The **risk** of harm to Data Principals.
3. The **potential impact on the sovereignty and integrity of India**, and the security of the State.

Examples of entities that may be designated as SDFs include large **social media platforms, e-commerce giants, telecom providers, and digital lending companies**.

**Additional obligations of SDFs include:**
- Appointment of a **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** based in India, who will act as the point of contact for the Data Protection Board and Data Principals.
- Appointment of an **independent data auditor** to audit the Data Fiduciary's compliance.
- Conducting a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** periodically.
- Performing **periodic audits** to ensure compliance.

*(2 marks for definition + 2 marks for criteria + 3 marks for obligations = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Data Protection Board of India (7 marks):**

The **Data Protection Board of India (DPB)** is established under **Section 18** of the DPDP Act as the **adjudicatory authority** for disputes related to the Act.

**Composition:**
- The Board consists of a **Chairperson** and other members appointed by the Central Government.

**Powers of the Board (Section 19):**
- Powers of a **civil court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (e.g., summoning, evidence, discovery).
- Power to **impose monetary penalties** under Section 33.
- Power to **direct Data Fiduciaries** to take corrective measures.
- Power to **refer matters for criminal prosecution** to the relevant authorities.

**Filing a Complaint (Section 27):**
1. A Data Principal must first file a complaint with the **Data Fiduciary's grievance officer**.
2. If the complaint is **not resolved within 30 days**, or if the Data Principal is dissatisfied, they may file a complaint with the **Data Protection Board**.
3. The Board may conduct an inquiry and pass appropriate orders.

*(2 marks for role + 3 marks for powers + 2 marks for complaint process = 7 marks)*

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in DPDP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing DPDP with IT Act:** Many students cite Section 43A of the IT Act (which dealt with "sensitive personal data") instead of DPDP Sections. This is **outdated** and will result in **loss of up to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting "Digital" Personal Data:** The DPDP Act **excludes non-digital personal data** from its scope. Citing it as a general data protection law is incorrect.
> 3. **Confusing Penalty Figures:** Memorize **₹250 Crore** (security), **₹200 Crore** (breach notification), **₹150 Crore** (SDF obligations), and **₹50 Crore** (general). Mixing them up is a common error.
> 4. **No Criminal Prosecution:** Unlike the IT Act, DPDP imposes **only monetary penalties** — no jail term. Forgetting this distinction loses marks.
> 5. **Not Mentioning "Itemized Notice":** Valid consent under DPDP requires an **itemized notice** in the 22 scheduled languages. Generic "I agree" checkboxes are NOT sufficient.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Key Features of DPDP**

- **DPDP = Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** — India's primary data protection law.
- **Extraterritorial scope (Sec 3(b))** — applies to data processed outside India if offered to Indian Data Principals.
- **Data Principal = Individual**; **Data Fiduciary = Controller**; **Data Processor = Processor**.
- **Consent must be Free, Specific, Informed, Unconditional, and Unambiguous** (5 attributes).
- **Itemized notice** is mandatory before/at the time of consent.
- **Data Principal Rights:** Access, Correction, Erasure, Grievance, Nomination.
- **Data Fiduciary Duties:** Notice, Consent, Security, Breach Notification, Erasure, Grievance Officer.
- **Significant Data Fiduciary (SDF):** Volume + Sensitivity + Risk → DPO + DPIA + Audit required.
- **Data Protection Board of India (DPB):** Adjudicatory body, civil court powers, imposes penalties.
- **Penalties:** Up to **₹250 Crore** (security) / **₹200 Crore** (breach notification) / **₹50 Crore** (general).
- **Children under 18:** Verifiable **parental consent** required.
- **Cross-border transfer:** Restricted via **government notification** (negative list).
- **Deemed Consent (Sec 7(b)):** Medical emergencies, employment, public functions.
- **Forensic Relevance:** Investigators are bound by DPDP when collecting network data; evidence must be lawfully obtained to be admissible.
- **Right to Erasure:** Limits retention of personal data even in forensic cases.
- **No criminal prosecution** under DPDP — only **monetary penalties**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
